Acanthocereus oaxacensis (Britton & Rose) Lodé sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
Acanthocereus tetragonus (L.) Hummelinck sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_common_name: Bejunco; Cruceta; Nopal de Cruz; Nopal de tres lomos; Órgano-alado de Pitaya; Triangle Cactus
iucn_locations: Honduras; Trinidad and Tobago; Guerrero; Panama; Quintana Roo; Colombia; Oaxaca; Bonaire; Veracruz; Guatemala; Sinaloa; Sonora; Nicaragua; Querétaro; Nayarit; Saba; Yucatán; San Luis Potosí; Sint Eustatius; Tamaulipas; Saint Martin (French part); Michoacán; Texas; Costa Rica; Sint Maarten (Dutch part); Mexico; Jalisco; United States; El Salvador; Campeche; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Cuba; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Chiapas; Colima; Florida; Belize
iucn_location_codes: HN; TT; MXS-GR; PA; MXT-QR; CO; MXS-OA; NLA-BO; MXG-VC; GT; MXN-SI; MXN-SO; NI; MXE-QU; MXS-NA; NLA-SA; MXT-YU; MXE-SL; NLA-SE; MXE-TA; MF; MXS-MI; TEX-OO; CR; SX; MX; MXS-JA; US; SV; MXT-CA; VE; CU; BQ; MXT-CI; MXS-CL; FLA-OO; BZ
iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Coastal Sand Dunes
iucn_doc_range: Acanthocereus tetragonus is one of the most widespread cactus species, occurring in the US (Texas and Florida), Mexico (16 states), Central America and the Caribbean, Lesser and Netherlands Antilles, Venezuela and Columbia (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations from 0 to 1,700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Acanthocereus tetragonus occurs in sandy or rocky soils in tropical deciduous, subdeciduous, and moist forests, spiny scrublands, thorny forests, and coastal dunes and cliffs. The species is tolerant of disturbance and also tolerated by people (i.e., humans tend to leave it in the landscape). The flowers are open at night and the species is pollinated by hawkmoths.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is widespread and very abundant throughout its range. There are no known threats, hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Acharagma aguirreanum (Glass & R.A.Foster) Glass sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_synonyms: Escobaria aguirreana (Glass & R.A.Foster) N.P.Taylor; Gymnocactus aguirreanus Glass & R.A.Foster
iucn_doc_range: Occurs in Sierra de la Paila over a range of 1 km². It is estimated that there are less than 1,000 plants.
iucn_doc_habitats: In semi-desert among calcareous rocks at an altitude of about 1,500 m.
iucn_doc_rationale: This listing is based on the fact that it occurs in a single tiny location (only 1 km²), with less than 1,000 plants in a continuing decline due to illegal collecting.

checklist_taxon: Acharagma roseanum (Boed.) E.F.Anderson sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_synonyms: Escobaria roseana (Boed.) Buxb.; Echinocactus roseanus Boed.
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican states of Coahuila (Hernández et al. 2004, Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011) and Nuevo León (subsp. galeanense).
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows on rocky limestone hills in xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: Overgrazing of the habitat by goats is the only threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has an extent of occurrence of under 6,000 km², it is known from less than ten locations which are severely fragmented (the subsp. galeanense is very disjunct) and there is continuing decline in the quality of the habitat due to overgrazing by goats, hence it is listed as Vulnerable.

Ariocarpus bravoanus H.M.Hern. & E.F.Anderson sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Work & other activities; Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Medicine - human & veterinary; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This cactus can be found on the edge of the Chihuahuan Desert, in the state of San Luis Potosí, Mexico. Early observations indicated that this species occurred in a single small area, where it had been accidentally discovered. The original location has become well-known to collectors and the population has been severely depleted, with more than 70% of the plants removed within the late 1990s. However, recent fieldwork has shown additional nearby subpopulations that are at present undisturbed. The decision to place Ariocarpus fissuratus var. hintonii as a subspecies under this species, greatly expanded the known range. The species occurs at elevations raging from 1,500 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in xerophytic shrubland among outcrops of limestone rocks. The habitat is locally classified as matorral desértico micrófilo (Rzedowski 1978).
iucn_doc_threats: One location is very near a village, and there is a serious threat of habitat alteration. The site is also known to collectors and there is definite evidence of illegal collecting. In fact, plants of this species have been confiscated in Europe. Other sites are far from villages or other human activities, so there is less likelihood that those localities will be affected by habitat alteration. However, the number of individual plants in each locality (except for the newly discovered one) is only in the thousands, so collectors could quickly decimate any of these small populations. Local people also use several Ariocarpus species, including A. bravoanus, for medicinal purposes. One of the subpopulations of the nominate subspecies was destroyed by agricultural development, which turned the area into a maize field. A graduate student from Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM) painted all the plants at the type locality with a red enamel paint and also put a copper nail into each plant with coloured beads attached as part of a study to determine the growth rates of the species. This served to highlight where the plants were to collectors, and as a result all the healthy and best-looking plants were removed. It is suspected that the growth rate and reproductive potential of the plants would be reduced as a consequence of the paint (Hernández 2008).
iucn_doc_rationale: The extent of occurrence for Ariocarpus bravoanus is about 2,000 km², the population is severely fragmented, and there is continuing decline due to the impacts of illegal collection, agricultural activities and other human disturbance. Some of the locations have been severely depleted by collectors and at least one was completely destroyed by agriculture. The species is therefore listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(ii,iii,v). Some localities are still untouched, but should collectors find these, the status of the species may need to be reassessed. Both subspecies would also qualify for listing as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Ariocarpus fissuratus (Engelm.) K.Schum. sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_locations: Chihuahua; Zacatecas; Texas; Durango; Coahuila; Mexico; United States
iucn_location_codes: MXE-CU; MXE-ZA; TEX-OO; MXE-DU; MXE-CO; MX; US
iucn_doc_range: The species is distributed in the Mexican states of Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, and Zacatecas, and in the United States in Texas (Hernández et al. 2004). It occurs from Presidio County, Texas southeast along the Rio Grande and near the lower Pecos River. In Mexico, it occurs near the Rio Grande in the adjacent states of Chihuahua and Coahuila. This cactus grows at altitudes between 500 and 1,170 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on limestone plains (and areas of gentle slopes) in regions of xerophyllous scrub, rarely occurring in gypseous soils (Powell and Weedin 2004). It occurs on hills or ridges in desert at altitudes from 500 to 1,170 m asl (Kartesz 1994, Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011).
iucn_doc_threats: There is illegal collection of the species from the wild but at a diminishing rate.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ariocarpus fissuratus is considered to be Least Concern as it is wide ranging, occurs in relatively large populations, and is not declining enough to trigger a threatened listing.

checklist_taxon: Ariocarpus kotschoubeyanus (Lem.) K.Schum. sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Zacatecas; Tamaulipas; Nuevo León; Coahuila; Querétaro; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-SL; MXE-ZA; MXE-TA; MXE-NL; MXE-CO; MXE-QU; MX
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming; Type Unknown/Unrecorded; Housing & urban areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is widely distributed in Mexico. It occurs from west of Saltillo, Coahulia and Nuevo León southwards into the state of Querétaro (Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011). The extent of occurrence is over 60,000 km². However, the area of occupancy is quite small because of the specialized nature of its habitat (Hernández et al. 2010).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in and around the edges of the Chihuahuan Desert, where it grows on calcareous or gypsum soils. The species is mostly confined to silty flood plains (Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011). Some of these areas become flooded periodically during the summer rainy season. As the plants never rise above ground level, they are very difficult to see; in addition, silt often blows over them in the dry season, completely hiding them (Anderson et al. 1994). The habitat is locally classified as matorral desértico micrófilo and mezquital extradesértico (Rzedowski 1978).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats to this species are human settlements, waste dumps, illegal collecting, and local medicinal uses. Some localities have been partly depleted by illegal collectors. Others were partly depleted by illegal collectors and for local medicinal uses and are also declining due to human activities (Sotomayor et al. 2001). Many of the areas where it occurs are suitable for agricultural expansion, depending on the availability of water for irrigation.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ariocarpus kotschoubeyanus has a very wide distribution (its extent of occurrence is about 60,000 km²) and has a very large population (ca 100,000 mature individuals). However, the distribution is extremely fragmented primarily because the species is an edaphic specialist (Hernández and Navarro 2007), but there is also habitat loss and degradation. The subpopulations are very disjunct, but known from at least 35 locations. Many of the locations are threatened by local people who collect the plants for medicinal purposes. The species is also impacted by habitat destruction and habitat degradation through human activities. This cactus will soon be extirpated at some locations. It is listed as Near Threatened because it nearly qualifies for listing in a threatened category under criterion B2ab(ii,v) because the area of occupancy is likely to be close to, if not below the threshold level for Vulnerable, plus the degree of fragmentation and continuing decline. The number of locations and size of the population are all that are keeping this species from being listed in a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Ariocarpus retusus Scheidw. sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_common_name: Biznaga-Maguey; Chaute; Chautle; Cimarrón; Falso Peyote; Peyote; Peyote Cimarrón
iucn_locations: Coahuila; Nuevo León; San Luis Potosí; Tamaulipas; Mexico
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, but is widely distributed and common in the states of Coahuila, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí and Tamaulipas (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species tends to favour limestone and rocky slopes (Goettsch and Hernández 2006) in semi-desert vegetation. The original range of this species extended from Coahuila and Nuevo León in the north to San Luis Potosí in the south. However, much of this range has been modified by agricultural development.
A. retusus is a perennial and obligate gypsophile, occurring regularly in Muhlenbergia grassland and Bouteloua grassland in Central Mexico (Meyer et al. 1992). Ariocarpus species are the only succulents present in the Muhlenbergia grassland (Meyer et al. 1992).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by illegal collecting and agricultural development, however, they do not represent major threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ariocarpus retusus is listed as Least Concern due to its large range, number of known subpopulations, number of plants, and low threat over its entire population.

checklist_taxon: Ariocarpus scaphirostris Boed. sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying; Work & other activities
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range: Early observations indicated that this species was restricted to one small locality in the Valley of Rayones in the state of Nuevo León, Mexico. More recently, G.S. Hinton (pers. comm.) has found a wider distribution in this location, and it is now confirmed that there are three distinct locations. The area of occupancy is about 12 km² (Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011) and the extent of occurrence is less than 100 km².
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in xerophytic shrubland (on the edge of the Chihuahuan Desert), on calcareous shale hills (very fine-grained soils) and slopes. The tubercles just stick out of the ground.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collecting and mining of limestone for construction materials are the main threats. Collection for medicinal purposes may also pose a threat. A student from Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM) is studying growth rates in this species and has probably painted plants with red enamel paint as she did with A. bravoanus (Hernández 2008), which has helped to draw attention of collectors to where the plants occur.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ariocarpus scaphirostris has a small range (extent of occurrence is <100 km² and area of occupancy is about 12 km²). It is known from three locations, and there is continuing decline due to the impacts of illegal collection and the destruction of part of a colony by quarrying. Hence, the species is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Ariocarpus trigonus (F.A.C.Weber) K.Schum. sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Nuevo León and Tamaulipas. The original range of this species extended from north of Monterrey, southwards on the east side of the Sierra Madre Oriental to approximately Ciudad Victoria. It is also commonly found in the Valley of Jaumave, west of Ciudad Victoria. However, much of this range has been modified by agricultural development, particularly that area east of the Sierra. The plants occur at numerous localities throughout the Valley of Jaumave.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in semi-desert shrubland among outcrops of calcareous rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by illegal collecting and land conversion for small holder farming of corn.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ariocarpus trigonus is listed as Least Concern due to its large range, number of known subpopulations, number of plants, and low threat over its entire population.

checklist_taxon: Armatocereus laetus (Kunth) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: Armatocereus laetus only occurs in the Ecuadorian province of Loja and the Peruvian regions of Cajamarca and Piura (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at altitudes between 1000 and 2000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in dry valleys, on rocky slopes and in shrublands (Nano 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: Armatocereus laetus has no threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Armatocereus laetus is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed and locally abundant, and because there are no major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Armatocereus matucanensis Backeb. ex A.W.Hill sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru. It occurs from Cajamarca to Huancavelica, at altitudes between 1,700 and 3,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It is usually found in matorrales and on rocky slopes in inter-Andean valleys, but it also survives in disturbed habitat.
iucn_doc_threats: There is no known threat to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Armatocereus matucanensis is classified as Least Concern due to its wide distribution and because there are no known threats. However, the taxonomy should be revised as some specialists consider that it includes two species (A. matucanensis and A. mataranus).

checklist_taxon: Armatocereus procerus Rauh & Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru. It can be found in Casma in the Ancash region, valles de Fortaleza, Pativilca, Huaura and Chillón in the Lima region, valles de San Juan, Chincha, Pisco, Ingenio and Nazca in the Ica department (Ostolaza Nano 2006). It grows at an altitude of 500 to 1,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows in deserts and rocky slopes (Ostolaza Nano 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: In the Valle del Chillón, there is an area where goats forage on the species. Sometimes it is used to make handicrafts. Other than that, there are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Armatocereus procerus is common and not facing any threats. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Armatocereus rauhii Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru, where it occurs in the Amazonas, Cajamarca and Piura regions (Anderson 2001, Ostolaza Nano 2006), at altitudes of 400 to 900 m asl (Ostolaza Nano 2006). Arakaki et al. (2006) mention that it occurs farther south in Ancash (Sihuas) and eastern La Libertad (Pataz), up to 1,500 m asl; this southern subpopulation is considered a different subspecies.
iucn_doc_habitats: Dry valleys, rocky slopes and scrublands are the main habitat of Armatocereus rauhii (Ostolaza Nano 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this abundant species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Armatocereus rauhii is classified as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, common, and not subjected to any threats.

checklist_taxon: Arrojadoa rhodantha (Gürke) Britton & Rose sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_common_name: Rabo de onça; Rabo de raposa
iucn_locations: Pernambuco; Minas Gerais; Bahia; Piauí; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Unintentional effects (species is not the target); Housing & urban areas; Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Mining & quarrying
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Bahia, Piauí, Pernambuco, Minas Gerais and Goias at altitudes from 220 to 1,200 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: Arrojadoa rhodantha can be found on various substrates (including the shade of dense caatinga forest and in the open on inselbergs), and entering the caatinga/campo rupestre ecotone (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The habitat is under degradation due to deforestation for logging and for agriculture, cattle ranching, urbanization, quarrying, as well as due to fires.
iucn_doc_rationale: Arrojadoa rhodantha is declining due to several threats, but it is still widespread and common throughout its range. It is unlikely to qualify for a threatened category in the near future. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Arthrocereus rondonianus Backeb. & Voll sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Dry
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it can be found at altitudes between 700 and 1,200 m asl on the Serra do Cabral and the west slope of Serra do Espinhaço, in central Minas Gerais.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a south-eastern campo rupestre element. It grows between rocks and in bushy places.
iucn_doc_threats: One of the subpopulations is affected by urbanization and man-made fires.
iucn_doc_rationale: Arthrocereus rondonianus has a small range. Since the species was last assessed in 2004 and listed under Vulnerable, more subpopulations have been found. It is not particularly abundant, however only one subpopulation is affected by urbanization and man-made fires. As a whole, the species is not declining and it occurs within protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Arthrocereus spinosissimus (Buining & Brederoo) F.Ritter sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Mato Grosso in Chapada dos Guimarães (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 800 to 900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species grows on very vertical sand stone cliffs, in cerrado and gallery forest.
Even though the habitat is not declining the micro-climate of the area is changing due to deforestation, the levels of humidity have changed having prolonged the dry seasons. This might affect the plants in the future.
iucn_doc_threats: The only threat is deforestation for fuel wood and timber ,which is causing the micro-climate to change (see habitats and ecology).
iucn_doc_rationale: Arthrocereus spinosissimus has no direct threats and the whole range is within a protected area, hence it is listed as Least Concern. However, attention is needed as the area has suffered from deforestation changing the micro-climate where the species occurs, this might have long term impacts on the population.

checklist_taxon: Astrophytum asterias (Zucc.) Lem. sec. Vázquez-Lobo & al. 2015
iucn_common_name: Seaurchin Starcactus; Star Cactus; Star Peyote; Biznaga-algononcillo de Estrella; Sand Dollar Cactus
iucn_locations: Nuevo León; Tamaulipas; Mexico; United States; Texas
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Desert - Hot; Savanna - Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Named species; Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Texas, United States and in Tamaulipas and Nuevo León, Mexico. This species occurs below 500 m asl, with most records being below 200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species inhabits Tamaulipan thorn scrub and grasslands, where it is often found growing in gravelly, sometimes saline or calcareous, clays or loams in the partial shade of other plants or rocks (NatureServe 2002).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats for this species are illegal collection, overgrazing, agriculture and cattle ranching. This species' habitat is fragmented due to root ploughing for agricultural and pasture land. This species is threatened by competition from the invasive buffel grass (Cenchrus ciliaris). There is herbivory by ground squirrels (Spermophilous spp.) and cotton-tail rabbits (Sylvilagus spp.) and general uprooting by hogs (porcines). The larvae of Cerambycidae beetles are known to eat and kill the plant.
iucn_doc_rationale:
This species qualifies as Vulnerable due to its extent of occurrence being approximately 17,000 km2 and its severely fragmented populations.  Additionally, there is a continuing decline in the area of occupancy, in the extent and quality of habitat, and in the number of mature individuals due to a wide range of threats.

The population size appears to be fairly small with an estimated 5,000 mature individuals. However, its extensive range with many suitable habitats, which has largely been unstudied (because of restricted access to private land), particularly in northern Mexico, suggests that estimates of population size are probably low. Future studies may well show this plant to be more abundant than presently believed. If further subpopulations are found, this may change the area of occupancy and hence this species will need to be reassessed.

checklist_taxon: Astrophytum capricorne (A.Dietr.) Britton & Rose sec. Vázquez-Lobo & al. 2015
iucn_common_name: Beznaga de Estropajo; Birrete de Obispo; Mechudo; Peyotillo
iucn_threat_names: Nomadic grazing; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This cactus occurs fairly widely in the Chihuahuan Desert of northern Mexico (Anderson 2001), in the states of Coahuila and Nuevo León (Hernández et al. 2004; Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs on rocky limestone hills in xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection and over-grazing by livestock, especially goats, are the main threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Astrophytum capricorne has a wide distribution range and is reasonably common. Hence, this species is listed as Least Concern. There are some threats, but these are not severe enough to warrant listing in a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Astrophytum coahuilense (H.Moeller) K.Kayser sec. Vázquez-Lobo & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Astrophytum myriostigma Lem. ssp. coahuilense H.Moeller
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Nomadic grazing; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is narrowly distributed in the southern part of the state of Coahuila and Durango, Mexico.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on limestone hills in xerophytic shrubland. Elevation, aspect and soil type seem to be the most significant factors that limit the distribution of A. coahuilense in the El Sarnoso mountains, Durango. Altitude may be the most important factor (Romero Méndez 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection and overgrazing by goats are the main threats. Illegal collection is likely to increase as it is a relatively newly-named species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Astrophytum coahuilense has a restricted extent of occurrence of just under 6,000 km². It is known from three locations that are severely fragmented, and there is continuing decline in the quality of the habitat due to overgrazing. In addition, the number of plants is declining due to illegal collection. Hence, it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Astrophytum myriostigma Lem. sec. Vázquez-Lobo & al. 2015
iucn_common_name: Algononcillo de Mitra; Birrete de Obispo; Biznaga; Mitra; Peyote Cimarron; Peyotillo
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Nuevo León; Tamaulipas; Mexico
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Roads & railroads; Nomadic grazing; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in the Mexican states of Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí and Tamaulipas (Hernández et al. 2004, Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011), at elevations ranging from 1,100 to 1,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats:
A. myriostigma is a desert scrub species and can also grow on scrubby alluvial plains (Salas de Léon et al. 1999). It prefers steep, east or west (and southwest) facing slopes between 10 and 34 degrees angle, preferably around 25 degrees, and prefers low densities. It is rarely found on northern slopes or below 1,160 m asl. This species has a 100% vertical distribution bordering the El Sarnoso mountains and prefers stony, calcareous soils (litosol-regosol calcárico) often found in thin layers in the foothills of mountain ranges. Its distribution is limited by its need for stony, calcareous or lime soils. This calcicole also needs carbonates to integrate calcium and make itself more rigid. A. myriostigma also needs well drained soils as it is sensitive to high water levels. This species is also sensitive to the cold (Romero Méndez 2006).
A. myriostigma seems to flower independently of day length, flowering asynchronously from the end of winter to the start of autumn, although they flower synchronously June-July. Temperature may be a factor limiting the northern and southern limits of distribution, since in these latitutes the east-west slopes are most exposed to the sun and these are the slopes on which A. myriostigma grows most densely. This species is found in habitat dominated by Agave lecheguilla, which acts as the main nurse plant to A. myriostigma. Nurse plants may influence the distribution of A. myriostigma (Romero Méndez 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: Some studies report systematic extraction of individuals from natural populations and destruction of habitat from mining activities that threaten the species regionally and probably nationally (Romero Méndez 2006). The habitat is also overgrazed by livestock (goats and cattle), and some subpopulations have been impacted by the building of roads.
iucn_doc_rationale: Astrophytum myriostigma is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range and is extremely abundant. There are a number of threats that are causing declines, but none of these are causing sufficiently significant population declines to warrant listing in a more threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Astrophytum ornatum (DC.) Britton & Rose sec. Vázquez-Lobo & al. 2015
iucn_locations: Guanajuato; Querétaro; San Luis Potosí; Hidalgo; Mexico
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying; Nomadic grazing
iucn_doc_range: Astrophytum ornatum is distributed only in the states of Querétaro, Hidalgo, Guanajuato and San Luis Potosí, Mexico. It grows at altitudes between 800 and 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in semidesert in xerophile scrub. It grows on rocks and is most common in canyons; it can also grow on rocks in deciduous forests. Its distribution in canyons is due to the specific parameters of humidity and temperature that are necessary for its growth and reproduction.
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat destruction due to trampling from livestock (goats), land-use changes, mining, and illegal collection are the predominant threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Astrophytum ornatum is listed as Vulnerable due to its limited geographic distribution (extent of occurrence less than 8500 km2), its occurrence in less than 10 locations (defined as one location by land use change and mining threats), and ongoing subpopulation declines due to conversion of habitat, illegal collection, and the effects of grazing and mining.

checklist_taxon: Austrocactus bertinii (J.F.Cels) Britton & Rose sec. Böhnert & al. 2025
iucn_synonyms: Austrocactus patagonicus (F.A.C.Weber ex Speg.) Hosseus; Cereus bertinii E.Cels ex Hérincq.
iucn_locations: La Pampa; Argentina; Aisén; Chile; Rio Negro; Chubut; Santa Cruz; Neuquén
iucn_location_codes: AGE-LP; AR; CLS-AI; CL; AGS-RN; AGS-CB; AGS-SC; AGS-NE
iucn_doc_range: Austrocactus bertinii is found in Argentina in Chubut, La Pampa, Neuquén, Río Negro, and Santa Cruz and in Chile in Aysén, from 0 to 1,000 m asl (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in cold areas, where it grows in the shelter of shrubs and covered by grass, with little exposure to sunlight (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species; it may be favored by livestock grazing. In the Chilean range it is threatened by cherry tree agriculture and urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: Austrocactus bertinii is widespread and abundant across its range. It may be favored by livestock grazing. Therefore, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Austrocactus coxii (K.Schum.) Backeb. sec. Böhnert & al. 2025
Austrocactus spiniflorus (Phil.) F.Ritter sec. Böhnert & al. 2025
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Nomadic grazing; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic of a few locations in central  Chile, west of the Metropolitana and northern O'Higgins region. It grows at elevations of 1,200 to 1,700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in Andean shrubland, fully exposed to the sun, but it needs some shadow. It grows in humid, flat, non-rocky, open places where the water from the thawing snow can stay for longer periods of time (Belov 2005). It is covered by snow for short periods, about 3 to 4 months.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection, urbanization  are a threat for this species. Tourism and goat grazing are potential threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Austrocactus spiniflorus has an extent of occurrence of approximately 1,400 km2, and there are a total of five locations. The major threats for this scarce species are illegal collection, urbanization and goat grazing, which affects the quality of the habitat and the species. Therefore, it is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Austrocactus subandinus E.Sarnes & N.Sarnes sec. Böhnert & al. 2025
Austrocylindropuntia pachypus (K.Schum.) Backeb. sec. Ritz & al. 2012
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: A. pachypus is endemic to Peru and found in the regions of Ancash (northern limit: valle del Río Casma) and Lima (southern limit) in valle del Río Rimac (Arakaki et al. 2006) from 1,350 to 2,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus only occurs on rocky slopes in matorral (shrubby) vegetation. It does not have a continuous distribution, as it is only present in valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is affected by human sprawl and agricultural farming activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is not abundant and has a restricted range. Agricultural activities are expanding in the valleys where this species is present and it is already being negatively affected by human activities. The species is listed as Near Threatened, as it almost qualifies for a threatened category under criterion B1. Further research and monitoring are needed to both quantify the various threats to this species and to determine the rate that populations are declining.

checklist_taxon: Austrocylindropuntia subulata (Muehlenpf.) Backeb. sec. Ritz & al. 2012
iucn_synonyms: Austrocylindropuntia exaltata (A.Berger) Backeb.; Pereskia subulata Muehlenpf.
iucn_locations: Argentina; Peru; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Ecuador (mainland); Ecuador
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_doc_range: Austrocylindropuntia subulata is found in Peru, Bolivia and Argentina (Anderson 2001, Hunt et al. 2006, Lemke 2008), at elevations of 2,000 to 3,700 m asl. It has been introduced in Ecuador. It is also widely cultivated (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: It occurs in inter-Andean valleys and matorrales.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Austrocylindropuntia subulata is classified as Least Concern due to its wide distribution, its presence in several protected areas and because it is not exposed to any major threat.

checklist_taxon: Austrocylindropuntia vestita (Salm-Dyck) Backeb. sec. Ritz & al. 2012
iucn_locations: Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Jujuy; Argentina; Salta
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Argentina in Jujuy and Salta, and in Bolivia in Cochabamba, Chuquisaca, La Paz, Potosi and Tarija. It grows at altitudes between 2,400 and 3,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in high altitude grasslands on rocky soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Austrocylindropuntia vestita is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is abundant, and there are no major threats.

checklist_taxon: Aylostera pygmaea (R.E.Fr.) Mosti & Papini sec. Ritz & al. 2016
Aztekium hintonii Glass & W.A.Fitz Maur. sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Nuevo León. It has a very limited range, with the extent of occurrence and area of occupancy being about the same - 50 km². It occurs at an elevation of around 1,200 m asl (Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in semi-desert shrubland. It is a soil specialist growing only on gypsum cliffs, i.e., it is a gypsophile (Anderson 2001).
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection is the main threat. However, now it is protected by local people (Pilbeam and Weightman 2006) who safeguard it in order to sell it themselves.
iucn_doc_rationale: Aztekium hintonii has an extremely small range, both the extent of occurrence and area of occupancy are about 50 km². The area is essentially a single location, and there is ongoing illegal collection of the species. However, the population is extremely large and the collecting does not seem to be causing any declines. Hence, the species is listed as Near Threatened because it almost qualifies for a Critically Endangered listing under criterion B1ab(v).

checklist_taxon: Aztekium ritteri (Boed.) Boed. sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_threat_names: Avalanches/landslides; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Nuevo León.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in xerophile shrublands in the semi-desert, on gypsum and limestone cliffs.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by illegal collecting, however, some populations of the species are protected by growing on inaccessible cliffs. It is also threatened by natural erosion processes that wash away habitat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Aztekium ritteri is listed as Least Concern due to its extensive range, large number of individuals, and low threat.

checklist_taxon: Bergerocactus emoryi (Engelm.) Britton & Rose sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_common_name: Golden Snakecactus; Snake Cactus; Órgano Aterciopelado; Golden-spined Cereus
iucn_locations: Sonora; United States; Baja California; Mexico; California
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas; Commercial & industrial areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in southern California in the United States and in northwestern Baja California and some of the Channel Islands in Mexico, from sea level to 60 m asl (Anderson 2001).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species often inhabits very dry habitats, such as steep southern slopes, rocky areas, exposed sea bluffs (Peinado et al. 1995) and sandy soils (Anderson 2001). It is considered a maritime succulent scrub (Oberbauer 1999). Bergerocactus emoryi is one of the very few cactus species able to resprout its base after fire (Barbour et al. 2007).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats for this species are urban development, goats (Oberbauer 1999, Anderson 2001).
iucn_doc_rationale: Bergerocactus emoryi has a wide range. Even though there are major threats in places, the species remains abundant throughout its range. It is considered to be Least Concern, but research on the population trends and threats are recommended.

checklist_taxon: Blossfeldia liliputana Werderm. sec. Anderson 2005
iucn_locations: Jujuy; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Catamarca; Mendoza; Salta; San Juan; Argentina; La Rioja
iucn_location_codes: AGW-JU; BO; AGW-CA; AGW-ME; AGW-SA; AGW-SJ; AR; AGW-LR
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species can be found in Argentina and Bolivia, at elevations of 1,000 to 2,500 m of altitude (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009). Gómez (2006) reports it in Argentina in Jujuy, Catamarca, Salta, La Rioja, Mendoza and San Juan, and in Bolivia in the province Tarata.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in monte, chaco and prepuna. It grows in dry places, but near the rivers, in sunny places and in cracks of rocky walls (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection is the only major threat to this species and even though it can be alarming it is still localized, as the species is wide ranging and very abundant.
iucn_doc_rationale: Given that Blossfeldia liliputana has a very wide range, is locally abundant and illegal collection affects only parts of its range, it is listed as Least Concern. However, there is concern about the levels of collection in some subpopulations in Argentina and some subpopulations have been extirpated. These declines, however, are not considered to be severe enough to meet the thresholds for listing the species as threatened or Near Threatened under criterion A. Monitoring of subpopulations in Argentina is required, because if collection intensity increases, this species could well qualify for a more threatened status.

checklist_taxon: Borzicactus fieldianus Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
Borzicactus hutchisonii G.J.Charles sec. Hunt 2016
Borzicactus icosagonus (Kunth) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
Borzicactus leonensis (Madsen) G.J.Charles sec. Hunt 2016
Borzicactus longiserpens (Leuenb.) G.J.Charles sec. Hunt 2016
Borzicactus neoroezlii F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2016
Borzicactus plagiostoma (Vaupel) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
Borzicactus sepium (Kunth) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
Borzicactus silvaticus Janke sec. POWO 2017+
Borzicactus tenuiserpens (Rauh & Backeb.) Kimnach sec. Hunt 2016
Brasiliopuntia brasiliensis (Willd.) A.Berger sec. Majure & al. 2012
iucn_common_name: Ambeba; Arumbeva; Cumbeba; Facho de Renda; Gerumbeba; Jurubeba; Mumbebo; Mumbeca; Palmadora; Palmatória; Palmatória do Diabo; Palmatória Grande; Rumbeba; Xiquexique do Sertao; Urumbeba
iucn_synonyms: Opuntia brasiliensis (Willd.) Haw.; Cactus brasiliensis Willd.
iucn_locations: Peru; Brazil; United States; Paraguay; Argentina; Bolivia, Plurinational States of
iucn_location_codes: PE; BR; US; PY; AR; BO
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Coastal Sand Dunes; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Motivation Unknown/Unrecorded; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas; Mining & quarrying; Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Construction or structural materials
iucn_doc_range: This widespread southern Neotropical cactus is found in western Paraíba, eastern and central-southern Pernambuco, Alagoas, Sergipe, northwestern, northern and eastern Bahia, northeastern and central-southern Minas Gerais and Espírito Santo (Brazil); in semi-humid forests of extra-Amazonian Brazil; in the Atlantic drainage eastwards of the Andes (Peru - only in Cuzco -, Bolivia, northern Argentina, Paraguay). It has become naturalized in some areas where it has been introduced, such as in Florida (USA). It occurs at elevations of 0 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in restinga, drier phases of mata atlântica, agreste, caatinga (especially along temporary water courses), mata de brejo, mata seca (on limestone), mata de galeria and mata do planalto, especially on deep sandy substrates and as a lithophyte (Taylor and Zappi 2004) .
iucn_doc_threats: The loss of forest habitat due to agricultural expansion - crops and livestock - is affecting this cactus. Furthermore, some subpopulations are affected by urban expansion, and others by quarrying activities. However, none of these threats is considered to have a major impact.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although much of its habitat, especially the Atlantic Forest and agreste, has been destroyed in Brazil and population declines have been recorded, Brasiliopuntia brasiliensis does occur in some national parks and has a very wide range in the southern Neotropics. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Browningia candelaris (Meyen) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Desert - Cold; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Commercial & industrial areas; Intentional use (species is the target); Housing & urban areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Construction or structural materials; Fuels; Handicrafts, jewellery, etc.
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs from Lima, Perú to north of Chile (Tarapaca region) (Hoffmann and Walter 1989), at elevations between 1,000 and 3,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in extremely dry areas with very rare rainfall (less than 100 mm/year). The dry season lasts 8 - 12  months and in some years it is possible to have no rainfall at all. It occurs on level areas or  slopes facing north, where it is fully exposed to the sun. 700 mature individuals have been counted in Tinajas, Peru, and 17,000 individuals are estimated to exist in Chile.
iucn_doc_threats: It is heavily used in northern Chile. In Arequipa, Peru it is negatively affected by human  sprawl. For instance, it is used as firewood in brick stone fabrics near Arequipa, which has led to a severe decline in mature individuals. Mining activities also affect it negatively. Climate change leads to prolonged drought periods, which in turn interferes with seedling establishment.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species should be classified as Vulnerable where population information is available, i.e., northern Chile and southern Peru (from Arequipa towards the south). The reason for this is an estimated population decline of at least 50% over the past 40 years, the lack of efficient conservation strategies, and the presence in only one protected area. However, as no information is available about the northern subpopulation, the species is classified as Data Deficient until such information becomes available to do a comprehensive assessment..

checklist_taxon: Browningia hertlingiana (Backeb.) Buxb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This cactus species is endemic to Peru. It occurs in dry Andean forests in the central regions of Apurímac, Ayacucho and Huancavelica at an altitude of 1,900 to 3,000 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006). The individuals in the southern extreme of the range (Valle de Cotahuasi) are morphologically different and may belong to another species (B. viridis), which is currently considered a synonym.
iucn_doc_habitats: B. hertlingiana occurs on slopes of inter-Andean valleys in dry Andean forests in central Peru (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: Agricultural activities are expanding in the range of this cactus and leading to a population reduction. The subpopulations on steep slopes are safe from this impact, as agriculture is not feasible there.
iucn_doc_rationale: Browningia hertlingiana is abundant and has a relatively wide range. Although some reduction in the population size has been observed, this impact is not considered significant enough to justify a classification in a threatened category or as Near Threatened. It is therefore classified as Least Concern. However, its population is affected by agricultural activities. This impact should be monitored and a re-assessment performed if necessary.

checklist_taxon: Browningia microsperma (Werderm. & Backeb.) W.T.Marshall sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_doc_range: Browningia microsperma occurs only in the Ecuadorian province of Loja and the Peruvian regions of Cajamarca, Lambayeque, and Piura (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at altitudes between 600 and 1200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Browningia microsperma inhabits steep, inaccessible cliffs, sometimes at the borders of rivers, in agricultural areas.
iucn_doc_threats: No threats exist to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Browningia microsperma is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, is locally abundant, occurs in a protected area, and there are no major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Calymmanthium substerile F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_doc_range: This cactus species is endemic to Peru, where it occurs in the northern regions of Amazonas and Cajamarca at an altitude of 500 to 1,000 m asl. (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs on rocky slopes, dry valleys and scrubs of the north of Peru (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats for the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very restricted, is very abundant and there are no ongoing threats affecting it. It is therefore listed as Least Concern, however monitoring is recommended.

checklist_taxon: Carnegiea gigantea (Engelm.) Britton & Rose sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_locations: Mexico; Sonora; Arizona; United States; California
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas; Named species; Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Other household goods; Handicrafts, jewellery, etc.
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in the Mexican state of Sonora and in the Unites States in Arizona and California (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 0 to 1,370 m asl (Paredes et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on well-drained land, on hillsides and on plains in thorn scrub and desert scrub (Paredes et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_threats: In Mexico, Carnegiea gigantea is primarily threatened by changes in land use for cattle ranching.  In the Unites States, this species is primarily threatened by urbanization. Saguaros are also threatened by collection from the wild, including indiscriminate and illegal trade (Paredes et al. 2000). There is a high demand for Saguaro ribs, the internal woody frame of this cactus species, that leads to over-collection. The species is threatened by competition from the invasive buffelgrass (Pennisetum ciliare) and from the increased frequency of fires associated with buffelgrass.
iucn_doc_rationale: Carnegiea gigantea has a wide range. Even though there is a decrease in the population, this species is still locally abundant and the observed decrease is not sufficient to trigger a threat listing. Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cephalocereus apicicephalium E.Y.Dawson sec. Tapia & al. 2017
iucn_doc_range: Cephalocereus apicicephalium occurs in Oaxaca and Chiapas (Hunt et al. 2006). In Oaxaca, it is found in Juchitán, Tehuantepec, Yautepec (Acosta Castellanos 2002) and in Chiapas, it occurs near Tuxtla and Aguacero.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in deciduous lowland forest (Acosta Castellanos 2002). Its distribution is restricted due to specific ecological requirements (Chávez 2004); it grows only on calcareous soils. In Chiapas it grows on the walls of canyons.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for the species due to the nature of the areas where it grows - largely inaccessible and not suitable for agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although its range is fairly restricted, Cephalocereus apicicephalium is reasonably common and not under any threat. It seems to be quite secure in the habitat where it grows, hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cephalocereus fulviceps (F.A.C.Weber ex K.Schum.) H.E.Moore sec. Tapia & al. 2017
Cephalocereus macrocephalus F.A.C.Weber ex K.Schum. sec. Tapia & al. 2017
Cephalocereus mezcalaensis Bravo sec. Tapia & al. 2017
Cephalocereus nizandensis (Bravo & T.MacDoug.) Buxb. sec. Tapia & al. 2017
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: Cephalocereus nizandensis is endemic to the area of Nizanda in Oaxaca, Mexico.
iucn_doc_habitats: Cephalocereus nizandensis grows on rocky ground in open dry deciduous forest.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to the species at present, but the area is now being developed for ecotourism and paths are being made. What impact this will have in the future is not known.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cephalocereus nizandensis has a small range with only four subpopulations but there are currently no signs of decline. However, the population comprises less than 1,000 mature individuals so it qualifies for listing as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Cephalocereus parvispinus S.Arias, H.J.Tapia & U.Guzmán sec. Arias & al. 2019
Cephalocereus polylophus (DC.) Britton & Rose sec. Tapia & al. 2017
Cephalocereus scoparius (Poselg.) Britton & Rose sec. Tapia & al. 2017
Cephalocereus senilis (Haw.) Pfeiff. sec. Tapia & al. 2017
iucn_common_name: Barba de Viejo; Cabeza de Viejo; Viejito; Old Man Cactus
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed only in the states of Hidalgo and Veracruz (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is one of the dominant elements of the xerophile scrub where it grows (Reyes-Santiago et al. 2003). The house finch eats the fruits/seed in June, July and November (Mendiola González and Ortiz-Pulido 2006). It is restricted to calcareous canyons within xerophile vegetation.
iucn_doc_threats: Juveniles are illegally overcollected for trade as ornamental plants. The species may be at risk from pastoral agriculture (Mendiola González and Ortiz-Pulido 2006). Attempts have been made to transport this species across the Mexican-US border (Robbins and Bárcenas Luna 2003). The cephalia of this species is cut to obtain fruits and seeds (E. Sánchez pers. comm. 2010).
iucn_doc_rationale: Cephalocereus senilis is listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(iii,v) due to a limited range (extent of occurrence is approximately 2,170 km2), occurs at one single location based on the threat related to illegal overcollecting across the range, and due to ongoing declines in habitat quality. Overcollection of juveniles is also an ongoing threat and likely causing an imbalance in age distributions of subpopulations.

checklist_taxon: Cephalocereus tetetzo (F.A.C.Weber ex J.M.Coult.) Diguet sec. Tapia & al. 2017
Cephalocereus totolapensis (Bravo & T.MacDoug.) Buxb. sec. Tapia & al. 2017
iucn_doc_range: Cephalocereus totolapensis is endemic to Oaxaca, Mexico, where it is only found in a canyon southeast of Oaxaca City.
iucn_doc_habitats: Cephalocereus totolapensis grows on steep slopes along the canyon in dry deciduous forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The slopes where this species occurs are being cleared to plant Agave for tequila production.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a fairly restricted range with the extent of occurrence being less than 20,000 km², it is known from less than five locations, it may possibly be treated as a single location because the main threat is the clearance of the habitat to plant Agave and this is rapidly affecting much of the canyon where it grows, hence it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Cereus aethiops Haw. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Mendoza; San Juan; La Pampa; Catamarca; Salta; Tucumán; La Rioja; Buenos Aires; Córdoba; Rio Negro; Entre Ríos; San Luis; Argentina
iucn_location_codes: AGW-ME; AGW-SJ; AGE-LP; AGW-CA; AGW-SA; AGW-TU; AGW-LR; AGE-BA; AGE-CO; AGS-RN; AGE-ER; AGW-SL; AR
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Temperate; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species grows in Argentina, where it occurs in Buenos Aires, Catamarca, Córdoba, Entre Rios, La Pampa, La Rioja, Mendoza, Río Negro, Salta, San Juan, San Luis and Tucumán (Hunt  et al.  2006). Kiesling and Ferrari (2009) report it at elevations between 0 and 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in a variety of habitats, in dry forest, shrublands, chaco, espinal, and pampa grasslands. The species can regenerate from subterranean structures if the species is cut.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats. However, in the Chaco, in the north-eastern part of its range, land use change for soybean plantations affects the populations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cereus aethiops has a wide range, and even though the populations are not abundant, they are stable and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cereus albicaulis (Britton & Rose) Luetzelb. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Piauí; Bahia; Pernambuco; Alagoas; Ceará; Minas Gerais; Rio Grande do Norte; Brazil

iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Unintentional effects (species is not the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Agro-industry plantations
iucn_doc_range: This species has been reported from Brazil, where it occurs in Piauí, Ceará, Pernambuco, Alagoas, Bahia, Rio Grande do Norte and Minas Gerais. It grows at 470 to 1,020 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in caatinga de altitude, caatinga and carrasco. It often grows on sandy substrates of the Cipó soil series (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are habitat loss due to deforestation for charcoal and fuel wood, cattle ranching, agriculture, and agroindustry of biodiesel.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though some subpopulations of Cereus albicaulis have a continuing decline mainly due to habitat loss, the species occurs over a vast area and within protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cereus bicolor Rizzini & A.Mattos sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Mato Grosso do Sul; Goiás; Pará; Rondônia; Mato Grosso; Brasília Distrito Federal; Brazil

iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Agro-industry farming; Mining & quarrying; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Mato Grosso, Goiás, Pará, Rondonia, Distrito Federal and Mato Grosso do Sul, Brazil, at elevations of 400 to 900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows mostly on limestone, crystalline, and sandstone rock outcrops in cerrado and seasonal tropical dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is quarrying of limestone and habitat loss due to agro-industry, agriculture and cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: The subpopulations of Cereus bicolor have declined and continue to do so mainly due to habitat loss. However, it occurs over a vast area and in habitats that are not significantly degrading. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cereus fernambucensis Lem. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Alagoas; Sergipe; Bahia; Espírito Santo; Paraíba; Rio de Janeiro; Pernambuco; Rio Grande do Norte; São Paulo; Brazil

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Rio Grande do Norte, Paraíba, Pernambuco, Alagoas, Sergipe, Bahia, Espírito Santo, São Paulo and Rio de Janeiro (Taylor and Zappi 2004), at elevations from 0 to 400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in humid/subhumid forest (restinga) element: on sand-dunes, rocks and growing through shrubs of the restinga, sometimes within reach of the sea spray, to 100 m throughout the coast of Eastern Brazil from Rio Grande do Norte southwards (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is habitat loss due to urbanization, tourism (resort development),  road construction, artificial landscaping, agro-industrial plantations of  coconut, pineapple, sugar cane, eucalyptus and forestry.
iucn_doc_rationale: Some subpopulations of Cereus fernambucensis have declined and continue to do so mainly due to habitat loss. However, it occurs over a vast area and within protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cereus hankeanus F.A.C.Weber ex K.Schum. sec. Jørgensen & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Salta; Santiago del Estero; Chaco; Formosa; Tucumán; Jujuy; Santa Fé; Argentina; Catamarca; Córdoba; Paraguay; La Rioja
iucn_location_codes: BO; AGW-SA; AGW-SE; AGE-CH; AGE-FO; AGW-TU; AGW-JU; AGE-SF; AR; AGW-CA; AGE-CO; PY; AGW-LR
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Savanna - Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is present in Argentina, where it can be found in the provinces of Catamarca, Jujuy, Formosa, Chaco, Santa Fe, Santiago del Estero, Córdoba, La Rioja, Tucumán, and Salta. It also occurs in the Chaco region of Bolivia and Paraguay (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations of 500 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in a variety of habitats. It occurs in forests, on hills, and on the edges of salt flats.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cereus hankeanus is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread, locally abundant and common, and there are no major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Cereus hexagonus (L.) Mill. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_common_name: Cactus Columnar Miramar;  Cactus de Columnas;  Cirial; Cirio; Miramar; Pitahaya; Lady of the Night Cactus
iucn_locations: Trinidad and Tobago; French Guiana; Suriname; Brazil; Colombia; Guyana; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of
iucn_location_codes: TT; GF; SR; BR; CO; GY; VE
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Moist; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Construction or structural materials
iucn_doc_range:
Cereus hexagonus occurs in Colombia, Brazil, French Guiana, Guyana, Surinam, Trinidad and Tobago, and Venezuela.
It is widely distributed in Cañón del Río Chicamocha (Boyacá-Santander, Colombia), where it is one of the most conspicuous and characteristic elements of the vegetation (Albesiano and Fernández-Alonso 2006). In Venezuela, its elevation ranges from 0 to 1,700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: In Venezuela, it mainly grows on slopes in the Cordillera de la Costa. In Los Llanos, it grows in flatlands and small hills (J. Nassar pers. comm. 2011).  In French Guyana it grows in sand dunes on the coast, forming large solid masses. It adapts well to moist environments (Cremers and Lauenberger 1988).  Fruits are dispersed by bats and birds. It is pollinated by hawkmoths. In Trinidad, the fruit bat Artibeus lituratus feeds on the skin and pulp of the fruit. It fruits July-August (Greenhall 1957).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cereus hexagonus is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread and there are no known threats. One of its habitats (mountain slopes) is not suitable for urban and agricultural development.

checklist_taxon: Cereus hildmannianus K.Schum. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+

iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas; Agro-industry farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Food - human; Construction or structural materials
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Brazil in the states of Mato Grosso do Sul, Rio do Janeiro, Paraná, São Paulo, Minas Gerais, Santa Catarina, Rio Grande do Sul, and in Uruguay, Paraguay and Argentina, from sea level to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in southern humid/subhumid forest, in rocky places and on dry shadows soils in mata de planalto, in cerrado and pampas where is found on rocky outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is habitat loss due to urbanization, agriculture, cattle ranching and forestry.
iucn_doc_rationale: Some of the subpopulations of Cereus hildmannianus have declined and continue to do so mainly due to habitat loss. However, it occurs over a vast area and within protected areas. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cereus insularis Hemsl. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Construction or structural materials
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Pernambuco in the archipelago Fernando do Noronha (Taylor and Zappi 2004), at sea level to 50 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in restinga in rocky habitats and cliffs.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Despite the very small range, Cereus insularis is listed as Least Concern because the whole range falls in a well-managed protected area and the species is very abundant.

checklist_taxon: Cereus mirabella N.P.Taylor sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Goiás; Piauí; Maranhão; Tocantins; Bahia; Minas Gerais; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZC-GO; BZE-PI; BZE-MA; BZN-TO; BZE-BA; BZL-MG; BR
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs at ca 150-750 m asl from south-western Maranhão, Tocantins, Piaui, Goias and western Bahia to central northern and western Minas Gerais (Rio São Francisco drainage) in Brazil, and disjunctly in east central Minas Gerais (Rio Doce/Rio Jequitinhohna watershed).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in cerrado element, mostly in sandy phases of the cerrado and more open places of the cerrado-caatinga ecotone.
iucn_doc_threats: The only major threat is habitat loss due to agro-industry of soya, maize and cotton.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species occurs over a vast area, however large extensions of the habitat where the species used to occur have been converted to agro-industry. From this it can be inferred that a reduction of over 50% of the population has occurred over the last 30 years (generation length is 10 years) and habitat conversion will not cease in the near future, hence the species qualifies as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Cereus mortensenii (Croizat) D.R.Hunt & N.P.Taylor sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: Cereus mortensenii is endemic to Venezuela, occurring in the states of Lara, Falcon, and Trujillo (Hunt et al. 2006, Ponce 1989).  Its elevation ranges from 200-1,000 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs on grassy plains in a mixture of shrubland and more open vegetation.  The open vegetation consists of naturally open areas of rock and earth that are inhospitable to most other plants. The reproductive system has not been studied.  However, it is likely pollinated by bats and dispersed by bats and birds (J. Nassar pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.  Although a portion of its distribution overlaps with urban and agricultural areas, a large part of its range still consists of habitats with low levels of disturbance.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Near Threatened because it has a restricted range and is scarce where it occurs.  However, there is not enough information on the population and threats to this species to determine if it is threatened. Further research is needed to determine the severity of possible threats as well as obtain information on its basic ecology (reproduction).

checklist_taxon: Cereus repandus (L.) Mill. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Colombia; Bonaire; Sint Eustatius; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Curaçao; Sint Maarten (Dutch part); Grenada; Saint Martin (French part); Saba
iucn_location_codes: CO; NLA-BO; NLA-SE; BQ; VE; CW; SX; GD; MF; NLA-SA
iucn_habitat_names: Savanna - Moist; Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas; Scale Unknown/Unrecorded; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Fuels; Other chemicals; Food - human; Other (free text); Construction or structural materials
iucn_doc_range: Cereus repandus occurs in the Netherlands Antilles, Venezuela, northeastern coast of Colombia, and Grenada, West Indies (Hunt et al. 2006, N. Taylor pers. comm. 2011, J. Nassar pers. comm. 2011). It grows from sea level up to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats:
Cereus repandus occurs in arid zones in Venezuela (Nassar et al. 2003). It utilizes nurse plants to become established. Nurse plants include thorny legume species, such Acacia farnesiana and Acacia macracantha (Larrea-Alcázar and Soriano 2006). Along with Stenocereus griseus, C. repandus forms vegetation units known as cardonales (Nassar et al. 2003).
Cereus repandus has fairly high levels of genetic diversity. Large continuous habitats promote the highest variation; less extensive arid zones such as isolated arid patches in the Andes and the eastern coastline have populations with low within-population genetic diversity (Nassar et al. 2003). Cereus repandus mainly relies on sexual reproduction, but very occasionally spreads vegetatively when branches fall from adult individuals and grow roots (Nassar et al. 2003). This cactus behaves genetically as a diploid species; it is self-incompatible and strictly bat-pollinated (Nassar et al. 2003). Leptoncteris curasoae and Glossophaga longirostris are critical pollinators (Petit 1995, Nassar et al. 1997). Seed dispersal is shared between bats and birds, but bird-mediated seed dispersal is assumed to be restricted to less than 12 ha whereas bats can fly 100 km in one night, and forage in patches 14 km apart (Nassar et al. 2003).
With S. griseus and Pilosocereus lanuginosus, C. repandus is one of the three most important species of columnar cacti in terms of density and annual production of fruit. Cereus repandus flowers and fruits in the wet season, providing nectar, pollen, and fruit for nectar feeders, frugivores and omnivores and reducing competition for pollinators and dispersers (J. Nassar pers. comm. 2011). The nectarivorous, pollivorous and frugivorous Miller's Long-tongued Bat (Glossophaga longirostris) is an important consumer of C. repandus fruit (Larrea-Alcázar and Soriano 2006). Cereus repandus is associated with 10 bird species which consume the fruit: 4 dispersers, 4 predators and 2 that eat the pulp (Soriano et al. 1999). Cereus repandus flowers are eaten by the green iguana (van Marken Liehtenbelt 1993), and seeds are consumed by ground-foraging ants of the genus Pheidole (Larrea-Alcázar and Soriano 2006).
iucn_doc_threats:
There are no major threats for this species.  However, there are localized threats including habitat reduction in Lagunillas, Merida, Venezuela and overexploitation in the Paraguana Peninsula, Falcon State, Venezuela for the construction of chairs. There is also urban development in Aruba and Curaçao (J. Nassar pers. comm. 2011).

In xeric and mesic zones near San Juan de Lagunillas, the natural vegetation of the Venezuelan Andes has been replaced by cultivation and extensive grazing by cows and goats. Taller thorny legumes which function as nurse plants to C. repandus have been removed. The removal of legumes along with grazing is increasing erosion and desertification (Larrea-Alcázar and Soriano 2008).  Cacti (including C. repandus) are considered 'fever-giving' weeds by some of the local people on Curaçao, and consequently are systematically removed (Petit and Pors 1996).
iucn_doc_rationale: Cereus repandus is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide distribution and threats appear to be relatively minor and localized.  It occurs in high densities in multiple localities.

checklist_taxon: Cereus saddianus (Rizzini & A.Mattos) P.J.Braun sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry farming; Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Mato Grosso (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 130 to 200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on termite mounts in cerrado.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are land use change for agro-industry of biofuels and cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species occurs in a very small area (extent of occurrence <100km<sup>2</sup>) considered one locality because it is subject to agro-industry that will keep expanding causing a continuing decline in the population, therefore it qualifies as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Cereus spegazzinii F.A.C.Weber sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Chaco; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Argentina; Salta; Formosa; Santa Fé; Paraguay; Santiago del Estero
iucn_location_codes: AGE-CH; BO; AR; AGW-SA; AGE-FO; AGE-SF; PY; AGW-SE
iucn_doc_range: This species is reported to occur in Argentina, in the provinces of Santa Fe, Santiago del Estero, Salta, Chaco, and Formosa. It also grows in Bolivia, Brazil, and Paraguay (Hunt et al. 2006). It can be found at elevations between 10 and 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in the interior and edge of dry forests.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species. In some areas it may be affected by deforestation for agricultural purposes.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cereus spegazzinii is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, is common and locally abundant, and there are no major threats affecting it. It is also present in many protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Chamaecereus saltensis (Speg.) Schlumpb. sec. Schlumpberger 2012
Chamaecereus schreiteri (A.Cast.) Schlumpb. sec. Zuloaga & al. 2019 (sub Lobivia schreiteri)
Cipocereus bradei (Backeb. & Voll) Zappi & N.P.Taylor sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the state of Minas Gerais, Brazil. It occurs between 500 - 1,200 m asl in Serra do Cabral and west slope of Serra do Espinhaço. It is known from six localities which would represent two different locations.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in south-eastern campo rupestre element, on crystalline rocks in campo rupestre, carrasco or cerrado.
iucn_doc_threats: There is habitat degradation from mining and intentional fires for the management of grasses, particularly in the western portion of the distribution of this cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because it has an extent of occurrence of ca 13,000 km2, and it is known from two locations. There is continuing decline in the extent and quality of its habitat and number of subpopulations, and therefore a reduction in the number of mature individuals can also be inferred.

checklist_taxon: Cipocereus crassisepalus (Buining & Brederoo) Zappi & N.P.Taylor sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Savanna - Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the state of Minas Gerais, Brazil. It is found north of Diamantina, Serra Negra and east side of Serra do Espinhaço at elevations from 500 - 1,200 m asl. It is known from eight localities that represent two locations.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in south-eastern campo rupestre (cerrado) element: in sandy cerrado/carrasco associated with crystalline rock outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is loss of habitat, especially due to the activities of charcoal production. The species hybridizes with C. minensis when they come into contact.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has a very restricted distribution (extent of occurrence is 1,800 km<sup>2</sup>), it occurs at only two locations, it is not present in any protected area, and there is continuing decline in the extent and quality of its habitat and number of subpopulations.

checklist_taxon: Cipocereus minensis (Werderm.) F.Ritter sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_synonyms: Cereus minensis Werderm.; Cipocereus minensis (Werderm.) Ritter subsp. minensis Cipocereus; Cipocereus minensis N.P.Taylor & Zappi subsp. leiocarpus Cipocereus
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the state of Minas Gerais, Brazil. It grows in Serra do Cipó and Lapinha, southwards to Cocais and Itabirito, central-southern Minas Gerais. It occurs at elevations from 500 to 2,000 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs on cliffs and between rocks in campo rupestre (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The species grows in an areas where there are frequent intentional fires for the management of grasses, these may affect it.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cipocereus minensis is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread and locally abundant, is present in several protected areas, there are no major threats affecting it, and it grows in many areas not easily accessible to people.

checklist_taxon: Cleistocactus baumannii (Lem.) Lem. sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Argentina; Paraguay
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in the Chaco Region of Argentina, Paraguay, and Bolivia (Pin and Simon 2004). It occurs at elevations between 10 and 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in sandy and clayey soils, in forest borders as well as within dense or open forests. It can also be found in the scrubs of the dry Chaco and in the eastern region of Paraguay. Furthermore, it grows in rocky areas and open sites (Pin and Simon 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cleistocactus baumannii is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, is locally abundant, and there are no major threats affecting it. It also occurs in many protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Cleistocactus brookeae Cárdenas sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in Chuquisaca, Santa Cruz and Tarija at altitudes from 800 to 1,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on cliffs alongside rivers, in humid forests within inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: Nothing is known about the threats this species is facing. There is local collection.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has an extent of occurrence of approximately 4,200 km<sup>2</sup>, however, it is fast growing and it is restricted to cliffs, which confer it protection. Because of these two facts it is very likely that the population is stable and not suffering from continued declines. The species is here listed as Least Concern, however, more field work is needed in order to assess the species more realistically.

checklist_taxon: Cleistocactus laniceps (K.Schum.) Rol.-Goss. sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Ayopaya, Cochabamba Province, Bolivia (Hunt et al. 2006). It can be found at elevations of 2,100 m to 2,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in deciduous forests within seasonally dry inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known direct threats to this species.
