iucn_doc_rationale: Cleistocactus laniceps is listed as Least Concern because the populations seem to be quite stable and there are no imminent threats. However, due to its restricted range, the population should be monitored for possible future threats.

checklist_taxon: Cleistocactus morawetzianus Backeb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru. It occurs in the Huancavelica region at an altitude of 2,000 to 2,500 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: The species may have been affected by the construction of a road leading to a hydroelectric plant around 2002, but this needs to be confirmed.
iucn_doc_rationale: Insufficient information is available about Cleistocactus morawetzianus to assess its conservation status. Road construction and other habitat modifications may present a significant threat and further studies are needed in order to properly assess these threats to the species as a whole. It is therefore classified as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Cleistocactus parviflorus (K.Schum.) Rol.-Goss. sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs from southeastern Cochabamba to extreme western Santa Cruz and northern Chuquisaca. It probably does not occur south of the Rio Caine. It can be found at elevations of 1,400 to 3,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in forests within seasonally dry inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: Clearance for subsistence farming is impacting the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cleistocactus parviflorus is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide distribution, is locally abundant where it occurs, and is present within a protected area.

checklist_taxon: Cleistocactus ritteri Backeb. ex Guiggi sec. Guiggi 2020
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs on both sides of Río La Paz in La Paz (Sud-Yungas and Inquisivi provinces) at elevations of 1,000 to 1,100 m (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows in Yungas forests near the La Paz river.
iucn_doc_threats: Nothing is known about the threats this species may be facing.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is classified as Least Concern because even though it has a small range, it is locally abundant and there are no apparent threats. Because of this, monitoring is recommended.

checklist_taxon: Cleistocactus samaipatanus (Cárdenas) D.R.Hunt sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in the provinces of Florida, Cordillera and Vallegrande in Santa Cruz, as well as in Luis Calvo province of Chuquisaca, at elevations of 1,000 to 2,000 m asl (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in forests within seasonally dry inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: There is no known threat to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cleistocactus samaipatanus is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide distribution and is locally abundant where it occurs.

checklist_taxon: Cleistocactus smaragdiflorus (F.A.C.Weber) Britton & Rose sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_synonyms: Cereus colubrinus Otto ex Foerster var. smaragdiflorus F.A.C.Weber
iucn_locations: Salta; La Rioja; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Argentina; Jujuy; Catamarca; Tucumán
iucn_location_codes: AGW-SA; AGW-LR; BO; AR; AGW-JU; AGW-CA; AGW-TU
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_threat_names: Nomadic grazing; Small-holder farming; Agro-industry farming
iucn_doc_range: This species has been recorded from Bolivia and Argentina, where it occurs in Jujuy, Salta, Tucumán, Catamarca, and La Rioja at 500 to 1,500 m asl (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: This succulent plant grows in the Andean mountains in chaco and yunga forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is land use change for agriculture and goat ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cleistocactus smaragdiflorus has a wide range, is abundant, occurs in protected areas and only parts of its range are under threat. Hence the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cleistocactus winteri D.R.Hunt sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in the province of Florida (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 1,300 to 1,400 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs on cliffs within forests, in seasonally dry inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: The southern subpopulation is negatively affected by local collectors, who use them as ornamental plants.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is classified as Endangered because it has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence is 250 km<sup>2</sup>), it is known from two isolated locations and collection is a major threat for this species.

checklist_taxon: Consolea falcata (Ekman & Werderm.) F.M.Knuth sec. Majure & al. 2021
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to northwestern Haiti. It is known from only a single locality on the coast, 100 m from the beach on a ridge on the west side of Saint Michelle (Strittmatter 2006). It occurs just above sea level.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs on bare coastal limestone soils with no associated vegetation. Mature individuals are small compared to other Consolea species.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats for this species are severe land degradation from deforestation and flooding events.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Critically Endangered because it has a very  restricted  distribution, it is known from only one location, and the  number of  mature individuals is less than ten. The entire population may  have gone extinct as a result of a flooding event in 2008, hence surveys are required to determine if any plants remain.

checklist_taxon: Consolea moniliformis (L.) A.Berger sec. Majure & al. 2021
iucn_locations: Puerto Rico; Haiti; Dominican Republic; Cuba
iucn_doc_range: Consolea moniliformis occurs in Cuba, Dominican Republic, Haiti and in Puerto Rico on Mona Island, Culebra and Desecheo (Hunt et al. 2006; Areces-Mallea 1996; P. Griffith and V. Negron-Ortiz, pers. comms. 2011). It occurs at elevations from sea level to about 50 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: In Cuba, it occurs in dry littoral forest (Areces-Mallea 1996). It also occurs on limestone. In Puerto Rico, it occurs in open dry forests and is sparsely distributed. In Santiago de Cuba it is cultivated in gardens.
iucn_doc_threats:
In Haiti, the major threat is deforestation. Threats in other parts of the range are not known.

In experiments in the Dominican Republic, no damage to C. moniliformis from Cactoblastis cactorum was observed, despite other cacti being affected by this non-native cactus moth. On the island of Desecheo, some feeding damage was observed (Zimmermann et al 2005).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern given that it occurs on several islands in the Caribbean. However, the conservation status for this species could change to a threatened category if more information becomes available on the status of the subpopulation on Haiti (suspected to have been perhaps extirpated) and the effects of Cactoblastis cactorum on Desecheo Island in Puerto Rico.

checklist_taxon: Consolea nashii (Britton) A.Berger sec. Majure & al. 2021
iucn_locations: Turks and Caicos Islands; Cuba; Bahamas
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Cuba, Turks and Caicos Islands and the Bahamas (Areces-Mallea 2001, Strittmatter 2006). It grows at elevations from 0-15 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in dry forest on limestone and potentially on sandy soils in coastal areas.
iucn_doc_threats: In the Bahamas, the major threat is the invasive Cactoblastis cactorum which decimated the population in the past, but it has now recovered.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern as it is distributed in multiple sites and it is relatively common. However, the impact of the invasive Cactoblastis in the Bahamas needs to be monitored and the populations in the Turks and Caicos surveyed.

checklist_taxon: Consolea rubescens (Salm-Dyck ex DC.) Lem. sec. Majure & al. 2021
iucn_locations: Virgin Islands, U.S.; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Montserrat; Saint Lucia; Guadeloupe; Puerto Rico; Saint Kitts and Nevis; Anguilla; Martinique; Barbados; Saint Martin (French part); Aruba; Saint Vincent and the Grenadines; Saint Barthélemy; Curaçao; Grenada; Sint Maarten (Dutch part); Trinidad and Tobago; Antigua and Barbuda; Dominica; Virgin Islands, British
iucn_location_codes: VI; BQ; MS; LC; GP; PR; KN; AI; MQ; BB; MF; AW; VC; BL; CW; GD; SX; TT; AG; DM; VG
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in the Lesser Antilles, the main island of Puerto Rico, and the Virgin Islands (Caja de Muertos, St. John, St. Thomas, and Tortola) (Strittmatter 2006). It occurs at elevations from 0 to 50 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in dry forest on sandy soils in the coastal area.
iucn_doc_threats: The are no known major threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Consolea rubescens is listed as Least Concern because it is distributed in multiple sites in the Caribbean, is abundant in places, and there are no known major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Consolea spinosissima (Mill.) Lem. sec. Majure & al. 2021
iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/supratidal - Coastal Caves/Karst; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Housing & urban areas; Named species
iucn_doc_range: Consolea spinosissima is endemic to Jamaica (Strittmatter et al. 2002). It occurs in three locations in the southern portion of the country in the coastal area. The altitudinal range is between 0 and 50 m. The extent of occurrence is estimated to be 900 km².
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found on the dogtooth limestone in the southern coast of Jamaica. It does not occur with other vegetation. This species has males and females that are easily distinguishable. The sex ratio is male biased. The flowers are visited by hummingbirds and bees (V. Negron-Ortiz pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_threats:
Cactoblastis cactorum is devastating to the recruitment of C. spinosissima in Jamaica, with small seedlings and young regrowth underneath the large trees highly susceptible to larval attack. The damage is so extensive that it may impede the reproduction and long-term survival of the species (Zimmermann et al. 2005).

Urban development is also one of the major threats to this species in Jamaica (V. Negron-Ortiz, pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has a very restricted distribution (extent of occurrence is 900 km2), it occurs in only three locations and there is continuing decline in the extent and quality of the habitat due to urban development. The larvae of  the invasive Cactoblastis moth are affecting the survival of seedlings and young plants. This could result in population declines in the future if there is reduced or no recruitment. The population size is already fairly small numbering about 270 mature individuals.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa atacamensis Middled. sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Copiapoa calderana F.Ritter subsp. atacamensis  (Middled.) Doweld; Copiapoa calderana F.Ritter subsp. atacamensis (Middled.) D.R.Hunt
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Intentional use (species is the target); Droughts
iucn_doc_range: Copiapoa atacamensis is an endemic species of Chile, located mainly in the region of Antofagasta, from Antofagasta to the south of Blanco Encalada, in the Botija valley, in coastal hills, is restricted to less than ten known localities and is distributed at elevations from 200 to 1,100 m asl (BIOTA 2010).
iucn_doc_habitats: This plant usually grows associated with the lower and upper zones of the coastal fog mantle (camanchaca), and is less frequent in the zone of higher humidity (600–800 m asl). The population in Morro Moreno is the most abundant (BIOTA 2010).
iucn_doc_threats: This species and its habitat are affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is their main source of water. Road construction affects this species and its habitat. In the past herbivory by goats and mules was also a threat, however, because of the droughts farming has ceased in the area. Collection pressure for international markets and ornamental use in private collections is also a threat (BIOTA 2010).
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa atacamensis has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 1,409 km2 and an area of occupancy (AOO) of 56 km2. This species has experienced severe declines due to prolonged droughts in recent years. The subpopulations in the south are the most affected by extended droughts resulting from a reduction of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change, and are considered as consisting of one to two localities. The more abundant subpopulations in the north are considered as consisting of three localities, with four to five locations in total. The species is also affected by illegal collection for international trade. It is therefore assessed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa australis (Hoxey) Helmut Walter & Larridon sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Copiapoa humilis Hutch. subsp. australis Hoxey
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Smog; Recreational activities; Nomadic grazing
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile where it is found on hilltops south of Huasco in the Atacama region at elevations of 400 to 1,000 m asl (Schulz 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is usually found in coastal desert on hilltops.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is habitat degradation as a result of extended droughts resulting from a reduction of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Local tourism also disturbs the species by the dumping of trash, starting of fires, and trampling. Goat grazing occurs in the area, which degrades the vegetation and soil causing desertification. A nearby thermoelectric plant affects the plants by soot deposition.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very restricted range, with an extent of occurrence (EOO) and area of occupancy (AOO) of 4 km<sup>2</sup>. This small area is affected by several threats including climate change and tourism, and it is considered one location. The population is experiencing a continuing decline and it is therefore assessed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa calderana F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Tourism & recreation areas; Droughts; Intentional use (species is the target); Garbage & solid waste; Mining & quarrying
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs from Chañaral to the littoral zone north of Caldera in Atacama. It grows at elevations of 0 to 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky coastal outcrops and rocky coastal hillside (lomas costeras), in dry areas with rare rainfall. The dry season lasts 8 to 12 months and in some years there is no rainfall at all.
iucn_doc_threats: Copiapoa calderana is threatened by illegal harvesting, habitat degradation for urban housing development, commercial businesses, tourism and pollution from industrial port construction (BIOTA 2010). The construction of pipelines for mining also affects its habitat. Climate affects this species but is not yet a major threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa calderana has a restricted range, with an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 405 km2 and an area of occupancy (AOO) of 76 km2. Based on threats, the species occurs in six locations. It is experiencing a continuing decline in the number of individuals from illegal collection and in habitat quality due to urban development and other threats. It does not occur in protected areas. This species is therefore assessed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa cinerascens (Salm-Dyck) Britton & Rose sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Intentional use (species is the target); Recreational activities
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile where it occurs from the National Park Pan de Azúcar, southward to the north of Obispito. It grows with Copiapoa serpentisulcata (Charles 1998, Hoffmann and Walter 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in hills and coastal plains (Hoffmann and Walter 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat to this cactus is the visitors of Pan de Azúcar National Park who kick the clumps of plants. Guanacos eat the flowers of this cactus, which can be a potential threat. The species is illegally collected, with tens of individuals seized in Italy during "Operation Atacama". This species and its habitat, especially the coastal subpopulations, are severely affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is their main source of water.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa cinerascens has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 526 km2. It occurs in five locations, where the species is threatened by illegal collection, vandalism and climate change, causing a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals and quality of its habitat. Hence, the species is listed as Endangered.

checklist_fullName: Copiapoa coquimbana (Karw. ex Rümpler) Britton & Rose
checklist_taxon: Copiapoa coquimbana (Karw. ex Rümpler) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_synonyms: Echinocactus coquimbanus Karw. ex Rümpler; Copiapoa schulziana I.Schaub & Keim
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Desert - Cold
iucn_threat_names: Large dams; Agro-industry farming; Droughts; Renewable energy; Tourism & recreation areas; Recreational activities
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Chile, mainly south of Huasco River, south to Puerto Oscuro and inland east of Vallenar, in the regions of Atacama and Coquimbo. It grows at elevations of 0 to 1,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in coastal hills (lomas costeras), in desert and thorny shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are urban development for tourism and the intensification of agriculture. Dam construction has affected subpopulations in the Elqui and Huasco valleys. Part of the range is affected by industrial agriculture for grape production for pisco. Some subpopulations are affected by solar and wind farms and off-road vehicles. Climate change affects this species because of extended droughts and fog (camanchaca) reduction.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa coquimbana has a relatively wide range, with an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 18,141 km2, is abundant, occurs within protected areas and even though there are threats in portions of its range, they are not sufficient to trigger a threatened listing. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa esmeraldana F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Copiapoa humilis (Phil.) Hutchison var. esmeraldana (F.Ritter) A.E.Hoffm.
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Avalanches/landslides; Named species; Droughts
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the region of Antofagasta, south of Esmeralda (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in coastal hills (lomas costeras), where there used to be heavy fog cover.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are illegal collection and guanacos that eat the flowers and the tuberous roots, causing extreme population fluctuations. The population is also affected by erosion; there is not much vegetation in the area and the scarce rains cause the slopes to erode. This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa esmeraldana is listed as Critically Endangered as it has a very restricted extent of occurrence (EOO) of 35 km2, it is known from a single location, and is threatened by illegal collection, erosion, predation from guanacos and extended droughts that are causing a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals and habitat quality.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa humilis (Phil.) Hutch. sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Echinocactus humilis Phil.; Copiapoa humilis (Phil.) Hutchison var. paposoensis (F.Ritter) A.E.Hoffm.; Copiapoa paposoensis F.Ritter [nom. inval.]
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying; Roads & railroads; Droughts; Nomadic grazing
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in Chile from Huasco to Tocopilla, in the regions of Atacama and Antofagasta. It grows at elevations of 0 to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Copiapoa humilis tends to be found in coastal mountains away from roads. It is therefore likely that there are more, currently unknown locations (Charles 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are mining activities within its area of distribution and to a lesser extent road construction. Goat grazing used to be a threat, but this activity is no longer practiced within its range. This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Guanacos extract plant roots during droughts. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water. The subpopulation at Tocopilla is particularly severely affected by drought. Illegal collection is another threat, as the species is easily extracted and transported due to its small size.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa humilis has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 3,240 km2, but it is severely fragmented due to the loss and fragmentation of habitat because of human activities. It is known from seven locations, where there are continuing declines in the number of mature individuals and habitat quality due to several threats including climate change and illegal collection. The species is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa hypogaea F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Named species; Droughts
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the region of Atacama at elevations of 0 to 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in fairly flat, gravelly areas near the top of low hills (Charles 1998). It grows on coastal hills (lomas costeras).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are illegal collection and guanacos, which eat the plants when other food sources are very scarce. This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa hypogaea has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of approximately 73 km2 and area of occupancy (AOO) of 16 km2. It occurs at three to four locations. There is a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals due to illegal collection and predation from guanacos, and a decline in habitat quality due to climate change. Hence, it is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa leonensis I.Schaub & Keim sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Copiapoa megarhiza Britton & Rose subsp. parvula Mächler & Helmut Walter
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Recreational activities; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in Quebrada El León, from 200 to 1,000 m asl (Schulz 2006). Its extent of occurrence (EOO) and area of occupancy (AOO) are estimated to be 4 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: This plant grows on rocky soil on coastal hills (lomas costeras).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by off-road vehicles including cars and motorcycles. This species is also affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water. Illegal trade for international collections also threatens this cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa leonensis has a very restricted range, with an extent of occurrence (EOO) and area of occupancy (AOO) of 4 km2. It is known from one location where the species is threatened by off-road vehicles and illegal collection causing a continuing decline in habitat quality and the number of mature individuals.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa longispina F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Copiapoa humilis (Phil.) Hutchison var. longispina (F.Ritter) A.E.Hoffm.; Copiapoa humilis (Phil.) Hutchison subsp. longispina (F.Ritter) Doweld
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile and is only known from one small population south of Copiapo (Schulz 2006). Its extent of occurrence (EOO) and area of occupancy (AOO) are estimated to be 4 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows inland where it grows on dry hills.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water. This species is illegally collected for international collections.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa longispina has an extremely restricted range, with an extent of occurrence (EOO) and area of occupancy (AOO) of 4 km2. Based on threats it is known from one location where the population experiences continuing declines in the number of mature individuals due to illegal collection, and habitat quality due to extended droughts. The species is listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa rupestris F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Copiapoa desertorum F.Ritter var. rupestris  (F.Ritter) A.E.Hoffm.
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Roads & railroads; War, civil unrest & military exercises; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it is found in the region of Antofagasta.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on coastal hills (lomas costeras).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water. Others threats include mining, road construction for tourism access and military activity, including heavy-duty vehicles. Illegal collection also affects this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa rupestris has a small range with an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 438 km2. It is known from three to five locations, where it is threatened by illegal collection, road building and climate change, which cause a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals and habitat quality. The species is listed here as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa serpentisulcata F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Desert - Cold
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Recreational activities; Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, occurring in Atacama to the north of Chañaral (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of 0–500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on coastal hills (lomas costeras) on granitic slopes.
iucn_doc_threats: There is illegal collection but this is not a major threat. In flat areas disturbance from off-road vehicles and tourism is a threat. This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water. There is mining prospecting in the region, which could represent a future threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a very restricted range, with an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 43 km<sup>2</sup> and an area of occupancy (AOO) of 24 km<sup>2</sup>. It is known from five locations, which are experiencing continuing declines in the number of mature individuals and habitat quality due to climate change and illegal collection. It is therefore listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa solaris (F.Ritter) F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Mining & quarrying; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, occurring in the Antofagasta region, south and north of Antofagasta city (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 300 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on coastal hills (lomas costeras) on granitic soils. The seeds are dispersed by ants (Schulz 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are mining and its consequences, such as dust. This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water. Illegal collection also affects this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 93 km<sup>2</sup> and an area of occupancy (AOO) of 44 km<sup>2</sup>. It is known from four locations and it is severely fragmented. The species is experiencing a continuing decline in EOO, AOO, the number of mature individuals, and habitat quality, mainly due to climate change, but it is also affected by illegal collection. The northernmost subpopulation has been nearly extirpated. The species is listed here as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Corryocactus aureus (Meyen) Hutchison sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru. It grows at an altitude of 2,000 to 2,500 m asl in the Arequipa region (Arakaki et al. 2006). Hunt (2006) states that the species is also found in Moquegua and Tacna. This difference is related to a taxonomic confusion, as the subpopulations in Moquegua and Tacna are morphologically different from the one in Arequipa (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: Corryocactus aureus forms colonies with short turgid stems connected by underground roots (Arakaki et al. 2006). It occurs in inter-Andean valleys and matorral.
iucn_doc_threats: There is mining activity within its range. However, it is not known whether this affects the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: There is considerable confusion regarding the taxonomy of this species. It is probable that C. aureus, as defined by Hunt (2006), actually includes two separate, morphologically distinct species (see Arakaki et al. 2006). The species is classified as Data Deficient until the taxonomy is resolved.

checklist_taxon: Corryocactus brachypetalus (Vaupel) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to the coast of Peru’s Arequipa region, specifically in Bella Unión, Atiquipa, Achanizo, Camaná (Caravelí province), San Gregorio, Quilca (Camaná province), Matarani, Mollendo, Cocachacra, and Punta de Bombón (Islay province). It grows at an altitude of 50 - 1,000 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006, F. Cáceres pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on lomas costeras.
iucn_doc_threats: In Mollendo and Camaná, the species has been affected by drought, human sprawl, overgrazing, and mining activities. Many individuals are affected by parasitic insects at their base. In Atiquipa, however, the populations are in a good state.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a relatively restricted range (extent of occurrence is 15,000 km<sup>2</sup>) and low population densities. It is threatened by human activities as well as natural factors, such as droughts, and parasitic insects. This has led to reductions in number of mature individuals and in the population size of around 50% in the last 10 years. Also, a reduction in habitat quality has been observed. The reasons for its decline are understood, but have not ceased and are not reversible. In Camaná, Peru, the species is considered locally extinct or extirpated. The species is therefore classified as Endangered under criterion A2ace.

checklist_taxon: Corryocactus melanotrichus (K.Schum.) Britton & Rose sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species in endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in La Paz, Cochabamba and Potosí, at altitudes between 2,100 and 3,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs on slopes within seasonally dry inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: Clearance for subsistence farming is impacting the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Corryocactus melanotrichus is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide distribution and is abundant where it occurs.

checklist_fullName: Coryphantha calipensis Bravo ex S.Arias, Gama & U.Guzmán
checklist_taxon: Coryphantha calipensis Bravo ex S.Arias, Gama & U.Guzmán sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
Coryphantha clavata (Scheidw.) Backeb. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Hidalgo; Guanajuato; San Luis Potosí; Aguascalientes; Jalisco; Zacatecas; Querétaro; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-HI; MXE-GU; MXE-SL; MXE-AG; MXS-JA; MXE-ZA; MXE-QU; MX
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico. It is fairly widespread, occurring in the states of Aguascalientes, Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí and Zacatecas.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on grasslands and sandy soils (Sivinski 2007, Cartron et al. 2008). It has been recorded growing on the lower slopes of limestone and volcanic hills, usually growing under other shrubs, e.g., agaves and hechtias. Plants are usually solitary.
iucn_doc_threats: Use of fire to improve grazing may be a threat in parts of the range, but overall there are no major threats.
iucn_doc_rationale:
Coryphantha clavata is a widespread and common species, which overall has no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.
This assessment is based on one published in 2002; it is assumed that there has been no change in status because it is a widespread and abundant species.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha compacta (Engelm.) Orcutt sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_use_trade_names: Medicine - human & veterinary; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to northwestern Mexico, where it occurs on the eastern slopes of the Sierra Madre Occidental in the states of Durango and Chihuahua.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species can be found in pine forests and grasslands (González-Elizondo and González-Elizondo 1993). It grows on plains and ridges of low hills in volcanic soils. Plants are usually solitary.
iucn_doc_threats: Land use change for agriculture may be a threat in places, but this is not having a significant impact at present.
iucn_doc_rationale:
Coryphantha compacta is a fairly widespread and very abundant species, which is under no major threat at present. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.
This assessment is based on one published in 2002; it is assumed that there has been no change in status because it is a widespread and abundant species.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha cornifera (DC.) Lem. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Querétaro; Jalisco; Zacatecas; Hidalgo; Guanajuato; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-SL; MXE-QU; MXS-JA; MXE-ZA; MXE-HI; MXE-GU; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Nomadic grazing
iucn_doc_range: Coryphantha cornifera is endemic to Mexico and occurs in several states. It has been reported from herbarium records from Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, Querétaro, San Luis Potosi and Zacatecas. Doubtful records exist from the State of Mexico, Chihuahua and Veracruz and from the literature it is reported in Durango (Dicht and Lüthy 2003, Hernández et al. 2004). Records from Texas and Arizona in the Unites States likely pertain to C. difficilis (H. Hernández and C. Gomez-Hinostrosa pers. comm. 2009). The species grows at elevations of 1,800-2,300 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: Coryphantha cornifera grows in limestone gravel on low hills and plains in xerophyllous scrub and grasslands. Plants are usually solitary.
iucn_doc_threats: Some subpopulations of  Coryphantha cornifera are being reduced by grazing by goats and cattle, as well as expanding agriculture, but generally these factors are not causing major declines.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Coryphantha cornifera has a relatively limited range, it is abundant and occurs in numerous protected areas. Therefore, this species is considered to be Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha delaetiana (Quehl) A.Berger sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it can be found in the states of Chihuahua, Durango and Coahuila (Dicht and Lüthy 2003).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on alluvial soils in plains that are only sparsely used for farming.
iucn_doc_threats: There is some farming for crops in parts of its range, but this is not a major threat.
iucn_doc_rationale:
Coryphantha delaetiana is a fairly widespread and very common species with no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.
This assessment is based on one published in 2002; it is assumed that there has been no change in status because it is a widespread and abundant species.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha delicata L.Bremer sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Tamaulipas; Nuevo León; Zacatecas; San Luis Potosí; Coahuila; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-TA; MXE-NL; MXE-ZA; MXE-SL; MXE-CO; MX
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is a Mexican endemic with a fairly wide range in the central states of   Coahuila, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas and Zacatecas.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in dry grasslands on limestone gravel or in alluvial soils on plains. It has also been recorded on the slopes and tops of hills.
iucn_doc_rationale:
Coryphantha delicata is listed as Least Concern because this is a widespread and common species with no major threats.
This assessment is based on one published in 2002; it is assumed that there has been no change in status because it is a widespread and abundant species.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha difficilis (Quehl) Orcutt sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: United States; Arizona; Texas; Coahuila; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: This Mexican endemic has a fairly restricted range being found in the area around the Sierra de la Paila in Coahuila State. There are supposedly reports of the species from the United States (Texas and Arizona) that require confirmation.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows as solitary plants in shrubland on alluvial plains and in limestone gravel on the slopes of the Sierra Paila and adjacent localities.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats, although some subpopulations are declining due to trampling by goats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Coryphantha difficilis has a more restricted range than other species, the extent of occurrence is still well over 20,000 km², and it is a common species with no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_fullName: Coryphantha durangensis (Runge ex K.Schum.) Britton & Rose
checklist_taxon: Coryphantha durangensis (Runge ex K.Schum.) Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Housing & urban areas; Commercial & industrial areas; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to the Mexican state of Durango. The subspecies cuencamensis is known only from a single locality near Cuencame whereas the typical subspecies occurs more widely.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in dry shrubland on the rocky slopes of volcanic hills. Plants branch and form small clumps. It has a generation length of about ten years.
iucn_doc_threats: The area is highly industrialized, hence some subpopulations have been impacted by habitat loss due to urban expansion, agricultural activities, and the like.
iucn_doc_rationale:
Although Coryphantha durangensis has an extent of occurrence less than 20,000 km² and there is continuing decline in parts of its range, it is known from more than ten locations and the population is not considered to be severely fragmented. Hence, the species as a whole is listed as Least Concern. However, subspecies cuencamensis has a much more restricted range and is listed separately as it is threatened.
This assessment is based on one published in 2002; it is assumed that there has been no change in status because it is a widespread and abundant species.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha echinoidea (Quehl) Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_doc_range: This species is easily confused with other similar species and thus its actual distribution is difficult to define. It is believed to occur only in state of San Luis Potosí, Mexico - however it is also reported in Tamaulipas. The species was reportedly first described from a collection supposedly made in the state of Durango, but its occurrence in that state has not been confirmed.
iucn_doc_habitats: Solitary plants grow in dry grassland areas and matorral, usually on the lower slopes of hills in limestone gravel.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats known to affect this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered to be Least Concern, where as further research is needed to define the actual limits and population size of this species, there are currently no major threats and the species is not currently declining fast enough to warrant placement in a threat category.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha echinus (Engelm.) Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Coahuila; Mexico; Chihuahua; Texas; United States
iucn_doc_range: This species has been recorded from the Big Bend area in western Texas (Unites States), and from the states of Chihuahua and Coahuila in Mexico. Coryphantha echinus occurs at elevations from 300 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats:
The plants of Coryphantha echinus are often solitary but can sometimes form large clumps. This species grows in very dry shrubland or degraded grasslands on alluvial plains covered with limestone gravel. The species has also been recorded on or near igneous hills and benches, growing with Larrea.
Coryphantha echinus flowers from April to July and its flowers are very ephemeral, lasting only for a couple of hours. Coryphanta echinus has its peak of anthesis at high noon, while most cacti in the Chihuahuan Desert have the peak of anthesis around mid afternoon (from Zimmerman and Parfitt (2003) Flora of North America 4: 227).
iucn_doc_threats: No major threats are known for Coryphantha echinus.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a widely distributed species with scattered but densely populated subpopulations. There are no major threats so Coryphantha echinus is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha elephantidens (Lem.) Lem. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Veracruz; Querétaro; Zacatecas; Guanajuato; Aguascalientes; Michoacán; México State; Puebla; Guerrero; Oaxaca; Hidalgo; Morelos; Jalisco; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXG-VC; MXE-QU; MXE-ZA; MXE-GU; MXE-AG; MXS-MI; MXC-ME; MXC-PU; MXS-GR; MXS-OA; MXE-HI; MXC-MO; MXS-JA; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico where it is known to occur in the states of Aguascalientes, Guerrero, Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, México, Michoacán, Morelos, Oaxaca, Puebla, Querétaro, Veracruz and Zacatecas (Dicht and Lüthy 2003, Hernández et al. 2004, Hunt et al. 2006). The subspecies C. elephantidens greenwoodii has a restricted range near Piedras Blancas, Veracruz. Subspecies bumamma is recorded from the States of Oaxaca and Michoacán.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in grasslands and xerophyllous scrub (H. Hernández pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by deforestation from small-holder agriculture and cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha elephantidens is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread and relatively common. One subspecies, C. elephantidens greenwoodii, in the state of Veracruz, is threatened by development in its restricted range.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha erecta (Lem. ex Pfeiff.) Lem. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Hidalgo; Guanajuato; San Luis Potosí; Querétaro; Mexico
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Mining & quarrying
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Querétaro and San Luis Potosí (Dicht and Lüthy 2003, Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in dry shrubland (often with agaves and various trees) on calcareous gravels and on igneous rock substrate. The plants branch basally to form clumps.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats. Some subpopulation are declining due to conversion of habitat to farmland, roads (paving) as well as mining activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a widely distributed and abundant species with no major threats. Hence, Coryphantha erecta is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha georgii Boed. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Nuevo León; Tamaulipas; San Luis Potosí; Guanajuato; Mexico
iucn_threat_names: Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to the Mexican states of    Guanajuato, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí and Tamaulipas.
iucn_doc_habitats: This occurs as solitary plants that grow in dry oak forests on volcanic soils of plains and slopes. The forest understorey is often grassy.
iucn_doc_threats: The habitat is being impacted by deforestation for expanding agriculture (conversion to pastures for livestock), harvesting for wood, and increased use of fires. Some of these threats are leading to increased soil erosion. However, the species appears to be able to survive after deforestation.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha georgii has an extent of occurrence greater than 20,000 km² and it is widely scattered with few plants at each location, this species occurs at more than ten locations and the population is not severely fragmented. There is ongoing decline in the quality of the habitat due to increasing human impacts, however, the species seems to be tolerant to some degree of disturbance and still occurs in areas after deforestation has occurred. Hence it is listed as Least Concern, but monitoring is required as it may qualify for listing in a threatened category in the future.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha glanduligera (Otto & A.Dietr.) Lem. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Tamaulipas; San Luis Potosí; Nuevo León; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to the Mexican states of   Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí and Tamaulipas.
iucn_doc_habitats: The solitary plants grow in shrubland on hill slopes in calcareous gravels, sometimes protected by other bushes such as Agaves and Opuntias.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha glanduligera has a fairly wide range and is a common species found at many locations that are not currently impacted by any human activities. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha glassii Dicht & A.Lüthy sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_doc_range: This species, only described in 2000 (Dicht and Lüthy 2002), is confined to the Mexican states of   Guanajuato and San Luis Potosí.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in dry grassland on slopes and tops of hills and mountains, and in calcareous gravels. The plants usually grow from large underground stems and form clusters of several stems.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats at present. All the subpopulations are healthy and untouched by human activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Coryphrantha glassii has a small range (extent of occurrence is less than 10,000 km²), and the population is small (probably larger than 1,000 mature individuals at about three locations), there are no major threats to the species as no human impacts have been seen at any of the known locations. Hence it is listed as Least Concern. However, this species requires monitoring as with any change in the situation it may very quickly become threatened.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha greenwoodii Bravo sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
Coryphantha hintoniorum Dicht & A.Lüthy sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Coahuila; Nuevo León; Mexico
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Agro-industry farming; Housing & urban areas; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Coahuila, Nuevo León and San Luis Potosí, at elevations of 1,800 to 2,600 m asl (Dicht and Lüthy 2003).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on sandy-loamy alluvial plains with gypsum and on deep soils on grassy areas close to pine forests (Dicht and Lüthy 2003). The generation length is 11 years.
iucn_doc_threats: A constant loss of plants due to agricultural activities (cultivation) has been observed in recent years. The size of the untouched prairie is diminishing from year to year.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species was discovered in 1997, and since then the area where it occurs has undergone continuing transformation for agricultural purposes, which has resulted in an estimated 30% population loss in a short period (six years). Therefore the species is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha jalpanensis Buchenau sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Querétaro; Hidalgo; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: Coryphantha jalpanensis has been recorded mainly from the state of Querétaro but also found in the neighbouring areas of San Luis Potosí and Hidalgo, Mexico. It grows at elevations between 700 and 1,350 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in dry shrubland on limestone rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: Despite the human activities in the surrounding areas, there are thought to be no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha jalpanensis has a small range (extent of occurrence is 3,000 km²), is known only from two subpopulations with a small population, but because it grows on rocks, it appears to be safe from human activities. There are farming activities in the surrounding areas. The species is listed as Least Concern, but it probably warrants close monitoring as its status could quickly change.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha kracikii Halda, Chalupa & Kupčák sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Durango, where it is known from a single location at 1,400 m asl (Dicht and Lüthy 2003).
iucn_doc_habitats: The habitat where this species grows is uncertain, however, it is very likely to be desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There is no information on threats known.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because the necessary information on its population size, trend and threats to evaluate its conservation status is not available.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha longicornis Boed. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_doc_range: This species appears to be confined to the area around the   Rio Nazas in Durango state, Mexico.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in dry grassland on hills and slopes, often under bushes or Oaks. The soils are volcanic and plants are usually solitary.
iucn_doc_rationale:
Although this species has a small range (extent of occurrence is about 4,500 km²), the population numbers several thousand plants with a good age structure and there do not appear to be any threats, hence it is listed as Least Concern.
This assessment is based on one published in 2002; it is assumed that there has been no change in status because it is an  abundant species and there were no major threats.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha neglecta L.Bremer sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Mexico where it occurs on the western slopes of the Sierra Madre Oriental from Monclova in Coahuila to Monterrey in Nuevo León state.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in semi-desert shrubland on limestone gravels or alluvial soils, usually at the foot of hills but also on ridges. Plants can be solitary or form clumps.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale:
Coryphantha neglecta is listed as Least Concern because this species has a wide range with many densely populated subpopulations and there are no major threats.
This assessment is based on one published in 2002; it is assumed that there has been no change in status because it is a widespread and abundant species.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha nickelsiae (K.Brandegee) Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_common_name: Nickels' Cactus; Nickels's Coryphantha; Biznaga-partida de Laredo; Nickels' Pincushion Cactus
iucn_locations: Mexico; United States; Texas; Coahuila; Nuevo León
iucn_doc_range: This cactus has been recorded from the eastern slopes of the Sierra Madre Oriental, occurring in Texas (United States) and in the Mexican states of Coahuila and Nuevo León (Vargas and Martínez-Ávalos 2011), at elevations of around 300 m asl (Benson 1982).
iucn_doc_habitats: Coryphantha nickelsiae grows in semi-desert shrubland on the edge of the Chihuahuan Desert. The species is often found on limestone outcrops or in alluvial soils at the foot of hills and ridges and usually grows under other bushes. Plants may be solitary or may have many stems forming low mats. This species flowers in summer (August to September).
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat were Coryphantha nickelsiae grows has been lost in some areas, e.g., around Monterrey, due to urban expansion (settlaments and roads).
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Coryphantha nickelsiae has a restricted range (extent of occurrence is less than 5,000 km²) and there has been some decline due to the expansion of human settlements, this species is listed as Least Concern because the population is still large and stable and impacts so far have been minimal.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha octacantha (DC.) Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Hidalgo; Aguascalientes; Querétaro; Tamaulipas; Guanajuato; San Luis Potosí; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-HI; MXE-AG; MXE-QU; MXE-TA; MXE-GU; MXE-SL; MX
iucn_threat_names: Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Mexico. It has been reported to be present in the states of Aguascalientes, Guanajauto, Hidalgo and Querétaro (Hernández et al. 2004). Dicht and Lüthy (2003) also report it as occurring rarely in the states of San Luis Potosí and in Tamaulipas.
iucn_doc_habitats: Plants are solitary or branch basally to form clumps. The species grows in dry grassland on limestone gravel or alluvial soils at the foot of hills and on ridges.
iucn_doc_threats: Although there is some illegal collection, this does not appear to be a major threat. The main threat to the species is increasing frequency of fires to renew grazing areas for livestock.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha octacantha is a widespread species found at innumerable localities, with a very large population, and with many plants occurring in huge groups in remote areas. Although there are some threats these are not severe enough to trigger a more threatened listing. Hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha ottonis (Pfeiff.) Lem. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico. It has a wide range, occurring in the states of   Tlaxcala, Guanajuato, Jalisco and Zacatecas.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found is grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: Some subpopulations of this species are threatened by overcollecting.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha ottonis is considered to be Least Concern due to its widespread and relatively abundant populations.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha pallida Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Small-holder farming; Nomadic grazing; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: Coryphantha pallida is endemic to Puebla and Oaxaca, Mexico, occurring in the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán Valley (Arias et al. 1997, López-Galindo et al. 2003, Méndez-Larios et al. 2006), at elevations of 1,000 to 2,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in low tropical caduceus forest and xerophyllous scrub on calcareous soils (Arias et al. 1997). It prefers low forest-shrub that is closely associated with mezquital. It is often associated with Prosposis laevigata (López-Galindo et al. 2003).
iucn_doc_threats: Hydraulic erosion, and chemical, physical and biological degradation in Tehuacán-Cuicatlán due to mining, agriculture and livestock are some of the major threats (López-Galindo et al. 2003). Illegal collection is another major threat; Coryphantha pallida is popular for its attractive flowers and the populations are in decline due to collection of plants, especially larger specimens.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha pallida has a relatively wide range, it occurs within the Biosphere Reserve of Tehuacán-Cuicatlán and it is locally propagated, hence the species is listed as Least Concern. However, there is evidence of illegal collection and habitat fragmentation affecting the population. Field studies are urgently needed to establish if the species qualifies as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha poselgeriana (A.Dietr.) Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Chihuahua; Coahuila; Nuevo León; San Luis Potosí; Zacatecas; Durango; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-CU; MXE-CO; MXE-NL; MXE-SL; MXE-ZA; MXE-DU; MX
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí and Zacatecas (Hernández et al. 2004), at elevations of 1,000 to 1,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in xerophile scrubs.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha poselgeriana has a large extent of occurrence, even though it is not very abundant there are no known threats. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha potosiana (Jacobi) Glass & R.A.Foster sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Other ecosystem modifications
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in one location approximately 30 km southwest of San Luís Potosí, Mexico. Its range is less than 1 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in flat grasslands with volcanic, gravelly soils.
iucn_doc_threats: Currently, this species is threatened by goats grazing over the range causing loss of individuals to trampling and habitat degradation from erosion. In the near future, it will be threatened by growing development from land use change for small holder agriculture of corn.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Critically Endangered because it has a range smaller than 1 km<sup>2</sup>, it is known from only one location, and is experiencing continuing decline. This species is also predicted to have a 80% population decline in the future (10 years) based upon the current rate of conversion to agriculture in the surrounding areas.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha pseudoechinus Boed. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Nomadic grazing
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Coahuila, Mexico.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found is xerophyllous scrub on calcareous soils.
iucn_doc_threats: Some subpopulations are affected by trampling by goats and other livestock.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha pseudoechinus is considered to be Least Concern due to its widespread distribution and relatively high abundance.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha pseudonickelsiae Backeb. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the state of Durango, Mexico (Dicht and Lüthy 2003).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on lava gravel on hills slopes (Dicht and Lüthy 2005), in desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: Some threats are likely to occur within the species range, however, there is no current information.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has an extent of occurrence well over 20,000 km<sup>2</sup>,<sup> </sup> even though the population size and trend is unknown and there might be threats in parts of its range, it is unlikely the species qualifies for a threatened category given the large area the species is found in. Hence, it is listed here as Least Concern, however, more research is necessary to more accurately assess its status.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha pseudoradians Bravo sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
Coryphantha pycnacantha (Mart.) Lem. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Puebla; Veracruz; Hidalgo; Tlaxcala; Oaxaca; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXC-PU; MXG-VC; MXE-HI; MXC-TL; MXS-OA; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Small-holder farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: Coryphanta pycnacantha is endemic to Mexico, where it is reported to occur in the states of Hidalgo, Puebla, Tlaxcala and Veracruz (Hunt et al. 2006). Arias et al. (1997) also report the species from the state of Oaxaca, at elevations of 1,400 to 2,100 m asl. The extent of occurrence is large (28,000 km²), but there are only a few locations left.
iucn_doc_habitats: Coryphantha pycnacantha inhabits xerophile scrubs, grasslands, deep and flat lava soils in grass or besides anthills. It flowers between June and September (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: Nearly all potential habitats are cultivated and the  known subpopulations are mostly reduced to very small areas along railway-tracks,  rubbish dumps, and other disturbed areas.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha pycnacantha grows on deep lava soils, which is usually the best arable land. The distribution to the east of Mexico City is in fact the most intensively cultivated area in Mexico. Nearly all potential habitats are cultivated and the known subpopulations are mostly reduced to very small areas along railway-tracks, rubbish dumps, and the like. There is only one large subpopulation that occurs within a tourist resort and is known to be more or less safe from agriculture. A very rapid and continuous decline in the extent of occurrence took place and will continue. Several locations are known, but most of them consist of a only few remaining plants. Hence, the species is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha ramillosa Cutak sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Chihuahua; Mexico; Coahuila; Texas; United States
iucn_doc_range: This species is reported from the states of Chihuahua and Coahuila in Mexico, and in the Unites States from Texas (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations from 110 to 1,050 m asl (Powell and Weedin 2004). In Texas, the species occurs near the Rio Grande in Brewster and southern Terrell counties. In Mexico, the species extends south into Coahuila, Mexico, occupying a substantial portion of the northern part of the state (Dicht and Lüthy 2005).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on limestone in xerophyllous scrub and in the desert on bare rock, talus, or scree. Coryphantha ramillosa also grows in Chihuahuan Desert succulent scrub on rocky slopes, ledges, and gravelly flats on Santa Elena or Boquillas limestones.
iucn_doc_threats: Coryphantha ramillosa is collected in great quantities by commercial dealers for direct sale from the field, which results in a localized but substantial threat. This species is also threatened by development which could modify habitat and make populations more readily accessible to collectors.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha ramillosa has a wide range, and even though it is scarce, there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha retusa (Pfeiff.) Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Temperate; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: Coryphantha retusa is endemic to Oaxaca and Puebla, Mexico (Acosta et al. 2003), where it occurs in xerophyllous scrub, open Quercus forest and grasslands at elevation of 1,700 to 2,300 m (Arias et al. 1987).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in xerophyllous scrub, open Quercus forest and grasslands (Arias et al.1987); flowering time is between June and October (Arias et al. 1987).
iucn_doc_threats: Little is known about the threats to this species because of the taxonomic uncertainty.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because of the current taxonomic uncertainty.

checklist_fullName: Coryphantha robustispina (Schott ex Engelm.) Britton & Rose
checklist_taxon: Coryphantha robustispina (Schott ex Engelm.) Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_common_name: Pima Pineapple Cactus; Biznaga-partida de Espinas Gruesas
iucn_locations: Sonora; Mexico; Chihuahua; New Mexico; Arizona; United States; Texas
iucn_location_codes: MXN-SO; MX; MXE-CU; NWM-OO; ARI-OO; US; TEX-OO
iucn_doc_range: The species is distributed in Chihuahua and Sonora in Mexico, and in the United States in Arizona, New Mexico and Texas. It grows at elevations from 700 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Coryphantha robustispina grows in xerophyllous scrub and grasslands on sandy substrates, alluvial fans, and gravelly soils.
iucn_doc_threats: Mining for sand and gravel affects the subpopulations of Coryphantha robustispina in Mexico.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha robustispina has a very large extent of occurrence. Although it is relatively scarce and there are threats in places, these are not sufficient to warrant concern. Hence, this species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha salinensis (Poselg.) Dicht & A.Lüthy sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Coahuila, Nuevo León and Tamaulipas (Dicht and Lüthy 2003).
iucn_doc_habitats: Given its distribution, it is very likely that Coryphantha salinensis occurs in desert scrub. It grows on alluvial plains and limestone gravel (Dicht and Lüthy 2003).
iucn_doc_threats: There do not appear to be any significant threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has an extent of occurrence well over 20,000 km<sup>2</sup>,<sup> </sup> even though the population size and trend is unknown and there might be threats in parts of its range, it is unlikely the species qualifies for a threatened category given the large area the species is found in. Hence, the species is here listed as Least Concern, however, more research is necessary to more accurately assess its conservation status.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha sulcata (Engelm.) Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Tamaulipas; Mexico; Coahuila; Nuevo León; Texas; United States
iucn_location_codes: MXE-TA; MX; MXE-CO; MXE-NL; TEX-OO; US
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Temperate; Grassland - Temperate
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: Coryphantha sulcata is found in Mexico in the states of Coahuila, Nuevo León and Tamaulipas and in the United States in Texas (Hunt  et al. 2006), at elevations below 150 m asl (Benson 1982).
iucn_doc_habitats: Coryphantha sulcata grows in a variety of habitats, including Tamaulipan thorn scrub, in the limestone hills of the Edward Plateau, with Juniper forest, and in grasslands. This species occurs in sandy or gravelly soil of plains in valleys, grasslands, and thickets at low elevations .
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats; however, land use affects some subpopulations. Likely threats include urbanization and consequent habitat loss (especially in south-central Texas), brush clearing, and low-level collection (most cacti subject to horticultural collecting).
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha sulcata has a very wide range, and even though it is not abundant, there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha vaupeliana Boed. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
Coryphantha vogtherriana Werderm. & Boed. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_threat_names: Motivation Unknown/Unrecorded; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is found in San Luis Potosí, Mexico, in Cerro de San Pedro municipality.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on calcareous soils in clusters hidden in the grass in forests. The habitat is now an induced grassland as a result of the felling of the Quercus woodland that previously dominated the area. The habitat is locally classified as pastizal inducido (induced grassland; Rzedowski 1978).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species. Minor threats include erosion, deforestation and trampling by animals pasturing in their habitat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha vogtherriana is listed as Least Concern because of its large population size, ability to adapt to modified habitats, and lack of major threats.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha werdermannii Boed. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Coahuila, Mexico, in the Cuarto Ciénagas region (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus can be found is xerophyllous scrub habitats, on calcareous alluvial deposits.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats, however some subpopulations are impacted by livestock trampling (goats).
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha werdermannii is listed as Least Concern due to its relative abundance and because it is currently not declining fast enough to be in a threat category. However, some subpopulations are declining due to trampling from livestock, and because of the very limited distribution this species could rapidly become Vulnerable if threats are detected in the future.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha wohlschlageri Holzeis sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Mexico, where it is found in the southeastern parts of the state of San Luis Potosí and adjacent areas of Tamaulipas.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on gravel slopes and plains (usually limestone) in submontane scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha wohlschlageri is listed as Least Concern, although it occurs in a very small area there are no major threats and the species is not declining at a rate sufficient to qualify for listing in a threat category.

checklist_taxon: Cremnocereus albipilosus M.Lowry & Winberg sec. Lowry & Winberg 2017
Cumarinia odorata (Boed.) Buxb. sec. Breslin & al. 2021
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Nuevo León; Tamaulipas; Mexico
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is restricted to a small area of ca 1,420 km² in northern San Luis Potosí, southern Nuevo León and Tamaulipas, where 15 localities are known (Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus is usually found in limestone slopes and canyons of Agave lecheguilla-Hechtia glomerata scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There is habitat destruction as a result of overgrazing by goats, but this is not a major threat. There are fluoride mines in the area, but these are not posing a threat at present. There is some low-level illegal collection, but this is also not a threat to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The known distribution of Cumarinia odorata is extremely narrow (ca 1,420 km²), but although the species is scattered in its occurrence this is not considered to be severely fragmented (it is all one subpopulation) and there is no evidence for continuing decline. The habitat is being impacted by goat over-grazing and there are mining activities in the vicinity, but these are no longer thought to be causing declines as they were in the previous assessment of this species. The species is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cumulopuntia sphaerica (C.F.Först.) E.F.Anderson sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Desert - Cold
iucn_doc_range: C. sphaerica occurs in Antofagasta, Atacama, Coquimbo and Tarapacá regions of Chile, and Ayacucho, Tacna, Moquegua, Arequipa, Ica and Lima, Peru. It grows at an altitude of 0 to 3,500 m asl (Kattermann 2010).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows at high altitudes close to the timber line, in somewhat dry areas where the drought may last 3 to 5 months. Precipitation levels are lower than 350 mm per year. The species thrives in areas fully exposed to the sun, both in level areas and slopes facing north (Belov 2005).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cumulopuntia sphaerica is classified as Least Concern due to its wide distribution, high abundance, its presence in several protected areas and because it is not affected by any threat.

checklist_taxon: Cumulopuntia subterranea (R.E.Fr.) F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2016
Cylindropuntia abyssi (Hester) Backeb. sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the state of  Arizona in the United States (Hunt et al. 2006).  The species occurs in the Grand Canyon and Peach Spring Canyon (hence the species name) from 500 to 950 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on limestone alluvium and silty soils on west facing and southeast facing slopes. The species also occurs in rocky washes at the base of the canyon and has been recorded on east facing slopes on volcanic rocks and on granite. Associates include Echinocactus polycephalus, Cylindropuntia bigelovii, C. acanthocarpa, Cylindrocactus farinaceus, Foquieria splendens, and Ancelia farinosa.
iucn_doc_threats: The whole area where Cylindropuntia abyssi is found, is protected and largely inaccessible.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although this species has a very small extent of occurrence (less than 20 km²), it is locally abundant and there are no present or potential future threats. The area where this species occurs is an Indian Reservation that is largely inaccessible and is generally protected. Hence, this species is listed as Least concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia anteojoensis (Pinkava) E.F.Anderson sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_doc_range: This attractive dwarf cholla is restricted to a small area in Cuatro Ciénegas, Coahuila, Mexico where it is only known from six discrete localities (Hernández and Navarro 2007), occurring at elevations around 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in semi-desert shrubland on alluvial plains growing in association with helophytes.
iucn_doc_threats: Overgrazing of the habitat by goats and expanding agriculture (for crops) are the main threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Listed as Vulnerable because the extent of occurrence is about 7,500 km², it is known from six locations, and there is continuing decline in the quality of the habitat due to the impacts of agriculture (overgrazing and expanding crop cultivation).

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia arbuscula (Engelm.) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Food - human
iucn_doc_range: The species is found in the Mexican state of Sonora and in the United States in Arizona. Records of this species from southern Sonora and northern Sinaloa are most likely misidentifications of C. thurberi. This species occurs from sea level to 1,000 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: Cylindropuntia arbuscula only occurs on the east side of the Sonoran Desert in desert scrub (Paredes et al. 2000). This species is sensitive to frost.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to Cylindropuntia arbuscula.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a widespread and fairly common species with no major threats.  Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia bernardina (Engelm. ex Parish) M.A.Baker sec. ???
Cylindropuntia calmalliana (J.M.Coult.) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Baja California Sur; Baja California; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is found in a relatively small area straddling the state borders of Baja California and Baja California Sur.
iucn_doc_habitats: A very robust spiny shrub that sometimes forms clumps in sandy areas. The species grows on sandy flats and gravelly hillsides in desert scrub. It grows in association with Yucca valida.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Cylindropuntia calmalliana has an extent of occurrence of less than 10,000 km², the species is locally common and there are no threats.  Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia caribaea (Britton & Rose) F.M.Knuth sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Bonaire; Trinidad and Tobago; Curaçao; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Sint Maarten (Dutch part); Haiti; Saba; Saint Martin (French part); Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Cuba; Sint Eustatius; Dominican Republic
iucn_location_codes: NLA-BO; TT; CW; BQ; SX; HT; NLA-SA; MF; VE; CU; NLA-SE; DO
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands
iucn_doc_range: Cylindropuntia caribaea is found in the Netherlands Antilles, Dominican Republic, Haiti, Trinidad and Tobago and Venezuela (Hunt et al. 2006). It is also found in the dry formations of Colombia, in the Canyon of Patia Rio and Juanambu (Ruiz et al. 2002). Ponce (1989) includes it in Cuba but this record is dubious (L. Majure and N. Taylor pers. comm. 2011). In the Dominican Republic, it is very common from Bani to Pedernales and almost continuous throughout this area (G.D. Gann pers. comm. 2011). In Venezuela, it occurs from sea level to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Its reproduction is mostly clonal and flowers are rarely seen (J. Nassar pers. comm. 2011). It lives in dry forest (G.D. Gann pers. comm. 2011). In Venezuela it lives in open areas and spiny scrublands (J. Nassar pers. comm. 2011). It is an exochoric species dispersed mainly by cladodes attaching to medium-sized to large mammals (J. Nassar pers. comm. 2011). In the Dominican Republic, it is an aggressive species able to dominate a major part of today’s habitat of Cyclura recordii (Ricord's iguana) on the south side of lake Enriquillo due to charcoal burning activity (Rupp et al. 2005).
iucn_doc_threats: No major threats affect this species.
