iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia caribaea is a common species in the areas where it is found, and no major threats are known to be affecting its subpopulations. Therefore, it is assessed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia cholla (F.A.C.Weber) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Baja California; Baja California Sur; Mexico
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico from the states of Baja California and Baja California Sur (Hunt et al. 2006). The species also occurs on many of the off-shore islands in the Gulf of California. Plants along the Gulf of California coast in Sonora require further investigation to determine that they are indeed C. cholla (R. Puente pers. comm. 2010).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in coastal sage scrub and desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia cholla is a widespread and very common (even weedy) species with no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia ciribe (Engelm.) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
Cylindropuntia delgadilloana Rebman & Pinkava sec. Majure & al. 2019
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Baja California (Hunt et al. 2006) and is restricted to a small valley east of the Sierra Pedro Marte.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in creosote scrub in alluvial soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species. The valley in which this species occurs is very dry and does not support agricultural activity.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Cylindropuntia delgadilloana has a small range, it is not facing any major threats and is locally abundant. Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern. However, this species should be monitored because land-use changes could pose a threat (e.g., drilling a well for water would open the area for agriculture).

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia echinocarpa (Engelm. & J.M.Bigelow) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Arizona; California; United States; Sonora; Nevada; Baja California; Utah; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: ARI-OO; CAL-OO; US; MXN-SO; NEV-OO; MXN-BC; UTA-OO; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Temperate
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is reported in Mexico from the states of Baja California and Sonora, and in the United States from the states of Arizona, California, Nevada and Utah (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at elevations from 50 to 1,700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Cylindropuntia echinocarpa occurs from southern Nevada and southwest Utah to northeast Baja California and Sonora. The species is widespread in Sonora from the Colorado River to the great desert, inside the Pinacate region. It grows in thorn shrubs in the Mohave desert at elevations from 300 to 1,200 m asl. It is less common in the Sonoran Desert below 500 m in elevation (Paredes et al. 2000). The species grows in sandy soils, sandy loam, and sandy gravel soils in oak-juniper woodlands, coastal sage scrub on sand dunes, and in desert shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia echinocarpa is a widespread species that can be locally abundant despite its scattered distribution. The species is found in many protected areas across its range and there are no known threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia fulgida (Engelm.) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Arizona; United States; Sinaloa; Sonora; Mexico
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *; Food - animal
iucn_doc_range: This species is reported from the Mexican states of Sinaloa and Sonora and from the state of Arizona in the United States (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows from sea level to 1,200 m asl (Benson 1982, Paredes et al. 2000).  This species has been introduced to other parts of the world where it has escaped and has become invasive, such as South Africa and Australia.
iucn_doc_habitats: In Arizona, this species occurs in the Sonoran desert scrubland in the desert grasslands. This species is a common plant in the Arizona high plains. In the Sonoran desert, it grows in wide open areas and in the thorn scrub of the foothills (Paredes et al. 2000). The species grows in coastal scrub, creosote scrub, and desert grasslands and is found on sandy flats and rocky slopes.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia fulgida is a widespread and extremely common species with no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia imbricata (Haw.) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: United States; Arizona; Veracruz; Nuevo León; México Distrito Federal; Durango; Puebla; Texas; Hidalgo; Chihuahua; Coahuila; Colorado; Michoacán; San Luis Potosí; Zacatecas; Jalisco; Querétaro; Mexico; Tlaxcala; Oaxaca; New Mexico; Tamaulipas; Sonora; Guanajuato; México State; Aguascalientes; Morelos
iucn_location_codes: US; ARI-OO; MXG-VC; MXE-NL; MXC-DF; MXE-DU; MXC-PU; TEX-OO; MXE-HI; MXE-CU; MXE-CO; COL-OO; MXS-MI; MXE-SL; MXE-ZA; MXS-JA; MXE-QU; MX; MXC-TL; MXS-OA; NWM-OO; MXE-TA; MXN-SO; MXE-GU; MXC-ME; MXE-AG; MXC-MO
iucn_doc_range: Cylindropuntia imbricata is a wide ranging cactus, occurring in the Mexican states of Aguascalientes, Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, México State, Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, Michoacán, Morelos, Nuevo León, Oaxaca, Puebla, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Sonora, Tamaulipas, Tlaxcala, Veracruz, Zacatecas, and México Distrito Federal. In the United States, the species is found in Arizona, Colorado, New Mexico, and Texas (Hernández et al. 2004). The species is widely distributed and common in the Chihuahua Desert Region (Martínez-Avalos and Jurado 2005, Goettsch and Hernández 2006). It is found at elevations from 1,600 to 2,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in xerophyllous scrub (Goettsch and Hernández 2006). It can tolerate habitat modification.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia imbricata is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread, common, has no major threats, and is found in multiple protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia kleiniae (DC.) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Puebla; Mexico; Nuevo León; Texas; San Luis Potosí; Hidalgo; Oklahoma; Arizona; Zacatecas; Aguascalientes; United States; Jalisco; Querétaro; Coahuila; New Mexico; Tamaulipas; Durango; Veracruz
iucn_location_codes: MXC-PU; MX; MXE-NL; TEX-OO; MXE-SL; MXE-HI; OKL-OO; ARI-OO; MXE-ZA; MXE-AG; US; MXS-JA; MXE-QU; MXE-CO; NWM-OO; MXE-TA; MXE-DU; MXG-VC
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Desert - Temperate; Desert - Hot
iucn_doc_range: This wide-ranging species occurs across most of northern Mexico (states of Aguascalientes, Coahuila, Durango, Hidalgo, Jalisco, Nuevo León, Puebla, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas,Veracruz and Zacatecas) and in the southwestern United States (Texas, Arizona, Oklahoma and New Mexico) (Hernández et al. 2004, Goettsch and Hernández 2006). There are records of 145 locations with an Extent of Occurrence (EOO) of 132,000 km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in xerophyllous scrub, desert, and semi desert (Goettsch and Hernández 2006). The reproductive potential of this species is enormous, being able to reproduce sexually and also asexually by detachable stem fragments.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia kleiniae is listed as Least Concern due to its wide range and abundance.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia leptocaulis (DC.) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Coahuila; Tamaulipas; Texas; Veracruz; New Mexico; Jalisco; Arizona; Durango; Sonora; México State; San Luis Potosí; Guanajuato; Querétaro; Zacatecas; Colorado; Baja California Sur; Mexico; Puebla; Chihuahua; United States; Nuevo León; Aguascalientes; Hidalgo
iucn_location_codes: MXE-CO; MXE-TA; TEX-OO; MXG-VC; NWM-OO; MXS-JA; ARI-OO; MXE-DU; MXN-SO; MXC-ME; MXE-SL; MXE-GU; MXE-QU; MXE-ZA; COL-OO; MXN-BS; MX; MXC-PU; MXE-CU; US; MXE-NL; MXE-AG; MXE-HI
iucn_doc_range: The species is widely distributed, occurring in the Mexican states of Aguascalientes, Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, México State, Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, Nuevo León, Puebla, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Sonora, Tamaulipas, Veracruz, and Zacatecas (Hernández et al. 2004). It is widely distributed in the Chihuahuan Desert Region (Martínez-Ávalos and Jurado 2005, Goettsch and Hernández 2006). In the United States it occurs in Arizona, Colorado, New Mexico, and Texas (Hernández et al. 2004). The population in the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán region in Puebla is disjunct from the main population. Records from Baja California Sur require confirmation (H. Hernández pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits xerophyllous scrub (Goettsch and Hernández 2006). It can survive in human modified habitats. The reproductive potential of this species is enormous, being able to reproduce sexually and also asexually by detachable stem fragments.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia leptocaulis is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread, common, and found in numerous protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia lindsayi (Rebman) Rebman sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Baja California Sur; Baja California; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Mexico from the states of Baja California and Baja California Sur (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at elevations from 50 to 625 m asl. The species occurs mostly in the south (except in the Cape region) and just extends into southern Baja California.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in sandy flats and sandy arroyos in desert scrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Cylindropuntia lindsayi has a scattered distribution, it is fairly widespread and locally common. There are no threats to the species; hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia molesta (K.Brandegee) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Baja California Sur; Baja California; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Mexico from the states of Baja California and Baja California Sur (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on sandy and gravelly alluvium in valleys and slopes of hills in dry scrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia molesta is widespread, reasonably common, and has no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia munzii (C.B.Wolf) Backeb. sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: California; Baja California; United States; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in Mexico, in northern Baja California extending northwards just into the US state of California (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at elevations from 0 to 465 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in sandy flats, alluvial washes and gravelly hills in desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Cylindropuntia munzii has a relatively restricted range (extent of occurrence less than 20,000 km²), the species is common and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia prolifera (Engelm.) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: California; United States; Mexico; Baja California
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Commercial & industrial areas; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: The species is reported from the Mexican state of Baja California and the US state of California (Hunt et al. 2006). It is also recorded from the Channel islands and on Guadelupe Island (Mexico). The species occurs at elevations from 0 to 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in coastal sage shrubland often on dunes.
iucn_doc_threats: In the US part of the range, the species is being impacted by urban expansion; however, in the Mexican part of its range, it is secure.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia prolifera has a very wide range and is common in the Mexican parts of its range. There are declines in the US part of the range, but this is not sufficient to justify a threatened listing. This species is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia ramosissima (Engelm.) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Mexico; Sonora; Arizona; California; Baja California; Nevada; United States
iucn_location_codes: MX; MXN-SO; ARI-OO; CAL-OO; MXN-BC; NEV-OO; US
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Food - animal; Medicine - human & veterinary; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in the Mexican states of Baja California and Sonora and the US states of Arizona, California, and Nevada (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations from 50 to 1,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Cylindropuntia ramosissima occurs from southern Nevada to northwest Baja California. In Sonora, the species only grows in sandy soils in northern El Pinacate, near the international border. The species is found on sandy soils, along streams and plains in the Sonoran Desert, and flowers in May (Paredes et al. 2000).  The species grows in the Mohave and Sonoran deserts in washes, flats, sandy loam, desert pavements and stony volcanic substrates.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia ramosissima is a widespread and reasonably common species with no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia sanfelipensis (Rebman) Rebman sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in the state of Baja California (Hunt et al. 2006) in the area of San Felipe at elevations from 350 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on sandy or rocky desert flats in scrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia sanfelipensis has a restricted range (extent of occurrence less than 20,000 km²). However, there are no threats and the species is abundant. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia santamaria (E.M.Baxter) Rebman sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of  Baja California Sur (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations from 0 to 200 m asl.  It is said to be restricted to Magdalena Island.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in coastal desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats, but collectors may pose a potential threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very restricted range (well under 20 km² for extent of occurrence and area of occupancy) and is known from a single location. It is not known if there are any threats, declines in the population, or declines in the extent or quality of the habitat. Collection by specialist collectors may pose a potential threat, hence the species is listed as Vulnerable under criterion D2. However, if there are current threats causing a continuous decline then the species could qualify for listing as Critically Endangered under criterion B in the future.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia tesajo (Engelm. ex J.M.Coult.) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_synonyms: Opuntia tesajo Engelm. ex J.M. Coult.
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico from the state of Baja California (Hunt et al. 2006), where it grows at elevations from 20 to 800 m asl. The species does not occur in Baja California Sur as reported by Hunt et al. (2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on gravelly flats, sand, and on alluvial fans in dry scrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia tesajo has a fairly wide range, is reasonably abundant, and there are no threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia tunicata (Lehm.) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Zacatecas; Aguascalientes; Coahuila; Tamaulipas; San Luis Potosí; Chihuahua; Ecuador; Jalisco; Puebla; Mexico; United States; Hidalgo; Chile; Cuba; Texas; Guanajuato; Nuevo León; Colombia; Ecuador (mainland); Peru
iucn_location_codes: MXE-ZA; MXE-AG; MXE-CO; MXE-TA; MXE-SL; MXE-CU; EC; MXS-JA; MXC-PU; MX; US; MXE-HI; CL; CU; TEX-OO; MXE-GU; MXE-NL; CO; ECU-OO; PE
iucn_doc_range: This species is spread widely by attaching to livestock fur and thus the original range is difficult to define. Its native range is likely central and northern Mexico. Currently it is widely distributed in Mexico, the United States, and Colombia. The species is also reported from the literature from Cuba, Ecuador, Peru and Chile (Hernández et al. 2004). It is reported for the Mexican states of Aguascalientes, Chihuahua (R. Corral pers. comm. 2009), Coahuila, Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, Nuevo León, Puebla, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas, and Zacatecas. In the Unites States it occurs in Texas (K. Heil pers. comm. 2009). It grows at elevations of 100 to 1,900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in xerophyllous shrublands.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species. It is invasive and has spread extensively outside its native range.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia tunicata is widely distributed, abundant, occurs in numerous protected areas, and is not declining at a rate sufficient to qualify for a threat category. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia whipplei (Engelm. & J.M.Bigelow) F.M.Knuth sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_locations: Arizona; Colorado; New Mexico; Utah; United States
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Temperate; Forest - Temperate; Grassland - Temperate; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the United States, occurring widely in the Great Basin in the states of Arizona, Colorado, New Mexico and Utah (Hunt et al. 2006). The species occurs at elevations ranging from 900 to 2,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is a low to upright tree that grows in temperate grassland and temperate scrub. The species also occurs in oak, pinyon or pine forest, and juniper woodlands. The species flowers in spring to early summer (May to July).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species - the more it is grazed the better it grows. The species can become a noxious weed in over-grazed grassland.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia whipplei is a very widespread and abundant species that can become a noxious weed in places. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia wolfii (L.D.Benson) M.A.Baker sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_synonyms: Opuntia echinocarpa (Engelm. & J.M.Bigelow) F.M.Knuth var. wolfii L.D.Benson
iucn_locations: California; Baja California; United States; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: This species has a fairly restricted range from southern California (United States) and across the border into Baja California (Mexico).  It occurs at elevations from 300 to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on sandy soils derived from decomposed granite in desert scrubland. Associated species include Foquieria splendens, Larrea tridentata, Ambrosia dumosa, and Juniperus californicus.
iucn_doc_threats: The species occurs on either side of a highway that may have destroyed some plants, but the extant roadside populations are currently not threatened. Future road widening activities could impact some plants bu this is not considered a significant threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Cylindropuntia wolfii has a restricted range (extent of occurrence is less than 5,000 km²) and there may have been some past disturbance in places, the species is still locally abundant and there are no current threats. Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

Deamia montalvoae Cerén, J.Menjívar & S.Arias sec. Cerén & al. 2018

Denmoza rhodacantha (Salm-Dyck) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Mendoza; Salta; San Juan; Catamarca; La Rioja; Tucumán; Argentina
iucn_location_codes: AGW-ME; AGW-SA; AGW-SJ; AGW-CA; AGW-LR; AGW-TU; AR
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic from Argentina, where it occurs in Mendoza, San Juan, La Rioja, Tucumán, Catamarca and Salta, at elevations between 800 and 2,800 m asl (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in slopes on monte habitat, in gravel soils.
iucn_doc_threats: <span id="result_box" class="short_text">The major threats to this species are mining activities and fires.
iucn_doc_rationale: Denmoza rhodacantha is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is locally abundant, is present in several protected areas, and is not utilised.

checklist_taxon: Discocactus placentiformis (Lehm.) K.Schum. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming; Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Intentional use (species is the target); Agro-industry farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Medicine - human & veterinary; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Brazil, where it is found east of the Rio São Francisco in central and northern Minas Gerais. It grows at altitudes between 550 and 1,275 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a widespread southeastern campo rupestre/cerrado element. It grows on arenitic rocks, quartz sand and gravel in cerrado/campo rupestre, and rarely within the southern limits of the caatinga.
iucn_doc_threats: When located close to villages, this species is affected by exploitation for the production of homemade candies and subject to horticultural collecting. Habitat loss due to conversion of its habitat into pasture land for cattle ranching, crops or Eucalyptus plantation also affects the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Discocactus placentiformis is widespread in central and northern Minas Gerais, Brazil. In parts of its range it is locally very abundant, with several thousand mature individuals, while in other areas the subpopulations are smaller and have suffered from exploitation for making candy as well as habitat loss due to agricultural development. The populations are still declining, but not fast enough to qualify for any of the threatened categories in the near future. The species is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Disocactus ackermannii (Haw.) Ralf Bauer sec. Cruz & al. 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican States of Chiapas, Oaxaca, Puebla and Veracruz, where it occurs at elevations of 1,800 to 2,500 m asl (Guzmán et al. 2003, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is a strict epiphyte and it occurs in cloud forests, where it prefers open spaces.
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat degradation is the major threat for the species, the cloud forests in Mexico are one of the most endangered habitats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Disocactus ackermannii is widespread and abundant, and even though some subpopulations are affected by habitat loss, in general they seem stable. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Disocactus anguliger (Lem.) M.A.Cruz & S.Arias sec. Cruz & al. 2016
Disocactus aurantiacus (Kimnach) Barthlott sec. Cruz & al. 2016
Disocactus biformis (Lindl.) Lindl. sec. Cruz & al. 2016
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_growth_form_names: Epiphyte; Succulent - form unknown; Succulent - shrub
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Guatemala (Quezaltenango, Sacatepéquez, Solalá, Chinaltenango; M. Véliz pers. comm. 2009) and El Salvador in Santa Ana, at elevations of 1,000 to 1,840 m asl (Bauer 2003; Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in medium evergreen moist forest (M. Véliz pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by habitat degradation for coffee plantations and pastures (M. Véliz pers. comm.  2009).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a small extent of occurrence (EOO is less than 5,000km<sup>2</sup>) and its population is severely fragmented. Even though the species is tolerated by humans, there is a continuing decline in the population due to the degradation of habitat by the expansion of the agriculture frontier for coffee plantations and pastures. Hence this species is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Disocactus crenatus (Lindl.) M.A.Cruz & S.Arias sec. Cruz & al. 2016
Disocactus eichlamii (Weing.) Britton & Rose sec. Cruz & al. 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Guatemala - from Escuintla, Chimaltenango, Quetzaltenango and Santa Rosa, and is found at elevations of 1,200 to 1,600 m asl (Bauer 2003, Hunt et al. 2006, Véliz 2008).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in cloud forest and is frequently found on cliffs that are difficult to access for humans.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are habitat decline from land use change for agriculture (maize crops), and wood extraction for subsistence of the trees the species grow on.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very small extent of occurrence (EOO is less than 5,000 km<sup>2</sup>), its habitat is severely fragmented, and extraction of the trees it grows on is resulting in a continuing decline in the population. Hence this species is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: ?Disocactus kimnachii G.D.Rowley sec. Bauer 2003
Disocactus lepidocarpus (F.A.C.Weber) M.A.Cruz & S.Arias sec. Cruz & al. 2016
Disocactus macdougallii (Alexander) Barthlott sec. Cruz & al. 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Temperate; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican State of Chiapas between 1,800 and 2,500 m asl (Hunt et al. 2006). According to Hunt et al. (2006) it has a very restricted distribution and is only known from the type locality. Hernández and Godínez (1994) state the species is known from two to five localities. According to S. Arias (pers. comm. 2009) it is known from three localities.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in cloud forest and pine-oak forest (S. Arias pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by habitat loss from subsistence maize agriculture within its very limited range.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has an extent of occurrence of less than 5,000 km<sup>2</sup>, it is known from three locations, and is experiencing continuing habitat loss.

checklist_taxon: Disocactus macranthus (Alexander) Kimnach & Hutchison sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Temperate; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Mexico in the States of Chiapas, Oaxaca and Veracruz (Hunt et al. 2006) and in Guatemala, however this has to be verified (M. Véliz pers. comm. 2009). This cactus is found at elevations ranging from 800 to 1,900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is distributed in tropical montane forest and oak forest.
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat degradation for coffee plantations and pastures, and also natural fragmentation of the cloud forest  are the major threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Disocactus macranthus has a wide range and is relatively abundant. Only in part of its distribution the species is affected by natural and human caused habitat fragmentation. Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Disocactus nelsonii (Britton & Rose) Linding. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_locations: Guerrero; Guatemala; Chiapas; Mexico; Honduras; Oaxaca
iucn_location_codes: MXS-GR; GT; MXT-CI; MX; HN; MXS-OA
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is distributed in Honduras, Guatemala and in the States of Chiapas and Guerrero in Mexico (Guzmán et al. 2003, Hunt et al. 2006). It may also occur in the Mexican State of Oaxaca but this has not yet been confirmed (S. Arias pers. comm. 2009). The species is found at elevations of 1,000 to 2,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs at high elevations in Chiapas (Anderson 2001). It grows in moist montane forest.
iucn_doc_threats: Deforestation, especially logging, is the main threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Disocactus nelsonii has a wide range, is reasonably common, and there do not appear to be any major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Disocactus phyllanthoides (DC.) Barthlott sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_synonyms: Nopalxochia phyllanthoides (DC.) Britton & Rose; Cactus phyllanthoides DC.
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, and is distributed in the states of Puebla and Veracruz (Guzmán et al. 2003). It was recently reported from the state of Hidalgo by Ponce-Vargas et al. (2006). The species is found at elevations of 1,500 - 1,850 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is an epiphyte (and sometimes grows on rocks). The species occurs in cloud forest.
iucn_doc_threats: This epiphytic species is threatened by the loss of forest habitat, which is being converted to coffee plantations, small scale agriculture and cattle ranching. In addition, around villages and towns the species is declining due to over-collecting.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered Vulnerable due to past and ongoing declines observed over the past three generations (30 years) as a result of deforestation for conversion to coffee plantations, small scale farming and cattle ranching. This epiphytic species is entirely dependent on forest and loss of forest results in direct loss of subpopulations and mature individuals.

checklist_taxon: Disocactus quezaltecus (Standl. & Steyerm.) Kimnach sec. Cruz & al. 2016
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: The species was supposedly endemic to Guatemala in Quetzaltenango (Bauer 2003, Hunt et al. 2006), however, it is now known to occur in southern Mexico in Chiapas State at Tacana, near the border with Guatemala (S. Arias pers. comm. 2009). It grows at elevations of 1,800 to 2,150 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in cloud forest (Bauer 2003).
iucn_doc_threats: One of the major threats for the species is wood extraction for subsistence from the trees on which the plant grows (M. Véliz pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_rationale: Disocactus quezaltecus has a restricted range, but it is relatively common and although there are threats in places these are not sufficient to warrant any concern. Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocactus horizonthalonius Lem. sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_common_name: Devilshead; Silverbell Cactus; Turk's Head Cactus; Biznaga-tonelmancamula; Turk's-head Cactus
iucn_locations: Tamaulipas; United States; Zacatecas; Arizona; Nuevo León; Querétaro; Mexico; Guanajuato; Aguascalientes; Coahuila; Texas; Chihuahua; Sonora; Durango; New Mexico; San Luis Potosí
iucn_location_codes: MXE-TA; US; MXE-ZA; ARI-OO; MXE-NL; MXE-QU; MX; MXE-GU; MXE-AG; MXE-CO; TEX-OO; MXE-CU; MXN-SO; MXE-DU; NWM-OO; MXE-SL
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is widely distributed in the Mexican states of Aguascalientes, Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, Guanajuato, Nuevo León, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Sonora, Tamaulipas and Zacatecas, and in the United States in the states of Arizona, New Mexico, and Texas (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in semidesert, in xerophyllous scrub, on several types of substrates. It occurs on limestone soils of rocky hills in the desert.
iucn_doc_threats: Land use change for urbanization and agriculture is a threat to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocactus horizonthalonius is wide-ranging and abundant. Even though there is illegal collection, it is not a threat. Hence the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocactus platyacanthus Link & Otto sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_locations: Querétaro; Nuevo León; Oaxaca; Puebla; Coahuila; Guanajuato; Hidalgo; San Luis Potosí; Zacatecas; Tamaulipas; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-QU; MXE-NL; MXS-OA; MXC-PU; MXE-CO; MXE-GU; MXE-HI; MXE-SL; MXE-ZA; MXE-TA; MX
iucn_threat_names: Nomadic grazing; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Food - animal; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, is widely distributed in the southern half of the Chihuahuan Desert within Coahuila, Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Nuevo León, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas and Zacatecas. It is also distributed in the southern Tehuacán Valley in Puebla and Oaxaca, where it is relatively common (Gómez-Hinostrosa and Hernández 2000; Hernández et al. 2004). The north and southern subpopulations are geographically isolated by the Transverse Neovolcanic Axis (Trujillo 1984). It is known from 240 localities (Hernández et al. 2010). It is found from 1,100 to 2,400 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in xerophyllous scrub and prefers calcareous soils (Arias et al. 1997). It does not tolerate soil disturbance from livestock.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by the collection for use in traditional candy known as acitrón or biznaga. Collectors cut tissue from the inside stem. It is mainly used as live forage for goats and donkeys that feed on both the reproductive structures (flowers and fruits) and on the stems without spines. Livestock also disturb the fragile soil causing a loss of suitable microhabitat for the establishment of new individuals (Jiménez-Sierra et al. 2007, Jiménez-Sierra and Eguiarte 2010).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Near Threatened because there have not been documented declines of more than 30 percent over 100 years. However, this slow-growing species is currently used at unsustainable levels, and there are few young individuals in the population. This is a string need for regulation on the use of this species for candy and livestock forage to ensure sustainable use levels. Further research is needed to understand the life history, demography, and impact of human use. Based upon current trends, this species will become threatened in near future without increased protections and enforcement.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus acifer (Otto ex Salm-Dyck) Jacobi sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_locations: Aguascalientes; Zacatecas; Jalisco; Durango; San Luis Potosí; Guanajuato; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-AG; MXE-ZA; MXS-JA; MXE-DU; MXE-SL; MXE-GU; MX
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs on the eastern side of the Sierra Madre in Mexico, where it can be found in the states of Aguascalientes, Durango, Guanajuato, Jalisco, San Luis Potosí, and Zacatecas (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at elevations of 2,100 to 2,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on rocky wooded mountains - it probably grows on rocks in moss in pine-oak forests.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is occasionally collected for use in making nativity scenes.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although the range of Echinocereus acifer may be limited (extent of occurrence possibly less than 10,000 km²), this species is known from a number of localities and is not severely fragmented. There are no major threats apart from occasional collecting for cultural use. Hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus adustus Engelm. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Handicrafts, jewellery, etc.; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This cactus occurs on the eastern slopes of the Sierra Madre Occidental in the Mexican states of Chihuahua and Durango (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 1,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in open pine-oak forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The species might be collected for use in making nativity scenes.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus adustus has a very wide range, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern. The species' range should be investigated as it may have quite a small area of occupancy, and the local threats may have more impact than currently thought.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus arizonicus Rose ex Orcutt sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_locations: Sonora; New Mexico; Arizona; United States; Mexico; Chihuahua
iucn_location_codes: MXN-SO; NWM-OO; ARI-OO; US; MX; MXE-CU
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: The species is distributed in the Mexican states of Chihuahua and Sonora, and in the United States in Arizona and New Mexico, at elevations of 1,050 to 2,500 m asl (Blum et al. 1998, Paredes et al. 2000) .
iucn_doc_habitats: E. arizonicus occurs in chaparral and oak forest (Paredes et al. 2000). The species grows in wooded hills, loamy sandy soil and open shrub desert (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat for this species is mining.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide range with an extent of occurrence of over 20,000 km<sup>2</sup> , it is often abundant and mining affects a small portion of its range. Hence the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus barthelowanus Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; War, civil unrest & military exercises; Tourism & recreation areas; Mining & quarrying; Nomadic grazing
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Mexico within Baja California Sur. It has been described as a rare microendemism on Magdalena and Margarita Islands (Riemann and Ezcurra 2005). It occurs at elevations of up to 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species can be found on low hills and sandy washes (Shreve and Wiggins 1964). Blum (1998) reported it in granite mountains in gravel soil. The species occurs in xerophyllous scrub, but these islands receive fog most of the year.
iucn_doc_threats:
The area where Echinocereus barthelowanus grows was subject to mining activities. On Margarita Island feral goats trample the plants and the soil which becomes eroded and prevents new plants from establishing. Also, in Margarita Island the marines are extending their quarters, hence the threat is urban expansion and the extraction of material for construction. There are plans to build a tourist development on Margarita Island.
In the past, during President Roosevelt's government, Margarita was used as a military base, where military training took place, and this probably affected the populations.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very small range, with an extent of occurrence of around 120 km<sup>2</sup> and only two known locations. This species faces many threats, is not abundant and is presumably declining. Hence is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus berlandieri (Engelm.) Haage sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_common_name: Órgano-pequeño de Berlandier; Berlandier's Hedgehog Cactus
iucn_locations: Coahuila; Mexico; Tamaulipas; Nuevo León; United States; Texas
iucn_location_codes: MXE-CO; MX; MXE-TA; MXE-NL; US; TEX-OO
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: The species is wide-ranging, distributed in northeastern Mexico in the state of Tamaulipas. It has been reported from the literature to occur in Coahuila and Nuevo León, and in Texas in the United States (Taylor 1985, Hernández et al. 2004, Powell et al. 2008). It grows in the Rio Grande Plain. In southern Texas it can be found from San Antonio, Bexar County, to the Rio Grande in Webb, Starr, and Cameron Counties, Hidalgo County, to northeastern Mexico. It has an approximate range extent of  less than 100 to 250 km2 (less than 40 to 100 square miles). It grows from sea level to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in Tamaulipan thorn scrub in mezquite thickets and grassland (Taylor 1985) and in deep fine soils of plains, washes, and low hills, in mesquite thickets, grassland. It grows on light sandy loam or red gravel. Blum et al. (1998) reported it in shrub desert on sandy - loamy soil.
iucn_doc_threats: Land use change for farming and urbanization is the main threat to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though the range of Echinocereus berlandieri is not large, it is abundant and has no direct threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus bonkerae Thornber & Bonker sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to United States of America, occurring in the state of Arizona (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in stony grassland in mineral, sandy-loamy soil at elevations of 1,300 - 2,000 m (Blum et al. 1998). It also occurs in rocky slopes in shrublands.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species. There is some cattle grazing that may damage this species through trampling. There are mining activities that may be affecting this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern because it is common and abundant across its range and there are no major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus brandegeei (J.M.Coult.) K.Schum. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_doc_range: The species is found in Mexico within Baja California Sur (van der Valk 2009, Anderson 2001) as well as a number of islands  in the Gulf of California (Anderson 2001).
iucn_doc_habitats: E. brandegeei  inhabits the driest region of Mexico and often grows in fully exposed situations in poor, rocky soil (Taylor 1985). The species occurs in shrub desert, stony hills, in mineral loamy, also former sea bed (shell shingle) loamy at 0 - 300 m (Blum et al. 1998)
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is agro-industrial cattle ranching. The constant trampling of cattle causes the soil to loosen up and then it gets washed away during the raining season. Very often the plants get washed away with the soil.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is distributed in a large area, it is common and occurs in protected areas. The only severe threat occurs in small parts of the species range. Hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus bristolii W.T.Marshall sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in the Mexican state of Sonora (Hunt et al. 2006), where it grows at elevations of 250 to 1,350 m. Taylor (1985) reports the species to also occur in adjacent south-eastern Arizona, in Cochise County.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in thornscrub in foothill (Paredes et al. 2000). Taylor (1985) found it in Northeast Sonora and border region of adjacent Southeast Arizona in the sonoran Desert and desert grassland.
iucn_doc_threats: Land use change is a threat affecting this species, however it is not a major. In the species range in the United States the main driver of land use change affecting subpopulations is urbanization. In the Mexican part of the species range is agro-industrial ranching and activities related to this, such as deforestation. However, the vast part of the subpopulations grow on poor, rocky soils not suitable for agriculture or ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered to be Least Concern in light of its presumed large population size and distribution and lack of major threats, however further research is necessary to ensure that this species is not in fact declining at a rate to qualify for a threat listing. The species is poorly known taxonomically and ecologically, there are no   recent collections and therefore there is no information on the current population trends to assess it. The area where it occurs has been   inaccessible for a long time.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus cinerascens (DC.) Lem. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_locations: México Distrito Federal; Tamaulipas; Zacatecas; México State; San Luis Potosí; Querétaro; Guanajuato; Hidalgo; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXC-DF; MXE-TA; MXE-ZA; MXC-ME; MXE-SL; MXE-QU; MXE-GU; MXE-HI; MX
iucn_doc_range: This wide-ranging species occurs in the central Mexican states of México, México Federal District, Hidalgo, Querétaro, eastern Guanajuato, western San Luis Potosí, Zacatecas, and Tamaulipas. It grows at elevations of around 1,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in thorny Prosopis scrub (mesquite) and xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: There are impacts to the habitat, but these are unlikely to affect the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus cinerascens is a very widespread and extremely common species with no major threats, hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus coccineus Engelm. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_common_name: Órgano-pequeño de Espinas Escarlatas; Scarlet Hedgehog Cactus
iucn_locations: United States; New Mexico; Chihuahua; Sonora; Mexico; Texas; Arizona; Colorado; Coahuila; Baja California
iucn_location_codes: US; NWM-OO; MXE-CU; MXN-SO; MX; TEX-OO; ARI-OO; COL-OO; MXE-CO; MXN-BC
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in the southwestern United States in Arizona. It has been reported from the literature to occur in the Mexican states of Baja California (Powell and Weedin 2004), Chihuahua, Coahuila and Sonora, and in the United States in Colorado, New Mexico, and Texas (Hernández et al. 2004). It grows at elevations ranging from 1,500 to 2,800 m asl (Scobell and Scott 2002).
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species occurs in habitats ranging from desert chaparral to coniferous forests (Scobell and Scott 2002). It grows in flat meadows, forest clearings, shrub desert, in stony, wooded slopes in loamy, humus, mineral soil and in granite with humus (Blum et al. 1998).
It is pollinated by hummingbirds and halictid bees, and it has both hermaphroditic and dimorphic populations (Scobell and Scott 2002, Scobell and Schultz 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus coccineus has a very large extent of occurrence, is abundant, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus dasyacanthus Engelm. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_common_name: Órgano-pequeño Arcoiris de Texas; Spiny Hedgehog Cactus
iucn_locations: Mexico; Texas; United States; Chihuahua; New Mexico; Arizona; Coahuila
iucn_location_codes: MX; TEX-OO; US; MXE-CU; NWM-OO; ARI-OO; MXE-CO
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Temperate; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Chihuahua and Coahuila in Mexico, and in Arizona, Texas, and New Mexico in the United States (Benson 1982, Powell et al. 2008). It grows at altitudes between 1,200 and 1,500 m asl (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in xerophyllous scrub, grasslands (Taylor 1985), and on rocks. It occurs in sandy valleys to stony hills in limestone (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is collected for trade, but this is not a threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus dasyacanthus has a very wide range, is abundant, and although it is collected this is not sufficient to warrant any concern. Hence, this species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus engelmannii (Parry ex Engelm.) Lem. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_common_name: Strawberry Cactus; Órgano-pequeño Fresa; Engelmann's Hedgehog Cactus
iucn_locations: Baja California; California; United States; Nevada; Arizona; Mexico; Sonora
iucn_location_codes: MXN-BC; CAL-OO; US; NEV-OO; ARI-OO; MX; MXN-SO
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: The species is reported in Mexico from the states of  Baja California and Sonora and in the United States from Arizona, California and Nevada (Hunt et al. 2006). This species occurs in the Mojavean, Arizona and Colorado Deserts at elevations from near sea level to 2,400 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in Sonoran desert scrub. E. engelmannii occurs on rocky soil, slopes, mountain ranges and also on deep  lands with fine sand on the plain desert valleys (Paredes et al. 2000). It occurs in Sonoran and Mojavean Desert, montane forest, chaparral, great plain grassland and juniper-pinyon woodland; near sea level to 2,400 m altitude. It grows in gravelly, sandy, or rocky soils of hillsides, washes, and canyons in the desert, plains, pine woods, chaparral, grass, and Great Basin shrub.
iucn_doc_threats: Land use change is a threat affecting this species, however it is not a major. In the species range in the United States the main driver of land use change affecting subpopulations is urbanization. In the Mexican part of the species range is agro-industrial ranching and activities related to this, such as deforestation. However, the vast part of the subpopulations grow on poor, rocky soils not suitable for agriculture or ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is wide spread and abundant and does not have any major threats. Hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus enneacanthus Engelm. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_common_name: Smallspine Pitaya; Órgano-pequeño Alicoche Real; Pitaya
iucn_locations: Nuevo León; Tamaulipas; Zacatecas; Chihuahua; Mexico; Coahuila; San Luis Potosí; New Mexico; United States; Durango; Texas
iucn_location_codes: MXE-NL; MXE-TA; MXE-ZA; MXE-CU; MX; MXE-CO; MXE-SL; NWM-OO; US; MXE-DU; TEX-OO
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This wide-spread species occurs in central, north and north-east of Mexico, and adjacent Texas and New Mexico in the United States (Taylor 1985). It has been reported from the Mexican states of Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas and Zacatecas, and in New Mexico (two locations) and Texas in the United States (Hernández et al. 2004). It grows at elevations of 0 to 1,700 m asl (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species grows in desert scrub and grasslands, on limestone soils, sometimes clay loam soils of rocky or gravelly hills, washes or plains in desert, brush/grassland.
The species is found in shrub desert  in mineral soil and sandy- loamy soil (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats affecting this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus enneacanthus has a very wide range, is abundant, and is not affected by any major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus fendleri (Engelm.) Sencke ex J.N.Haage sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_locations: Chihuahua; Coahuila; United States; New Mexico; Sonora; Mexico; Colorado; Arizona; Texas
iucn_location_codes: MXE-CU; MXE-CO; US; NWM-OO; MXN-SO; MX; COL-OO; ARI-OO; TEX-OO
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Temperate; Forest - Temperate; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Nomadic grazing; Utility & service lines; Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Roads & railroads
iucn_doc_range: This wide-spread species occurs in the Mexican states of Chihuahua, Coahuila, and Sonora, and in the United States in Arizona, Colorado, New Mexico, and Texas, at elevations of 30 to 2,400 m asl (Taylor 1985, Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species occurs in semidesert scrub, grasslands and pine-juniper woodland (Taylor 1985), on sandy, gravelly soils of grasslands and woodlands, and on edges of deserts.
It can be found in grasslands, stony hills, grassy hills and shrub desert in stony - loamy soil and sandy soil (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: In parts of its range the species is threatened by fires (Sivinski 2007), road construction (US Fish and Wildlife Service 2003) and transmission lines (Harris Environmental Group, Inc. 2004). More recently the construction of windmill farms has been affecting it. Livestock trampling affects some subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus fendleri has a very wide range, is abundant, and although there are threats in places these are not sufficient to warrant any concern. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern. The increase in windmill farm construction is becoming a major threat and should be considered in future evaluations.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus grandis Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to the Islands of San Esteban in Sonora, and Las Animas ("San Lorenzo norte") and San Lorenzo in Baja California, Mexico.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to the species at present, but there is a potential threat from an alien invasive species - Buffel Grass (Pennisetum ciliare).
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus grandis has a very restricted range, and at present there are no major threats. However, there is a potential threat from an invasive grass species (buffel grass) and if this happened the island would be considered a single location. However, the species occurs in three islands which spreads the risk of extinction, hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus knippelianus Liebner sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in the states of Coahuila and Nuevo León at elevations around 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs throughout much of the mountainous grassland and pine forests of the states of Coahuila and Nuevo León, on limestone soil.
iucn_doc_threats: There is some illegal collection of this species, but it is not a major threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus knippelianus is listed as Least Concern due to its extensive range, large number of individuals, and lack of major threats.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus koehresianus (G.Frank) W.Rischer sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
Echinocereus laui G.Frank sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Sonora (Hunt et al. 2006), it is found at elevations of 1,480 to 1,500 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in moderate to steep volcanic slopes in grasslands and oak woodland.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats known for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern because even though it has a very restricted range, it is locally abundant and there are no threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus leucanthus N.P.Taylor sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry farming; Commercial & industrial areas; Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in México in the states of Sonora and Sinaloa, on the coastal plain near the shore from the vicinity of Guaymas southward to Los Mochis, Sinaloa (Taylor, 1985; Paredes et al. 2000; Felger, pers. comm.). It is known from only two localities.
iucn_doc_habitats: E. leucanthus occurs on the thornscrub of the coast where it typically grows inside Lycium californicum low scrub (Paredes et al. 2006). It occurs in fine textured sandy silty soil of low coastal desertscrub from near the shore to about 5 km inland (Felger, pers. comm.).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are coastal development and agricultural expansion.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because its extent of occurrence is less than 5,000 km<sup>2</sup>, it occurs in only two known locations, and there is a continuing decline in the area, extent and quality of its habitat.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus longisetus (Engelm.) Lem. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in the states of Coahuila and Nuevo León (Hernández et al. 2004). It grows on the eastern slopes of the Sierra Madre Oriental.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on limestone in xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: No direct threats are known, but the habitat is impacted by overgrazing from goats. This is unlikely to be a major threat as the species has high reproductive rates and seedlings establish very quickly.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus longisetus appears to have a relatively wide range (extent of occurrence is probably more than 20,000 km²), is known from many localities, and although there is little population information it may be common. Hence, although there are general threats to the habitat, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus mapimiensis E.F.Anderson, W.C.Hodgs. & P.Quirk sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Coahuila (Hernández et al. 2004). It is known from only a single location at present near Mapimí.
iucn_doc_habitats: The habitat is unknown, but it presumably grows in xerophytic shrubland on limestone.
iucn_doc_threats: The habitat may be impacted by land-use practices, especially overgrazing by goats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although poorly known, this species has a very restricted range being known from only a single locality. The species is outside of any protected areas and is hence susceptible to any land-use changes which could very quickly drive it towards extinction. Hence it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus maritimus (M.E.Jones) K.Schum. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_locations: Baja California Sur; Baja California; Mexico
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Garbage & solid waste; Agro-industry farming; Housing & urban areas; Work & other activities; Roads & railroads; Renewable energy
iucn_doc_range: Echinocereus maritimus is distributed in Mexico, on the western coast of Baja California, Baja California Sur and adjacent islands, at elevations of 0 to 50 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in littoral sand dunes and on rocks a few kilometres in land, mostly at low elevations (Taylor 1985). It grows in shrub desert and in flat hills near coast in stony-loamy soil (Blum et al. 1998) and sandy loamy terrain in coastal areas.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is urban development in the coastal area in the form of fish camps which consist of informal temporary settlements of fishermen that leave a lot of rubbish behind and they also open access paths that give way to further development and impacts such as tourism disturbance. Agriculture also affects the species, mainly tomato plantations. The production of alternative sources of energy, from tides poses a threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has an estimated extent of occurrence of 12,000 km<sup>2</sup>, it is distributed only on the coastal area along Baja California and Baja California Sur which is subject to development in many forms which has fragmented the population severely. Even though the species occurs within protected areas the threats in most of its range will not cease in the near future. Hence it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus metornii G.Frank sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Coahuila (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in the desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: Data to assess the conservation status of Echinocereus metornii is insufficient and therefore is here listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus nicholii (L.D.Benson) B.D.Parfitt sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_synonyms: Echinocereus engelmannii (Parry ex Engelm.) Lem. var. nicholii L.D.Benson
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: The species has been reported in Mexico from the state of Sonora, and in the United States from the state of Arizona (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at elevations from 300 to 900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in Sonoran desert scrub. In the Waterman Mountains, west of Tucson, it grows on limestone (Paredes et al. 2006) and granitic and basaltic slopes.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species. Mining affects, however, a small portion of its range.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus nicholii is wide-ranging and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus nivosus Glass & R.A.Foster sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, and is found only in the southeastern part of the state of Coahuila on the Sierra Madre Oriental at elevations of around 2,000 m asl. The species is known only from two collections from the same location (Taylor 1985; note Taylor treats this incorrectly as two localities).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in harsh mountain habitats in grassland with limestone rocks, exposed to sun and wind (Taylor 1985). It forms hemispherical mounds with many stems.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection is the main threat to the species, however, much of the area is inaccessible.
iucn_doc_rationale: As far as is known, this species has a very small extent of occurrence, certainly less than 100 km² and its area of occupancy may only be a few square kilometres. The species is very attractive and is highly sought after by collectors, although part of the population grows in inaccessible areas. Given its small range, the threat of collection, and that it is known from a single location, it is listed as Critically Endangered. If the population in the future is found to be more extensive and not as threatened, a lower category of threat may be warranted.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus palmeri Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, with the nominate subspecies occurring in the states of Chihuahua and Durango, whereas the subspecies mazapil has a disjunct occurrence in the state of Zacatecas (Taylor 1985, Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 2004, Hernández et al. 2004). It grows at elevations of 1,400 to 1,900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The typical species grows on grassy slopes of the Sierra Madre Oriental foothills in pine-oak forests, whereas the subspecies mazapil occurs on the plains in xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: The habitat for subspecies mazapil in the state of Zacatecas is under threat because the soils are suitable for agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale:
The species as a whole is very widespread with a large range. Although not common, Echinocereus palmeri is listed as Least Concern because there are no major threats.
The subspecies mazapil has a much more restricted range (extent of occurrence less than 5,000 km²), and it grows in very specialized habitat near the shores of lakes in silty formations derived from pleistocene dry lakes. It is under threat because of expanding agricultural activities and, hence, it qualifies for listing as Endangered under criterion B1ab(iii,v)+2ab(iii,v).

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus parkeri N.P.Taylor sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_locations: Coahuila; Nuevo León; Tamaulipas; San Luis Potosí; Zacatecas; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-CO; MXE-NL; MXE-TA; MXE-SL; MXE-ZA; MX
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Coahuila, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas, and Zacatecas in the southern Chihuahuan Desert, at elevations of 1,450 to 1,900 m asl (Hernández et al. 2004, Martínez-Ávalos and Jurado 2005).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs on the top of limestone hills in xerophytic shrubland (Goettsch and Hernández 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: Overgrazing of the habitat is the only threat and not a major one.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus parkeri is a widespread and common species with no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus pectinatus (Scheidw.) Engelm. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_locations: Durango; Zacatecas; Tamaulipas; Nuevo León; Guanajuato; Arizona; Chihuahua; Coahuila; Aguascalientes; Sonora; United States; San Luis Potosí; Mexico; Texas; New Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-DU; MXE-ZA; MXE-TA; MXE-NL; MXE-GU; ARI-OO; MXE-CU; MXE-CO; MXE-AG; MXN-SO; US; MXE-SL; MX; TEX-OO; NWM-OO
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Problematic native species/diseases; Intentional use (species is the target); Trend Unknown/Unrecorded
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This wide-ranging species is distributed in the Mexican states of Aguascalientes, Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, Guanajuato, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, Sonora, Tamaulipas and Zacatecas, and in the United States in the states of Arizona, New Mexico and Texas at elevations of 400 to 1,900 m asl (Taylor 1985, Hernández et al. 2004, Martínez-Ávalos and Jurado 2005).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in xerophyllous scrub and grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is found in Mexican trade, individuals are collected from wild populations (Bárcenas 2003). The species is eaten by the larvae of a moth, many populations are devastated by this (Goettsch pers. comm. 2009), however it is difficult to establish if this is major threat. In parts of its range the species is affected by fires (Thomas 2006).
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus pectinatus has a very wide range, is abundant, and although there are threats in places these are not sufficient to warrant any concern. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus pentalophus (DC.) Engelm. ex J.N.Haage sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_common_name: Lady-finger Cactus; Órgano-pequeño Alicoche Falso; Lady-finger Hedgehog Cactus
iucn_locations: Nuevo León; Jalisco; Texas; Mexico; Coahuila; San Luis Potosí; United States; Hidalgo; Guanajuato; Tamaulipas; Querétaro
iucn_location_codes: MXE-NL; MXS-JA; TEX-OO; MX; MXE-CO; MXE-SL; US; MXE-HI; MXE-GU; MXE-TA; MXE-QU
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Nomadic grazing
iucn_doc_range: This wide-ranging species is distributed in the Mexican states of Guanajuato, Jalisco, Nuevo León, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas, Coahuila and Hidalgo. In the United States the species is found in Texas. It occurs at elevations of 200 to 2,100 m asl (Blum et al. 1998, Hernández et al. 2004, Martínez-Ávalos and Jurado 2005). The species occurs in Rio Grande Plain. In southern Texas it grows near Rio Grande from Webb County to Cameron County, and to the north on the coastal plain. Southward it can be found to San Luis Potosí, Mexico.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in xerophyllous scrub in fine soils of flats and plains in the mesquite and grass country. It grows in shrub desert, wooded slopes and wooded hills in mineral - humus soil and sandy - loamy soil (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for the species, however, land use change affects some subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus pentalophus has a very wide range, is abundant, and although there are threats in places these are not sufficient to warrant any concern. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus polyacanthus Engelm. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Chihuahua and Durango (Hunt et al. 2006). It also occurs in the Sierra Madre Occidental in eastern Sonora, where it is found at altitudes between 1,300 and 2,000 m asl (Paredes et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_habitats: E. polyacanthus grows on rocky outcrops in oak and pine woodlands. Blum et al. (1998) reported it from rocky mountain slopes in volcanic or sedimentary rocks with humus pockets.
iucn_doc_threats: There are not major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus polyacanthus is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, locally common across its range, and there are no major threats.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus poselgeri Lem. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_common_name: Dahlia Hedgehog Cactus; Órgano-pequeño Sacasil; Dahlia Apple Cactus
iucn_locations: Nuevo León; Tamaulipas; Coahuila; United States; Texas; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-NL; MXE-TA; MXE-CO; US; TEX-OO; MX
iucn_use_trade_names: Medicine - human & veterinary; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in the Mexican states of Coahuila, Nuevo León, and Tamaulipas, and in the USA in Texas (Hernández et al. 2004), from low elevations up to 1,150 m asl (Taylor 1985).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in xerophyllous scrub. This species prefers to clamber through woody vegetation for support of its slender, drab stems (Taylor 1985). It occurs in sandy soils of valleys and hills in semidesert brushlands at low elevations.
iucn_doc_threats: Some subpopulations are threatened by the illegal collection of plants for medicinal and veterinary purposes.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus poselgeri is considered Least Concern, as although it is widely used, it is still abundant and widespread and not declining at a rate sufficient to qualify for a threatened category.

checklist_fullName: Echinocereus primolanatus Fritz Schwarz ex N.P.Taylor
checklist_taxon: Echinocereus primolanatus Fritz Schwarz ex N.P.Taylor sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in Coahuila, it was known only from the southern reaches of the Sierra de la Paila, but has more recently been reported from the Cuatro Ciénegas Region<span lang="EN-GB">. It grows from sea level to 1,500 m (Taylor 1985).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in fine silty soil probably over limestone (Taylor 1985) presumably in xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats for this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although this species appears to have a small extent of occurrence (<10,000 km²) and nothing is known about the population and trends, or about any threats to this species, it is listed as Least Concern as it does not appear to be declining at a rate sufficient to qualify for a threat category at this time. It occurs in an area that has been very poorly studied.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus pseudopectinatus (N.P.Taylor) N.P.Taylor sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_synonyms: Echinocereus bristolii W.T.Marshall var. pseudopectinatus N.P.Taylor
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Mexico in the state of Sonora and in the southern portion of the state of Arizona, in the United States (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at altitudes between 830 and 1,350 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in the thorn scrub on foothills and in grasslands of the desert (Paredes et al. 2000). It tends to grow in rocky outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: It grows in heavily grazed areas but does not seem to be affected by it (T. Van Devender pers. comm. 2011). There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus pseudopectinatus is listed as Least Concern because there are no evident threats and it is locally common in many portions of its range. It does not seem to be affected by heavy grazing.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus pulchellus (Mart.) K.Schum. sec. Sánchez & al. 2020
iucn_locations: Querétaro; Zacatecas; Jalisco; Oaxaca; Nuevo León; San Luis Potosí; Puebla; Aguascalientes; Hidalgo; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-QU; MXE-ZA; MXS-JA; MXS-OA; MXE-NL; MXE-SL; MXC-PU; MXE-AG; MXE-HI; MX
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Nomadic grazing; Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, and is reported from herbarium records from the states of Hidalgo, Oaxaca, Puebla, Jalisco, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, Zacatecas, and Aguascalientes ( T. Terrazas, M. Cházaro and  H. Arreola pers. comm. 2009). The species is reported from the literature from the state of Querétaro (Hernández et al. 2004), however, it has not been found in the area after extensive exploration (E. Sánchez pers. comm. 2010). It has a remarkably wide distribution in the cooler highlands of Mexico (Taylor 1985). It is found at elevations of 2,000 - 2,483 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The preferred habitat of the species is grassy plains above 2,000 m asl (Taylor 1985). The species flowers between April and June, and it takes 60 days for the fruit to mature (Navarro-Carvajal and Flores-Martínez 2002).
iucn_doc_threats: Its preferred habitat is also favoured by humans for agricultural purposes (Taylor 1985). Plants are killed by cattle that trample on them, however, on many occasions there is regeneration. Goats also eat parts of the plant but rarely kill it. In the specific case of E. pulchellus pulchellus some populations are threatened by habitat change (desmonte) and by the extraction of materials for construction (Navarro-Carvajal and Flores-Martínez 2002).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered to be Vulnerable due to its relatively small population size (9,500 mature individuals), and because it is known from several subpopulations, each with a maximum of 50 mature individuals. The populations are severely fragmented and are under pressure from human activities such as agriculture, cattle ranching and extraction of construction materials.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus rayonesensis N.P.Taylor sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, found in the state of Nuevo León, at elevations ca to 1,000 m (Hunt et al. 2006). It is probably confined to a single canyon.
iucn_doc_habitats: Echinocereus rayonensis grows on steep slopes, in slate, probably in the same xerophytic shrubland habitat as Aztekium.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats known for this species, the slopes are inaccessible.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species presumably has a very restricted range and probably a small population size, however there are no threats known and the area is inaccessible which confers protection to the species. Hence it is here listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus reichenbachii (Terscheck ex Walp.) J.N.Haage sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_common_name: Lace Hedgehog Cactus; Órgano-pequeño de Colores; Reichenbach's Hedgehog Cactus
iucn_locations: Texas; Colorado; Nuevo León; Tamaulipas; United States; Coahuila; Mexico; New Mexico; Oklahoma
iucn_location_codes: TEX-OO; COL-OO; MXE-NL; MXE-TA; US; MXE-CO; MX; NWM-OO; OKL-OO
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Coahuila, Nuevo León and Tamaulipas, Mexico, and in Colorado, New Mexico, Oklahoma and Texas in the United States, occurring at elevations from sea level to 1,500 m asl (Taylor 1985, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in grasslands, thorn shrublands and mesquite woodlands (Carr 2002). It occurs in shrubland/chaparral, grassland/herbaceous in gravelly, rocky, or sandy soils of various origins: Lime-stone, granite.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for the species, however, land use change and fire affect some subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus reichenbachii has a very wide range, is abundant, and although there are threats in places these are not sufficient to warrant any concern. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus rigidissimus (Engelm.) Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_common_name: Rainbow Hedgehog Cactus; Arizona Rainbow Cactus
iucn_synonyms: Cereus pectinatus (Scheidw.) Engelm. var. rigidissimus Engelm.
iucn_locations: Mexico; Chihuahua; United States; Arizona; New Mexico; Sonora
iucn_location_codes: MX; MXE-CU; US; ARI-OO; NWM-OO; MXN-SO
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: The species is distributed in Chihuahua and Sonora in Mexico, and in Arizona and New Mexico in the United States, at elevations of 1,200 to 2,000 m asl (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows on rocky benches in grasslands and oak woodlands (Benson 1982). The species occurs in grassland, stony, rocky slopes, and steep rock faces in igneous limestone (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for the species, however, land use change is affecting some subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus rigidissimus has a very wide range, is abundant, and although there are threats in places these are not sufficient to warrant any concern. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus santaritensis W.Blum & Rutow sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_locations: New Mexico; Arizona; United States; Mexico; Sonora
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in southeastern Arizona and adjacent New Mexico (United States) and Sonora (Mexico). It grows at elevations of 1,300 to 2,710 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows in sky islands in pine-oak forest, chaparral, and riparian woodland.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by natural fires and mining activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus santaritensis has a relatively wide range and is likely to be more widespread than currently known. It is very locally abundant and the threats are localized. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus scheeri (Salm-Dyck) Scheer sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
