iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: Echinocereus scheeri occurs in the Mexican states of Chihuahua, Durango, and Sonora (Paredes et al. 2000). It grows at elevations of 300 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in steep cliffs. It grows in canyons inside low dry forest and in pine-oak woodlands (Paredes et al. 2000). Blum et al. (1998) reported it from steep rocky mountains, forest clearings and crevices in granite, in humus pockets.
iucn_doc_threats: Part of the species range is threatened by mining and deforestation.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus scheeri has a wide range, is locally abundant, and there are no known major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus schereri G.Frank sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_doc_range: This species grows in the Mexican state of  Durango where it was collected near Mina Navidad on the western slopes of the Rio Nazas valley (Hunt et al. 2006). It appears to be known from the type collection only.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in the slopes of Río Nazas Valley (Hunt et al. 2006), presumably in xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: Nothing is known about the threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species appears to be known only from the type collection made in 1990 and there is no other information on the population size or trends or about any threats to the species, hence is listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus schmollii (Weing.) N.P.Taylor sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_common_name: Cola de Borrego; Organito de Víbora; Pitayita; Lamb's Tail Cactus
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Roads & railroads; Mining & quarrying; Small-holder farming; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: Echinocereus schmollii is endemic to Mexico. Hernández et al. (2004) report the species only from the state of Querétaro. The species is only found in two small disjunct areas in the Querétaro semi-desert (Hernández-Oria et al. 2007). It is narrow elevational range, occurring on a mesa area (a flat area), at elevations of 1,660 to 1,890 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats:
This species occurs in semidesert (Hernández-Oria et al. 2007), and has a narrow range of tolerance in soil type.
Even when individuals reach an adequate size to produce flowers, flower and seed production is scarce. In the wild population regeneration is mainly driven by vegetative reproduction. In cultivation, plants produce more flowers and seeds, especially when fertilized with phosphorus.
iucn_doc_threats: The populations of this species have been historically threatened by anthropogenic activities, such as road construction, fires, vegetation clearance, and also by farming and soil degradation (Hernández-Oria et al. 2006). Currently the predominant threat is habitat destruction due to mining practices, and there is more mining planned for the future. As it grows in flat areas it is especially susceptible to agricultural activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered to be Endangered due to a very small and restricted population (less than 250 mature individuals), and a small extent of occurrence (less than 1,000 km2), and its populations are in decline due to habitat degradation and illegal collecting.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus sciurus (K.Brandegee) Dams sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas; Agro-industry farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: Echinocereus sciurus is endemic to Mexico and occurs in the states of Baja California Sur and Sinaloa (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of up to 100 m asl. The area where the species is known in Sinaloa has been converted to agricultural fields and it is very likely that the species has been extirpated.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in Sonoran Desert and arid subtropical regions, mostly near the coast on hills at low elevations (Taylor 1985).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for the species in Baja California Sur are urban and tourism development and smaller holder cattle ranching; cows trample on the plants and affect the establishment of new plants because they cause erosion. In Sinaloa the major threat is land use change for agro-industry of sugar cane.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has an extent of occurrence of approximately 5,000 km<sup>2</sup>, the subpopulations are severely fragmented as a result of land use change throughout its range, and it does not occur within any protected areas. The species is confined to coastal areas where development is  taking place rapidly. Hence the species is listed as Endangered. It is estimated that at least 50% of the population has been lost  through habitat loss, however the generation length of the species is unknown, and therefore criterion A cannot be applied.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus scopulorum Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring inland in  the state of  Sonora and Tiburón Island. Grows at elevations from 5 to 800 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: Very common on bare areas and on rocky hills and mountains, in Sonoran Desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide range, is sparse but there are no major threats and occurs in a protected area. Hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus spinigemmatus A.B.Lau sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in the Mexican states of Jalisco and Zacatecas (Hunt et al. 2006) growing at altitudes of 1,000 to 1,800 m. It is found in the drainage areas of Rio Huaynamota and Rio Balanos.
iucn_doc_habitats: This plant occurs in lava soils with humus on conglomerate rocks presumably in xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: Nothing is known about any threats for Echinocereus spinigemmatus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although this species appears to have a very small extent of occurrence, nothing is known about the locality where it occurs, the population size and trends, or about any threats. Hence is listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus stramineus (Engelm.) F.Seitz sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_common_name: Strawberry Hedgehog Cactus; Órgano-pequeño Sanjuanero; Strawberry Cactus
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Tamaulipas; Zacatecas; Coahuila; Chihuahua; Texas; Mexico; Nuevo León; Durango; United States; New Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-SL; MXE-TA; MXE-ZA; MXE-CO; MXE-CU; TEX-OO; MX; MXE-NL; MXE-DU; US; NWM-OO
iucn_doc_range: The species is distributed in Mexico in the states of Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas and Zacatecas, and in the United States in New Mexico and Texas, at elevations from 1,200 to 2,100 m asl (Taylor 1985, Hernández et al. 2004, Martínez-Ávalos and Jurado 2005).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus is found on rocky limestone slope in full sun, in desert scrub and pine forest forming large mounds of many stems (Taylor 1985). It grows in hilly to mountainous shrub desert in limestone and igneous soil (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus stramineus has a very wide range, is abundant, and grows in inaccessible places not suitable for farming or agriculture. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus subinermis Salm-Dyck ex Scheer sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_doc_range: Echinocereus subinermis is distributed in Mexico, in the states of Chihuahua, Sinaloa and Sonora (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs on the region of the Río Mayo at the south of Sonora. It grows on shady rocky places at elevations from 450 to 1,150 m on the low deciduous forest and oak woodlands (Paredes et al.  2000). Taylor (1985) reported the species from the Pacific drainage of Sierra Madre Occidental, at or above 200 m altitude. According to Blum et al. (1998) the species grows in wood clearings and rocky slopes in loamy- sandy soil and igneous rocks at 50 - 1,200 m (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is poorly known taxonomically and ecologically. Echinocereus subinermis has not been collected recently and the information on its distribution is uncertain and on population trends unknown. Hence it is listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus triglochidiatus Engelm. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_common_name: Claretcup Hedgehog; Kingcup Cactus; Mojave Mound Cactus; Órgano-pequeño Copa de Vino; Mound Hedgehog Cactus
iucn_locations: Mexico; New Mexico; Arizona; California; Colorado; Baja California; Nevada; Utah; United States; Texas
iucn_location_codes: MX; NWM-OO; ARI-OO; CAL-OO; COL-OO; MXN-BC; NEV-OO; UTA-OO; US; TEX-OO
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Temperate; Grassland - Temperate; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Chihuahua, Mexico and in the United States in Arizona, Colorado, California, New Mexico, Nevada, Texas (?) and Utah (Hernández et al. 2004). It grows at elevations of 150 to 3,000 m asl (Taylor 1985).
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species occurs in a wide variety of habitats from xerophyllous scrub to pine-oak forests. It grows in rocky or grassy hillsides, ledges, and canyons; mostly on igneous rocks, sometimes limestone.
According to Blum et al. (1998) it grows in forest clearings, seldom meadows, in desert- forest regions and slopes with pine forest, in loamy- sandy soil, in humus pockets and in red loam soil.The species is pollinated by hummingbirds and probably bees (Grant and Grant 1979).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus triglochidiatus has a very wide range, is abundant, and there are no major threats. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus viereckii Werderm. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Temperate; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, found in the states of Nuevo León and Tamaulipas, at elevations of 350 to 2,000 m (Hernández et al. 2004, Taylor 1985).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on wooded hills and cliffs on east flanks of the Sierra Madre Oriental (Taylor et al. 1985).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats to this species are road construction and mining.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern because even though its range is relatively small (extent of occurrence is 20,000 km<sup>2</sup>), and there are threats in parts, it grows on cliffs which confers it some protection and it is relatively abundant. In addition, the species occurs at more than ten locations and is not severely fragmented. The species would benefit from more information regarding the extent of the threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Echinocereus waldeisii Haugg sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
Echinocereus websterianus G.E.Linds. sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_threat_names: Named species; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: Species endemic to the island of San Pedro Nolasco off the Mexican state of Sonora (Felger et al. in press).
iucn_doc_habitats: Endemic to Isla San Pedro Nolasco in the Californian Gulf. The species is widely distributed across most of the island. Grows on steep rocky  slopes beside Agave chrysoglossa, Mammillaria multidigitata and Opuntia bravoana (Paredes et al. 2000, Felger et al. in press)/ Grows in the Cerntral Gulf Coast subdivision of the Sonoran Desert.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection for the horticultural trade was a threat in the past (Taylor 1985). There is no illegal collection today and no people reside on the island. A potential future threat is invasion by buffel grass (Cenchrus ciliaris).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very restricted range, but at present there are no major threats. However, there is a potential threat from an invasive grass species (see threats) and if this happened the island would be considered a single location. Hence it is listed as Near Threatened as it almost qualifies for a threatened listing under criteria B1ab(iii)+2ab(iii); D2.

checklist_taxon: Echinopsis aurea Britton & Rose sec. Schlumpberger & Renner 2012
iucn_locations: Córdoba; Catamarca; La Rioja; San Luis; Santiago del Estero; Salta; Argentina
iucn_location_codes: AGE-CO; AGW-CA; AGW-LR; AGW-SL; AGW-SE; AGW-SA; AR
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in Catamarca, Córdoba, La Rioja, Salta, San Luis, and Santiago del Estero (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 550 to 1,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in a variety of habitats woodlands, dry chaco forest, Prosopis forest, high-altitude grasslands, monte shrublands, and water eroded places.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by collection.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinopsis aurea is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is frequent, abundant, occurs in several protected areas, and has no major threats.

checklist_taxon: Echinopsis breviflora (Backeb.) M.Lowry sec. Schlumpberger & Renner 2012
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in Salta at elevations of 3,000 to 3,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is not exposed to any threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinopsis breviflora is listed as Least Concern because, even though it has a small range, the population is stable and does not face any threats because it grows in places of difficult access.

checklist_taxon: Echinopsis calochlora K.Schum. sec. Schlumpberger 2021
iucn_locations: Mato Grosso do Sul; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Brazil; Mato Grosso
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Bolivia in Santa Cruz, and in Brazil in Mato Grosso and Mato Grosso do Sul (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at elevations of 500 to 1,000m asl.
iucn_doc_threats: Mining for iron poses a threat for Brazilian subpopulations (Fundação Biodiversitas 2005).
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinopsis calochlora has a wide range, is abundant, and regenerates fast. Even though mining threatens the species in part of the species' range in Brazil and it is regionally listed as Endangered, overall it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinopsis chrysantha Werderm. sec. Schlumpberger & Renner 2012
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in Salta (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of 2,500 to 2,900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in prepuna in riparian vegetation among rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: The only known present threat is illegal collection, however this is not a major threat. A potential future threat is the development of roads for quarrying transport in the area where the species occurs.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Vulnerable because it has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence < 200 km<sup>2</sup>), and it is not abundant or protected. It is very likely that quarrying and its associated effects, such as road building, will impact the range of the species.

checklist_taxon: Echinopsis densispina Werderm. sec. Schlumpberger & Renner 2012
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Jujuy, Argentina (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at altitudes from 2,300 to 3,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in sand and soft soils in prepuna.
iucn_doc_threats: There is some subsistence farming within the range of this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Echinopsis densispina has a relatively restricted range, the population of this species seems to be stable and not subject to any major threat. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinopsis haematantha (Speg.) D.R.Hunt sec. Schlumpberger & Renner 2012
Echinopsis marsoneri Werderm. sec. Schlumpberger & Renner 2012
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Salta and Jujuy, Argentina. It grows at altitudes between 2,800 to 4,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in rocky areas in the altiplano.
iucn_doc_threats: Extraction for use as an ornamental is not considered to be a threat, as this cactus grows well from seeds. Other than that, there are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Echinopsis marsoneri has a relatively restricted range, its population seems to be stable and not subject to any major threat. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Echinopsis oxygona (Link) Zucc. ex Pfeiff. sec. TROPICOS 1986+
iucn_locations: Paraguay; Rio Grande do Sul; Santa Fé; Corrientes; Salta; Formosa; Uruguay; Brazil; Argentina; Tucumán; Entre Ríos; Chaco
iucn_location_codes: PY; BZS-RS; AGE-SF; AGE-CN; AGW-SA; AGE-FO; UY; BR; AR; AGW-TU; AGE-ER; AGE-CH
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Grassland - Temperate
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder farming; Named species; Trend Unknown/Unrecorded; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: Echinopsis oxygona is found in Argentina (Chaco, Corrientes, Entre Ríos, Formosa, Salta, Santa Fe, Tucumán), Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul), Paraguay, and Uruguay (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations from 50 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on walls and rocky outcrops in pampa grasslands and deciduous forests.
iucn_doc_threats: In the plains of Brazil the major threats are grazing, cattle trampling, agriculture, forestry of Eucalyptus, invasive grasses, and fire. In Uruguay, Argentina and Paraguay, there are no major threats affecting this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern because it has a widespread range, is stable in most of it and there are no major threats affecting it. It is present in many protected areas. However, in Brazil,  this species is affected by many threats and the populations are decreasing.

checklist_taxon: Echinopsis silvestrii Speg. sec. Anderson 2005
Echinopsis tubiflora (Pfeiff.) Zucc. ex A.Dietr. sec. Anderson 2005
Epiphyllum baueri Dorsch sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_doc_range: Epiphyllum baueri is found in Panama, where it occurs in the Colón province and in Colombia, where it occurs in the Chocó department (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations of up to 250 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in tropical humid forests. It is an epiphyte.
iucn_doc_threats: There is no information on threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because its distribution is not well known and there is no information on its population size and trends and threats. However, given that it occurs in relatively intact forests, with very little access, it is likely that this species may be Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Epiphyllum cartagense (F.A.C.Weber) Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Costa Rica, where it occurs in Cartago, Guanacaste and San José. It has been collected from both slopes along the central Cordillera and grows at elevations of 600 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in montane wet forest.
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat destruction is the only threat, but this is not major as extensive areas of suitable habitat remain in good state.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Epiphyllum cartagense has a relatively restricted range (the extent of occurrence is less than 20,000 km²), the species is very abundant, is not declining because there are no major threats, and occurs in many large protected areas. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Epiphyllum chrysocardium Alexander sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
Epiphyllum hookeri Haw. sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_locations: Ecuador; Belize; Honduras; Panama; El Salvador; Mexico; Guatemala; Costa Rica; Nicaragua
iucn_location_codes: EC; BZ; HN; PA; SV; MX; GT; CR; NI
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Shifting agriculture; Agro-industry farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range:
The species is distributed in Belize, Costa Rica, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, Mexico, Nicaragua, and Panama, at elevations of 0 to 2,300 m  (Hunt et al. 2006). It has been reported from Florida in the United States, but the Floristic Inventory of South Florida (Floristic Inventory of South Florida 2001-2011) considers it as not native.

This species has been recorded in Costa Rica, from Alajuela, Cartago and Limón; Honduras from Atlántida; Mexico from Chiapas, México State, Oaxaca, Quintana Roo and Veracruz; and Guatemala from Alta Verapaz, Escuintla, Jalapa, Quetzaltenango, San Marco and Suchitepéquez (Bauer 2003). It has also been recorded in the El Oro and Manabí provinces of Ecuador.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in a wide range of habitats (Hunt et al. 2006). In Costa Rica, it is found in moist forest on both slopes (B. Hammel  pers. comm. 2009). In Guatemala it only occurs in median and high forest (M. Véliz  pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by deforestation driven by land use change for agriculture, such as pineapple, and cattle grazing. Its habitat is very fragmented.
iucn_doc_rationale: Epiphyllum hookeri is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread, common in suitable habitat, and found in numerous protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Epiphyllum oxypetalum (DC.) Haw. sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Mexico, in the States of Chiapas, Tabasco and Veracruz, and in Guatemala in the departments of Izabal, Alta Vera Paz and Quiché at elevations between 200 and 600 m asl (Hunt et al. 2006, M. Véliz pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical moist forest.
iucn_doc_threats: Land use change for agriculture, cattle ranching and logging are the main threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Epiphyllum oxypetalum is widespread, common, and there are no direct threats to it. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Epiphyllum phyllanthus (L.) Haw. sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_locations: Alagoas; Espírito Santo; Suriname; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Bahia; Guyana; Pernambuco; Brazil; Ceará; French Guiana; Argentina; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Rio de Janeiro; Peru; Ecuador; Paraguay; Colombia; Minas Gerais
iucn_location_codes: BZE-AL; BZL-ES; SR; VE; BZE-BA; GY; BZE-PE; BR; BZE-CE; GF; AR; BO; BZL-RJ; PE; EC; PY; CO; BZL-MG
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Mangrove Vegetation Above High Tide Level; Savanna - Moist; Savanna - Dry
iucn_doc_range: This is a widespread species in the Neotropics. It occurs from Colombia in the north southwards to Argentina. It is not present in Mesoamerica or the Caribbean (Cuba); records from these place refer to the sister species E. hookeri. Epiphyllum phyllanthus is the most widespread cactus species in Eastern Brazil, where it has been reported from Ceará, Pernambuco, Alagoas, Bahia, Minas Gerais, Espirito Santo and Rio de Janeiro, near sea level to at least 1,300 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows as an epiphyte in mata atlântica, caatinga-agreste, mata do planalto, mata ciliar and cerrado, as well as in mangrove habitat (Vegas-Vilarrúbia 2000, Taylor and Zappi 2004). It occurs in wet forests (Ponce 1989).
iucn_doc_threats: Given the wide range of this species, it will experience some habitat loss. However, it is generally very tolerant of disturbance, as it is found growing on trees in the middle of cities.
iucn_doc_rationale: The frequency of Epiphyllum phyllanthus has been reduced due to deforestation in parts of Brazil. Nevertheless, it is still very widespread, extremely common, and fairly tolerant of disturbance. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Epiphyllum pumilum Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_locations: Oaxaca; Guatemala; Veracruz; Mexico; Belize; Tabasco; Chiapas
iucn_location_codes: MXS-OA; GT; MXG-VC; MX; BZ; MXT-TB; MXT-CI
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_doc_range:
The species is distributed in Belize, Guatemala in Izabal and Alta Verapaz, and in the Mexican states of Chiapas, Oaxaca, Tabasco and Veracruz, at elevations ranging from 10-600 m (Hunt et al. 2006; Véliz 2008).
It is unclear whether many of the existing occurrence records concern this species or E. hookeri.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical dry forest and tropical humid forest.
iucn_doc_threats: No major threats exist for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is abundant, there are no major threats for the species and it occurs within several protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Epiphyllum thomasianum (K.Schum.) Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_locations: Guatemala; Colombia; Mexico; Costa Rica; Chiapas; Ecuador; Panama; Nicaragua

iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species has a wide range, occurring from Mexico (State of Chiapas) through Guatemala (Departments of Escuintla and Sacatepéquez), in Nicaragua (north central and along the Caribbean coast), Costa Rica (Cartago, San José), Panama, and northern Colombia (Magdalena; Rosii 1998, Bauer 2003, Guzmán et al. 2003, Hunt et al. 2006). There is supposedly a record from Ecuador but this is not confirmed. It grows at altitudes between 50 and 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in lowland and montane wet forest.
iucn_doc_threats: Loss of habitat due to tourist development, urbanization, large-scale agriculture, and logging are the main threats in parts of the species' wide range.
iucn_doc_rationale: Epiphyllum thomasianum is a very widespread and common species, is very frequent to abundant, and although there are threats these are not sufficient to trigger a threatened listing. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Epithelantha bokei L.D.Benson sec. Aquino & al. 2019
iucn_common_name: Biznaga-blanca de Boquillas; Boke's Button Cactus
iucn_locations: United States; Coahuila; Texas; Mexico; Chihuahua
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Mexico the states of Coahuila and Chihuahua, and in Texas in the United States.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on limestone hills (Hunt et al. 2006), and it is an edaphic specialist growing on very specific calcareous strata. It grows in xerophyllous scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by illegal collection. It is collected along other similar-looking species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Epithelantha bokei is locally abundant and it grows in many inaccessible areas. The species is listed as Least Concern, however some subpopulations are threatened by illegal collection. Ironically, these occur within the Big Bend National Park.

iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in semi-desert among calcareous rock.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collecting and encroachment of agriculture are major threats for some subspecies.
iucn_doc_rationale: Epithelantha micromeris has a wide range and tends to occur in high densities. It can be found in several protected areas. Some subpopulations are affected by illegal collection and agriculture, but this does not threaten the species as a whole. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.
