iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it can be found in the O'Higgins and Maule regions, from Pupuya to Constitución, at elevations from 30 to 100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on coastal terraces with ocean influence, on well drained but poor soils, in sclerophyllous scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are urbanization in the southern and northern portions of the range. Road construction for forestry activities occurs in the central part of the range, cattle and horse ranching in the southern subpopulations, and illegal collection throughout the range.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce aspillagae has an extent of occurrence of 3,000 km2, there are three locations affected by several threats that cause a continuous declines in the numbers of mature individuals and quality of the habitat. Hence, it is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce atroviridis (F.Ritter) P.C.Guerrero & Helmut Walter sec. Guerrero & al. 2020
Eriosyce aurata (Pfeiff.) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it is found in the regions of Atacama, Valparaíso and Metropolitana, at elevations of 100 to 3,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in desert scrub and sclerophyllous scrub, on rocky north-facing slopes in the andean pre-cordillera.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is illegal collection, the species has very high extraction rates. It suffers from vandalism.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce aurata has a very wide range and, even though there is heavy extraction, this impact is localized. There are a many locations and it occurs within several protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern. The species could benefit from studies on extraction rates and legislation to prevent it.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce bulbocalyx (Werderm.) Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: San Luis; San Juan; La Rioja; Argentina
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in La Rioja, San Juan and San Luis. It grows at elevations of  500 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in monte shrubland and chaco forest, in rocky shallow soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats for the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce bulbocalyx is listed as Least Concern because of it has wide range, is abundant, there are no threats, and it occurs in protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce calderana (F.Ritter) Ferryman sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: Eriosyce calderana is endemic to the Atacama region, Chile, where it occurs from Morro Copiapó to Chañaral at elevations of 0 to 400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows between rocks in coastal hills (lomas costeras).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for Eriosyce calderana is illegal collection.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very small range (extent of occurrence approximately 1,400 km<sup>2</sup>), it is not abundant and is heavily collected by cactophile tourists. There is an estimated population size of less than 2,500 mature individuals, due to collection there is a continuing decline and the maximum number of mature individuals in a subpopulation is 150. Hence, the species is here classified as Endangered under criterion C2a(i).

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce caligophila R.Pinto sec. Guerrero & al. 2020
Eriosyce chilensis (Hildm. ex K.Schum.) Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs from north of Valparaíso to southern Coquimbo regions, on coastal areas only, at elevations of 0 to 50 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in coastal cliffs in sclerophyllous forest.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce chilensis has a very small range (extent of occurrence and area of occupancy both approximately 45 km2), which is considered a single location because the species is impacted by illegal collection, especially by the extraction of mature individuals. Hence, it is listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(v).

checklist_fullName: Eriosyce clavata (Söhrens ex K.Schum.) Helmut Walter
checklist_taxon: Eriosyce clavata (Söhrens ex K.Schum.) Helmut Walter sec. Zuloaga & al. 2019
Eriosyce crispa (F.Ritter) Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Agro-industry farming; Mining & quarrying; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: Eriosyce crispa is endemic to the Atacama region in Chile, from Huasco to Totoral, at elevations of 0 to 900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in coastal hills (lomas costeras) among rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are agriculture of fruit trees (agro-industry), mining industry (southern part of the range), irregular settlements of people (also known as tomas, northern part of the range) and illegal collection.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce crispa has an extent of occurrence of 2,400 km2, it is known from five locations and several threats throughout its range are causing a continuous decline in the number of mature individuals and quality of the habitat. Hence, the species is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce curvispina (Bertero ex Colla) Katt. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Maule; Valparaíso; O'Higgins; Coquimbo; Santiago; Chile
iucn_location_codes: CLC-MA; CLC-VA; CLC-OH; CLC-CO; CLC-SA; CL
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying; Agro-industry farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it is found in the regions of Coquimbo, Valparaíso, Metropolitana, O'Higgins and Maule, at elevations of 0 to 2,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in sclerophyllous forest on rocky areas.
iucn_doc_threats:
The distribution of this cactus overlaps with areas of high human density. It is therefore affected by land use change due to agriculture, mining, urbanization, desertification processes and illegal collection.


.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce curvispina is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, and although there are some localized threats related to land use change and illegal collection, overall the population is stable. Furthermore, it grows in several protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce engleri (F.Ritter) Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occcurs in the high mountains between Santiago and Valparaiso, from Valparaiso to the Metropolitana region, at elevations of  1,300 to 2,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in andean shrublands on rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce engleri has a reduced extent of occurrence of approximately 1,000 km2 and is not abundant. However it has no threat, the population is stable and it grows in inaccessible places. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce esmeraldana (F.Ritter) Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Roads & railroads; Named species
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in north and south of Esmeralda locality, Antofagasta region of Chile (Hoffmann and Walter 1989). It grows from 0 to 400 m asl.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is under collection pressure for ornamental purposes. Guanacos (Lama guanicoe) use it as food item. Road construction also affects this cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce esmeraldana has a small range (extent of occurrence <100 km2) and only occurs in two locations. Collection for its use as an ornamental and road construction have caused population declines. These threats will not cease in the near future. Hence, it is listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(v).

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce garaventae (F.Ritter) Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: Eriosyce garaventae is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the region of Valparaiso, at elevations of 1,700 to 1,900 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in Andean shrubland on rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has an area of occurrence of less than 20 km<sup>2</sup>, however there are no threats and it grows in inaccessible areas and in protected areas, hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce heinrichiana (Backeb.) Katt. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry farming; Mining & quarrying; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to the Atacama and southern Coquimbo regions of Chile. It grows at elevations of 0 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in thorny scrub on rocky soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are illegal collection, mining and grape plantations for wine.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce heinrichiana is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range (extent of occurrence 16,000 km2), it is only affected by localized threats that are not leading to a continuing decline of the population, and it is found within protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce iquiquensis (F.Ritter) Ferryman sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Droughts
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs the regions of Arica y Parinacota, Tarapacá and Antofagasta from south of Tocopilla to north of Antofagasta. It grows at elevations of 3 to 1,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in extreme desert on rocky substrate.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are illegal collection and intensified droughts, presumably caused by global climate change.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce iquiquensis has a restricted, severely fragmented range (extent of occurrence 3,000 km2). It is known from three locations and is subject to illegal collection. The area where it occurs is experiencing aridification, which threatens the survival of the species as many dead plants are already observed. Hence, the species qualifies for listing as Endangered under criterion B1ab(iii,v).

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce islayensis (C.F.Först.) Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Droughts; Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_doc_range: The species is distributed in southern Peru (Arequipa, Marcona, Moquegua and Tacna) and northern Chile in Arica where the species is probably extinct as all the observed individuals in the region are dead. At elevations of 0 to 1,000 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The specie is found in coastal range (lomas costeras).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by urban development in Peru in Mollendo and Camana, in Mollendo the development of recreational areas is a major threats.In the southern part of the distribution, in Chile it is presumed that climate change is killing the populations because of prolonged periods of draught.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Near Threatened as it could qualify as  Vulnerable under Criterion C, and the existing information on generation length is insufficient to apply threat categories. However in the southern part of the species' distribution in Chile is probably extinct and in the rest of the species range there are threats.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce krausii (F.Ritter) Katt. sec. Guerrero & al. 2020
Eriosyce kunzei (C.F.Först.) Katt. sec. Guerrero & al. 2020
Eriosyce limariensis (F.Ritter) Katt. sec. Guerrero & al. 2020
Eriosyce litoralis (F.Ritter) P.C.Guerrero & Helmut Walter sec. Guerrero & al. 2020
Eriosyce marksiana (F.Ritter) Katt. sec. Guerrero & al. 2020
Eriosyce napina (Phil.) Katt. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Desert - Cold
iucn_threat_names: Named species; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs from the north of Coquimbo region (El Trapiche, Quebrada Río Choros) to Atacama region (Totoral). It grows from 10 to 600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This geophytic species is dispersed by wind. It occurs in the Atacama desert and in shrubby vegetation.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is under collection pressure for ornamental purposes. Guanacos (Lama guanicoe) use it as food item.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce napina is listed as Vulnerable due to its restricted range (extent of occurrence <10,000 km2), its presence in no more than 10 locations, and because its population is decreasing due to its collection for ornamental purposes.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce occulta Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Roads & railroads
iucn_doc_range: Eriosyce occulta is endemic to Chile. It is found in the southern portion of the Antofagasta region, in the surroundings areas of Taltal, at elevations of 50 to 600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs on small cliffs in dry environments (Hoffmann and Walter 1989).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are illegal collection and road construction and reconstruction after the rainy season. The materials and movement of heavy vehicles during road reconstruction is what affects the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce occulta is listed as Endangered because it has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence ca 190 km2), it occurs in one location and the whole population experiences continuing decline due to illegal collection and road construction. In addition, the population size is less than 1,000 individuals, and the largest subpopulation has around 50 mature individuals.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce odieri (Lem. ex Salm-Dyck) Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This species only occurs in the central northern coastal area of the Atacama desert in Chile. It grows at altitudes between 0 and 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This geophytic cactus grows in valleys and basins of the coastal region. Its seeds are dispersed by wind.
iucn_doc_threats: <span class="apple-style-span">This cactus is threatened by collection for ornamental purposes.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce odieri is listed as Vulnerable due to its restricted range (extent of occurrence <10,000 km2), its presence in no more than 10 locations, and because its population is decreasing due to its collection for ornamental purposes.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce paucicostata (F.Ritter) Ferryman sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the region of Antofagasta, between Taltal and Antofagasta. It grows at elevations of 50 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in coastal hills (lomas costeras) on rocky soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce paucicostata has a a restricted range (extent of occurrence is approximately 11,000 km2), however there are no major threats and the population is stable. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce recondita (F.Ritter) Katt. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the region of Antofagasta in Morro Moreno and Quebrada la Chimba. It grows at elevations of 600 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in coastal hills (lomas costeras) on rocky and sandy soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The only major threat known for Eriosyce recondita is illegal collection.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce recondita is known from two localities where it is stable, however the population size is way less than 1,000 individuals and there is illegal collection. Even though at present this is not a major threat, it could cause serious declines in the long term if it continues. Hence, the species is listed as Vulnerable under criterion D1.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce rodentiophila F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Chile, occurring in the regions of Atacama and Antofagatsa, from north of Taltal to south of Chañaral, at elevations of 0 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in coastal hills (lomas costeras) on rocky, granitic soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is the collection of mature individuals and seeds for propagation.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has an extent of occurrence of 5,000 km2, it is known from between two and seven locations, and the major threat is illegal collection which is causing a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals. The seeds are also collected and this has an effect on the recruitment of new individuals. Hence it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce senilis (Backeb.) Katt. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming; Agro-industry farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it can be found in the regions of Coquimbo, Valparaíso and Metropolitana, from Elqui Valley to the north of Metropolitana region. It grows at elevations of 600 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in sclerophyllous forest and thorny scrub on rocky soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is illegal collection. Also, land use change for agriculture and urbanization degrades the habitat close to where the species occurs.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce senilis is listed as Vulnerable because it has a relatively small range (extent of occurrence =16,000 km2), the populations are severely fragmented, and illegal collection is causing a continual decline in the number of mature individuals. Furthermore, land conversion is degrading the habitat surrounding the species.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce simulans (F.Ritter) Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Chile, where it can be found in the northern coast of Coquimbo region, at elevations of 100 to 450 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in coastal hills (lomas costeras) on slopes. Generation length is 15 years.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is illegal collection. There is a mining project underway that will severely affect the population.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence is 74 km<sup>2</sup>). Its generation length is 15 years. Currently the only threat (illegal collection) is not causing continuing declines severe enough to trigger a red listing. However, there is a mining project in the area, which is underway which will affect the habitat quality and quantity of 50% of the population in the near future. Hence, it is listed as Endangered under criterion A3c.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce sociabilis (F.Ritter) Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Droughts
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the region of Atacama, north and south of Totoral Bajo. It grows at elevations of 0 to 400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in Atacama desert on rocky soils (Belov 2005).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are illegal collection and prolonged droughts, presumably caused by global climate change.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce sociabilis has an estimated population size of less than 250 mature individuals and is subject to illegal collection. This impact will not cease in the near future, and could drive the species to extinction if it is not halted. In addition, the species is affected by prolonged droughts, which are probably related to global climate change. Hence, the species is listed as Endangered under criterion D.

checklist_fullName: Eriosyce spectabilis Katt., Helmut Walter & J.C.Acosta
checklist_taxon: Eriosyce spectabilis Katt., Helmut Walter & J.C.Acosta sec. Guerrero & al. 2020
Eriosyce strausiana (K.Schum.) Katt. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: La Rioja; Mendoza; Rio Negro; San Juan; Neuquén; Argentina
iucn_location_codes: AGW-LR; AGW-ME; AGS-RN; AGW-SJ; AGS-NE; AR
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in La Rioja, Mendoza, Neuquén, Río Negro and San Juan, at elevations of 500 to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in monte shrubland and high altitude grasslands, on rocky and sandy soils in alluvial fans.
iucn_doc_threats: In part of the range the species is potentially threatened by agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce strausiana is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is abundant, occurs in protected areas and there are no major threats.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce subgibbosa (Haw.) Katt. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Atacama; O'Higgins; Maule; Valparaíso; Biobío; Coquimbo; Chile
iucn_location_codes: CLN-AT; CLC-OH; CLC-MA; CLC-VA; CLC-BI; CLC-CO; CL
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the regions of Biobio, Maule, O´Higgins, Valparaiso, Coquimbo and Atacama. It grows at elevations of 0 to 200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in sclerophyllous forest on rocky soils (Belov 2005).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is illegal collection and urbanization in tourism areas, but these are localized impacts.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce subgibbosa has a wide range, is abundant, occurs in protected areas, and the few threats are very localized. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce taltalensis (Hutchison) Katt. sec. Guerrero & al. 2020
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the regions of Antofagasta and Atacama, always in coastal areas. It grows at elevations of 0 to 700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in coastal hills (lomas costeras) on rock crevices.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection is the only major threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce taltalensis has a relatively wide range, but it is very scarce. There is pressure on the subpopulations from illegal collection, the species occurs within a protected area and it grows in inaccessible areas (cliffs). The total population size is estimated to be 700 mature individuals, therefore it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce tenebrica (F.Ritter) Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
Eriosyce umadeave (Frič ex Werderm.) Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic of Argentina, where it occurs in Salta and Jujuy provinces at 2,500 to 3,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found on rocky soils of the puna and pre-puna.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is the extraction of wild specimens to use them as ornamentals.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce umadeave has a restricted range (extent of occurrence of approximately 1,300 km2) and its known from two locations. Extraction of cacti to use them as ornamentals causes population declines. This threat will not cease in the near future. Hence, it is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce villicumensis (Rausch) Katt. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in San Juan, it grows at elevations ca 1,900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in monte vegetation (creosote bush).
iucn_doc_threats: The area where the species grows has no water availability whatsoever, hence it is not suitable for human settlements or activities such as agriculture or farming. The only threat is quarrying, but this is very localized.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though this species' range is small (extent of occurrence = 500km<sup>2</sup>), it is abundant and there are no threats affecting it, in addition the area where it grows is very dry and unsuitable for human settlements and activities. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce villosa (Monv.) Katt. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in northern Coquimbo and Atacama regions. It grows at elevations of 0 to 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in coastal hills (lomas costeras) on rocky soils (Hoffmann and Walter 1989).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are illegal collection and industrialization to process steel.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce villosa has a restricted range (extent of occurrence 5,000 km2). The threats are, however, localized and are not impacting the population at a level that would warrant listing in a threatened category. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Eriosyce wagenknechtii (F.Ritter) Katt. sec. Guerrero & al. 2020
Escontria chiotilla (F.A.C.Weber ex K.Schum.) Rose sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Construction or structural materials; Food - human; Fuels
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the States of Guerrero, Michoacán, Oaxaca and Puebla (Arias et al. 1997), at elevations of 800 to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical deciduous forest and xerophyllous scrub (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Escontria chiotilla is a common and fairly widespread species with no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Espostoa blossfeldiorum (Werderm.) Buxb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This Peruvian endemic is found in Jaén and Celendín (Cajamarca), Huancabamba (Piura), and Balsas (Amazonas), at altitudes of 500 to 2,000 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in dry and moist montane forests, where it is surrounded by abundant vegetation.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is not affected by any threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Espostoa blossfeldiorum has a relatively wide range and stable populations. It is not exposed to any threats. This species is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Espostoa calva F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: Esposta calva is a Peruvian endemic found near Balsas, Valle del Marañón, in the Amazonas administrative region (Arakaki et al. 2006). It can be found at altitudes of 1,800-2,500 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This tree-like cactus can only be found in dry forests.
iucn_doc_threats: It is difficult to reach the area where this cactus occurs. This isolation protects it from human activities. Therefore, it is currently not exposed to any threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: This cactus is only known from a single, very restricted location. Although it is currently not exposed to any threat and its unlikely to be affected in the future because it grows in areas of difficult access, therefore it is classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Espostoa cremnophila Hoxey sec. Hoxey 2014
Espostoa frutescens Madsen sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: Espostoa frutescens is found in Loja, El Oro and Azuay provinces in Ecuador. It grows at altitudes between 500 and 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in open areas within dry forests.
iucn_doc_threats: No threats currently exist to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Espostoa frutescens has a restricted range of less than 500 km2, but this might be an underestimate. It therefore probably qualifies for classification in a Threat category under criterion B1ab(iii). However, is common, abundant and the habitat is not affected. It is therefore classified as Near Threatened.

checklist_taxon: Espostoa hylaea F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This Peruvian endemic can be found in Magdalena and Bagua (both Bagua province, Amazonas region), at lower altitudes (around 500 m) (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is restricted to dry forests, where it is surrounded by abundant vegetation.
iucn_doc_threats: It appears that this species is currently not exposed to any threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: This cactus is only known from a single, very restricted location. At present it is not exposed to any threat and even though it is scarce its population seems stable. Hence is classified as Least Concern. It is recommended to monitor the population of E. hylaea due to its local rarity.

checklist_taxon: Espostoa lanata (Kunth) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_common_name: Peruvian Old Man; Snowball Cactus; Snowball Old Man; Cotton Ball Cactus
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: Espostoa lanata is found in the departments of Azuay and Loja, Ecuador, and the Peruvian regions of Amazonas, Áncash, Cajamarca, Huánuco, Lambayeque and Piura (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at 500 to 2,000 meters above sea level.
iucn_doc_habitats: Due to its wide range, it occurs in different habitat types. It grows in inter-Andean valleys, thorny desert matorral and dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: Removing the woolly hair from the lateral cephalia may interfere with flowering, but this needs to be investigated. Other than that, there are no known threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Espostoa lanata has a wide range, stable populations, and is not exposed to any major threat. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Espostoa melanostele (Vaupel) Borg sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru, where it is found in southern Lambayeque, La Libertad, Lima, Áncash, and northern Ica. It grows at altitudes between 800 and 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus can be found in thorny dry matorral. It is shrub-like and can reach 2 m height.
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is not exposed to any threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a wide extent of occurrence, is common in large parts of its range and is currently not exposed to any threat. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Espostoa mirabilis F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru, where it is found in the Amazonas, La Libertad and Cajamarca regions (Arakaki et al. 2006) at altitudes of 500 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This shrubby cactus occurs in dry forests along the Río Marañón.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Espostoa mirabilis is common where it occurs. It has a restricted range, but apparently stable populations. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Espostoa senilis (F.Ritter) N.P.Taylor sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This Peruvian endemic can be found in the Áncash and La Libertad regions, at altitudes of 2,000 to 2,500 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006). There is an additional record from Cajamarca.
iucn_doc_habitats: E. senilis occurs in thorny dry matorral.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats known for this species at present.
iucn_doc_rationale: Espostoa senilis has a restricted range and is locally rare. However, it does not seem to be exposed to any threat and its population is stable. It is therefore classified as Least Concern. It is recommended to monitor the population of E. senilis due to its local rarity.

checklist_taxon: Espostoa superba F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru and is only found near Jaén, in northern Cajamarca region, at altitudes of 560 to 800 m (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This tree-like cactus can reach up to 7 m height. It grows in thorny dry matorral and dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: This cactus is only known from a single locality. It seems to be very rare, as only a few individuals have been observed. Its extent of occurrence is very restricted. The population and habitat trends, as well as the threats, are not known. Espostoa superba is therefore classified as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Espostoa utcubambensis G.J.Charles sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This Peruvian endemic is only found in the Amazonas region at altitudes of 1,350 to 1,950 m asl. It was first described from a specimen collected in the catchment area of the Utcubamba river (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus can reach a height of 4 to 5 m. It occurs in thorny dry matorral.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this large cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Espostoa utcubambensis occurs in only one valley and has a restricted range. It is common within its natural range, has stable populations, and is not affected by any threats. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Espostoopsis dybowskii (Rol.-Goss.) Buxb. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Dry
iucn_threat_names: Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas; Mining & quarrying; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: The distribution of this isolated species is markedly disjunct between northern and eastern Bahia (Rio de Contas Valley) in Brazil, the southern form showing some differences (notably smaller stems and more exserted olive-brown fruits) that could justify subspecific status. The better-known northern population, whence the type came, covers an extensive area within the município of Jaguarari and in some parts of this region the plant dominates the vegetation, forming impenetrable groves around and upon quartzitic outcrops. The southern populations are found on both gneissic inselbergs and quartzite outcrops. Its disjunct distribution and the differences between the plants from the two areas implies that the species may be an ancient relict in decline. The species occurs at elevation of 300 to 750 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It is found in eastern caatinga element, dominant or co-dominant on gneiss/granite inselbergs or quartzitic rock outcrops and in the surrounding caatinga (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: In the northern populations the major threats are habitat loss for agriculture, cattle ranching and urbanization. The southern populations are affected by the same threats plus mining and fires.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a relatively large extent of occurrence (EOO; ca. 18,000), it has two locations both affected by several threats which cause a continuing decline in the populations. Hence this species is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Eulychnia acida Phil. sec. Larridon & al. 2018
iucn_habitat_names: Desert - Cold; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Construction or structural materials; Fuels; Food - human; Handicrafts, jewellery, etc.
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it can be found in Coquimbo and halfway in Atacama region at elevations of 0 to 1,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in desert scrub and sclerophyllous forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are agriculture of fruit trees and goat grazing.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eulychnia acida has a wide range, it is abundant, the threats are localized, and it occurs within protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Eulychnia barquitensis F.Ritter sec. Walter & Guerrero 2022
Eulychnia breviflora Phil. sec. Larridon & al. 2018
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Desert - Cold
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the regions of Coquimbo and Atacama at elevations of 0 to 400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in coastal hills (lomas costeras) and thorny scrub on sandy soils.
iucn_doc_threats: No major threats are known for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eulychnia breviflora has a wide range of around 25,000 km2, is abundant, there are no known threats, and it occurs within protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Eulychnia castanea Phil. sec. Larridon & al. 2018
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it is found in the regions of Valparaíso and Coquimbo, from los Molles to National Park Fray Jorge, at elevations of 0 to 50 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in thorny scrub on rocky soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is urbanization in tourism areas and eolic parks.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eulychnia castanea has a restricted range (extent of occurrence 5,000 km2), however it is abundant, threats are localized, and it occurs within protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Eulychnia chorosensis P.Klaassen sec. Larridon & al. 2018
Eulychnia iquiquensis (K.Schum.) Britton & Rose sec. Larridon & al. 2018
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs from the Camaraca mountain to Chañaral, always in coastal areas, as well as in the I, II and III regions of Chile (Hoffmann and Walter 1989). The subpopulation in Peru occurs on the coast of Arequipa, Chala and Atiquipa. It can be found at elevations between 0 and 1,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species only occurs in lomas costeras on the coastal range of Peru and Chile.
iucn_doc_threats: High mortality rates north of Antofagasta, Chile are fragmenting the subpopulations. Prolonged droughts are affecting part of the Peruvian subpopulation.
iucn_doc_rationale:
Eulychnia iquiquensis is restricted to a narrow coastline of approximately 1,600 km length and no more than 5 km width in Chile. The Peruvian and Chilean subpopulations are separated by approximately 600 km. The area of occupancy may therefore be lower than 2,000 km2, but should be carefully assessed. Although the species is considered to be rare in Peru and fragmentation is occurring in northern Chile, there are no severe threats affecting the species as a whole. Furthermore, it is present in several protected areas. The species is thus classified as Least Concern.
However, it should be taken into account that the Peruvian and Chilean subpopulations are considered separate species by the Cactus Specialists. If both subpopulations were assessed separately, it is likely that they would both be classified in a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Eulychnia ritteri Cullmann sec. Larridon & al. 2018
Eulychnia taltalensis (F.Ritter) Hoxey sec. Larridon & al. 2018
Eulychnia vallenarensis P.C.Guerrero & Helmut Walter sec. Guerrero & Walter 2019
Facheiroa ulei (Gürke) Werderm. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, it occurs in northern Bahia east of San Francisco river, at elevations of 400 to 950 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on quartzitic and sandstone rocks in caatinga.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats. There was mining in the area but it has now ceased. There is some small hold cattle ranching but this does not affect the populations. The area where it grows in is very rocky and the rock is of no use nor the land.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide range, the populations are stable and there are no threats, hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus alamosanus (Britton & Rose) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: Species found in México in the states of  Chihuahua, Sinaloa and Sonora (Hunt et al. 2006) at 450 - 1,300 m altitude (Pilbeam and Bowdery 2005; T. Van Devender pers. comm.)
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in upper tropical deciduous forests up to pine-oak forests on the western edge of the Sierra Madre Occidental in the Álamos area in southeastern Sonora and adjacent Chihuahua.
iucn_doc_threats: There is increased logging in the area where the species occurs that may potentially affect its populations. There is also an active mine and mining exploration in the area that may pose a threat in the future.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Near Threatened because it has a restricted extent of occurrence and very small number of populations (< 10 locations). The species is close to being Vulnerable using D criteria, and more information is needed on area of occupancy. There are currently no imminent threats that are causing a continuing decline in the extent of occurrence, area of occupancy, or extent or quality of its habitat - but potential future threats could be cause for concern.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus echidne (DC.) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Tamaulipas; Nuevo León; Hidalgo; Guanajuato; San Luis Potosí; Querétaro; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-TA; MXE-NL; MXE-HI; MXE-GU; MXE-SL; MXE-QU; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Nuevo León, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, and Tamaulipas, at elevations of 300 to 1,860 m asl (Hernández et al. 2004, Pilbeam and Bowdery 2005).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in xerophyllous, submontane scrub (Goettsch and Hernández 2006) and tropical dry forest (E. Sánchez pers. comm. 2010).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species. Land use changes present regional threats to subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ferocactus echidne is considered to be Least Concern due to abundant and widespread subpopulations, and because it is not declining at nearly the rate to qualify for a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus flavovirens (Scheidw.) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán Valley in Mexico (Arias et al. 1997), at elevations of 1,500 - 1,900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in xerophyllous scrub and flowers between October and March (Arias et al. 1987).
iucn_doc_threats:
Land use change for agriculture is the main threat for this species.
The range of this species is predicted to be reduced by up to 90% under climate change scenarios in which temperature increases by 2°C and precipitation decreases by 15% compared to current climate conditions (approximately between year 2060 and year 2100; Téllez-Valdés and Dávila-Aranda 2003).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a narrow extent of occurrence (EOO is 2,000 km<sup>2</sup>), is severely fragmented and is not abundant. Several known subpopulations have disappeared as a consequence of land use change, which is still a threat because the subpopulations are very close to agricultural land, even those inside the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán Biosphere Reserve. Hence is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus fordii (Orcutt) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Baja California Sur; Baja California; Mexico
iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Coastal Sand Dunes; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Garbage & solid waste; Agro-industry farming; Intentional use (species is the target); Housing & urban areas; Work & other activities
iucn_doc_range: Ferocactus fordii is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Baja California and Baja California Sur (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations from 0 to 1,200 m asl (Paredes et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in the Sonoran desert scrub (Paredes et al. 2000) in flat sandy areas, and in coastal sand dunes and flat grassy areas (Pilbeam and Bowdery 1995).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are illegal collection, urban development in the coastal area, salt industry, and fish camps. The latter consist of informal, temporary settlements of fishermen that leave a lot of rubbish behind. They also open access paths, which give way to further development and impacts from tourism. The southern subpopulation is affected by the production of alternative sources of energy.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ferocactus fordii has a relatively restricted range (extent of occurrence of approximately 10,000 km2). It is heavily collected illegally and is affected by several threats, such as urban development and industrial activities, which cause a continuing decline in habitat quality and the extent of the species range. There are only two known disjunct subpopulations.  Hence, it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus glaucescens (DC.) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Querétaro; Hidalgo; San Luis Potosí; Guanajuato; Mexico
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Querétaro, and San Luis Potosí  (Hernández et al. 2004), at elevations of 550 to 2,300 m asl (Martínez-Avalos and Jurado 2005, Pilbeam and Bowdery 2005).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in deciduous dry forests and dry shrubland habitats.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats, however locally subpopulations are declining due to land use change.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ferocactus glaucescens is considered Least Concern due to its relatively widespread and abundant subpopulations, because there are no major threats, and because it occurs in several protected areas.

checklist_fullName: Ferocactus haematacanthus (Salm-Dyck) Bravo ex Backeb. & F.M.Knuth
checklist_taxon: Ferocactus haematacanthus (Salm-Dyck) Bravo ex Backeb. & F.M.Knuth sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_authority: (Monv. ex Salm-Dyck) Bravo ex Backeb. & F.M.Knuth
iucn_synonyms: Echinocactus electracanthus Lem. var. haematacanthus Monv. ex Salm-Dyck
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán Valley, Mexico, and is distributed only in the States of Puebla and Veracruz (Arias et al. 1997; Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 1,800 - 2,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in xerophyllous scrub and oak chaparral on calcareous soils. It flowers between March and May and produces fruits between July and November (Arias et al. 1987).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is habitat fragmentation as a result of land conversion to agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a restricted extent of occurrence (EOO approximately 4,000 km<sup>2</sup>), its population is severely fragmented and it is suffering a continuing decline due to land use change for agriculture. Hence it is listed as Endangered under Criterion B1. The species is large and potentially long lived, and there are about ten subpopulations with a total population of less than 2,500 mature individuals (potentially substantially less) and almost certainly meets the criteria for Endangered under Criterion C.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus hamatacanthus (Muehlenpf.) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_common_name: Biznaga-de-Limilla; Fishhook Cactus; Turk's Head; Biznaga-barril Costillona; Tobusch Fishhook Cactus
iucn_locations: Tamaulipas; Texas; Nuevo León; Coahuila; San Luis Potosí; Mexico; New Mexico; Durango; Zacatecas; United States
iucn_location_codes: MXE-TA; TEX-OO; MXE-NL; MXE-CO; MXE-SL; MX; NWM-OO; MXE-DU; MXE-ZA; US
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Roads & railroads
iucn_doc_range:
This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Coahuila, Durango, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas and Zacatecas, and in the United States in New Mexico and Texas (Hernández et al. 2004). The species occurs at elevation of 10 to 2,150 m asl (Pilbeam and Bowdery 2005).
The northernmost distribution in Otero, New Mexico and El Paso, Texas is taken from Benson (1982) and has not been confirmed since.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in xerophyllous scrub, on a diversity of soils and on hills, in soils from igneous rock and on old river gravels in the desert and grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: Land use change and road construction affect several subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ferocactus hamatacanthus has a wide range and is abundant. Even though some subpopulations are affected by road construction and land use change, these are not a major threats to the species as a whole. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus herrerae J.G.Ortega sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Industrial aquaculture; Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in the states of Sinaloa and Sonora. It occurs mainly along the coasts from 0 - 400m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs on the coastal plains from Guaymas, Sonora, to the centre of Sinaloa.
iucn_doc_threats: Land use change for cattle pasture and shrimp farming has removed much of the range and it is projected to continue to do so.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although this is an abundant species, it has suffered a substantial reduction in the available habitat over the last 20 years (generation length 10 years). This habitat loss is the result of the rapid expansion of coastal shrimp farms and cattle ranching, which is projected to continue into the future. Therefore, a population reduction of 30% is estimated to take place considering both past and future, which warrants the species a Vulnerable listing.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus histrix (DC.) G.E.Linds. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Veracruz; Jalisco; Querétaro; Hidalgo; Aguascalientes; Durango; Guanajuato; San Luis Potosí; Zacatecas; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXG-VC; MXS-JA; MXE-QU; MXE-HI; MXE-AG; MXE-DU; MXE-GU; MXE-SL; MXE-ZA; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Nomadic grazing; Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - animal; Food - human; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico where it is widely distributed in the states of Aguascalientes, Durango, Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Veracruz and Zacatecas (Hernández et al. 2004), occurring between 1,200 and 2,600 m (Pilbeam and Bowdery 2005).
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species occurs in xerophyllous scrub, oak forests and submontane scrub.

The development of an adult plant from seed takes decades, but a much faster growth can be achieved with stems produced by branching when the apical meristem is disturbed (Del Castillo and Trujillo 1991).
iucn_doc_threats:
In addition to the direct exploitation, goats and cattle degrade the microhabitats reducing the success of germination and seedling establishment (Del Castillo 1986).  Human consumption of adult plants for producing "dulces de acitron" (candy) was a significant threat in many areas, but especially near human population centres, as this requires the destruction of mature plants to obtain the parenchyma. This practice is now banned by law, and the candy may only be sold commercially if it is produced from cultivated plants.

In recent years the expansion of the city of Querétaro has resulted in the destruction of the Bajío tropical dry forest in areas where thousands of plants of this species occurred. There has been some mitigation but the damage is irreversible (Sánchez et al. 2011).
iucn_doc_rationale:
Ferocactus histrix has clearly undergone a significant population decline in the last 30 to 100 years (one generation is estimated to be at least 30 years) as a result of widespread over-collection of mature individuals, flowers and fruits and loss of plants through clearance of habitat for urban expansion. The use of this species has also resulted in diminished potential for re-establishment because of the removal of flowers and seeds which is further exacerbated by over-grazing by cattle and goats. The percentage reduction is not known with any certainty, but given the levels of usage over much of the range, it seems reasonable to suspect that there has been more than a 20% population reduction over the past 100 years. The species is therefore listed as Near Threatened at it almost qualifies for a threatened listing under criterion A2cd.

If more accurate information were available on the generation length and percentage reduction, it is possible that this species would qualify for listing in a more threatened category. Further research is needed on the use and trade of this species, on the effects of the over-exploitation on subpopulations and on the species generally. There is an urgent need for further research on 1) the rates of population decline due to over-harvesting, 2) the generation length and 3) demographic and age structure as a result of over exploitation.

The Mexican Authorities have taken steps to reduce the impacts of over-collection on the wild population. The species is protected by law, and the candy may only be sold commercially if it is from certified cultivated plants. There is also a commercial substitute available for making similar candy and this may help to reduce the pressure on the wild population.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus recurvus (Mill.) Borg sec. Villaseñor 2016
Ferocactus robustus (Link & Otto) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Food - animal
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, and is distributed in the State of Puebla at elevations between 1,500 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows in xerophyllous scrub, including Izotal (Casas and Caballero 1996). Most of the population occurs on plains.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat is land use change for agriculture and ranching. The species has low germination rates, which is a critical problem for sexual regeneration, and it also has a low number of seeds (Carrillo et al. 2005).
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is narrowly distributed (extent of occurrence approximately 5,000 km<sup>2</sup>), with a small number of subpopulations (locations <10), and is experiencing ongoing decline as a consequence of habitat loss. Hence, it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_fullName: Ferocactus uncinatus (Galeotti ex Pfeiff.) Britton & Rose
checklist_taxon: Ferocactus uncinatus (Galeotti ex Pfeiff.) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
Ferocactus wislizeni (Engelm.) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_common_name: Candy Barrel Cactus; Southwestern Barrel Cactus; Biznaga-barril de Nuevo México; Fishhook Barrel Cactus
iucn_locations: Mexico; Sonora; United States; Chihuahua; New Mexico; Arizona; Texas
iucn_location_codes: MX; MXN-SO; US; MXE-CU; NWM-OO; ARI-OO; TEX-OO
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Construction or structural materials
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in the Mexican states of Sonora and Chihuahua, and in the United States in Arizona, New Mexico and Texas. It occurs at elevations of 200 to 1,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky, gravelly or sandy soils of hills, flats, canyons and alluvial fans in desert or grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is legally and illegally used for landscaping, which can be a local threat. Land use change in the lowlands is also a threat, including urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because there has been extensive loss of lowland habitat within the species range over the last three generations (generation length between 20 to 50 years), resulting in a population decline of at least 30%.

checklist_taxon: Frailea pumila (Lem.) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Argentina; Paraguay; Uruguay; Rio Grande do Sul; Entre Ríos; Brazil

iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder farming; Named species; Trend Unknown/Unrecorded
iucn_doc_range: This species occurrs in Argentina (Entre Rios), Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul), Paraguay (Paraguarí) and Uruguay (Salto, Tacuarembó, Rivera and Artigas), at elevations from 100 to 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops and stony fields in pampa grasslands, a habitat that has a very high potential to be used for agriculture.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats in Brazil are grazing and cattle trampling, agriculture, forestry of Eucalyptus, invasive grasses and fire.
iucn_doc_rationale: Frailea pumila is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is locally abundant, is present in protected areas and is not utilised. However, it should be taken into account that it is exposed to several threats, including farming and agricultural activities, and that close to 40% of the population has disappeared in Brazil, therefore, a regional assessment is necessary.

checklist_taxon: Frailea schilinzkyana (F.Haage) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_synonyms: Echinocactus schilinskyanus F.Haage f. ex K.Schum.
iucn_locations: Misiones; Paraguay; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Brazil; Entre Ríos; Uruguay; Corrientes; Argentina; Rio Grande do Sul
iucn_location_codes: AGE-MI; PY; BO; BR; AGE-ER; UY; AGE-CN; AR; BZS-RS
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Grassland - Temperate
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Agro-industry plantations; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Rio Grande do Sul (Brazil); Entre Rios, Corrientes, and Misiones provinces (Argentina); Paraguarí and Concepción departments (Paraguay); and in Uruguay. It occurs at elevations of up to 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on rocky outcrops and stony fields, a habitat type that has very high potential to be used for agriculture. In Brazil it grows in grasslands. In general, Frailea species have a relatively short lifespan but rapid reproduction.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are trampling by grazing cattle, agriculture, forestry (Eucalyptus plantations), and fire.
iucn_doc_rationale: Frailea schilinzkyana has a relatively small range. It is not abundant and there have been important population reductions (in the range of 45%, and up to 60% in certain range countries) in the last 15 years (generation length is 3-5 years) due to land use change. The species grows in areas that have high potential use for agriculture and deforestation. Hence, the species is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Geohintonia mexicana Glass & W.A.Fitz Maur. sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2013
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Nuevo León, where it occurs at elevations of 1,200 m. It has a very limited range with an extent of occurrence and area of occupancy being about the same - 25 km².
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on gypsum cliffs and hillsides, it is a soil specialist - a gypsophile (Anderson 2001, Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011).
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection is the main threat, however, now it is protected by the local population (Pilbeam and Weightman 2006). The local population is safeguarding the species in order to sell it themselves.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has an extremely small range (extent of occurrence and area of occupancy is about 25 km²). The area is essentially a single location and there is ongoing illegal collection of the species. However, the population is extremely large and the collecting does not seem to be causing population declines at a rate sufficient to qualify for a threat category. Hence the species is listed as Near Threatened because it almost qualifies for a Critically Endangered listing under criterion B1ab(v).

Gymnocalycium amerhauseri H.Till sec. Charles 2009
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, occurring in Córdoba, mostly between 1,450 to 1,600m asl (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: It occurs in the higher regions of the mountains, in grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats, however it can be affected by the occasional fire. A potential threat is tourism development in the area.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is known from only one locality where the population size is not larger than 250 mature individuals. There are no threats affecting it at present, however, the area is developing quickly for tourism and is close to a balneario. It is very likely that the species will be merged with G. erinaceum, but as currently treated, it is considered Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium andreae (Boed.) Backeb. & F.M.Knuth sec. Charles 2009
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in Pampa de la Esquina, Córdoba, at 1,500 to 2,700 m of altitude (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in pockets of soil between rocks high in Sierra Grande, in grasslands. The environment looks like moorland with rocky outcrops (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_threats:
Although this cactus has a limited distribution in an area grazed by animals, it appears to be surviving without obvious damage to its environment (Charles 2009).
Grazing seems to benefit the populations. The species is collected locally, but this does not affect the populations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium andreae has a restricted range, however the population is stable in most of the range and there are no major threats. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium bayrianum H.Till sec. Charles 2009
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the provinces of Tucumán and Salta in Argentina. It is reported to occur between 1,000 and 1,500 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in cracks in red sandstone, in rocky places (Charles 2009), in xerophyllous scrub..
iucn_doc_threats: This plant has a much larger distribution than originally thought and  although there is evidence that goats eat the plants, there are plenty  of healthy individuals (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_rationale: Although there is limited information on this species, it appears to be more widespread than originally thought, is locally abundant and apart from grazing by goats there are no other significant threats. Hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium berchtii Neuhuber sec. Charles 2009
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in San Luis at elevations of 700 to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in chaco forest. The plants grow both within the forest and occasionally out in the open. It grows among or near rocks, particularly in bedrock with vertical strata and where the soil is shallow (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for this species. Even though there is goat grazing in the area, the plants are not affected and they benefit because the grass is kept short and there is not competition for light.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though Gymnocalycium berchtii has a small range (extent of occurrence approximately 1,000 km2) , the subpopulations are stable and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_habitats: It is often buried in crumbling granite, where it can be difficult to find if not in flower or fruit (Charles 2009). It grows in high-altitude grasslands and chaco forest. The species is tolerant to disturbance.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium calochlorum is listed as Least Concern because, even though the range is not particularly wide, it is abundant and there are no major threats.

iucn_locations: Uruguay; Rio Grande do Sul; Brazil; Misiones; Argentina; Paraguay
iucn_location_codes: UY; BZS-RS; BR; AGE-MI; AR; PY
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Intentional use (species is the target); Named species; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul), Uruguay (Melo) and probably in Paraguay. In Argentina it has been registered in Misiones (Apóstoles and probably in Santa Ana). It grows from 100 to 250 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops in pampa grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are grazing and trampling by cattle, agriculture, forestry of Eucalyptus, invasive grasses and fire. It is also collected as an ornamental.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium denudatum has a relatively wide range, however the populations are steadily decreasing. A population decline of 60% has been estimated in Brazil and Uruguay in the last 30 years (generation length 10 years).  This species is listed as Critically Endangered in Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil. There are several threats throughout the species range that have not ceased and are not reversible, and it does not occur within any protected area. This cactus is therefore listed as Endangered.

iucn_doc_rationale: Although Gymnocalycium eurypleurum has a restricted range (extent of occurrence is 9,500 km2), there are no known threats to this species, it seems to be common, and it is present in a protected area. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in Catamarca and La Rioja at elevations of 1,000 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in monte shrubland in steep slopes of low hills, on rough stony sandy hillsides and in steep exposed hillsides in crumbling granite. It often shares its habitat with the northernmost populations of Pyrrhocactus bulbocalyx (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: There has been some collecting near roads over the years, and there is recent evidence suggesting the practice is continuing (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium glaucum is listed as Least Concern because even though it has a restricted range (extent of occurrence is 4,500 km2), it is abundant and the only threat is very localized.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium horstii Buining sec. Charles 2009
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Named species
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Rio Grande do Sul (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations up to 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops in pampa grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats to this species are trampling by cattle grazing, agriculture, forestry of Eucalyptus, invasive grasses, illegal collection, and fire.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium horstii has a relatively wide range (extent of occurrence is 40,000 km2), but the species is scarce and the population severely fragmented. The area where it occurs is under several pressures, such as cattle grazing, agriculture, and illegal collection, which have caused reductions of around 60 percent of the population in the last 10 years. The threats will not cease in the near future. Hence, it is listed as Endangered.

iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in monte shrubland.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium hossei has a relatively wide range, is abundant and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Uruguay, the eastern portion of Entre Rios province, Argentina and the southernmost area of Brazil. It occurs between 0 and 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows at the edges of quartz and rocky soils in pastizal pampeano.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat to this species is deforestation for agriculture and invasion of Eucalyptus (Charles 2009) and, to a lesser extent, livestock
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium hyptiacanthum is listed as Least Concern because it occurs over a large area (extent of occurrence > 160, 000 km2), however special attention needs to be taken because this species occurs in relatively small and fragmented subpopulations and it is threatened in parts of its range by land use change and invasive species and the population is experiencing declines.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium kieslingii O.Ferrari sec. Charles 2009
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in La Rioja and Catamarca, at elevations of 500 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in monte shrubland on mountain foothills, on gentle slopes of crumbling granite usually under bushes (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat in the northern part of the species range is specimen collecting. Potential threats are road improvement, and agriculture destroyed many plants but overall its future looks secure (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium kieslingii is listed as Least Concern because its range is relatively wide, it is abundant, and there are no major threats.

iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium marsoneri is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is locally abundant, and there are no known threats.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium mesopotamicum R.Kiesling sec. Charles 2009
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Corrientes, Argentina (Hunt et al. 2006), and occurs at elevations of approximately 200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows somewhat concealed in grassy places with stones where the soil is shallow (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: There is grazing in the area where this species occurs, however, it is not intensive as it is impossible to grow artificial grassland because the area has many rocky outcrops. Grazing even favours the growth of these plants as it buffers competition for light. The only damage that can be caused by grazing animals is trampling on the plants. Another threat is quarrying, this has happened in the past and one locality was severely affected, however more plants were found in the surrounding grasslands and no more quarrying has taken place.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has been reported from a very small area and even though it might be found in surrounding areas, the area of potential habitat is still small (extent of occurrence 200-500 km<sup>2</sup>). At present there are no serious threats to these plants as the land where it grows is not arable. Hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium mihanovichii (Frič & Gürke) Britton & Rose sec. Charles 2009
iucn_locations: Chaco; Paraguay; Argentina; Formosa; Bolivia, Plurinational States of
iucn_doc_range: According to Hunt et al. (2006) this species occurs in Argentina, Bolivia and Paraguay. Pin and Simon (2004) report it for the Alto Paraguay, Boquerón and Presidente Hayes departments in Paraguay. Charles (2009) reports it from the Chaco Austral in Chaco province, Argentina, north into the Chaco region of Paraguay to the Chaco Boreal. It occurs at elevations from 0 to 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows semi-buried in low, open forest, in scrubs and with diverse short plants of the "chaco seco" (dry chaco; Pin and Simon 2004). It also grows in flat sandy grounds under the protection of bushes and palms (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is livestock grazing and is still localised.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium mihanovichii is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is locally common, occurs in several protected areas and the only known threat is localised.

iucn_doc_range: Pin and Simon (2004) report this species as endemic to Paraguay, occurring in the Paraguarí department. Hunt et al. iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in forests, on mountain tables and hilly areas in rocky outcrops near river courses.
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is threatened by agricultural expansion, grazing and the increased use of the land by the local people (Charles 2009). It is collected for commercial use.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium paraguayense is listed as Vulnerable because it has a relatively restricted range (extent of occurrence is 2,500 km2), there are less than ten locations, and its population is decreasing due to the collection of individuals, and a continuing decline in the quality of its habitat.
