iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in La Rioja at elevations of 1,000 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in monte shrubland in mountainous areas.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium rhodantherum is listed as Least Concern because, even though the range is small, the subpopulations are stable and there are no major threats.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium ritterianum Rausch sec. Charles 2009
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it is found in La Rioja at elevations of 1,500 to 2,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in chaco forest and high-altitude grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium ritterianum is listed as Least Concern because, even though the species range small (extent of occurrence is approximately 700 km2) , it is abundant and there are no major threats.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium robustum R.Kiesling, O.Ferrari & Metzing sec. Charles 2009
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in northern Córdoba and southern Santiago del Estero, at elevations of 300 to 600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on a series of low hills comprising crumbling granitic rock (Charles 2009), in chaco forest. It is tolerant to disturbance.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats. The soil where the species grows is not suitable for agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium robustum is listed as Least Concern because even though the species' range is not wide (extent of occurrence is 3,500 km2) , it is abundant and there are no major threats.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium saglionis (Cels) Britton & Rose sec. Charles 2009
iucn_locations: La Rioja; Tucumán; Salta; Catamarca; San Juan; Argentina
iucn_location_codes: AGW-LR; AGW-TU; AGW-SA; AGW-CA; AGW-SJ; AR
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in the provinces of Jujuy, Salta, Tucumán, Catamarca, La Rioja and San Juan at 500 to 2,600 m asl (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on rocky hills within monte and chaco habitats.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium saglionis is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is locally abundant, is present in several protected areas and there are no major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium striglianum Jeggle ex H.Till sec. Charles 2009
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, widely occurring in Mendoza at elevations of 600 to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in sandy or gravelly loam soil, under dense thorny bushes often among grass (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by the effects of urban expansion and grazing (Charles 2009). Some of the area where this species grows has been converted to vineyards, however, this is not likely to continue.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium striglianum has a wide range (extent of occurrence over 20,000 km2). There are localized threats, but these are not sufficient to trigger a threatened listing. Hence, this species is here listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium uebelmannianum Rausch sec. Demaio & al. 2011
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in La Rioja, at elevations of 2,200 to 2,800 m asl (Hunt et al.  2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in shallow rocky soils in grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats. One potential threat could be collection, however the sites are difficult to access.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium uebelmannianum is listed as Least Concern because it has a relatively wide range, is abundant, and there are no major threats.

checklist_taxon: Haageocereus acranthus (Vaupel) Backeb. sec. Kew 2019
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This Peruvian endemic is only found in northern Ica and Lima (Hunt et al. 2006), where it grows at altitudes between 200 to 2,700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It can be found in desert matorral and inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: Human sprawl and agriculture are affecting the species near the city of Lima (i.e. in the surrounding valleys), but this is not considered to be a major threat as this cactus is also present in more distant areas.
iucn_doc_rationale: Haageocereus acranthus has a wide range, is common, and is not exposed to any major threat. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Haageocereus australis Backeb. sec. Zuloaga & al. 2019
Haageocereus decumbens (Vaupel) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: The species is distributed in Arequipa, Peru and north of Chile, in Arica, at elevations of  200 to 700 m asl
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in coastal hills (lomas costeras) on rocky and sandy soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats known for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Haageocereus decumbens is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is relatively abundant, and it has no major threats.

checklist_taxon: Haageocereus fascicularis (Meyen) F.Ritter sec. Zuloaga & al. 2019
Haageocereus limensis (Salm-Dyck) F.Ritter, nom. inval., sec. TROPICOS 1986+
Haageocereus platinospinus (Werderm. & Backeb.) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru, where it can be found in the regions of Arequipa (Aplao in Castilla province, El Pedregal in Caylloma province), Moquegua (Puquina in Sanchez Cerro province) and Tacna (Tarata province; Arakaki et al. 2006). It has also been observed in Ica (C. Ostolaza pers. comm. 2011). It occurs at altitudes of 800 to 2,650 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It can be found in desert matorral and inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: In Arequipa, the species is affected by the extraction of volcanic stones. Mining activities are also affecting another subpopulation (mina Cerro Verde).
iucn_doc_rationale: Haageocereus platinospinus has a wide distribution and is abundant. Although it has conservation problems in the Arequipa region due to mining activities, its global population does not seem to be threatened for now. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Haageocereus tenuis F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru, where it can be found in the Lima region at altitudes of 200 to 250 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: H. tenuis occupies arid coastal habitats (lomas costeras) (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is severely threatened, as part of the population occurs near a large road where it is exposed to contamination (both through littering and air pollution). A poultry farm nearby emits air pollution, both feathers and small particles, that is also affecting the cacti. A large proportion of this subpopulation has died over the past years, but the exact reason (i.e., how the contamination kills it) is unknown. The remainder of the population lies on a hill and is thus a bit farther away from the road. This part of the population seems to be relatively safe from pollution. In the 2004 study by Novoa et al. (2010) nearly 60% of dead branches were found in H. tenuis; the patches near the road had a significantly lower density and more dead branches than the patches that are located more than 400 m away from the road.
iucn_doc_rationale: Haageocereus tenuis has an extremely restricted range of only a few square kilometers. Part of the population is located near a road and a poultry farm; both seem to have severely affected the population through contamination, although it is not clear how this has led to the observed die-off of a large proportion of this subpopulation. It is estimated that at least 70% of the original population has disappeared over the past 10 years, and as this impact has not ceased and is not reversible, an estimated 70% of the population will be gone in 10 years or so. In 2004 the entire population consisted of only 500 individuals, but there are fewer individuals now. The species thus qualifies for listing as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Haageocereus versicolor (Werderm. & Backeb.) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Desert - Cold
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru, where it occurs in the Lambayeque, Piura and Tumbes regions at altitudes of 0 to 1,500 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in the desert and thorny dry matorral.
iucn_doc_threats: Currently there are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Haageocereus versicolor has a wide range, is abundant, and is not exposed to any threats. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.

Harrisia gracilis (Mill.) Britton sec. Franck 2016
iucn_locations: Haiti; Bahamas; Honduras; Jamaica; Florida; Dominican Republic; United States; Cayman Islands; Puerto Rico

iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands
iucn_threat_names: Other impacts; Agro-industry farming; Storms & flooding; Housing & urban areas; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: Harrisia gracilis occurs in the Bahamas, Cayman Islands, Dominican Republic, Haiti, Jamaica, Puerto Rico, in the United States of America (Florida) (Hunt et al. 2006), and in Honduras (the Swan Islands) (A. Franck pers. comm. 2011). Its elevation is estimated between sea level and 40 m asl (K. Bradley and A. Franck pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_habitats: Harrisia gracilis in Florida grows in Florida hammocks, pune rocklands and coastal strand. There is no current information available about the habitat and ecology of this species in other parts of its range.
iucn_doc_threats: Sea level rise due to climate change is a potential threat to this species. Tropical cyclones affected two of the three Florida populations in 2007 (K. Bradley pers. comm. 2011). Throughout its range, this species is affected by habitat loss due to urbanization and agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: Harrisia gracilis is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread, occurring throughout much of the Caribbean and into the southeastern United States. The widespread status of this species is based on current taxonomic treatments. However, if future taxonomic work treats this taxon as multiple species, it will need to be re-assessed as some of the split species may be threatened. Most of the information on threats and population status of this species is from occurrences in the United States, where this taxon is recognized as four distinct species, some of which are considered threatened. There is very little information on the populations and threats of Harrisia gracilis outside of the United States.

checklist_taxon: Harrisia martinii (Labour.) Britton sec. Franck 2016
iucn_locations: Argentina; Paraguay; Santa Fé; Uruguay; Formosa; Entre Ríos; Chaco; Bolivia, Plurinational States of
iucn_location_codes: AR; PY; AGE-SF; UY; AGE-FO; AGE-ER; AGE-CH; BO
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Argentina, in the provinces of Chaco, Entre Ríos, Formosa and Santa Fe, as well as in Paraguay (Hunt et al. 2006). Pin and Simon (2004) cite it for the Central, Concepción, Cordillera, Paraguarí, and Presidente Hayes departments of Paraguay. It is also found in Uruguay between Bella Unión and Paysandú, and in southern Bolivia. It occurs at elevations between 10 and 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in hilly areas among shrubs, and in forests and forest clearings. It is favored by anthropogenic activities.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats affecting this cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Harrisia martinii is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, is abundant throughout its range, and there are no major threats affecting it. It occurs in several protected areas.

iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Argentina, Bolivia, Uruguay and in Paraguay in the Alto Paraguay, Boquerón, Central, Cordillera, Paraguarí, and Presidente Hayes departments (Pin and Simon 2004). It grows at elevations between 10 and 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in the Chaco Region of Argentina, Paraguay, and Bolivia. It also occurs in Uruguay. It grows in the sotobosque, in the borders, as well as inside the Dry Chaco forest of the western region (Pin and Simon 2004). It grows in clearings within the Chacoan province, and below shrubs, mostly "jarillas" (Larrea spp.), in the Monte province (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species. It is favored by livestock grazing.
iucn_doc_rationale: Harrisia pomanensis is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, is abundant throughout its range, and there are no major threats affecting it. It occurs in many protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Harrisia regelii (Weing.) Borg sec. Franck 2016
iucn_locations: Santa Fé; Entre Ríos; Paraguay; Uruguay; Argentina; Chaco; Corrientes; Formosa
iucn_location_codes: AGE-SF; AGE-ER; PY; UY; AR; AGE-CH; AGE-CN; AGE-FO
iucn_doc_range: This species was considered endemic to Argentina, but recent collecting surveys found it in Uruguay (I. Grela pers. comm. 2010). In Argentina it occurs in Entre Rios, Chaco, Corrientes, Formosa, and Santa Fe. It may also occur in Paraguay. It is found at elevations between 100 and 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows at the edges and in the interior of forests, as well as on roadsides.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Harrisia regelii  is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread, common, and there are no major threats affecting it. It is present in several protected areas.

iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in Chuquisaca, Cochabamba and Santa Cruz at altitudes between 1,100 and 3,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in sparse forests within seasonally dry inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: Clearance for subsistence farming and trampling by goats are affecting this species, although both are considered to have a low impact.
iucn_doc_rationale: Harrisia tetracantha is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is common within its area of distribution, and is not exposed to any major threat.

checklist_taxon: Hatiora cylindrica Britton & Rose sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Rio de Janeiro; Bahia; Espírito Santo; Minas Gerais; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Coastal Sand Dunes; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder plantations; Tourism & recreation areas; Small-holder farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Brazil in Rio de Janeiro, Bahia, southwestern Minas Gerais and possibly in Espírito Santo. It grows from sea level to 1,200 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004). It is known from five, possibly six places.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in humid forest, epiphytic and terrestrial (in sand dunes and probably as a lithophyte), restinga, mata de brejo and mata atlântica (Taylor and Zappi 2004). It can also be found on coastal rocks and dunes.
iucn_doc_threats: Bahian and doubtless other localities are declining due to growth in tourism and deforestation for expanding agriculture and urbanization, but may be included in protected areas (Taylor and Zappi 2004). There is also deforestation due to land use change for Eucalyptus plantations. Of the five localities where it occurs only one has any security, all the others are under threat and one may have gone completely.
iucn_doc_rationale: Hatiora cylindrica has a small range (area of occupancy is less than 500 km2), is known from five widely separated sites (locations), and is severely fragmented. Only one of the sites is safe; all the others are under severe threat, and at least one has possibly been lost. Hence, the species is listed as Endangered under criterion B2.

checklist_fullName: Hatiora herminiae (Porto & A.Cast.) Backeb. ex Barthlott
checklist_taxon: Hatiora herminiae (Porto & A.Cast.) Backeb. ex Barthlott sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil occurring in Minas Gerais and Sao Paulo, at 1,500 to 2,000 m asl (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows as an epiphyte on Araucaria in cloud forest (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat is collection by people - it has beautiful flowers so people are attracted to it.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has an extent of occurrence of approximately 500 km<sup>2</sup>, it is known from two locations and there is continuing decline in mature individuals due to collection, hence is listed as Endangered.

iucn_doc_range: This cactus is common in southeastern and southern Brazil, southeastern Paraguay and northeastern Argentina. In Brazil, it has been recorded from eastern Pernambuco to southeastern Minas Gerais. It occurs from sea level to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a disjunct humid forest element: it grows as an epiphyte or lithophyte in the Atlantic forest, including mata de brejo (NE Brazil) and restinga. It is an extremely variable species, with individuals from different parts of the range looking very different from each other.
iucn_doc_threats: In the north, in the Atlantic forest, there is considerable habitat loss due to deforestation for expanding agriculture (sugar cane) and expanding urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although there has been decline in the northern part of its range, Lepismium cruciforme is still very widespread and sufficiently common. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_habitats: This is a southern humid forest element, it grows as an epiphyte in the Atlantic forest, including mata de neblina (Taylor and Zappi 2004).

iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Argentina, in the provinces of Buenos Aires, Catamarca, Chaco, Corrientes, Entre Ríos, Formosa, Jujuy, Misiones, Salta, Santiago de Estero and Tucumán. In Bolivia it can be found in Beni, La Paz and Santa Cruz. In Brazil it grows in Paraná, Rio Grande do Sul, Santa Catarina and São Paulo. It also occurs in Paraguay and Uruguay.  It can be found at altitudes up to 1,900 m asl (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in seasonal forest (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows as an epiphyte, a bit like ivy - it clings to the trees.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Lepismium lumbricoides is an extremely widespread and abundant species and is hence listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Lepismium warmingianum (K.Schum.) Barthlott sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is common in southeastern and southern Brazil (central-southern Minas Gerais and Domingos Martins in southern Espírito Santo), eastern Paraguay, and northeastern Argentina (Misiones province). It occurs at altitudes between 40 and 700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This southern humid forest element lives as an epiphyte or lithophyte in the Mata Atlântica.
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is threatened by habitat loss in the northern parts of its range, and it is probable that it has vanished from many of the northern localities where it had been collected in the past. The main threats are human sprawl, expanding agricultural areas, and general deforestation.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although there has been considerable habitat loss in the northern parts of its range, Lepismium warmingianum is still sufficiently widespread and common in the southern part of its range to maintain its listing as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Leptocereus arboreus Britton & Rose sec. Barrios & al. 2020
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Marine Coastal/supratidal - Coastal Caves/Karst; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Scale Unknown/Unrecorded; Small-holder farming; Housing & urban areas; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Cuba, found south of Cienfuegos and Sancti Spiritus (Gonzalez-Torres 2007). The extent of occurrence is estimated to be around 2,000 km<sup>2</sup>. The species occurs at elevations around 50m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Leptocereus arboreus occurs in coastal thickets or dry forests on limestone shallow rocky soils (Gonzalez-Torres 2007). It is treelike and forms whitish bell-shaped flowers (Anderson 2001) that are bat pollinated (Fleming et al. 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats to this species are agriculture, forestry, fires and urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has a restricted range (extent of occurrence is 2,000 km<sup>2</sup>), it occurs at a few locations (no more than five) and there is continuing decline in the extent and quality of the habitat due to anthropogenic activities.

checklist_fullName: Leptocereus assurgens (C.Wright ex Griseb.) Britton & Rose
checklist_taxon: Leptocereus assurgens (C.Wright ex Griseb.) Britton & Rose sec. Barrios & al. 2020
iucn_doc_range: Leptocereus assurgens is endemic to western Cuba, where it is found on top of limestone hills in Pinar del Rio Province (Gonzalez-Torres 2007). It occurs at elevations of up to 280 m asl. The estimated extent of occurrence is around 800 km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on limestone hills (Gonzalez-Torres 2007) in shrublands.
iucn_doc_threats: The area where it occurs is not easily accessed, therefore it is unlikely that there are major threats affecting it.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Leptocereus assurgens has a restricted range (its extent of occurrence is approximately 800 km2), it is listed as Least Concern because there are no known major threats affecting it. However, further research is needed to determine its population status and trends.

checklist_taxon: Leptocereus carinatus Areces sec. Barrios & al. 2020
iucn_threat_names: Scale Unknown/Unrecorded; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: Leptocereus carinatus is endemic to Sierra del Chorrillo, Najasa, Camaguey in Cuba (Hunt et al. 2006, Gonzalez-Torres 2007). The elevational range for this species is 200 to 250 m asl. The area of occupancy is estimated to be 6 km2 (measured as the area of the hills where it occurs).
iucn_doc_habitats: The habitat where this species occurs is a low limestone hill that supports semi-deciduous forest (Gonzalez-Torres 2007). Leptocereus carinatus flowers nocturnally (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is habitat degradation from fires, agriculture, and livestock (Rifa et al. 2005).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Critically Endangered because it occurs at only one location with an area of occupancy of around 6km<sup>2</sup>, it has a small population size, and there is continuing decline in the area of occupancy and the extent and quality of the habitat due to anthropogenic activities.

checklist_taxon: Leptocereus ekmanii (Werderm.) F.M.Knuth sec. Barrios & al. 2020
Leptocereus leonii Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range:
Leptocereus leonii is endemic to Sierra de Anafe and Sierra del Esperón, Cuba (Hunt et al. 2006, González-Torres 2007). It occurs at elevations up to 200 m asl. The identity of the plants referred to as this taxon from Cayman Brac is doubtful (N. Taylor pers. comm. 2011). The area of occupancy is estimated to be 8 km2 (measured as the area of the hills where it occurs).
This plant was first collected in the early 1990's and it has not been seen since (L.R. Gonzalez-Torres and D. Barrios pers. comm. 2012).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in semi-deciduous forest on low limestone hills (Gonzalez-Torres 2007).
iucn_doc_threats: Leptocereus leonii is affected by limestone quarrying in the haystack karstic mountains of western Cuba (Oldfield 1997, L.R. Gonzalez-Torres and D. Barrios pers. comm. 2012). The area has also suffered from deforestation and is now fragmented and degraded (L.R. Gonzalez-Torres and D. Barrios pers. comm. 2012).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species was last seen in the 1990's in the small area where it was originally reported from. This area has a size of approximately 8 km<sup>2</sup> and is affected by quarrying. It is uncertain whether the species has gone extinct in the wild, and it is therefore listed as Critically Endangered. It is strongly advised that research is conducted in order to determine whether the species is still present in the area, and if so its population size and trends.

checklist_taxon: Leptocereus maxonii Britton & Rose sec. Barrios & al. 2020

checklist_fullName: Leptocereus nudiflorus (Engelm. ex C.Wright) D.Barrios & S.Arias
checklist_taxon: Leptocereus nudiflorus (Engelm. ex C.Wright) D.Barrios & S.Arias sec. Barrios & al. 2020
Leptocereus paniculatus (Lam.) D.R.Hunt sec. Barrios & al. 2020
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Mining & quarrying
iucn_doc_range: Leptocereus paniculatus is found in the south west of the Dominican Republic, where it can often be seen from main roads, growing in stony, degraded soils (www.cactuspro.com retrieved 2010) and in Haiti (Hunt et al. 2006). The lower elevation is around 2 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in dry forests and shrublands.
iucn_doc_threats: There are several extractive activities in the area where the species occurs (e.g., quarrying for limestone, grazing).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because it is estimated that the single existing population has less than 10,000 mature individuals and is continuously declining.

checklist_taxon: Leptocereus prostratus Britton & Rose sec. Barrios & al. 2020
Leptocereus quadricostatus (Bello) Britton & Rose sec. Barrios & al. 2020
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Named species; Storms & flooding; Housing & urban areas; Roads & railroads; Habitat shifting & alteration
iucn_doc_range: Leptocereus quadricostatus is found in Puerto Rico and in the British Virgin Islands, on the island of Anegada (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at or near sea level. There are two locations of this species in Puerto Rico (one of which is a small area in the dry southwest region of Guánica) and one in Anegada. The Anegada subpopulation is restricted to an area of less than 100 m x 100 m on one of the limestone cays of the Western Salt Ponds.
iucn_doc_habitats: An erect or arching shrub found in coastal dry forests and shrubland. The Anegada subpopulation occurs on open limestone with very little soil. No study of the breeding biology has been undertaken, but individuals have been seen in flower and fruit on Anegada.
iucn_doc_threats:
The main threats to this species are invasive grasses, fire, and tourism development.
Anegada is under severe development pressure resulting in both loss of habitat to residential and tourism infrastructure, and further fragmentation is expected due to upgrading and construction of new roads. However, the location of L. quadricostatus is in an area unlikely to be damaged from this type of development.

The highest point on Anegada is approx. 10 m above sea level. Most of the habitat for L. quadricostatus is <2 m above sea level and so global climate change will reduce the quality and area of habitat available to L. quadricostatus. Natural disasters are a current and on-going threat e.g., hurricanes, coastal inundation and earthquakes.
The Puerto Rican subpopulations are in areas zoned for residential development which may threaten the surviving plants.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has a restricted range (extent of occurrence is approximately 4,400 km<sup>2</sup>), it is known from only a few locations (less than five), and there is a continuing decline in the extent and quality of its habitat due to invasive grasses, fire, and urban development. Surveys are required to determine the population size; if there are found to be fewer than 50 mature individuals as in 2003, it would once again qualify for listing as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Leptocereus scopulophilus Areces sec. Barrios & al. 2020
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Cuba, where it was found on limestone and rocky summits of Somorrostra hill, 25 km southeast of La Habana (Areces-Mallea 1993, Hunt et al. 2006). This site was surveyed in 2006 and the species does not occur there any more (Gonzalez-Torres 2007). The current known distribution is on the Pan de Matanzas Hill in Matanzas Province (Gonzalez-Torres 2007). The area of occupancy of this species is estimated to be 3,500 m2 (L.R. Gonzalez-Torres and D. Barrios pers. comm. 2012).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs on the southeastern slopes of the Pan de Matanzas Hill on karstic soils in the undergrowth of semi-deciduous tropical forest (Gonzalez-Torres et al. 2007).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats to this species are the exploitation of forest resources and mining for limestone.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Critically Endangered because the only remaining area in which it is found is less than 4km<sup>2</sup> and there is a continuing decline in the extent and quality of its habitat. Part of the population has already been extirpated (the type locality has disappeared due to mining activities), and there is a plausible threat from deforestation or mining for limestone where the species still occurs, which could partially or completely eliminate the last remaining population.

checklist_taxon: Leptocereus sylvestris Britton & Rose sec. Barrios & al. 2020
iucn_threat_names: Trend Unknown/Unrecorded; Housing & urban areas; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to southeastern Cuba, where it is found around Manzanillo, Holguin and Santiago de Cuba. It occurs at elevations between sea level and 100 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in coastal dry tropical forests.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is the deforestation for small scale timber, agriculture and tourist development. Fires may also be a problem in the area.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Near Threatened because it is rare and there is continuing decline in the extent and quality of the habitat where this species may occur. Further information is needed to better determine its actual distribution and the status and trend of its population so that a determination of threat levels can be made. It has been reported from only a relatively small protected area.

checklist_taxon: Leptocereus wrightii León sec. Barrios & al. 2020
iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/supratidal - Coastal Caves/Karst; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Unspecified species; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: Leptocereus wrightii is endemic to Cuba and is known from 10 plants on the outskirts of La Habana. The only previously known population disappeared during the development of the neighborhood of Villa Panamerica (Palmarola 2009). The species now occurs in a single location and its estimated area of occupancy is 4 km2 (a single grid cell of 2 x 2 km).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in karstic rocks in the transition zone between the coastal thicket and the remains of a dry tropical forest (Gonzalez-Torres et al. 2009). Leptocereus wrightii is bat pollinated (Fleming et al. 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats to this species are fire, damage by herbivores, and urban development (Gonzalez-Torres et al. 2007).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Critically Endangered because it is known from one site (estimated area of occupancy is 4 km<sup>2</sup>) in the outskirts of Havana with only ten adult plants, and its population is continuously declining given that there is no regeneration of plants. Unless the natural habitat is restored, it is unlikely that the species will recover.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs throughout much of the semi desert in the states of Coahuila, Durango, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas, and Zacatecas. It is found from 1,500 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in semi desert on calcareous soil. Usually the plants occur singly, often growing in clumps of Agave or Hechtia.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by illegal collection and cattle and goat grazing in its habitat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Leuchtenbergia principis is listed as Least Concern due to its extensive range and large number of individuals. Although there are threats to certain subpopulations they are not causing sufficient declines to qualify for a threatened category.

Lophophora diffusa (Croizat) Bravo sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_synonyms: Lophophora echinata Croizat var. diffusa Croizat
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Food - human; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range: This cactus grows in an area of about 300 km² in a valley north of Vizarrón in Querétaro, Mexico, at elevations of 1,300 to 1,600 m asl. A subpopulation existed near Mesa de León (Cadereyta, 1,800 m asl) but it has been almost completely extirpated in the last 20 years; a few conglomerates still exist where the soil is deep enough to allow the tuberous root to grow (E. Sánchez pers. comm. 2010). This same area used to harbour the southernmost range of Creosote bush (Larrea tridentata) in the northern hemisphere.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in semi-desert, on calcareous soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by illegal collecting and use by indigenous people for medicine, and by collectors for ornamental trade. There is an ongoing population decline in the vicinity of highways that pass through the range due to collectors, who also sell the plant locally as a narcotic. This species is illegal to keep in cultivation, which results in increasing demand and extraction of specimens from the wild.
iucn_doc_rationale: Lophophora diffusa is considered Vulnerable due its very limited range (extent of occurrence 300 km²), its occurrence in less than ten locations, its small population size (less than 3,000 individuals), and an ongoing decline in mature individuals due to illegal collecting. There has been a decline of at least 30% in the last 20 years, and three generations is estimated to be approximately 10-15 years.

checklist_taxon: Lophophora fricii Haberm. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Coahuila (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats:
A button-like geophytic cactus found growing in two very different habitats, on plains with xerophyllous scrub, mesquite and agave, and on limestone slopes and mountain tops.

Genetic studies are in progress for Lophophora fricii (M. Terry pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collecting for producing ointments poses a threat to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a very limited range, however, there is not enough information on the scope of the threats and population trends to assess its status properly, hence it is listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Lophophora williamsii (Lem. ex Salm-Dyck) J.M.Coult. sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_locations: Nuevo León; United States; San Luis Potosí; Tamaulipas; Zacatecas; Coahuila; Texas; Mexico; Chihuahua; Durango
iucn_location_codes: MXE-NL; US; MXE-SL; MXE-TA; MXE-ZA; MXE-CO; TEX-OO; MX; MXE-CU; MXE-DU
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Other (free text); Pets/display animals, horticulture; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range: The species is distributed in the United States in Texas, and in Mexico in the states of   Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas and Zacatecas. It has a wide altitudinal range, being recorded from 100 up to 1,900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This geophytic, button-like cactus species occurs in xerophyllous scrub including Tamaulipan thorn scrub. It grows on calcareous soils.
iucn_doc_threats:
Illegal collecting and land use change affect several subpopulations. Commercial harvesting by religious groups in the U.S has drastically decimated subpopulations and the plant has become less available through time because often the whole plant rather than the edible part is removed. In addition harvesting is excessive (Terry et al. 2011). Land use change for agriculture is a significant threat, as the land is completely ploughed, thus eliminating all vegetation including L. williamsii and its seed bank.

The plant is used to make ointments which are produce at a large scale and are widely available in Mexico, studies on the composition and pharmacology of such ointments are under-way (M. Terry pers. comm. 2013).
iucn_doc_rationale:
Many subpopulations of Lophophora williamsii are heavily harvested in the wild throughout the range of the species, some to the point of extirpation, providing evidence that at least 30% of the population will be lost. This reduction is very likely to be irreversible given that whole individuals are harvested and population regeneration is thus unlikely. Even though the species has a very large range, land use change is a significant threat for some subpopulations and the levels of exploitation are not likely to stop in the near future. As a precautionary measure, the species is listed as Vulnerable under criterion A4acd (three generations is 150 years).

According to expert W.A.Fitz-Maurice (pers. comm. 2009) the species should be listed as Least Concern because of the large number of mature individuals. In Mexico, collection is illegal and people are punished for collecting it. Because of its hallucinogenic properties this species is classed as a drug. However, the plant is used to make ointments at an industrial scale, which are sold widely in Mexico and this trade is not regulated and the ointment is not classed a drug.

iucn_doc_habitats: It grows in dry exposed places in grassland habitats (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: No threats exist to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Maihuenia patagonica is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is locally abundant, is present in several protected areas and there are no major threats affecting it.

iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Argentina in Neuquén and in Chile in Bío-Bío, La Araucania, Maule and O´Higgins (Hunt et al. 2006), approximately from 500 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: In the south part of its distribution this species is associated with Araucaria forest in relatively humid zones in the border of the subantarctic province (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009). In Chile, this species grows in volcanic soils.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is affected by land use change for pine plantations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Maihuenia poeppigii is widespread, locally abundant, and in the majority of its range there are no threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Argentina, where it is widespread in Mendoza, Neuquén, Río Negro, Chubut, Buenos Aires and Santa Cruz. It also grows in Chile in Aisén region. It can be found at elevations of 0 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in monte shrubland and in the Patagonian grasslands on rocky soils. It is very well adapted to the cold climate (Macaya and Bustamante 2005)
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Maihueniopsis darwinii is widespread, abundant, there are no major threats affecting the species, and it occurs within protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Chiapas, Colima, Guerrero, Oaxaca and Puebla. It grows at altitudes between 500 and 2,200 m asl (Pilbeam 1999, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in tropical deciduous and temperate forest on karstic soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats, however in some areas habitat destruction for cattle ranching and small-scale farming is having an impact on the subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria albilanata is listed as Least Concern due to a wide distribution, it is sometimes locally abundant, and it is not declining at nearly the rate sufficient to qualify for a threatened category. However, there is an isolated subpopulation (M. albilanata reppenhagenii) that is currently in decline due to habitat loss (cattle ranching and goats), and upon further taxonomic revision it could be raised to the species level and should be re-evaluated.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Tamaulipas, Mexico. It occurs at an elevation of 820 m asl on steep volcanic rock face in an area of 2,000 m<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is a microendemic. It is found on steep volcanic rock in semi-desert.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by illegal collecting because of its very small population size. Its location is now well known to both commercial and amateur collectors and is protected only by the difficulty of access.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria anniana is listed as Critically Endangered under criterion B due to its occurrence in a single location, its extremely small  range (20 x 100 m), and a continuing population decline as a result of illegal collecting. In the 2002 assessment of the species and  80% decline was estimated (Fitz Maurice 1993), however, the population has not been visited since 1992 and surveys are needed. In addition, according to W.A. Fitz-Maurice's observations in 1992, this species had a small population consisting of less than 500 plants.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria backebergiana F.G.Buchenau sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in Mexico State and Guerrero at around 1,900 m (Pilbeam 1999, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows hanging on cliffs (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: Threats to this species are poorly understood.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Data Deficient as very little is known about its ecology and threats, and therefore is not possible to evaluate its conservation status.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria beneckei Ehrenb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Sinaloa; Jalisco; Oaxaca; Colima; Guerrero; Michoacán; Nayarit; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXN-SI; MXS-JA; MXS-OA; MXS-CL; MXS-GR; MXS-MI; MXS-NA; MX
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This is a widespread species in the south of Mexico. It has been reported for the states of Colima, Guerrero, Jalisco, Michoacán, Nayarit, Oaxaca and Sinaloa (Pilbeam 1999), where it grows at elevations of 50 to 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in dry deciduous forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The building of dams in the State of Jalisco is resulting in the drowning of some subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although there are localized threats in parts of its range, Mammillaria beneckei is a very widespread species that is very abundant. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria bocasana Poselg. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Small-holder farming; Intentional use (species is the target); Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of San Luis Potosí and Zacatecas at elevations around 1,800 m asl (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found among volcanic rock in semi-desert.
iucn_doc_threats: There is some illegal collecting by tour groups and individuals. Increased agricultural use and housing expansion pose a small threat. The plants are collected for sale in the local market for nativity scenes.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria bocasana is listed as Least Concern because its large subpopulations are found over a relatively large area and current levels of decline are relatively small.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria bombycina Quehl sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Temperate; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, and is known to occur in the states of Aguascalientes and Jalisco (Pilbeam 1999). The area covers around 16 x 10 km. It occurs from 2,340-2,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It occurs in high mountains, in Quercus forests on steep slopes and grows in leaf-litter and sometimes on inaccesable cliffs (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_threats: In the past illegal collection was a major threat (Pilbeam 1999), however, today this species is widely available from propagation and the pressure in wild populations is minimum.
iucn_doc_rationale: When this species was first found in 1988, it used to be under great pressure from illegal collection. An estimated population reduction of 80% in a ten year period was inferred. However, it is now available in the market from propagation and this has reduced the pressure on wild populations. Furthermore, other subpopulations have since been found. Nonetheless, a past reduction in the population of around 30% (generation length five years) due to illegal collection is estimated. Even though the species has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence =160 km<sup>2</sup>, one location) it is listed as Vulnerable because there is no continuing decline of the population.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria candida Scheidw. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003 (sub Mammilloydia candida)
Mammillaria carmenae Castañeda sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Tamaulipas, where it occurs at elevations of 850 to 1,900 m asl (Pilbeam 1999, Hunt et al. 2006). It is known from a single locality (Martínez-Ávalos 1998, Hernández et al. 2010).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in pine forest, in the crevices of rocks on north facing landslips (Martínez-Avalos 1998, Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal trade is the main threat to this species (Bárcenas 2003).
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria carmenae has a very small range, the extent of occurrence is less than 100 km². The two localities can be treated as a single location, as the main threat is illegal collection which affects both areas. There is a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals as well as the extent of occurrence and area of occupancy due to illegal collection. Hence, the species is listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria carnea Zucc. ex Pfeiff. sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2015
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This widespread species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the States of Guerrero, Oaxaca and Puebla (Pilbeam 1999). It grows at elevations between 500 and 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows in association with nurse plants (Rodríguez-Ortega and Ezcurra 2001). It occurs in tropical deciduous forest and xerophyllous scrub, tetechera, cardonal, mezquital, and in the margins of orchards (Arias et al. 1997, Paredes-Flores et al. 2007).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no direct threats for the species, however, land use change due to agriculture affects part of its range.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria carnea is widespread, abundant, and although there are threats in places and some sub-populations are declining, these are not sufficient to warrant any concern. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria crinita DC. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Querétaro; Jalisco; Michoacán; Aguascalientes; Hidalgo; Guanajuato; San Luis Potosí; Zacatecas; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-QU; MXS-JA; MXS-MI; MXE-AG; MXE-HI; MXE-GU; MXE-SL; MXE-ZA; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Small-holder farming; Intentional use (species is the target); Housing & urban areas; Temperature extremes
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Aguascalientes, Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, Michoacán, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, and Zacatecas at elevations of 1,400 to 2,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on volcanic rocks and soil in xerophyllous scrub, open oak (Quercus), and low desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: The severity of threats varies with the forms and locations. Certain subpopulations and forms are highly threatened by illegal collection and a quarry in the case of Mammillaria crinita tezontle. Development for agriculture and urbanization impacts some subpopulations, but does not represent a major threat to the overall population. Temperature extremes such as the 100-year freeze on the Altiplano in December 1997 present a serious but unpredictable threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria crinita is listed as Least Concern due to its large population, extensive subpopulations, and widespread nature with little evidence of decline from human activities. The distinctive form of M. crinita tezontle is highly threatened and requires site level conservation of its very limited range.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria decipiens Scheidw. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Hidalgo; Querétaro; Guanajuato; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in Guanajuato, San Luis Potosí, Querétaro and possibly in Hidalgo, at elevations of 1,550 to 2,150 m asl (Pilbeam 1999, Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species lives in canyons, generally at lower elevations, in volcanic soils in matorral.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species, however some subpopulations are threatened by land use change and flooding (degradation of arroyo habitat).
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria decipiens is listed as Least Concern due to its relatively widespread and abundant subpopulations.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria dixanthocentron Backeb. ex Mottram sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in northwestern Oaxaca and southern Puebla at elevations of 640 to 2,500 m asl (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in dry tropical deciduous forest and Quercus-Pinus forest (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat fragmentation is a major threat.<span lang="EN-GB"> The range of this species has been predicted to be reduced by up to 50% under climate change scenarios in which temperature was increased by 2°C and the precipitation decreased by 15% with respect to current climate conditions (approximately between year 2060 and year 2100; Téllez-Valdés and Dávila-Aranda 2003).
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria dixanthocentron is very abundant, there are no major direct threats for the species, and there is no evidence of population decline. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria duoformis R.T.Craig & E.Y.Dawson sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Morelos, Puebla and Oaxaca at elevations of 1,000 to 2,400 m asl (Arias et al. 1997; Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs among rather thick vegetation (Pilbeam 1999), in tropical deciduous forest and secondary grasslands (Arias et al. 1997). It flowers between April and May (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: There are probably no direct threats for the species. However, habitat degradation is likely to affect it.
iucn_doc_rationale: There is not enough information to establish the conservation status of Mammilaria duoformis.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria duwei Rogoz. & P.J.Braun sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is found at one location northeast of the city of Guanajuato, Mexico, at 1,800 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found on volcanic rock in xerophyllous scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by illegal collection and land development.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria duwei is listed as Critically Endangered due to a very limited range (extent of occurrence and area of occupancy 35 km2), it is known from only one location, has a small population size, and there is a continuing decline due to overcollection and development. Estimates of population size and range are, however, dubious for this taxon as the description does not reliably differentiate it from M. crinita.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria elongata DC. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Mining & quarrying; Shifting agriculture; Nomadic grazing; Small-holder farming; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Guanajuato, Hidalgo and Querétaro at about 1,300 to 2,300 m asl (Pilbeam 1999, Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus can be found in calcareous soils in matorral.
iucn_doc_threats: Mining, land use conversion for agriculture, and habitat degradation due to grazing of goats are impacting some subpopulations of this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria elongata is listed as Least Concern due to its relatively high abundance in its limited range, and because it is not currently declining fast enough to qualify for a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria erythrosperma Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to San Luis Potosí, Mexico, where it occurs in the Sierra Alvarez at elevations of 2,000 to 2,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found among calcareous rocks on sloping terrain in semi-desert and oak forest.
iucn_doc_threats: These plants face some threats from illegal collecting, both at commercial and amateur level.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria erythrosperma is listed as Least Concern because even though it is illegally collected, it has many subpopulations over a relatively large area (extent of occurrence is 2,500 km2).

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria fittkaui Glass & R.A.Foster sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Temperate; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Guanajuato and Jalisco, at an elevation of approximately 1,800 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows on rocks or on hills (Pilbeam 1999). It occurs in subtropical dry forests and oak forests.
iucn_doc_threats: Populations are isolated and there is some collection for ornamental use - but there are no major threats to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though the range of Mammillaria fittkaui is relatively narrow, the subpopulations are quite isolated and there are no major threats affecting them. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria formosa Galeotti ex Scheidw. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Tamaulipas; Zacatecas; Coahuila; Nuevo León; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-SL; MXE-TA; MXE-ZA; MXE-CO; MXE-NL; MX
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This is a wide-ranging species endemic to Mexico. It can be found in the states of Coahuila, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas and Zacatecas (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows under bushes in xerophytic shrubland, on hills and plains.
iucn_doc_threats: Overgrazing of the habitat by goats is the only threat, but this is not having any real impact on the species.
iucn_doc_rationale:
Mammillaria formosa is a very widespread and abundant species with no major threats, hence it is listed as Least Concern.
The subspecies are also all Least Concern, but there are some concerns about subspecies pseudocrucigera, which appears to be scarcer than the others.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria gasseriana Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico. Since it was discovered near San Pedro (Coahuila) by Moeller (Fitz Maurice 1997), the species has also been found at Viesca (Coahuila) and in Durango (about 100 km apart) and it may occur at other places in between (W.A. Fitz Maurice pers. comm. 2009). The subpopulation in southwestern Coahuila is reported to have somewhat diminished (W.A. Fitz Maurice 2002). The area of occupancy of the species across all three locations is less than 10 km². It grows at about 1,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on volcanic and calcareous rock in xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is moderately threatened by illegal collecting. One of the sites is a standard stop for cactus tour groups from Europe and is now well-known to both commercial and amateur collectors. There is probably overgrazing of the habitat by goats, but that is not a major threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Based on the currently available information, Mammillaria gasseriana is only known from three widely separated locations, which together have an extent of occurrence of 2,500 km². There is a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals due to illegal collection from the wild. One of the locations is well-known to collectors. Furthermore, the species does not occur in any protected area. Hence, this cactus is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria geminispina Haw. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Guanajuato; Veracruz; San Luis Potosí; Hidalgo; Querétaro; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-GU; MXG-VC; MXE-SL; MXE-HI; MXE-QU; MX
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí and possibly Veracruz (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in xerophyllous scrub on calcareous soils. It forms colonies.
iucn_doc_threats: Historically the species was overcollected, however today this threat has been reduced.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria geminispina is listed as Least Concern due to its relatively high abundance within its limited range.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria glassii R.A.Foster sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Coahuila and Nuevo León (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in the mountains, in shade on the edge of steep canyons and on canyon walls. The vegetation is open pine oak woodland.
iucn_doc_threats: The increased frequency of fires (set by people to create grazing areas for livestock) may pose a threat to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria glassii has a relatively restricted range (its extent of occurrence is just under 20,000 km²), but there are many subpopulations and no significant threats to warrant any concerns about continuing declines. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria grusonii Runge sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it can be found in the states of Coahuila and Durango at elevations of 800 to 1,850 m asl (Pilbeam 1999). It may possibly occur in Sinaloa.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus appears to occur in a variety of habitats, from xerophytic shrubland to grassland and woodland. It grows on rocky limestone and on volcanic soils in the Sierra Madre.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no real threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria grusonii is a widespread species, and although it is not abundant or common, there are no threats to cause any concern. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria haageana Pfeiff. sec. Cervantes & al. 2023
iucn_locations: México State; Guerrero; Tlaxcala; Puebla; Oaxaca; Veracruz; Morelos; México Distrito Federal; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXC-ME; MXS-GR; MXC-TL; MXC-PU; MXS-OA; MXG-VC; MXC-MO; MXC-DF; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Temperate; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: The species is widespread and endemic to Mexico, occurring in the States of Guerrero, México State, Morelos, Oaxaca, Puebla, Tlaxcala, and Veracruz and in México Distrito Federal (Pilbeam 1999, Guzmán et al. 2003). It is found at elevations of 450 to 2,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The elevation range where this species occurs is wide, therefore it is found in many different vegetation types such as deciduous tropical forest, xerophyllous scrub, oak forest and secondary vegetation, juniper forest, pine forest, lava flows, and often in disturbed areas (Arias et al. 1997). It occurs in Cardonal-tetechera (García-Suárez et al. 2003). The plants are commonly associated with nurse plants, but can also occur in bare areas (Mandujano et al. 2002).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is locally threatened by the overcollection of wild populations for use as an ornamental. A subpopulation near Mexico City went locally extinct as a result of overcollection of wild individuals. However, this harvesting is not a major threat to the global population.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria haageana is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread, common, and found in numerous protected areas. However, subpopulations near population centers are at risk of local extinction as a result of overcollection and habitat destruction.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria hernandezii Glass & R.A.Foster sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is a Mexican micro endemic known to occur only in a small area in the Sierra Mixteca in the state of Oaxaca at elevations of ca 2,300 m asl (Pilbeam 1999). It occurs in five isolated sites (Rodríguez-Ortega et al. 2006; S. Arias pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in open spaces in oak-pine woodland (Arias et al. 1997, Rodríguez-Ortega et al. 2006). It flowers between October and December (Arias et al. 1997). It grows barely above ground, so may be overlooked.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is the change of microhabitats as a result of goat overgrazing which makes it difficult for the establishment of seedlings (Jiménez-Sierra et al. 2007, Ureta 2006 in Jiménez-Sierra et al. 2007).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a restricted extent of occurrence (less than 5,000 km²), its range is severely fragmented and there is a continuing decline in the quality of the habitat and numbers of mature individuals due to the impacts of grazing by goats. Hence, it is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria herrerae Werderm. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_common_name: Golf ball; Biznaguita; Bola de hilo
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Querétaro, Mexico, where it grows at elevations of ca 1,800 to 2,050 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on sloping calcareous rock in semi-desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is highly threatened by illegal collecting. The population of this species has been diminished by an estimated 95% over the past 20 years. The habitat is now well-known to both commercial and amateur collectors. A nearby commercial cactus nursery has almost stripped the site. Local children collect and offer plants to visitors. A very small amount of residential development is in progress in the area.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria herrerae is considered Critically Endangered due to its small range (area of occupancy = 0.87km2 and extent of occurrence = 3.4km2, one location) and a continuing decline due to illegal collection. This threat has caused a serious population reduction (>95%) over the last 3 generations (approximately 20 years) based on direct observation which is continuing. The species would greatly benefit from a demography study.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria heyderi Muehlenpf. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_common_name: Heyder's Pincushion Cactus; Biznaga China; Little Nipple Cactus
iucn_locations: Zacatecas; Nuevo León; San Luis Potosí; Sonora; Mexico; Chihuahua; Durango; Coahuila; Arizona; Yucatán; Tamaulipas; New Mexico; United States; Texas
iucn_location_codes: MXE-ZA; MXE-NL; MXE-SL; MXN-SO; MX; MXE-CU; MXE-DU; MXE-CO; ARI-OO; MXT-YU; MXE-TA; NWM-OO; US; TEX-OO
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This is a widespread species that occurs in the Mexican states of Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, Sonora, Tamaulipas, Veracruz (reported from the literature), Yucatán, Zacatecas, and in Arizona, New Mexico, and Texas in the USA, at elevations between 10 to 2,000 m asl (Pilbeam 1999; Hunt et al. 2006). The Nature Serve reports it from Arizona, New Mexico, Oklahoma and Texas.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in xerophyllous scrub, also in grassland (Golubov et al. 2000; Goettsch and Hernández 2006). It grows in gravelly limestone soils in desert and grasslands from near sea level; deeper soils of valley and plains, and hillsides.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species, however land use change affects some subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria heyderi has a very large extent of occurrence, is abundant, and there are no known major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria huitzilopochtli D.R.Hunt sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Oaxaca (north), with an extent of occurrence close to 1,800 km2 and 7 known sites at elevations of 500 to 1,000 m asl (Arias et al. 1997, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits tropical deciduous forest. It flowers between October and December, and produces fruits between December and March (Arias et al. 1997). The seedlings only grow under the shade of nurse plants (Flores-Martínez et al. 2008).
iucn_doc_threats:
Climate change is a potential threat to the species. Under climate change scenarios in which temperature was increased by 2°C and the precipitation decreased by 15% with respect to current climate conditions (approximately between year 2060 and year 2100), the species reduced its present range dramatically (Téllez-Valdés and Dávila 2003).
The species has an intrinsic limitation for population growth, and seedling establishment is very low.
The populations are threatened by goat trampling (Peters and Martorell 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria huitzilopochtli has a very narrow range, however, it occurs at a very high density and the decline in population is not known to be sufficient to trigger a red lisitng. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern. Special attention should be given to the effect of goat trampling on the population.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria humboldtii Ehrenb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Nomadic grazing
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Hidalgo, where it occurs at elevations of 1,350 to 1,500 m asl (Pilbeam 1999, Hernández et al. 2004). It is only known from a few localities.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on steep rocky hillsides on limestone and non-calcareous areas in xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection of this species is a major threat, especially as it is not abundant. Overgrazing of the habitat by goats is also an ongoing threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria humboldtii has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence less than 100 km²). It is known essentially from a single location and is not very common. This cactus is highly sought after by collectors, which is a major threat. It is also affected by overgrazing of the habitat by goats. Furthermore, it does not occur in any protected area. Hence, the species qualifies for listing as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria jaliscana (Britton & Rose) Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Temperate
iucn_threat_names: Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming; Agro-industry farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, and is known to occur (from herbarium records) in the states of Jalisco and Zacatecas, and from the literature in Michoacán (Hernández et al. 2004) at elevations of 1,200 to 2,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species prefers to grow on rocks (rupiculous) in oak (Quercus) forests.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is overcollected in many areas for medicinal and ornamental purposes. It is also threatened by habitat conversion from fire used to open areas for agave cultivation for tequila.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered to be Vulnerable due to past and ongoing populations declines, greater than 30% over the past 10 years. The rate has likely been greater over the past three generations (30+ years) however there are no good records or means to measure these rates so a conservative approach is taken with 10 years. Declines are due to direct loss of habitat and of subpopulations following the clearing of native habitats (where this cactus occurs) for agave cultivation. In the past 10 years, large agro-industrial operations have caused a reduction of at least 30%.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria karwinskiana Mart. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Guatemala; Mexico; Puebla; Chiapas; Michoacán; Guerrero; Colima; Oaxaca; Morelos
iucn_location_codes: GT; MX; MXC-PU; MXT-CI; MXS-MI; MXS-GR; MXS-CL; MXS-OA; MXC-MO
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in southern Mexico and Guatemala. In Mexico it occurs in the States of Chiapas, Colima, Guerrero, Oaxaca, Morelos, Michoacán, and Puebla (Arias et al. 1997, Pilbeam 1999). Arias et al. (1997) report this species for Guerrero but not for Morelos. It is found at altitudes from 150 to 2,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in tropical deciduous forest and xerophyllous scrub (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by deforestation for small-holder agriculture and goat-ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria karwinskiana has a very wide range, is abundant, and although there are threats in places these are not sufficient to warrant any concern. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria klissingiana Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Nuevo León; Tamaulipas; Mexico
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is found in the states of Tamaulipas, Nuevo León and San Luis Potosí (Pilbeam 1999). It occurs at elevations of 630 to 2,300 m asl (Pilbeam 1999, Martínez-Avalos and Jurado 2005).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on top of vertical limestone cliffs in indirect sun and sometimes amongst rocks in sparse vegetation (Pilbeam 1999). It is also observed on rocks in low deciduous forest and on exposed rocks of road cuttings (M. Smith pers.comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: llegally collected plants have been seized in Mexico and the Netherlands (Bárcenas 2003).
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria klissingiana is a widespread and very common species. It appears to be tolerant of disturbance as it often grows on roadsides and on road cuttings, and often re-establishes itself after road-widening activities. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria lasiacantha Engelm. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_common_name: Small Pincushion Cactus; Biznaga de Espinas Pubescentes; Lace-spine Nipple Cactus
iucn_locations: United States; Coahuila; Chihuahua; Durango; Mexico; Zacatecas; San Luis Potosí; Nuevo León; Texas
iucn_location_codes: US; MXE-CO; MXE-CU; MXE-DU; MX; MXE-ZA; MXE-SL; MXE-NL; TEX-OO
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This wide-ranging species occurs in the Mexican states of Coahuila and Durango. The literature also reports it from Chihuahua, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, Sonora and Zacatecas. It also occurs in Texas in the USA, and it has been reported from the literature to occur in Arizona and New Mexico (Hernández et al. 2004). It grows at elevations of 700 to 2,400 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on limestone soils of hills and tablelands in desert habitat.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats for this species are illegal collection for the horticultural trade and possibly land use changes.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria lasiacantha is widely distributed and abundant, with no noticeable declines. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria lenta K.Brandegee sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Coahuila, where it occurs at elevations between 1,000 and 1,800 m asl. The species appears over a range of approximately 16,000 km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on steep calcareous rock, in semi-desert shrublands.
iucn_doc_threats: There is some illegal collecting, but it is not a significant threat. Goat overgrazing and trampling also represents a minor threat to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria lenta is listed as Least Concern because there is little evidence of decline. However, due to the small number of subpopulations and its natural rarity the species requires monitoring because it could decline rapidly with an increase of threats.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria longimamma DC. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Nomadic grazing; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Guanajuato, Hidalgo, and Querétaro (Hernández et al. 2004). It grows at elevations of 1,000 to 2,400 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus is found on limestone and in volcanic soils, under low shrubs and sparse vegetation.
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is illegally collected in the wild as an ornamental, although this is localized. In some areas this species is trampled by cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria longimamma is listed as Vulnerable due to a limited geographic distribution (extent of occurrence ca 15,000 km2) and severe fragmentation, and an ongoing decline due to impacts of livestock ranching (trampling) and illegal overharvesting.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria luethyi G.S.Hinton sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in the state of Coahuila, Mexico, at elevations of around 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on sloping calcareous rocky terrain in semi desert.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is the most sought after cactus by collectors because of its rarity and unusual spines. If the localities were to be discovered by collectors, the populations would be subject to major declines. The precise locality is known only to a small number of experts. Despite the readily available populations in commercial cultivation, an intensive search for the localities continues by amateur and commercial collectors.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because it is known from only two locations and has an area of occupancy less than 20 km<sup>2</sup>. If the two locations were publicly known the threat from amateur collectors would be very high leading to the species becoming Critically Endangered or Extinct in a very short period of time.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria magnifica F.G.Buchenau sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Morelos and Puebla at 1,000 to 1,550 m of altitude (Pilbeam 1999; Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is probably found in deciduous forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a poorly known taxon and the information needed on its range, population and threats to assess its conservation status is not available. Hence, the species is here listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria magnimamma Haw. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: México Distrito Federal; Tlaxcala; San Luis Potosí; Hidalgo; Tamaulipas; Zacatecas; Jalisco; Guanajuato; Veracruz; México State; Querétaro; Nuevo León; Puebla; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXC-DF; MXC-TL; MXE-SL; MXE-HI; MXE-TA; MXE-ZA; MXS-JA; MXE-GU; MXG-VC; MXC-ME; MXE-QU; MXE-NL; MXC-PU; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Temperate; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is widely distributed. It occurs in the states of  Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, México State, Nuevo León, Puebla, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas, Tlaxcala, Veracruz, Zacatecas, and México Federal District, growing at elevations of 100 to 2,700 m asl (Pilbeam 1999, Gómez-Hinostrosa and Hernández 2000, Hernández et al. 2004).
