iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs mainly in xerophyllous vegetation (Gómez-Hinostrosa and Hernández 2000, Valverde 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: In parts of its range the species is threatened by local extraction for ornamental plant trade and by the expansion of urbanized areas (Cabrera-Luna and Gómez-Sánchez 2005).
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria magnimamma is considered to be Least Concern due to its wide range, abundance, occurrence in numerous protected areas, and because it is not declining at a rate sufficient to qualify for a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria mammillaris (L.) H.Karst. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Guadeloupe; Saint Kitts and Nevis; Bonaire; Trinidad and Tobago; Martinique; Saint Martin (French part); Montserrat; Antigua and Barbuda; Saba; Saint Vincent and the Grenadines; Curaçao; Saint Lucia; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Grenada; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Sint Eustatius; Aruba; Dominica; Sint Maarten (Dutch part)
iucn_location_codes: GP; KN; NLA-BO; TT; MQ; MF; MS; AG; NLA-SA; VC; CW; LC; BQ; GD; VE; NLA-SE; AW; DM; SX
iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: Mammillaria mammillaris is found in the Netherlands Antilles, the Lesser Antilles, Trinidad and Tobago, and Venezuela (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at elevations up to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in clearings in spiny scrublands and thorny forests. It also occurs on offshore rocky islands.
iucn_doc_threats: Part of its habitat is being converted for agricultural activities such as crop production. It is unknown whether collecting from the wild (for ornamental plants) is affecting the population status since this species is relatively common (Nassar pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria mammillaris is listed as Least Concern because it is a common species with a widespread distribution and abundant populations in Venezuela.

checklist_fullName: Mammillaria marcosii W.A.Fitz Maur., B.Fitz Maur. & Glass
checklist_taxon: Mammillaria marcosii W.A.Fitz Maur., B.Fitz Maur. & Glass sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2015
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Guanajuato, Mexico, where it is found east of Xichú and grows over a range of less than 10 km². It is found at an altitude around 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found on steeply sloping volcanic rocky terrain in semi-desert (submontane matorral). Generation length is 5-10 years.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by extensive illegal overcollecting. The habitat is now well known to both commercial and amateur collectors.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria marcosii is considered to be Critically Endangered due to its very small range, its occurrence in a single location, a small population size, and rapid and ongoing decline in population size estimated to be greater than 80% over the next 3 generations (generation length is estimated at 5–10 years). Furthermore, it does not occur in any protected area. The population consists of fewer than 150 plants, which are found in a single small area of less than 10 km². Since this species was discovered in 1996 (Fitz Maurice et al. 1997), the population has diminished by an estimated 90% due to illegal collecting.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria mathildae Kraehenb. & Krainz sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying; Housing & urban areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs east and north of the Querétaro river, along the outskirts of the city, at elevations of 1,800 to 1,900 m asl. The species is known from seven localities (five locations). The largest population occurs in an area of 0.015 km<sup>2</sup>. The area of occupancy is a maximum of 2 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species has very specific habitat requirements. It grows in canyons (cañadas) among volcanic rock uplifts in remnants of tropical deciduous forest with elements of xerophyllous matorral (Garcia-Rubio pers. comm. 2009). Many publications associate this species with the semi-desert that has been created by urbanization and farming, but recent publications contradict this. It cannot tolerate the removal of the canopy trees including Lysiloma microphylla, Bursera fageroides and Ceiba aesculifolia.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by illegal collecting and land development for urbanization around Querétaro. The type locality is a standard stop for cactus tour groups from Europe and is now well-known to both commercial and amateur collectors. A single private landowner owns the land with 30% of the Juriquillas population (133 individuals, 15 x 25 m<sup>2</sup>). The primary threat is deforestation for urban development. One of the main populations, La Cañada, is in the site of a stone mine which threatens the integrity of its habitat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria mathildae is listed as Endangered because it has an area of occupancy of less than 2 km2, it is found in five locations, and has experienced declines in its range, area of occupancy, area of habitat, and number of individuals. Continued urbanization is causing deforestation of some of its localities and is the main threat to this species. In order to prevent further declines, protection is needed for the remaining population. There has been an observed population decline of 37% from 2003 to 2006 in the Juriquilla subpopulation.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria mercadensis Patoni sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Michoacán; Durango; Sinaloa; Jalisco; Aguascalientes; San Luis Potosí; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXS-MI; MXE-DU; MXN-SI; MXS-JA; MXE-AG; MXE-SL; MX
iucn_threat_names: Temperature extremes; Housing & urban areas; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs the states of Aguascalientes, Durango, Jalisco, Michoacán, San Luís Potosí, and Sinaloa. It is found at elevations from 1,800 to 2,400 m asl.   This species grows over an area of 20,000 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows among volcanic rocks in semi-desert.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is subject to limited threats from illegal collecting, agricultural use, and housing expansion. Temperature extremes have reduced some subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria mercadensis is listed as Least Concern due to its large number of subpopulations over a large area and a large number of individuals, although some continuing decline is in evidence. It is present in several protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria microhelia Werderm. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Nomadic grazing; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: Mammillaria microhelia is found over a range of less than 1,000 km² in the central part of the state of Querétaro, Mexico, at elevations of 2,000 to 2,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows amongst volcanic rock in semi-desert (matorral) and submontane deciduous forest with Quercus depressipes, Q. eduardi, Q. grisea and Q. potosina.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collecting, deforestation of deciduous forests, and grazing (trampling) all impact the species and are causing a population decline.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria microhelia is considered to be Endangered due to its limited range (extent of occurrence is less than 1,000 km2), its occurrence in less than five locations, and an ongoing decline due to habitat degradation (grazing) and possibly decline of mature individuals caused by illegal overcollecting.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria moelleriana Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Durango and Zacatecas at elevations of   about 2,200 to 2,600 m asl. It is widely distributed over a range of 25,000 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows among volcanic rocks in semi-desert.
iucn_doc_threats: These plants seem to be little threatened by illegal collecting although the habitats are known to both commercial and amateur collectors.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria moelleriana qualifies as Least Concern due to its many widely separated subpopulations occurring over a very large area, much of it of difficult access, and there is little evidence of decline as a result of illegal collecting.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria mystax Mart. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Temperate; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico. Arias et al. (1997) report the species from the states of Guerrero, Oaxaca and Puebla at elevations of 1,000 to 2,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical dry deciduous forest (Vovides et al. 1997), Quercus forest and xerophyllous scrub (Arias et al. 1997). It flowers from February to April (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats. However, it is affected indirectly from land use change for farming and cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria mystax has a relatively wide range and is locally abundant. Although there are threats in places, these are not sufficient to warrant any concern. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria nana Backeb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Querétaro; Guanajuato; Mexico
iucn_threat_names: Temperature extremes; Housing & urban areas; Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Guanajuato, Querétaro and San Luis Potosí at elevations of   1,600 to 1,800 m asl.   The species appears over an area of 18,000 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found on volcanic soil in xerophyllous scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There is little interest in collecting so this represents only a small threat. Increased agricultural use and housing expansion within its habitat also pose a small threat. Temperature extremes such as the 100 year freeze on the Altiplano in December 1997 present a serious but unpredictable threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria nana qualifies as Least Concern due to its extensive subpopulations over a large area, where little decline from human activities is in current evidence.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria oteroi Glass & R.A.Foster sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is known from five localities in the state of Oaxaca (Arias et al. 1997, Pilbeam 1999). It occurs at elevations of 1,800 - 2,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species occurs in Quercus-Juniperus forest. It flowers between January and March (Arias et al. 1997).

The spatial distribution of this species is aggregated, based on the size of the clusters it can be suggested that the populations in the study area have not been disturbed in a long time and the species is undergoing a phase of colonization (Flores-Martínez et al. 2001).
iucn_doc_threats: Soil erosion as a result of human activities (due to forest clearance for fuel wood) and trampling due to goats are the main threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because it has a limited extent of occurrence approaching the 5,000 km² threshold for Endangered, it is severely fragmented and known from only five locations, and there is a continuing decline in the extent and quality of the habitat due to deforestation and increasing erosion.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria parkinsonii Ehrenb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the state of Querétaro (Hernández et al. 2004) and into Hidalgo along the border of the Querétaro river, at elevations of 1,200 to 2,400 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: Mammillaria parkinsonii grows on limestone and calcareous substrate (Pilbeam 1999). It occurs in xerophyllous scrub and submontane matorral.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection and land use change are threats across the range of the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria parkinsonii is listed as Endangered due to a limited distribution (extent of occurrence ca 2,500 km2), severe fragmentation, a continuing decline due to illegal collection, and degradation of its habitat across its range. Clarification of the taxonomic status of this species is a research priority.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria pectinifera F.A.C.Weber sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2015
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying; Nomadic grazing; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas; Garbage & solid waste; Named species
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: A species with a highly restricted and patchy distribution, it is endemic to the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán Valley, occurring only in the state of Puebla, Mexico at elevations of 1,700 - 2,300 m asl (Arias et al. 1997; Zavala-Hurtado and Valverde 2003).
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species grows in xerophyllous scrub and grasslands, on calcareous soils (Arias et al. 1997). It exhibits high habitat specificity with relatively deep alkaline soils, high water retention capacity, and relatively high surface stoniness (Zavala-Hurtado and Valverde 2003).

This species has abiotic nurses (i.e. rocks) that increase the survival of seedlings (Peters et al. 2008). The species flowers from December to March. Seeds are retained among the plant tubercules, and are released gradually.

Reproduction starts at an age of eight years.

It has been observed that the species is attacked by the larvae of a beetle that eat the tissue from inside the steams killing the plants.
iucn_doc_threats:
The major threats are mining rock for construction, dumping of rubbish, collecting plants for horticulture and grazing of cattle and goats (Anderson et al. 1994).

The species is highly susceptible to land use change, however, intermediate disturbance helps preserve populations (Martorell and Peters 2005).

Herbivory by beetle larvae (Cerambycidae; Coleoptera) has been reported as an important cause of death in M. pectinifera (Valverde and Zavala-Hurtado 2006).

Special attention should be paid to erosion, since this process can lead to the rapid disappearance of this species (Martorell and Peters 2005).

One populations is located in the edge of Tecamachalco, México, and is threatened by urban growth.

The range of this species has been predicted to be reduced by up to 90% under climate change scenarios in which temperature was increased by 2°C and the precipitation decreased by 15% with respect to current climate conditions (approximately between year 2060 and year 2100; Téllez-Valdés and Dávila-Aranda 2003).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is quite narrowly distributed (extent of occurrence is 4,000 km<sup>2</sup>), with the localities well fragmented (evidenced by genetic differentiation), and is subject to continuing decline as a consequence of several threats. Hence it is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria peninsularis (Britton & Rose) Orcutt sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Roads & railroads; Housing & urban areas; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Recreational activities
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in Baja California Sur in the Cape region at elevations of 10 to 200 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in shady areas under bushes in desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are road construction, extraction of construction materials for roads, urbanization and tourism development on the coastal areas. Also a threat are off road motorcycles and horses for tourists to ride.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a small extent of occurrence (EOO ~ 2,500 km<sup>2</sup>), and there are severe impacts due to urban and tourism development and activities in the whole of its range which have fragmented the population severely and are causing a continuing decline. These threats are ongoing and will intensify in the near future. Hence the species is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria pennispinosa Krainz sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Desert - Hot; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in one location northwest of Gómez Palacio in the north of Durango, Mexico, where it grows at altitudes from 1,300 to 1,500 m asl. The area of occupancy is conservatively estimated to be a maximum of 1 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: It is found in crevices in steep volcanic rock in desert.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by illegal collecting. The habitat is a standard stop for cactus tour groups from Europe and is now well-known to both commercial and amateur collectors.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria pennispinosa is listed as Critically Endangered due to its very restricted area of occupancy of less than 1 km2, its occurrence in a single location, and ongoing population decline due to illegal collection.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria petterssonii Hildm. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Aguascalientes; Jalisco; Durango; Guanajuato; San Luis Potosí; Zacatecas; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-AG; MXS-JA; MXE-DU; MXE-GU; MXE-SL; MXE-ZA; MX
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it can be found in the states of Aguascalientes, Durango, Guanajuato, Jalisco, San Luis Potosí and Zacatecas (Pilbeam 1999, Hernandez et al. 2004). Hunt et al. (2006) do not record it from Durango or San Luis Potosí. It grows at elevations of 1,300 to 2,600 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: This hemispherical species is found growing on rocky slopes in open oak woodland on volcanic soils.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is widespread and seems to be abundant. Furthermore, it grows in areas where there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria picta Meinsh. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Coahuila; Tamaulipas; San Luis Potosí; Nuevo León; Mexico
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Housing & urban areas; Intentional use (species is the target); Temperature extremes
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Coahuila, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, and Tamaulipas.   The species appears over an area of more than 40,000 km<sup>2</sup>.<sup> </sup> It occurs at elevations around 1,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found on calcareous rock in semi-desert.
iucn_doc_threats: There is little interest in collecting this species and small consequent threat. Increased agricultural use and housing expansion also pose a small threat. Temperature extremes such as the 100 year freeze on the Altiplano in December 1997 present a serious but unpredictable threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria picta is listed as Least Concern due to its large number of individuals over a relatively large area, where little decline is in current evidence.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria plumosa F.A.C.Weber sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_use_trade_names: Handicrafts, jewellery, etc.; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it can be found in the states of Coahuila and Nuevo León. It grows at elevations of 730 to 1,350 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on limestone cliffs in sparse xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is illegally collected for the ornamental trade. The local community in the area also collects plants from the wild and sells them at local markets at Christmas time, as they are used to decorate nativity scenes.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria plumosa has a restricted range (extent of occurrence under 7,500 km²), there is continuing decline due to ongoing collection, but the number of locations probably exceeds ten and the population is not likely to be severely fragmented. The species is listed as Near Threatened, as it almost qualifies for Vulnerable under criterion B1ab(v).

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria polyedra Mart. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is known to occur in the States of Guerrero, Oaxaca and Puebla, at elevations of 900 to 1,900 m asl (Arias et al. 1997, Pilbeam 1999, Jiménez-Ramírez et al. 2003).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in tropical deciduous forest (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: There are currently no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Mammillaria polyedra has a relatively restricted range (the extent of occurrence is less than 20,000 km²), the species is locally abundant and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria polythele Mart. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Intentional use (species is the target); Nomadic grazing
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Hidalgo, Querétaro, and Guanajuato, at elevations of 1,700 to 2,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows among quite dense vegetation, with Sedum humifusum and Calibanus hookeri, and in semishade (Pilbeam 1999). It grows in xerophyllous shrubland and in oak forests.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal trade of wild specimens has been reported and the plants are also used locally for consumption and ornamental use (Bárcenas-Luna 2003; Cabrera-Luna and Gómez-Sánchez 2005). It is also threatened by overgrazing by goats and deforestation of oak forests.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria polythele is listed as Least Concern, as it is widespread and abundant across its range. There are several threats affecting subpopulations but the species is not declining at a rate sufficient to warrant listing in a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria pottsii Scheer ex Salm-Dyck sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_common_name: Rattail Nipple Cactus; Biznaga de Potts; Pott's Nipple-cactus
iucn_locations: Mexico; Durango; Texas; Coahuila; United States; Chihuahua; San Luis Potosí; Zacatecas
iucn_location_codes: MX; MXE-DU; TEX-OO; MXE-CO; US; MXE-CU; MXE-SL; MXE-ZA
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in the states of Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, San Luis Potosí, Zacatecas in Mexico, and in Texas in the USA (Hernández et al. 2004). The species is reported at elevations of 800 to 2,100 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in flaking limestone with little if any humus. It occurs in pure limestone crevices (Pilbeam 1999) in xerophyllous scrub.  It also grows in gravelly areas of hills and washes in desert.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria pottsii has a very wide range, is abundant, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria prolifera (Mill.) Haw. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_common_name: Texas Nipple Cactus; Biznaga Prolifera; West Indian Nipple-cactus
iucn_locations: Texas; Tamaulipas; Dominican Republic; Querétaro; Haiti; Coahuila; Mexico; Nuevo León; San Luis Potosí; Cuba; United States
iucn_location_codes: TEX-OO; MXE-TA; DO; MXE-QU; HT; MXE-CO; MX; MXE-NL; MXE-SL; CU; US
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: The species has been reported from Mexico (Coahuila, Nuevo León, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí and Tamaulipas), the USA (Texas), Cuba, the Dominican Republic, and Haiti (Pilbeam 1999, Hernández et al. 2004). It is found at elevations of 400 to 2,250 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in submontane scrub. In Texas, it occurs in grasslands at low elevations.
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat destruction through logging poses a threat to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria prolifera is listed as Least Concern because it has a very large extent of occurrence and is abundant. Although there are threats in places, they are not sufficient to warrant any concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria rekoi (Britton & Rose) Vaupel sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to central and northern Oaxaca, Mexico, where it grows at elevations of 700 to 3,000 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in Quercus forest on limestone cliffs, basalt and clay soils (Vovides et al. 1997, Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no specific threats to the species although the habitat where it occurs is being degraded by human activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria rekoi is a widespread and relatively abundant species with no major threats, hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria rettigiana Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Temperature extremes; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Guanajuato, Mexico, where it grows at about 1,700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows among volcanic rocks (igneous soils) in semi-desert (matorral).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is found very close to several cities and towns and is declining due to urban development. There is some illegal collecting by tour groups and individuals. Increased agricultural use and housing expansion pose an increasing threat. There is evidence of a substantial decrease in the number of individuals as a result of the 1997 freeze, with some subpopulations being nearly extirpated.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria rettigiana is considered Endangered due to a very limited range (extent of occurrence ca 200 km2), it is found in one location and is declining due to illegal overcollection and destruction of habitat due to urban growth and expansion.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria rhodantha Link & Otto sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: México Distrito Federal; Tlaxcala; Veracruz; Guanajuato; Querétaro; Jalisco; Hidalgo; Aguascalientes; Michoacán; México State; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXC-DF; MXC-TL; MXG-VC; MXE-GU; MXE-QU; MXS-JA; MXE-HI; MXE-AG; MXS-MI; MXC-ME; MX
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is widely distributed in the states of Aguascalientes, México Federal District, México State, Guanajuato, Jalisco, Michoacán, Querétaro, Tlaxcala, Veracruz, and Hidalgo (Hernández et al. 2004). Pilbeam (1999) reports the species at elevations of 1,700 to 3,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is reported originally "from Mexico, in the mountains" (Pilbeam 1999). Based on this information, the altitudinal range and some of the localities the species is reported to occur, it probably grows in pine-oak and fir forests.
iucn_doc_threats: Parts of the range of this cactus are impacted by deforestation for agricultural expansion - livestock and crops.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria rhodantha has a wide range, is very abundant, and although there are some threats these have minimal impact. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria schwarzii Shurly sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Guanajuato, Mexico. It grows near San Felipe on a steep volcanic rock face about 100 m (plan view) by 4 km long. The extent of occurrence is less than 100 km<sup>2</sup>. It grows at altitudes between 2,150 and 2,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on steep volcanic rock (igneous) in semi-desert (matorral) and oak forest.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is highly threatened by illegal collecting. The locality is a standard stop for cactus tour groups from Europe and is now well-known to both commercial and amateur collectors.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria schwarzii is considered Critically Endangered due to a very restricted range (extent of occurrence less than 100km2), it is only found in one location, and there is a continuing decline in the number of subpopulations and mature individuals due to extensive overcollecting, and there are extreme fluctuations in the number of mature individuals. In terms of cactus, this species has among the highest rates of natural population fluctuation, which makes it susceptible to rapid declines.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria scrippsiana (Britton & Rose) Orcutt sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the States of Jalisco, Nayarit, Zacatecas (Pilbeam 1999) and Sinaloa (Vega-Aviña et al. 2000). It grows at elevations of 900 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in tropical deciduous forests. It grows on volcanic rock (rupiculous) and man-made walls (Pilbeam 1999). It has also been reported to be epiphytic (Gómez-Reyna and Pérez-Verdía Trapero 2005).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria scrippsiana has a wide range, is very abundant, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria senilis Lodd. ex Salm-Dyck sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: Mammillaria senilis is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the state of Durango at elevations of 2,000 to 2,800 m asl. The species range is over 60,000 km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: Mammillaria senilis grows among volcanic rocks in semi desert.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collecting poses a small threat for Mammillaria senilis.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria senilis qualifies as Least Concern due to its large number of subpopulations over a large area and a large number of individuals, where some small continuing decline is in evidence. The number of subpopulations makes conservation measures a low priority.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria sinistrohamata Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the state of Zacatecas at elevations of about 1,900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found among volcanic rocks in semi-desert.
iucn_doc_threats: There is some illegal collecting and it is also threatened by land use change for agriculture and cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria sinistrohamata is listed as Data Deficient because it cannot be distinguished from similar species. Therefore, population numbers and range cannot be determined. If it is a distinct species, it would qualify as Critically Endangered because of its small range, single location, and declining population.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria sphacelata Mart. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the States of Oaxaca and Puebla (Arias et al. 1997). It is widespread (Mendéz-Larios et al. 2006) and grows at elevations of 700 to 2,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky slopes (Pilbeam 1999). It occurs in Tetechera, Cardonal, Mezquital, thorny scrub, Tetechera-Candelillar, and orchards (Paredes-Flores et al. 2007).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats, but land use change for agriculture and cattle ranching affects some subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria sphacelata has a relatively wide range, is abundant, and although there are some threats these have minimal impact. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria spinosissima Lem. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in the states of Guerrero, Mexico and Morelos  at elevations of 1,600 to 1,900 m (Fernández-Nava 1998; Pilbeam 1999; Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical dry forests and xerophyllous scrub (Fernández-Nava 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a poorly known taxon, there are taxonomic uncertainties concerning its validity. The information on population and threats needed to assess its conservation status is not available. Hence, the species is here listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria supertexta Mart. ex Pfeiff. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Temperate; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán Valley, occurring only in the Mexican state of Oaxaca, at elevations of 400-2,200m asl (Arias et al. 1997, Pilbeam 1999). It is known from five localities (Peters and Martorell 2000).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical deciduous forests. It grows hanging from limestone cliffs (Pilbeam 1999). Arias et al. (1997) reports the species from Pinus-Juniperus forests. It is reported from xerophyllous scrub by Vovides et al. (1997). It flowers in November (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat to this species is cattle ranching which affects the  habitat and the cattle trample on the plants. This species is not tolerant to habitat modification.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered due to a very limited range (extent of occurrence aproximately 2,000km<sup>2</sup>, known from five locations), and in continuing decline due to expanding cattle and farming activities.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria tonalensis D.R.Hunt sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_use_trade_names: Medicine - human & veterinary; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican State of Oaxaca (northwest), where it occurs at elevations between 1,000 and 1,400 m asl (Pilbeam 1999, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in tropical deciduous forest, on vertical cliffs in the Tonalá Canyon.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria tonalensis is a poorly known species. All seven collections are from the same area, which appears to indicate that it may have a very small range. However, the species grows in a relatively inaccessible habitat that does not appear to be under any threat, hence there is no decline. As a result the species is listed as Least Concern, however, field work is required to determine the true extent of the range, the population size and the nature of any threats.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria uncinata Zucc. ex Pfeiff. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Querétaro; México Distrito Federal; Guanajuato; Aguascalientes; México State; Nuevo León; San Luis Potosí; Coahuila; Puebla; Jalisco; Zacatecas; Hidalgo; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-QU; MXC-DF; MXE-GU; MXE-AG; MXC-ME; MXE-NL; MXE-SL; MXE-CO; MXC-PU; MXS-JA; MXE-ZA; MXE-HI; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Temperate; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is widespread occurring in the states Aguascalientes, Coahuila, México Federal District, Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, México State, Nuevo León, Puebla, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, and Zacatecas at elevations of 1,500 to 2,800 m asl (Arias et al. 1997, Pilbeam 1999, Hernández et al. 2004, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits low areas on volcanic rock, open Quercus forests, xerophyllous scrub and grasslands (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: The flat areas where this species grows are also preferred for agriculture and, thus, the area of occupancy has been reduced, however there are no major threats known.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria uncinata is listed as Least Concern as it is wide ranging, abundant in appropriate habitats, and because it is not declining at a rate sufficient to qualify for listing in a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria weingartiana Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is reported from the state of Nuevo León, Mexico, where it grows near Ascención and Juanito at altitudes of 2,000 to 2,300 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is known from flat, grassy areas with numerous ant nests (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_threats: This species faces a significant threat from illegal collection for the international trade.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria weingartiana is considered to be Least Concern as it is somewhat common where it occurs and due to the lack of any major threats. In addition, there is no reason to believe the rates of decline are sufficient to meet thresholds for a threatened category at this time.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria wiesingeri Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it can be found in Metzquititlán (Hidalgo), and in the State of México. It grows at altitudes between 2,000 and 3,000 asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus can be found growing among oak and pine trees, in pine needle litter, often in dense shade (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection and land use change could both pose threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria wiesingeri is insufficiently known to be assessed. Hence, it is listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria winterae Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in the Mexican states of Nuevo León and Coahuila, where it grows at altitudes of 900 to 2,200 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in mountains and exposed areas (Pilbeam 1999), and on low shaley limestone hills. It occurs in pine woodland and in xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: Overgrazing and trampling by goats may be a threat to the species, but this is not likely to be significant.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria winterae has a fairly widespread range, and although there is no precise population information it is likely to be fairly common and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria zeilmanniana Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Guanajuato, Mexico. It was rediscovered near San Miguel de Allende in Cañada Virgen in 1987 (Fitz Maurice 1988). The range is less than 1 km², it grows at elevations of around 1,700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on steep volcanic rock (igneous) face in canyon walls in semi-desert and deciduous forests.
iucn_doc_threats: The small population of this species is threatened by illegal collection because it is popular with cactus enthusiasts.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria zeilmanniana is listed as Critically Endangered because it has a very small population size (less than 250 mature individuals), an extremely restricted range (extent of occurrence and area of occupancy less than 1 km2), is found at a single location, and the number of mature individuals is declining because of illegal collection.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria zephyranthoides Scheidw. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: México State; Hidalgo; Querétaro; Oaxaca; San Luis Potosí; Guanajuato; Puebla; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXC-ME; MXE-HI; MXE-QU; MXS-OA; MXE-SL; MXE-GU; MXC-PU; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Nomadic grazing
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Guanajuato, Hidalgo, México, Oaxaca, Puebla, Querétaro, and San Luis Potosí (Pilbeam 1999, Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations of 1,800 to 2,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species occurs in xerophyllous scrub and grassland.
It flowers between March and May (Arias et al. 1997). The most critical stage in its life cycle is reproduction because the production of flower buds is scarce, many of them are aborted and a small proportion turns into fruits, thus the survival of individuals is jeopardized by grazing (Navarro-Carvajal and Juárez-Tentle 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by overgrazing of livestock, but it is not a major threat. Populations are negatively impacted by livestock stepping on young individuals that, as a result, will not reach reproductive age (Navarro-Carvajal and Juárez-Tentle 2006).
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria zephyranthoides is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread, has a large population, and is not declining significantly throughout its range.

checklist_taxon: Marshallocereus aragonii (F.A.C.Weber) Backeb. sec. Arias 2021
Matucana aurantiaca (Vaupel) Buxb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru. It is found in the Áncash, Cajamarca, La Libertad and Piura regions at an altitude of 2,000 to 3,000 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006, Hunt 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in thorny dry matorral and inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: There is a reforestation project near the city of Cajamarca where Eucalyptus trees are grown. This is affecting the cacti negatively. It is also illegally collected for exportation.
iucn_doc_rationale: Matucana aurantiaca is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is abundant, and there are no threats.

checklist_taxon: Matucana aureiflora F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This Peruvian endemic only occurs near the city of Cajamarca at an altitude of 2,800 to 2,900 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is affected by urban sprawl (expansion of the city of Cajamarca) and, to a lesser degree, by agricultural activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species occurs in an extremely restricted range of 20 km<sup>2</sup>, which represents a single location. It is not common, and the population as well as habitat quality and quantity are rapidly declining due to urban sprawl. It therefore qualifies for listing as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Matucana formosa F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru, where it is found in the Cajamarca, La Libertad and Amazonas regions at altitudes of 800 to 1,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in dry forest.
iucn_doc_rationale: Matucana formosa has a restricted range (its extent of occurrence is less than 1,000 km2), however the population is stable, it is abundant and the only threat (local extraction) is not sufficient to trigger a red listing. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Matucana hoxeyi (G.J.Charles) G.J.Charles sec. Hunt 2016

checklist_fullName: Matucana huagalensis (Donald & A.B.Lau) Bregmann, Meerst., Melis & Pullen
checklist_taxon: Matucana huagalensis (Donald & A.B.Lau) Bregmann, Meerst., Melis & Pullen sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: Matucana huagalensis is endemic to Peru, where it is found only in the southern portion of the Cajamarca region, at altitudes of 2,000 to 2,500 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This plant grows in dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: Part of the species range has been converted to potato fields according to a narrative of Lau's trip to the area in 1999. The species was found in the area again during an expedition in 2008  (Cieza-Padilla and Briones-Rojas 2008). The two potential major threats for the species are the expansion of agricultural fields in the surrounding areas which is continuing (Cieza-Padilla and Briones-Rojas 2008) and illegal collection. Given its rarity, it is very likely that the species is sought after by  collectors even though remoteness of the site confers some protection.  If the locality is found by unscrupulous collectors it could be  devastating for the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Matucana huagalensis is known from a single location and has a very restricted range; the area of occupancy is likely to be less than 20 km2. The type locality was destroyed by land use change for potato fields, but the species was reported years later in the surrounding areas. Even though the area is remote and difficult to access the major potential threat is the expansion of agricultural fields in the only known locality, which is a very likely event, as well as over collection in the event that the locality becomes widely known. The species is therefore listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Matucana intertexta F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru. It is only found in the Cajamarca region at altitudes of 2,000 to 2,600 m asl, and is usually solitary (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This solitary species occurs in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection and trade represents a threat that is difficult to quantify.
iucn_doc_rationale: Matucana intertexta has a restricted range (extent of occurrence is 800 km2) and is illegally collected to be sold at the national and international level as an ornamental plant. The impact of this activity is difficult to quantify, and neither the population nor the habitat trends are known. This cactus is therefore classified as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Matucana klopfensteinii Cieza & Pino sec. Kew 2019
Matucana krahnii (Donald) Bregmann sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru. It is only found in Chachapoyas, north of Balsas, San Vicente (all in the Amazonas region) in northern Peru, at altitudes of 800 to 1,800 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: M. krahnii is usually solitary and occurs in dry forest (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is illegally collected to be sold as an ornamental plant, both at the national and the international level. The impact of this activity is difficult to quantify.
iucn_doc_rationale: Matucana krahnii has a relatively restricted range (Extent of Occurrence is 1,500 km2). Although the population seems to be stable, it is affected by illegal collection for its use and trade as an ornamental. There are more than five locations because even though the species is affected by the same threat, the area is not impacted by collection at the same time. It is therefore classified as Vulnerable under criterion B1ab(v).

checklist_taxon: Matucana madisoniorum (Hutchison) G.D.Rowley sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru. It is found only in the Amazonas region at an altitude of 400 m asl, and is usually solitary (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: Matucana madisoniorum is usually solitary (Arakaki et al. 2006). It grows in dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: M. madisoniorum is now rare at the original type location due to collecting and goats degrading the steep hillside while grazing and trampling. (Hunt 2006). This impact has not ceased.
iucn_doc_rationale: Matucana madisoniorum occurs in an extremely restricted area of no more than 16 km2. This area represents a single location, which means that the species could be wiped out by a single event. The species was formerly described as common, but it is now rare due to illegal collection for its use and illegal trade as an ornamental plant. Furthermore, there is goat herding in its habitat, which is further affecting the species. The species therefore qualifies for listing as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Matucana oreodoxa (F.Ritter) Slaba sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru, and is found only in the Áncash region at altitudes of 2,500 - 3,000 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: The only potential threat is illegal collection, however, the inaccessibility of the subpopulations confers it some protection.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Vulnerable because it is known from one location and the area of occupancy is likely to be less than 20 km<sup>2</sup>. It is presumed that  there are no major threats at present in the area where it occurs. However, a potential threat is illegal collection, if the locality is discovered, collection will have severe consequences.

checklist_taxon: Matucana paucicostata F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru, and can be found in the Áncash and La Libertad regions at an altitude of 1,800 to 2,800 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: It is usually solitary and occurs in inter-Andean valleys (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is illegally collected and sold as an ornamental, but the impact of this activity is difficult to quantify.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species qualifies for listing as Vulnerable because it has a restricted range (Extent of Occurrence is less than 2,500 km<sup>2</sup>) and occurs in less than ten locations. Illegal collection occurs in all of the locations at some point in time and the population is thought to be decreasing due to this impact.

checklist_taxon: Matucana pujupatii (Donald & A.B.Lau) Bregmann sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_synonyms: Borzicactus madisoniorum (Hutchison) G.D.Rowley  var. pujupatii Donald & A.B.Lau
iucn_doc_range: Matucana pujupatii is endemic to Peru, it is found only in the Cajamarca region at an altitude of 400 to 500 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006). It is only known from the type locality (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The only known major threat is illegal collection.
iucn_doc_rationale: Matucana pujupatii is reported from a very small area (extent of occurrence less than 100 km2), and it is known that it is sought after by collectors. However, it is not known whether this is causing a continuing decline in the population. The species is therefore listed as Near Threatened because if it is established that illegal collection is causing a continuing decline, it would qualify to be listed as Critically Endangered under Criterion B1ab(v).

checklist_taxon: Matucana rebutiiflora G.J.Charles sec. Hunt 2016
Matucana ritteri Buining sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru’s Lima region (Otusco) and is found at an altitude of 2,000 to 2,500 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: M. ritteri is usually solitary (Arakaki et al. 2006). It occurs in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: It is collected in the wild and exported illegally for ornamental use. This represents a threat to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: M. ritteri has an extremely restricted range (extent of occurrence 10 km2). It is known from three sites, which are considered to represent a single location. It is collected and illegally exported. It is therefore classified as Critically Endangered.

checklist_fullName: Matucana tuberculata (Donald) Bregman, Meerst., Melis & A.B.Pullen
checklist_taxon: Matucana tuberculata (Donald) Bregman, Meerst., Melis & A.B.Pullen sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This Peruvian endemic is only found in Aircapampa, Huamachuco province, La Libertad region at an altitude of 1,500 and 2,500 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: M. tuberculata is usually solitary (Arakaki et al. 2006). It can be found in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: The illegal collection for international trade is considered to be a threat to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a restricted range (extent of occurrence ca 800 km2) and there are only two known locations. It is being collected illegally and traded at an international level and the population is declining due to this threat. This cactus is therefore classified as Endangered under criterion B1ab(v).

checklist_taxon: Matucana weberbaueri (Vaupel) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru, found only in the Amazonas region at an altitude of 2,000 to 2,500 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in montane oriental forest on humid substrates. M. weberbaueri is usually solitary (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The only threat is the illegal collection of the plant.
iucn_doc_rationale: Matucana weberbaueri is known from one location (extent of occurrence 100 km2) and it is subject to illegal collection that causes steady declines in the population. With the current information and evidence of threat, it is listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(v). If more subpopulations are discovered the species will have to be reassessed.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus conoideus Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Housing & urban areas; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This cactus can be found near Serra do Periperi, in the Municipality of Vitória da Conquista, in south-eastern Bahia, Brazil. It grows at elevations of 1,000 to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is an Eastern caatinga / Northern campo rupestre element that grows below and between shrubs in quartz gravel, in campo sujo / cerrado de altitude.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are habitat loss due to quarrying and urbanization, specifically the growth of the city of Victória da Conquista. The species survival in the wild is  severely threatened by extraction of the quartz gravel in which it  grows, and was formerly impacted by commercial collection for the  European horticultural market.
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus conoideus has a very small range (extent of occurrence less than 100 km2), it is known from one location, and the population is in continual decline. Part of the population is protected, however the rest are quickly disappearing. This cactus is therefore listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus curvispinus Pfeiff. sec. Majure & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Sint Eustatius; Honduras; Colombia; Chiapas; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Cuba; Costa Rica; Saint Martin (French part); Jalisco; Mexico; Saba; Oaxaca; Curaçao; Veracruz; Bonaire; Guatemala; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Michoacán; Colima; Panama; Nicaragua; Sint Maarten (Dutch part)
iucn_location_codes: NLA-SE; HN; CO; MXT-CI; BQ; CU; CR; MF; MXS-JA; MX; NLA-SA; MXS-OA; CW; MXG-VC; NLA-BO; GT; VE; MXS-MI; MXS-CL; PA; NI; SX
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Coastal Sand Dunes
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Named species; Roads & railroads
iucn_use_trade_names: Medicine - human & veterinary; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Food - human
iucn_doc_range: Melocactus curvispinus is a widely distributed species, occurring in Colombia, Costa Rica, Cuba, Guatemala, Honduras, Mexico, Netherlands Antilles, Nicaragua, Panama, and Venezuela (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations from 0 to 1,250 m asl (Taylor 1991; Hunt et al. 2006).  In Colombia, this species occurs in the departments of Boyacá, Cundinamarca, Huila, Norte de Santander, Tolima, Valle, Guajira, and Magdalena (Hunt et al. 2006). In Costa Rica, it grows in the department of Guanacaste (as M. curvispinus ssp. curvispinus; Hunt et al. 2006). In Guatemala, the species is distributed in the department of El Progreso (as M. curvispinus ssp. curvispinus; Hunt et al. 2006). In Mexico, it occurs in the states of Chiapas, Colima, Michoacán, Oaxaca, and Veracruz (as M. curvispinus ssp. curvispinus), and Jalisco (as M. curvispinus ssp. dawsonii; Hunt et al. 2006). In Panama, the species is found in Caipira. In Venezula, the species is widely distributed, occurring in Lara, Tachira, Trujillo (as M. curvispinus ssp. curvispinus), Anzoategui, Aragua, Carabobo, DF, Falcon, Nueva Esparta, Sucre, and Zuila (as M. curvispinus ssp. caesius; Hunt et al. 2006). In Cuba, the species is found in Camagüey, Holguín and Santa Clara (as M.curvispinus ssp. koolwijkianus; Hunt et al. 2006). In the Netherlands Antilles, it occurs in Aruba (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits arid and semiarid lands of tropical and subtropical zones (Taylor 1991). In Mexico, the species grows on rocky cliffs, sandy dunes and in  deciduous forest. In Costa Rica, the species occurs on serpentine soils on rocky cliffs and in deciduous forest. In Venezuela, it occurs in sandy flat lands and in rocky slopes. The species is pollinated by hummingbirds and bees, although it is also self-compatible and autogamous (Nassar amd Ramírez 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: In the Mexican state of Jalisco and along the west coast, the major threats are highway construction and tourist development. In the Mexican state of Chiapas, invasive grasses pose a major threat to Melocactus curvispinus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus curvispinus is listed as Least Concern because it has an extremely large range and is very abundant in the southern parts of its range. However, there are significant threats in the northern parts of its range. While the species as a whole is Least Concern, some of the subspecies, especially in the northern parts of the range, would probably be listed as threatened because of their small ranges, small population sizes, and ongoing threats.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus ernestii Vaupel sec. Taylor & Zappi 2004
iucn_locations: Sergipe; Pernambuco; Alagoas; Bahia; Paraíba; Minas Gerais; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZE-SE; BZE-PE; BZE-AL; BZE-BA; BZE-PB; BZL-MG; BR
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Other (free text); Food - animal; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Eastern Brazil in Paraíba, Pernambuco, Alagoas, Sergipe, Bahia and Minas Gerais at 50m - 1,190m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in eastern and southern caatinga element, its locally dominant on exposed crystalline/sandtone rocks and specially gneissic inselbergs, including outcrops in brejo forest, which is the ecotone between caatinga and atlantic forest and in rocky places in caatinga (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are quarrying and collection, but this happens at a very localised scale and does not threatened the species as a whole.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide range, is abundant and occurs in a protected area. It only has very localised threats, hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus intortus (Mill.) Urb. sec. Majure & al. 2022
iucn_common_name: Barrel Cactus; Mother-in-law's Pincushion; Turk's Cap; Turk's Head
iucn_locations: Saint Martin (French part); Saint Barthélemy; Dominica; Virgin Islands, U.S.; Martinique; Puerto Rico; Grenada; Anguilla; Antigua and Barbuda; Montserrat; Saint Lucia; Bahamas; Virgin Islands, British; Saint Kitts and Nevis; Barbados; Guadeloupe; Turks and Caicos Islands; Saint Vincent and the Grenadines
iucn_location_codes: MF; BL; DM; VI; MQ; PR; GD; AI; AG; MS; LC; BS; VG; KN; BB; GP; TC; VC
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands; Marine Coastal/supratidal - Coastal Caves/Karst
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: The species is widely distributed in the Caribbean region in the Bahamas, Dominican Republic, Puerto Rico, the British and United States Virgin Islands, and the West Indies (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at low altitudes between sea level and 100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in coastal limestone areas and cliff formations. In the southernmost portion of the range, it inhabits volcanic rocky areas.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is likely coastal tourism development.
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus intortus is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed in many of the Caribbean Islands and is locally abundant in many places.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus zehntneri (Britton & Rose) Luetzelb. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Bahia; Piauí; Pernambuco; Alagoas; Sergipe; Maranhão; Rio Grande do Norte; Tocantins; Ceará; Paraíba; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZE-BA; BZE-PI; BZE-PE; BZE-AL; BZE-SE; BZE-MA; BZE-RN; BZN-TO; BZE-CE; BZE-PB; BR
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming; Agro-industry farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Medicine - human & veterinary; Other (free text); Food - human
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to north-eastern Brazil in Piauí, Ceará, Rio Grande do Norte, Paraíba, Pernambuco, Alagoas, Sergipe, Maranhao, Tocantins and Bahia, it grows at elelvations of 200 -1,000m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats:
This species is widespread in caatinga and northern campo rupestre (Taylor and Zappi 2004). It occurs in a wide varieity of soil types, including soil, sand and rocks of various types, including limestone, gneiss/granite (inselbergs), sandstones, quartzitic and other crystalline formations (Taylor and Zappi 2004).

It is consider as an indicator species of caatinga, since its distribution coincides with this habitat.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is habitat loss due to agriculture, cattle ranching and gathering of fuel wood.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide range and is abundant. Even though caatinga habitat is declining fast, the species occurs in several other areas which are not affected and it can grow on several substrates which are not sought after. Hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Micropuntia pulchella (Engelm.) M.P.Griff. sec. Majure & al. 2019
Mila caespitosa Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Housing & urban areas; Intentional use (species is the target); Agro-industry farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru, where it occurs in the southern Áncash, Lima and northern Ica regions at an altitude of 300 to 800 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006). Its main distribution lies within the Lima region.
iucn_doc_habitats: It occurs on rocky soils in "lomas costeras".
iucn_doc_threats: It is affected by urban expansion, especially near the city of Lima (Arakaki et al. 2006) but also in other parts of its range. Agricultural activities are also extending within its range, especially near Lima. The collection for its use as an ornamental plant is not considered to be threatening the population as a whole.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mila caespitosa has a wide range and is common where it occurs. However, its main distribution is near the city of Lima, which is rapidly expanding into its habitat. Both urbanization and agricultural activities have reduced the habitat quality and quantity and have led to a population decline. The species has a generation length of approximately 15 years, and it is estimated that over a period of 45 years, counting both past and present, at least 30% of the population has disappeared due to human impacts. The causes for this population reduction are understood, but have not ceased and are not reversible. The species thus qualifies for listing as Vulnerable under criterion A4ac.

checklist_taxon: Miqueliopuntia miquelii (Monv.) F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it can be found in the regions of Coquimbo and Atacama from Elqui Valley to Copiapó. It grows at elevations of 0 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in desert, in a wide array of soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats affecting this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Miqueliopuntia miquelii is widely distributed, is abundant, the populations are increasing, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mitrocereus militaris (Audot) Bravo sec. ??? (sub Pachycereus militaris)
Morangaya pensilis (K.Brandegee) G.D.Rowley sec. Sánchez & al. 2014
Myrtillocactus cochal (Orcutt) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Baja California; Baja California Sur; Mexico
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Baja California Norte and Baja California Sur, Mexico. It grows at altitudes from sea level to 1,200 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in Mediterranean scrub to subtropical shrublands.
iucn_doc_threats: There is intense coastal development in the northern part of its range.
iucn_doc_rationale: Myrtillocactus cochal is widespread and common. Parts of its range have been lost, but this is not sufficient to warrant listing in a threatened category. Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Myrtillocactus geometrizans (Mart. ex Pfeiff.) Console sec. Hernández & Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011
iucn_locations: Tamaulipas; Querétaro; Oaxaca; Michoacán; Jalisco; Durango; Aguascalientes; Puebla; Hidalgo; San Luis Potosí; México State; Guanajuato; Nuevo León; México Distrito Federal; Veracruz; Zacatecas; Morelos; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-TA; MXE-QU; MXS-OA; MXS-MI; MXS-JA; MXE-DU; MXE-AG; MXC-PU; MXE-HI; MXE-SL; MXC-ME; MXE-GU; MXE-NL; MXC-DF; MXG-VC; MXE-ZA; MXC-MO; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Construction or structural materials; Food - animal; Food - human
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is widespread in the central states. It occurs in Aguascalientes, México Federal District, Durango, Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, México State, Michoacán, Nuevo León, Oaxaca, Puebla, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas, Veracruz, and is reported from the literature from Zacatecas (Hernández et al. 2004). Galindo-Becerril and Fernández-Nava (2002) report it for the state of Morelos in the municipality of Amacuzac. It grows at elevations of 1,000 to 2,000 m asl. It is known from 267 localities and has an area of occupancy of 51,000 km2 ( H. Hernández, C. Gómez-Hinostrosa, and M. Cházaro pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical deciduous forest, xerophyllous scrub and less frequently in grassland (Arias et al. 1997). It flowers between February and April and the flowers last for one day only.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species. Some habitats are disturbed by goats. The species has many different uses in the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán Valley, however the impact of harvest is low (Pérez-Negróna and Casas 2007).
iucn_doc_rationale: Myrtillocactus geometrizans is listed as Least Concern due to its wide distribution, because it is locally abundant, and because it is not declining at nearly the rate sufficient to qualify for listing in a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Myrtillocactus schenckii (J.A.Purpus) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in the Mexican States of Chiapas, Oaxaca and Puebla (Arias et al. 1997) and in Guatemala. It grows at elevations of 1,300 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical deciduous forest and xerophyllous scrub (Arias et al. 1997). The seeds are dispersed by birds, bats and humans.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats. However, traditional agro-silvicultural systems that enhances the maintenance of diversity are being substituted by practices that eliminate the vegetation cover, which does not benefit the species (Casas et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_rationale: Myrtillocactus schenckii is considered to be Least Concern due to relatively abundant populations, lack of threats and its occurrence in protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Neoraimondia arequipensis (Meyen) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Desert - Cold
iucn_threat_names: Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying
iucn_use_trade_names: Medicine - human & veterinary; Fuels; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru, where it occurs in the regions of Arequipa, Ayacucho, Cajamarca, Hauncavelica, Ica, Lima, La Libertad, Lambayique, Piura and Ancash. N. arequipensis grows at an altitude of 0 to 2,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: N. arequipensis is found on rocky coast line, coastal hills, desert scrub and inter-Andean Valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: In the southern parts of the populations the major threat is mining and is used as fire wood and for medicinal purposes.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Least Concern because it has a very wide range and even though there are declines and strong threats in parts of its range, these are localised and not sufficient to trigger a red listing.

checklist_taxon: Neoraimondia herzogiana (Backeb.) Buxb. & Krainz sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in Chuquisaca, Cochabamba, Santa Cruz and Tarija (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 800 to 2,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in moist chaco, mainly in plains and river valleys. It can also grow in disturbed areas used for cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat is trampling from cattle, however this impact is minor as it mainly affects the seedlings. The species is tolerated by humans.
iucn_doc_rationale: Neoraimondia herzogiana has a wide range, is abundant, and the population seems to be stable. Furthermore, there are no major threats affecting it. This cactus is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Neowerdermannia chilensis Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: The species is found in Moquegua, Peru and Arica y Parinacota, Chile, at elevations of  3,000 to 4,100 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in Andean shrubland on rocky and sandy soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for the species, but part of the range in Chile is potentially threatened by mining.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is wide ranging, is common and there are no major threats, hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Neowerdermannia vorwerkii Frič sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Jujuy; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Salta; Peru; Argentina
iucn_doc_range: According to Hunt et al. (2006) this species occurs in Argentina, in Jujuy and Salta, and in Oruro, Bolivia. Kiesling and Ferrari (2009) report it for the Puna of Jujuy, and western Salta (Argentina), Bolivia, and for a small portion of Peru. It occurs in the Puna region at elevations of 3,000 to 4,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a cryptic species. It grows buried in rocky soils in high-altitude grasslands (Santecchia and Rajal 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: It is reported by Hoffmann (1996) that up to 30,000 plants of this species are harvested each year for consumption during the festive season. However, it is not known how this extraction affects the population of this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Neowerdermannia vorwerkii is collected every year in great quantities, it is not clear how this is affecting its population. In many areas of its wide range it seems to be relatively abundant. Therefore, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Nyctocereus serpentinus (Lag. & Rodr.) Britton & Rose sec. Arias & Aquino 2019
Obregonia denegrii Frič sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2013
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Scale Unknown/Unrecorded; Roads & railroads; Housing & urban areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Medicine - human & veterinary; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Tamaulipas. It occurs at an altitude of about 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in semi-desert (or Tamaulipan Shrubland according to Anderson et al. (1994)), in the Valley of Jaumave. The generation length is 10 years.
iucn_doc_threats: Erosion is being accelerated by the grazing activities of livestock, and uprooted plants have been recorded as a result (Anderson et al. 1994). Illegal commercial collecting and collecting by local people affect some subpopulations. Road construction and urban development at one site may also pose a threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Obregonia denegrii is considered to be Endangered due to a very small geographic range (extent of occurrence ca 2,000 km2) and ongoing declines due to illegal overcollecting and habitat modification due to grazing. The area of occupancy is estimated to be 350 km² and there are no more than five subpopulations, all of them are found in one location. The population consists of less than 5,000 plants, and it continues to decrease due to collecting.

checklist_fullName: Opuntia abjecta Small ex Britton & Rose
checklist_taxon: Opuntia abjecta Small ex Britton & Rose sec. Majure & al. 2017
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Habitat shifting & alteration; Tourism & recreation areas; Recreational activities; Named species
iucn_doc_range: Opuntia abjecta occurs in Florida, USA, on Long Key, Crawl Key and Big Pine Key (Majure et al. in prep.). A fourth subpopulation was recorded on Big Munson Island, Florida (K. Bradley pers. obs.) but was not found during recent field work in 2010 and is thought to be extirpated from the area.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species mainly occurs in depressions in Key Largo limestone (Britton & Rose 1919).
iucn_doc_threats: Cactoblastis cactorum is a major threat to this species. Habitat destruction on Big Pine Key is exacerbated by a nearby fishing camp and road widening adjacent to the population. Kalanchoe, a non-native species, is encroaching on the rockland habitat at Big Pine Key. Sea level rise from climate change is a potential future threat. Key Deer have been observed to have adverse effects on the Big Pine Key population, through both herbivory and disturbance.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia abjecta is listed as Critically Endangered because it is known from three locations and its extent of occurrence is approximately 65 km2. Two populations are comprised of fewer than 50 mature individuals. Habitat quality is negatively affected by disturbance from humans, key deer, and non-native invasive plant species that reduce sexual reproductive capability.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia arechavaletae Speg. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Santa Catarina; Argentina; Rio Grande do Sul; Brazil; Entre Ríos; Buenos Aires; Uruguay
iucn_location_codes: BZS-SC; AR; BZS-RS; BR; AGE-ER; AGE-BA; UY
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Uruguay, southern Brazil (Río Grande do Sul and Santa Catarina), and eastern Argentina (Buenos Aires and Entre Ríos provinces) (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009). It grows at elevations from 100 to 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on cliffs at the edges of rivers, on rocky areas mainly in paranaense forest, and on roadsides.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a relatively large range, it is common and there are no major threats affecting it. Hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia atrispina Griffiths sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Coahuila, Mexico and in Texas, USA (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 450 to 600 m (Benson 1980). The Nature Serve reported from Chihuahuan Desert and to a lesser extent the Edwards Plateau and the Rio Grande Plain. Texas from Presidio Co. to Taylor and Uvalde Cos. and in Bee Co. It has an aproximate range extent of <100-250 square km (less than about 40 to 100 square miles)
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in grasslands and xerophyllous scrub in limestone soils of hills and alluvial fans in desert or grassland at 450-600 m..
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though the species has a narrow range, its successful sexual and vegetative reproductive strategies help keep stable populations. There are no major threats known either, hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia auberi Pfeiff. sec. Hunt 2016
Opuntia aurea E.M.Baxter sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_doc_range: This endemic to the United States is confined to the states of Arizona and Utah. In Utah it occurs in southeast Washington County and in south Kane County; in Arizona it occurs along the Utah border north of Pipe Spring, Mohave County. The species occurs at elevations of 1,500 to 1,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a clump forming shrub found in pinyon-juniper woodlands on red sands.
