iucn_doc_threats: There is some mining in the area where it occurs (surface mining for gypsum - but not on the red sands where this species  occurs) and much of the area is used for cattle grazing, but none of these pose a threat to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia aurea has a restricted range (its extent of occurrence is less than 5,000 km²), but it is abundant where it occurs and there are no significant threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern. Monitoring may be required to ensure that the threats do not increase as they could impact the species and cause a change in its conservation status.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia aureispina (S.Brack & K.D.Heil) Pinkava & B.D.Parfitt sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_synonyms: Opuntia macrocentra Engelm. var. aureispina S.Brack & K.D.Heil
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Texas, USA. It has also been reported from the literature from Coahuila, Mexico (Hernández et al. 2004). It grows at elevations of 5 to 600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on limestone in xerophyllous scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: No major threats are known for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though Opuntia aureispina has a limited range, its population is dense, there are no major threats, and it occurs within a protected area. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia austrina Small sec. Majure & al. 2017
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Temperate; Forest - Temperate; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Named species; Agro-industry farming
iucn_doc_range: Opuntia austrina is endemic to Florida, USA. It occurs throughout a wide range of elevations, from close to sea level to one of the highest places in Florida that is at least 24 m above sea level. The extent of occurrence of this species is approximately 34,000 km2. It is present in 8-12 locations.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species primarily occurs in Florida scrub habitat, such as on the Lake Wales ridge.
iucn_doc_threats: In certain areas the habitat has been fragmented by agriculture and citrus groves. The invasive cactus moth, Cactoblastus cactorum, is also a threat to the species. The majority of the area where the species was originally described is now urban, including the greater Miami, Florida area.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia austrina is is listed as Least Concern because it very widespread within its range and it occurs in protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia basilaris Engelm. & J.M.Bigelow sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_common_name: Beavertail Pricklypear; Nopal del Castor; Beavertail Prickly-pear
iucn_locations: Sonora; Nevada; Arizona; California; United States; Utah; Baja California; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXN-SO; NEV-OO; ARI-OO; CAL-OO; US; UTA-OO; MXN-BC; MX

iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in the south of Nevada, southwest of Utah, south of California and Arizona (United States) and the very north of Sonora and northern Baja California, Mexico (Paredes et al. 2000). It grows at elevations from 100 to 1,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in sandy or rocky soil with chipping in plains, valleys, streams and hills generally in hot deserts. In Sonora it grows in rocky and granite soil (Paredes et al. 2000). The Nature Serve report it from sandy soils, gravelly or rocky soils of plains, valleys, washes, or canyons in the desert, woodland, grassland.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no significant threats to the species as a whole, but the California subpopulations have been impacted by human development (urban expansion and off-road vehicles).
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia basilaris has a very wide range, is common and although there are threats in parts of its range which are impacting some of the varieties, there are no concerns for the species overall. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia boldinghii Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Trinidad and Tobago; Curaçao; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of
iucn_doc_range: Opuntia boldinghii is found in semi-arid and coastal regions of Venezuela, Curaçao in the Netherlands Antilles, and Trinidad (Benson 1982, Ponce 1989, Anderson 2001, Hunt et al. 2006, Pantaleón et al. 2009). It is present in approximately 11 locations. It occurs near sea level, up to 9.5 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: O. boldinghii grows in semi-arid and coastal areas (Pantaleón et al. 2009)
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia boldinghii is assessed as Least Concern as it has a wide range (its extent of occurrence is 200,000 km2) and it is known from approximately 11 locations. Although there is no information about current threats to this species, population levels and extent of occurrence preclude it from being considered threatened.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia bonaerensis Speg. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
Opuntia bravoana Baxter sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Sonora; Sinaloa; Baja California Sur; Mexico
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - animal; Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: A Mexican endemic, this species was originally known from the very southern part of Baja California Sur, but it is now known that it also occurs in the states of Sonora and Sinaloa (the specimens were confused with O. durangensis and O. wilcoxi) (R. Puente-Martinez pers. comm.).
iucn_doc_habitats: This shrubby species is found in coastal scrub and in dry subtropical forest.
iucn_doc_threats: Although there is agricultural activity within the range of the species (especially in Sinaloa) this does not pose a threat to this species, if anything it responds well to disturbance.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia bravoana is now known to be more widespread than originally thought and has a wide range, is reasonably common, and there are no significant threats. The species is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia camanchica Engelm. & J.M.Bigelow sec. A.M.Powell & Weedin 2004
Opuntia caracassana Salm-Dyck sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Curaçao; Sint Maarten (Dutch part); Colombia; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Trinidad and Tobago; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Sint Eustatius; Bonaire; El Salvador; Saint Martin (French part); Saba
iucn_location_codes: CW; SX; CO; BQ; TT; VE; NLA-SE; NLA-BO; SV; MF; NLA-SA
iucn_doc_range: Opuntia caracassana is found in the Netherlands Antilles, El Salvador, Venezuela and Trinidad and Tobago (Hunt et al. 2006). The record from El Salvador is dubious (Anderson 2001). It is also present in the northwest coast of Colombia (Santa Marta and Cartagena). It grows at altitudes between sea level and 1,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Opuntia caracassana is found in spiny scrublands and easily colonizes disturbed areas.  The species is highly clonal and also produces seeds. It is pollinated by insects and dispersed by birds (J. Nassar pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia caracassana is assessed as Least Concern as it has a wide distribution and there are no known major threats affecting its population.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia cespitosa Raf. sec. Majure & al. 2017
Opuntia chaffeyi Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Agro-industry farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is restricted to a small area (ca. 63 km²) in the state of Zacatecas, Mexico. Only three separate localities are known.
iucn_doc_habitats: Grows in the silty flood plain of a dried lake with scarce desert vegetation, mostly Prosopis and Larrea.
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat destruction mostly by cattle is a major threat. The species is also harvested for medicinal purposes. The area could potentially be impacted by agricultural expansion if bore holes are sunk to get water for irrigation.
iucn_doc_rationale: The known distribution of the species is extremely narrow (extent of occurrence is ca 63 km² and the area of occupancy is ca 12 km²), and it is known from three separate localities which are not severely fragmented. The estimated number of individuals is critically low, only about 15 mature individuals in total. The habitat is expected to decline in the future mostly due to cattle and agricultural development.

iucn_common_name: Dollar-joint Pricklypear; Pancake Pricklypear; Nopal Verdoso; Dollar-joint Prickly-pear
iucn_locations: United States; California; Baja California Sur; Mexico; Coahuila; Baja California; New Mexico; Utah; Chihuahua; Nevada; Sonora; Arizona

iucn_doc_range:
This species is widely distributed, occurring in the Mexican states of Chihuahua, Coahuila, Baja California, Baja California Sur and Sonora, and in the United States in Arizona, California, Nevada, New Mexico and Utah (Hernández et al. 2004). It grows at elevations of 800 to 2,300 m asl.
The occurrence of the species in the Baja Islands needs to be confirmed.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on igneous substrates from xerophyllous scrub to coniferous forests, in rocky or sandy soils of ledges, steep slopes, canyons, or sometimes flats in the desert or just above it.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats for this species. However, a potential threat to species of the genus Opuntia is the invasion of the cactus moth Cactoblastis cactorum, which can exterminate populations completely (Zimmerman et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia chlorotica has a very wide range, is abundant, and there are no major threats affecting it. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.

iucn_doc_range: This species is found in Argentina in Corrientes, Entre Ríos, Buenos Aires, Santiago del Estero, Chaco, Formosa, Córdoba and Santa Fe. It also occurs in Brazil in Rio Grande do Sul and in Paraguay, Bolivia and Uruguay (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at elevations between 0 and 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in forests and savannas of the Chaco region. It is favored by anthropogenic activities.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species. It is invasive in Australia and the Mediterranean region.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia elata is listed as Least Concern because is widely distributed, it is locally common and abundant, and there are no major threats affecting it. It is present in many protected areas and is invasive in Australia and the Mediterranean Region.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia elatior Mill. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Sint Eustatius; Saint Martin (French part); Sint Maarten (Dutch part); Colombia; Aruba; Saba; Panama; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Bonaire
iucn_location_codes: NLA-SE; MF; SX; CO; AW; NLA-SA; PA; VE; BQ; NLA-BO
iucn_habitat_names: Artificial/Terrestrial - Rural Gardens; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range:
This species occurs in Colombia, Costa Rica, Lesser Antilles, Netherlands Antilles, Panama, and Venezuela (Hunt et al. 2006). In Colombia it is distributed in the semiarid Andean region of La Tatacoa, in the Huila Department (A. S. Albesiano pers. comm. 2010, Figueroa and Galeano 2007). In Venezuela, the species is reported to occur in the state of Bolivar (Díaz and Chitty 2007, Ponce 1989).
Its distribution in the eastern Lesser Antilles is excluded from this assessment because it is dubious. According to B. Hammel (pers. comm. 2009), the Costa Rica record is incorrect; it is a misidentification or a misnaming, and is probably Opuntia lutea. B. Hammel (pers. comm. 2009) also thinks the Panama record is unlikely. Opuntia elatior may be an introduced species in Australia (Britton and Rose 1919, Anderson 2001).
iucn_doc_habitats: In Colombia, this cactus is known from open areas and dry gullies in the semiarid region of La Tatacoa (A.S. Albesiano pers. comm. 2010). It has been distributed widely by humans (Anderson 2001). No additional information is available.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is not thought to be facing major threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia elatior is assessed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, has been distributed widely by humans, and is not facing major threats at present.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia excelsa Sánchez-Mej. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is known only from tropical deciduous forests in the states of Colima and Jalisco (Hunt et al. 2006, Padilla-Velarde et al. 2006). Padilla-Velarde et al. (2006) also report the species to occur in the state of Nayarit.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in tropical deciduous forest. The numbers of fruits produced by Oputia excelsa ranges from 0 to 479 (Reyes-Argüero et al. 2006), and the plants may start producing fruits when they have 33 cladodes (Bullock and Martijena 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia excelsa is listed as Least Concern as it is common and abundant in its limited range and there are no major threats affecting it.

iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Canada in British Columbia, to W. Manitoba; near Kaladar, Ontario. In the United States, it can be found in Washington, Colorado, Utah, Michigan, Illinois, California, Nevada, Arizona, New Mexico, Kansas, Oklahoma, Texas, Oregon, Idaho, Indiana, Iowa, Montana, Wyoming, Minnesota, Wisconsin, Nebraska, and North and South Dakota. It grows at altitudes between sea level and 2,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in sandy, gravelly, or rocky soils of valleys, low hills, or mountainsides. It has been recorded on outcrops of granite, quartzite and basalt. It grows in Ponderosa Pine, juniper, and pinyon woodlands, temperate grasslands, sage scrub and also out in barren rocky areas. It flowers infrequently.
iucn_doc_threats: Given the wide range of this species in largely uninhabited areas there are no significant threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia fragilis is a very widespread species and although scattered, it is locally common with no significant threats. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia fuliginosa Griffiths sec. Hunt 2016

iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Unspecified species; Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: Opuntia fuliginosa is endemic to Mexico. It is widespread in the southwest, occurring in the states of Hidalgo, Guerrero, Guanajuato,  Jalisco, Michoacán, Morelos, Nayarit, Oaxaca and Querétaro (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in dry deciduous forest and dry shrubland. It occurs in lowland areas and on steep slopes (Puente-Ovalle and Miranda-Medrano 2005).
iucn_doc_threats: The species has only localized threats, which do not apply to its whole range. In the municipality of Autlán de Navarro, Jalisco, the species has   several uses as food, medicine and fuel in several communities   (Puente-Ovalle and Miranda-Medrano 2005). All products are extracted from wild populations, which are under  pressure from land use changes, senescence, pests, and diseases  (Puente-Ovalle and Miranda-Medrano 2005).
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia fuliginosa is a widespread and abundant species with only localized threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia galapageia Hemsl. sec. Anderson & Walkington 1971
iucn_synonyms: Opuntia helleri K.Schum; Opuntia insularis Stewart; Opuntia megasperma J.T.Howell; Opuntia myriacantha F.A.C.Weber; Opuntia saxicola J.T.Howell; Opuntia galapageia Hemsl. var. barringtonensis E.Y.Dawson; Opuntia galapageia Hemsl. var. galapageia; Opuntia galapageia Hemsl. var. gigantea (J.T.Howell) D.M.Porter; Opuntia galapageia Hemsl. var. inermis E.Y.Dawson; Opuntia galapageia Hemsl. var. macrocarpa E.Y.Dawson; Opuntia galapageia Hemsl. var. mesophytica J.Lundh; Opuntia galapageia Hemsl. var. myriacantha (F.A.C.Weber); Opuntia galapageia Hemsl. var. orientalis (J.T.Howell) D.M.Porter; Opuntia galapageia Hemsl. var. megasperma (J.T.Howell) D.M.Porter; Opuntia galapageia Hemsl. var. profusa E.F.Anderson & Walk.; Opuntia galapageia Hemsl. var. zacana E.F.Anderson & Walk.
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Housing & urban areas; Named species
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs on the Galápagos Islands, Ecuador. It is present in Darwin,Wolf, Pinta, Pinzón, Rábida, Santiago, Isabela, Santa Cruz, Santa Fé, Marchena and Genovesa islands at elevations ranging from sea level up to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: A shrubby or tree-like succulent species which grows in dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is predation by feral animals such as donkeys and goats. However, eradication control has been operating and the species shows signs of recovery in most places. Conversion of some habitat to urban areas or agriculture has resulted in the loss of plants.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species occurs across all of the Galápagos islands, it is common in some places, but some of the subpopulations (treated as varieties) are very scarce and have undergone significant reductions in the past due to the impacts of various threats, including feral animals and expanding agriculture and urbanization. However, overall the species as a whole is fairly common and is sufficiently secure on many of the islands and most of the threats identified are now largely past threats, hence it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the state of Sonora (Paredes et al. 2000). There was a reported record from southern Arizona (Pima County), but it corresponds to O. santarita. It grows from sea level to 900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in Sonoran Desert scrub, coastal scrub and foothills (Paredes et al. 2000), in desert grassland, gravelly slopes, rocky hillsides, outwash bajadas, and edges of Sonoran Desert in sandy or gravelly soils (Shreve and Wiggins 1964, Martin et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Opuntia gosseliniana has a wide range, it is sparsely distributed. However, it occurs in a variety of different habitats and there are no significant threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Costa Rica, Guatemala, Honduras and Nicaragua.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in deciduous tropical lowland deciduous forest, on coastal rocks, and in savannah.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened in parts of its range by habitat destruction for development.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide range, is not severely fragmented, is known from many locations and is abundant where found. The threats are not major at this stage hence is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_threats: Major threats to this species include habitat loss and overcollection for ornamental purposes.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia humifusa is assessed as Least Concern due to its wide distribution and large number of locations.

iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species, some subpopulations are declining due to habitat loss.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia hyptiacantha is listed as Least Concern in light of its presumed abundance and wide distribution.

Opuntia jamaicensis Britton & Harris sec. Guiggi & Mariotti 2022
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Jamaica, from Saint Catherine (Britton and Rose 1919). It occurs from sea level to eight meters (Adams 1972).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in plains in tropical dry shrubland. Adams (1972) reports that it forms pure stands near mangrove and logwood thickets and salinas in heavy silt alluvium.
iucn_doc_threats: There is no information on threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because little is known about it. However, its range is restricted to a small area in Jamaica and it is possible that this species is threatened.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to a very small area (Sierra de la Laguna) in the Cape Region of Baja California Sur, Mexico. It grows at altitudes between 500 and 600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in tropical dry deciduous forest on granitic soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to the species as it occurs high up the mountains in a protected area.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Opuntia lagunae has a restricted range (its extent of occurrence is about 500 km²), there are no threats to the species and it occurs entirely within a protected area. The species is therefore listed as Least Concern.

It is found between 1,400 and 2,600 m asl (S. Arias pers. comm. 2009). A subpopulation exists in Chiapas, which is likely out of the native range of the species.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in lowland forest and xerophyllous scrub. In Chiapas, it is found in tropical dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species. A potential threat to species of the genus Opuntia is the invasion of the non-native cactus moth Cactoblastis cactorum, which can exterminate populations completely (Zimmerman et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia lasiacantha is listed as Least Concern because it is common, widespread, there are no major threats, and it is found in several protected areas.

iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in California (United States) and in Baja California and just into Baja California Sur (Mexico). It grows at altitudes between 10 and 400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species grows in coastal sage scrub and chaparral on sandy or rocky soils of hills, beaches, and bluffs.
It is highly clonal and responds well to any disturbance - it can be very weedy as it covers whole hill-sides. It recovers well after fires.
iucn_doc_threats: The habitat of the species is being lost due to urban expansion along the Californian coast, however, this is not thought to pose a significant threat to this weedy species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia littoralis has a fairly wide range, and is considered a weed in places, so although there are threats to the habitat, these are not considered to be significant enough to warrant a threatened listing. The species is therefore listed as Least Concern.

It has also been reported in Baja California, Mexico and occurs at altitudes between sea level and 500 m asl.

iucn_doc_threats: There is habitat loss in places due to expanding urban areas, but this is not considered a significant threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia oricola has a fairly wide range, is locally common, and tolerant of disturbance. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia pachyrrhiza H.M.Hern., Gómez-Hin. & Bárcenas sec. Majure & R.Puente 2014
iucn_locations: Querétaro; San Luis Potosí; Nuevo León; Mexico
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs on the summits of La Trinidad and El Azul mountain ranges, in San Luis Potosí, at one locality in Querétaro, and also in Nuevo León, Mexico. The species is known from twenty localities.
iucn_doc_habitats:
It is found in areas of disturbed grassland and chaparral of Quercus striatula and Agave asperrima on the tops of mountains. The soils are relatively deep-dark clay, with limestone outcrops.

The cladodes of this species die back in the winter months and regrow in the spring, this is an adaptation to freezing and the impacts of fires.
iucn_doc_threats: The natural vegetation is almost totally modified, due to livestock raising.  The species is under some pressure due to its medicinal uses.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species qualifies as Endangered because it has a small extent of occurrence (ca 4,500 km²), its population is severely fragmented, the quality of its habitat is expected to decline in the long term due to the intensification of human activities, and there is a reduction in the number of individuals due to collecting for medicinal use.

iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Construction or structural materials; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican states of Oaxaca, Tlaxcala and Puebla, where it is found at elevations of 900 to 1,800 m asl (Arias et al. 1997, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in vegetation dominated by xerophyllous elements such as tetechera, mezquital, throrn scrub, tetechera candelillar and izotal, and prefers limestone soils (Arias et al. 1997, Paredes-Flores et al. 2007). It flowers between March and July (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for the species, it tolerates habitat modifications and propagates vegetatively, easily recolonizing disturbed areas.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia pilifera is listed as Least Concern due to its wide range, relatively abundant population, and occurrence in protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia pinkavae B.D.Parfitt sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: Opuntia pinkavae is not uncommon in northwestern Arizona (Coconino and Mohave Counties) and extreme southwestern Utah, United States. It occurs at elevations of 1,370 to 1,560 m asl (Parfitt 1997).
iucn_doc_habitats: This shrub occurs from the arid grasslands to the margins of pinyon-juniper woodlands. It is also found to persist in grasslands that have been damaged by excessive grazing and subsequent [soil] erosion. The substrate is usually fine, red sand (Parfitt 1997) or limestone loam soils.
iucn_doc_threats: No threats to this species are known.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Opuntia pinkavae has a restricted range (its extent of occurrence is less than 5,000 km²), the species is locally common and there do not appear to be any threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia pittieri Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the western mountain range in Colombia, although its distribution is potentially wider. The type locality is from Dagua valley (Britton and Rose 1919). There is no elevation information available.
iucn_doc_habitats: It has been recorded from tropical dry forest (Ruiz et al. 2002).
iucn_doc_threats: No information is available about this species threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because there is not enough information to assess it. Research is needed to adequately evaluate the conservation status of this species.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia polyacantha Haw. sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_locations: Oklahoma; New Mexico; Utah; California; Wyoming; Chihuahua; Idaho; Nevada; Canada; Alberta; Colorado; Washington; Arizona; South Dakota; North Dakota; Saskatchewan; United States; Texas; Nebraska; Kansas; British Columbia; Mexico; Montana
iucn_location_codes: OKL-OO; NWM-OO; UTA-OO; CAL-OO; WYO-OO; MXE-CU; IDA-OO; NEV-OO; CA; ABT-OO; COL-OO; WAS-OO; ARI-OO; SDA-OO; NDA-OO; SAS-OO; US; TEX-OO; NEB-OO; KAN-OO; BRC-OO; MX; MNT-OO
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Temperate; Grassland - Temperate
iucn_doc_range: This is a wide-ranging species. It can be found in Chihuahua, Mexico and in the southwestern United States, from Texas and New Mexico extending north to Washington and Wyoming (Hernández et al. 2004) and Canada. It grows at elevations of 300 to 2,400 m asl (Benson 1982).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in grasslands, pine-juniper forests, sagebrush, xerophyllous scrub and lower montane forest. It grows on sandy soils of flats, washes, canyonsides, and hillsides in the desert, in woodlands, and on plains.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species. A potential threat to species of the genus Opuntia is the invasion of the cactus moth Cactoblastis cactorum, which can exterminate populations completely (Zimmerman et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia polyacantha has a very wide range, is abundant, and there are no threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia pottsii Salm-Dyck sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_locations: Arizona; Texas; United States; New Mexico; Chihuahua; Sonora; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: ARI-OO; TEX-OO; US; NWM-OO; MXE-CU; MXN-SO; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species has been reported from the state of Chihuahua in Mexico (the species probably also occurs in the state of Sonora as there is suitable habitat), and from the states of Arizona, New Mexico and Texas, in the United States (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in sandy soil, alluvial hillsides and plains, in desert scrubs and grasslands (mesquite and yucca grassland) (Paredes et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no significant threats to this species although some areas would have been impacted by agricultural activities and there may be some localized declines.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia pottsii has a wide range, and although a cryptic species it is probably reasonably common and resistant to most threats as it has underground tubers. Agricultural activities would have impacted some areas, but not significantly enough to warrant a more threatened listing. The species is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia puberula Pfeiff. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Jalisco; Oaxaca; Guatemala; Puebla; Sinaloa; Chiapas; Guerrero; Nayarit; Mexico; Michoacán; Querétaro
iucn_location_codes: MXS-JA; MXS-OA; GT; MXC-PU; MXN-SI; MXT-CI; MXS-GR; MXS-NA; MX; MXS-MI; MXE-QU
iucn_doc_range: This is a widespread species in western Mexico and Guatemala (Hunt et al. 2006). It is known from the Mexican states of Chiapas, Guerrero, Jalisco, Michoacán, Nayarit, Oaxaca, Puebla, Querétaro, and Sinaloa (Hernández et al. 2004). However, the specific distribution of this species is unknown due to taxonomic uncertainty (T. Terrazas, M. Cházaro, S. Arias and H. Arreola  pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in low tropical deciduous forest (Pérez-García et al. 2001). Like other Opuntias, it presents a low germination response (Godínez-Álvarez and Valiente-Banuet 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: No major threats are known to affect this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia puberula is listed as Least Concern due to its abundance and its widespread distribution in Mexico. It urgently needs taxonomic clarification.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia pubescens H.L.Wendl. ex Pfeiff. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Argentina; San Luis Potosí; Guanajuato; Mexico; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Guatemala; Sinaloa; Ecuador; Oaxaca; Peru; Ecuador (mainland); Chiapas; Nayarit; Puebla; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Veracruz; Tamaulipas; Paraguay; Hidalgo; Guerrero; Sonora; Querétaro; Jalisco
iucn_location_codes: AR; MXE-SL; MXE-GU; MX; BO; GT; MXN-SI; EC; MXS-OA; PE; ECU-OO; MXT-CI; MXS-NA; MXC-PU; VE; MXG-VC; MXE-TA; PY; MXE-HI; MXS-GR; MXN-SO; MXE-QU; MXS-JA
iucn_habitat_names: Artificial/Terrestrial - Pastureland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_doc_range: This widespread species occurs in Bolivia, Ecuador, Guatemala, Mexico, Peru, Paraguay, and Venezuela, at altitudes from 0 to 2,500 m (Hunt et al. 2006). In Mexico the species is found in the states of Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, Oaxaca, Puebla, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Sonora, and Tamaulipas (Hernández et al. 2004).  Other authors report the species from Chiapas (Breedlove 1987) and Veracruz (Sosa and Gómez-Pompa 1994). It has also been recorded from Guerrero, Nayarit and Sinaloa (Guzmán et al. 2003). In Venezuela, its distribution is patchy and it occurs in the states of Lara, Mérida, and Táchira.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in dry deciduous forest and dry shrublands. It also grows in disturbed habitats, such as on roadsides and on dry overgrazed mountains (Pin and Simon 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species; it is very tolerant of disturbance.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a very widespread and abundant species that is tolerant to disturbance. Hence, Opuntia pubescens is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia pumila Rose sec. Majure & al. 2012
Opuntia pusilla (Haw.) Haw. sec. Majure 2021
iucn_locations: North Carolina; Florida; South Carolina; Georgia; Alabama; Mississippi; United States
iucn_location_codes: NCA-OO; FLA-OO; SCA-OO; GEO-OO; ALA-OO; MSI-OO; US
iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Coastal Sand Dunes; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: The species is widely distributed in the southeastern United States, where it occurs in coastal regions from Mississippi to North Carolina. Its range includes northern Florida and presumably Galveston, Texas (Benson 1982) although this has not been verified.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in sandy habitats on coastal dunes, beaches, and woods near sea level. It also occurs in open areas and along edges of deciduous forests. There are numerous populations that occur in sandy areas along large rivers (L. Majure pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_threats: The cactus moth Cactoblastus cactorum occurs within the range of this species and may be having a significant impact on this cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia pusilla is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed and there are no major threats at present.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia pycnantha Engelm. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Isla Magdalena and Isla Santa Margarita area in southwestern Baja California Sur, Mexico. There is another collection from the other side of the Peninsula by Raul Puente (pers. comm. 2010) but the identification needs confirmation. It grows at altitudes between sea level and 50 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a prostrate shrub growing in coastal scrub in sandy saline soils and on dunes, often in association with Atriplex species.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species, as there are few people in the area apart from fishermen and tourists who go to the area to dive. The only possible threat might be hurricanes, but the species is unlikely to be impacted by those - they are more likely to break up and disperse the pads.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia pycnantha has a restricted range (its extent of occurrence is less than 2,000 km²) but it is locally common and there are no threats to the species. Furthermore, it grows on islands that are given protection. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia quimilo K.Schum. sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Corrientes; Chaco; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; La Rioja; Santiago del Estero; Catamarca; Santa Fé; Paraguay; Formosa; Tucumán; Argentina; Jujuy; Salta
iucn_location_codes: AGE-CN; AGE-CH; BO; AGW-LR; AGW-SE; AGW-CA; AGE-SF; PY; AGE-FO; AGW-TU; AR; AGW-JU; AGW-SA
iucn_doc_range: Hunt et al. (2006) reports the species for Argentina, in Catamarca, Chaco, Corrientes, Formosa, Jujuy, La Rioja, Salta, Santiago del Estero, Santa Fe, and Tucumán; in Bolivia, in Tarija; and in Paraguay. Pin and Simon (2004) found the species in Paraguay in the Boquerón and Alto Paraguay departments. This species occurs at elevations between 500 and 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: In Paraguay this cactus grows in sandy soil, in the xerophilous Chacoan forest (Pin and Simon 2004). Kiesling and Ferrari (2009) report that it grows in alkaline and salty soils, sometimes with Stetsonia coryne and Cleistocactus baumannii.
iucn_doc_threats: In some areas it may be affected by deforestation.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia quimilo is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread and abundant, and there are no major threats affecting it. It is favored by anthropogenic activities, such as livestock grazing, because it is dispersed by animals. It occurs in many protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia quitensis F.A.C.Weber sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: Opuntia quitensis occurs in Colombia (Fernández-Alonso and Estupiñán 2005), Peru (Apurimac department) and the Provinces of El Oro, Pichincha, Chimborazo, Azuay, and Loja in Ecuador. It grows at altitudes of 0 to 3,000 m asl (Loaiza et al. 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: In Ecuador, O. quitensis grows in dry scrublands, dry mountain forests and dry inter-Andean forests (Aguirre et al. 2006). It is dioecious (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is not exposed to any threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia quitensis is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed and locally abundant, occurs in many protected areas, and there are no major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia rastrera F.A.C.Weber sec. Hernández & al. 2014
Opuntia repens Bello sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Puerto Rico; Virgin Islands, British; Virgin Islands, U.S.
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range:
Opuntia repens is found in the Puerto Rican archipelago (Hunt et al. 2006), Salt Island in the British Virgin Islands, Tobago, and Little Hans Lollick Island in the US Virgin Islands (Heatwole et al. 1981). The record in Tobago is dubious because it is disjunct and the substrate is very different from other occurrences (P. Griffith pers. comm. 2011). Therefore, the Tobago distribution has been excluded from this assessment. 
This species has been found in on the US Virgin Islands of St. Thomas, Tortola, Virgen Gorda, Culebra, Monito, Desecheo, Mona, Caja de Muertos, and Vieques (Acevedo-Rodriguez et al. 1996). In Puerto Rico it has been observed between 1 and 20 m asl but there is no additional information on elevation from the rest of the occurrences. The species is present in approximately 10 to 12 locations.
iucn_doc_habitats: Opuntia repens is a crawling plant (Heatwole et al. 1981) that forms thickets (Hunt et al. 2006). In St. Thomas it was observed on rocky outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no specific major threats to this species. However, it may be affected by habitat loss due to human expansion and tourism.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia repens has a relatively wide range (its extent of occurrence is 27,000 km2), and no specific major threats are known to be affecting it. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia rioplatensis Font sec. Hunt 2016
Opuntia robusta Pfeiff. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Coahuila; México Distrito Federal; Morelos; Jalisco; Veracruz; United States; Guanajuato; San Luis Potosí; Mexico; Nuevo León; Durango; Querétaro; Hidalgo; Tamaulipas; Sonora; Aguascalientes; Michoacán; Tlaxcala; Arizona; Chihuahua; Puebla; Zacatecas; México State
iucn_location_codes: MXE-CO; MXC-DF; MXC-MO; MXS-JA; MXG-VC; US; MXE-GU; MXE-SL; MX; MXE-NL; MXE-DU; MXE-QU; MXE-HI; MXE-TA; MXN-SO; MXE-AG; MXS-MI; MXC-TL; ARI-OO; MXE-CU; MXC-PU; MXE-ZA; MXC-ME
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Temperate; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is widely distributed in arid and semi-arid regions. In Mexico it occurs in Aguascalientes, Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, México, Michoacán, Morelos, Nuevo León, Puebla, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Sonora, Tamaulipas, Tlaxcala, Veracruz, Zacatecas, and México Federal District. In the United States it is found in Arizona (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in arid and semi-arid regions mainly in xerophyllous and crassulacean vegetation, but also in Pinus and Quercus forests and grasslands (Enríquez-Enríquez and Koch 2003, Castillo-Argüero et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no current major threats to this species. A potential threat to species of the genus Opuntia is the invasion of the cactus moth Cactoblastis cactorum, which can exterminate populations completely (Zimmerman et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia robusta is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread, common, has no major threats, is found in numerous protected areas, and is spread by human activities.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia rufida Engelm. sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_locations: Chihuahua; Coahuila; Mexico; United States; Zacatecas; Texas; Durango
iucn_location_codes: MXE-CU; MXE-CO; MX; US; MXE-ZA; TEX-OO; MXE-DU
iucn_doc_range: The species is distributed in the Mexican states of Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango and Zacatecas, and in the United States in Texas (Hernández et al. 2004). It grows at elevations of 600 to 1,050 m asl (Benson 1982).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in xerophyllous scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: No major threats are known. A potential threat to species of the genus Opuntia is the invasion of the cactus moth Cactoblastis cactorum, which can exterminate populations completely (Zimmerman et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia rufida has a very wide range, is abundant, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia sanguinea Proctor sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: Opuntia sanguinea is endemic to Jamaica (Hunt et al. 2006). It appears to be restricted to St. Thomas Parrish in Jamaica, which is the only locality from where it has been reported (Proctor 1982). It is only known from one location at an elevation of 100 meters.
iucn_doc_threats: Cactoblastis cactorum is known from other species in Jamaica and is possibly affecting O. sanguinea. There is no additional information available on threats affecting this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because it is only known from its type locality and information on population, distribution, and threats is lacking.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia scheeri F.A.C.Weber sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in Mexico in the states of Aguascalientes, Guanajuato, Jalisco and Querétaro (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is very likely distributed in shrublands.
iucn_doc_threats: A potential threat to species of the genus Opuntia is the invasion of the cactus moth Cactoblastis cactorum, which can completely exterminate populations (Zimmermann et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: It is currently not possible to assess Opuntia scheeri due to uncertainty about the taxonomic status, distribution and population size. Hence, it is listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia schumannii F.A.C.Weber ex A.Berger sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: Opuntia schumannii is found in Venezuela (Hunt et al. 2006, Ponce 1989) and Colombia (Anderson 2001, Britton and Rose 1919), although precise locality information is unknown.  In Colombia, O. schumannii, is distributed in the Boyaca Department in the semiarid region within the Villa de Leyva municipality (Albesiano, Adriana,S. pers. comm.). This species is only known from two localities. Its area of occupancy is approximately 850 km2. The area of occupancy was calculated from the known area of suitable habitat and excludes urban areas in Colombia and Venezuela (Ruiz et. al 2002).
iucn_doc_habitats: It only occurs in arid zones in tropical dry forest in the Tachira region of Venezuela and Cucuta in Colombia (Ponce 1989). In Colombia, it has been recorded growing along roadsides in open areas (Albeziano, Adriana,S. pers. comm.).
iucn_doc_threats: In Cucuta, the species is threatened by urbanization and mining. In Venezuela, urbanization is the major threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because its area of occupancy is estimated to be only 850 km<sup>2</sup>, it is only known from two locations close to the Colombia-Venezuela border, and its habitat is threatened by mining and urbanization.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia setispina Engelm. ex Salm-Dyck sec. Majure 2021
Opuntia soederstromiana Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: Opuntia soederstromiana is endemic to Ecuador (Chimborazo, Cotopaxi, Manabí, Napo, Pichincha, Santo Domingo and Tungurahua provinces), where it grows at altitudes between 1,000 and 3,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in dry forests at high altitudes.
iucn_doc_threats: This species may be affected by agriculture and cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia soederstromiana is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed and locally abundant, occurs in many protected areas, and there are no major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia stenopetala Engelm. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Coahuila; Zacatecas; Nuevo León; Guanajuato; Querétaro; Hidalgo; Tamaulipas; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-SL; MXE-CO; MXE-ZA; MXE-NL; MXE-GU; MXE-QU; MXE-HI; MXE-TA; MX
iucn_doc_range: This wide-ranging species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the Chihuahuan Desert region from Querétaro and Hidalgo to Coahuila (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in xerophyllous scrub and matorral.
iucn_doc_threats: No major threats are known. A potential threat to species of the genus Opuntia is the invasion of the cactus moth Cactoblastis cactorum, which can exterminate populations completely (Zimmerman et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia stenopetala is listed as Least Concern as it is widespread and abundant across its range, and because it is not exposed to any major threat.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia streptacantha Lem. sec. Martínez-González & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Aguascalientes; Jalisco; Michoacán; Querétaro; Guanajuato; Oaxaca; Tamaulipas; Hidalgo; San Luis Potosí; Zacatecas; Puebla; Durango; México State; Nuevo León; Tlaxcala; México Distrito Federal; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-AG; MXS-JA; MXS-MI; MXE-QU; MXE-GU; MXS-OA; MXE-TA; MXE-HI; MXE-SL; MXE-ZA; MXC-PU; MXE-DU; MXC-ME; MXE-NL; MXC-TL; MXC-DF; MX
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico where it is widely distributed, It occurs in the states of Aguascalientes, Mexico Federal District, Durango, Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Jalisco, Mexico State, Michoacán, Nuevo León, Oaxaca, Puebla, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas, Tlaxcala and Zacatecas (Hunt et al. 2006), It grows at elevations of 1,600 to 2,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in xerophyllous shrublands.
iucn_doc_threats: Some extensions of "nopaleras" have been covered into agricultural lands. A potential threat to species of the genus Opuntia is the invasion of the cactus moth Cactoblastis cactorum, which can exterminate populations completely (Zimmerman et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia streptacantha is listed as Least Concern due to a wide distribution, and because it is locally abundant and not declining at nearly the rate sufficient to qualify for listing in a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia stricta (Haw.) Haw. sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_locations: Texas; Australia; Campeche; Jamaica; South Carolina; Ecuador; Georgia; Florida; Dominican Republic; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Brazil; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Yucatán; Tabasco; Alabama; Veracruz; Virgin Islands, U.S.; Oaxaca; Querétaro; Sint Maarten (Dutch part); Paraíba; Alagoas; Cuba; Bahia; Bahamas; South Africa; Mexico; Bonaire; San Luis Potosí; Sergipe; Pernambuco; Tamaulipas; Saint Martin (French part); United States; Sint Eustatius; Cayman Islands; Saba; Quintana Roo; Curaçao
iucn_location_codes: TEX-OO; AU; MXT-CA; JM; SCA-OO; EC; GEO-OO; FLA-OO; DO; BQ; BR; VE; MXT-YU; MXT-TB; ALA-OO; MXG-VC; VI; MXS-OA; MXE-QU; SX; BZE-PB; BZE-AL; CU; BZE-BA; BS; ZA; MX; NLA-BO; MXE-SL; BZE-SE; BZE-PE; MXE-TA; MF; US; NLA-SE; KY; NLA-SA; MXT-QR; CW
iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Coastal Sand Dunes; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Construction or structural materials; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range:
This species is widespread, occurring in the Bahamas, Brazil, Cayman Islands, Cuba, Dominican Republic, Ecuador (Guayas and Manabí provinces), Jamaica, Lesser Antilles, Mexico, Netherland Antilles, United States, US Virgin Islands, and Venezuela (Hunt et al. 2006). It is generally found close to sea level, but there are some records from higher elevations, up to 1,000 m, in Querétaro, Mexico.In Mexico, this species occurs in the states of Campeche, Querétaro, Quintana Roo, San Luis Potosí, Veracruz, and Yucatán (Hunt et al. 2006). Records from Oaxaca and Tamaulipas require verification. In the United States, O. stricta occurs in the states of Alabama, Florida, Georgia, South Carolina, and Texas (Hunt et al. 2006). In Brazil it can be found in the states of Paraíba, Pernambuco, Alagoas, Sergipe, and Bahia (Taylor and Zappi 2004).  It is introduced and invasive in Europe, Africa, and Australia.

The species is thought to be introduced in Ecuador (N.P. Taylor pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs on coastal dunes and tropical dry forest. It adapts well to modified and degraded habitats in areas near sea level.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species. A potential threat to species of the genus Opuntia is the invasion of the cactus moth Cactoblastis cactorum, which can completely extirpate populations (Zimmermann et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia stricta is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread, common, has no major threats, and is found in many protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia strigil Engelm. sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_doc_range: The species is found in Texas (United States) and expected to occur in Coahuila (Mexico). It grows at elevations of 900 to 1,350 m asl (Benson 1982).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in xerophyllous scrub on limestone.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though Opuntia strigil has a limited range, it is abundant and there are no known threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia sulphurea Gillies ex Salm-Dyck sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Mendoza; San Juan; Santiago del Estero; Tucumán; Corrientes; Buenos Aires; Córdoba; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Santa Fé; Formosa; San Luis; Neuquén; Jujuy; Uruguay; Salta; Rio Negro; La Rioja; Paraguay; Chaco; Argentina; Catamarca
iucn_location_codes: AGW-ME; AGW-SJ; AGW-SE; AGW-TU; AGE-CN; AGE-BA; AGE-CO; BO; AGE-SF; AGE-FO; AGW-SL; AGS-NE; AGW-JU; UY; AGW-SA; AGS-RN; AGW-LR; PY; AGE-CH; AR; AGW-CA
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Other (free text); Food - animal
iucn_doc_range: According to Hunt et al. (2006) the species occurs in Argentina in Catamarca, Chaco, Córdoba, Jujuy, La Rioja, Mendoza, Neuquén, Río Negro, Salta, Santiago del Estero, Santa Fe, Corrientes, Formosa, Buenos Aires, San Luis, San Juan, and Tucumán. In Bolivia it occurs in Cochabamba, Santa Cruz, and Tarija. In Paraguay, it is present in Boquerón and Alto Paraguay departments (Pin and Simon 2004). It is also present in western Uruguay. Kiesling and Ferrari (2009) report that it grows between sea level and 3,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in stony areas in dry places, such as hillsides and clearings of the xerophilous mountains. Markedly nitrophilous, it likes areas that are eutrophied due to livestock (Pin and Simon 2004). It also grows in clayey soils.
iucn_doc_threats: Some marginal subpopulations may be affected by agricultural activities. However, this does not represent a major threat to the species. It is favored by livestock grazing.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia sulphurea is listed as Least Concern given that it is widely distributed, is common and abundant throughout its range, and there are no major threats affecting it. It occurs in many protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia tapona Engelm. ex J.M.Coult. sec. Hunt 2016
Opuntia tehuacana S.Arias & U.Guzmán sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the xerophytic portion of the floristic province of the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán Valley in the Mexican States of Oaxaca and Puebla (Méndez-Larios et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits xerophyllous vegetation (Méndez-Larios et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats and the species is tolerant to disturbance.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia tehuacana is listed as Least Concern due to relatively abundant populations, absence of threats, and its occurrence in protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia tehuantepecana (Bravo) Bravo sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_synonyms: Opuntia dillenii (Ker.-Gawl.) Haw. var. tehuantepecana Bravo
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is restricted to the region of Tehuantepec in Oaxaca. It occurs from sea level to 200 m asl (Acosta et al. 2003, Salas-Morales et al. 2007).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in low deciduous thorn forest (Salas-Morales et al. 2007).
iucn_doc_threats: Some subpopulations are trampled by livestock and are being reduced, but in general the species is abundant and tolerant to some degree of habitat modification.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia tehuantepecana is listed as Least Concern as it is relatively abundant within its limited range, tolerant to a degree of habitat modification, and occurs in protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia tomentosa Salm-Dyck sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Puebla; Oaxaca; México Distrito Federal; Tlaxcala; Tamaulipas; Querétaro; Veracruz; Nuevo León; San Luis Potosí; Michoacán; Guanajuato; Aguascalientes; Jalisco; Morelos; México State; Guerrero; Hidalgo; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXC-PU; MXS-OA; MXC-DF; MXC-TL; MXE-TA; MXE-QU; MXG-VC; MXE-NL; MXE-SL; MXS-MI; MXE-GU; MXE-AG; MXS-JA; MXC-MO; MXC-ME; MXS-GR; MXE-HI; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Temperate; Grassland - Temperate
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is widespread. It occurs in the Mexico Federal District and the states of Aguascalientes, Guanajuato, Guerrero, Hidalgo, México State, Michoacán, Morelos, Nuevo León, Oaxaca, Puebla, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas, Tlaxcala, and Veracruz. It grows from 1,090 to 2,600 m asl (Arias et al. 1997, Hernández et al. 2004). It has been reported from the literature in the state of Jalisco (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits Quercus forests, xerophyllous scrub and grasslands (Arias et al. 1997). It tolerates disturbance.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species at present. A potential threat to species of the genus Opuntia is the invasion of the cactus moth Cactoblastis cactorum, which can exterminate populations completely (Zimmerman et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia tomentosa is listed as Least Concern because it is common, wide ranging, occurs within many protected areas, and has no major threats.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia tortispina Engelm. & J.M.Bigelow sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
Opuntia velutina F.A.C.Weber sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in the states of Guerrero, Morelos, Oaxaca and Puebla where it grows  at elevations of 1,100 to 1,900 m (Arias et al. 1997; Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in tropical deciduous forest and xerophyllous scrub (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by deforestation from small-holder agriculture and cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because confusion remains about its taxonomy. Further research is needed to define the species and determine the range and population.

checklist_taxon: Opuntia wilcoxii Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - animal; Medicine - human & veterinary; Food - human
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican states of Sonora and Sinaloa. Records from Chihuahua are probably misidentifications of Opuntia durangensis. The species occurs mostly in the coastal areas but also extends further inland to the east. It grows at altitudes between sea level and 600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a tree that grows in sandy soils, alluvial plains and on granitic slopes in tropical dry deciduous forest and in desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no significant threats to this species. Land is cleared for pastures, but the species quickly recovers after use.
iucn_doc_rationale: Opuntia wilcoxii has a wide range, is very common in places, and there are no major threats. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_fullName: Oreocereus celsianus (Lem. ex Salm-Dyck) A.Berger ex Riccob.
checklist_taxon: Oreocereus celsianus (Lem. ex Salm-Dyck) A.Berger ex Riccob. sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Jujuy; Argentina
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Jujuy, Argentina, and in southern Bolivia. It grows at elevations of 2,600 to 3,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in puna on rocky, well-drained soils. It grows in slopes with orientation to the east and north (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Oreocereus celsianus is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is abundant, and there are no major threats.

checklist_taxon: Oreocereus ritteri Cullmann sec. Hunt 2006
Oreocereus tacnaensis F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2006
Oreocereus trollii (Kupper) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Salta; Argentina; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Jujuy
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Roads & railroads
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Chuquisaca, Potosí and Tarija, Bolivia and in Salta and Jujuy, Argentina. It grows at altitudes between 3,000 and 4,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in puna in rocky well-drained soils.It grows in slopes. In some localities it can be found with O. celsianus (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is collected by tourists and locally, that is, sporadic collecting occurs. The subpopulations close to roads are heavily collected. A potential threat is the construction of a new road in the area of distribution.
iucn_doc_rationale: Oreocereus trollii is listed as Least Concern because it has a relatively wide range, is abundant, occurs in a protected area and the threats are localized. However, it is recommended that the Argentine subpopulations are monitored because they suffer severely from extraction at roadsides.

checklist_taxon: Oroya borchersii (Boed.) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: Oroya borchersii is endemic to Peru’s Áncash region, where it grows at an altitude of 3,900 to 4,300 m asl, in dispersed areas in the Santa river catchment (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on hard rocky soils in the arid puna.
iucn_doc_threats: Gold mining is affecting the species. In past projects, the species was removed from the mining site and re-planted in another areas (in one experience, 12,000 individuals were transplanted from a single site), but it is not known whether other mining projects will do the same or whether they may reduce the population locally.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a wide range and is very common. It is currently not exposed to any major threat, although the potential impact of mining activities should be monitored. Oroya borchersii is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Oroya peruviana (K.Schum.) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru’s Apurímac, Ayacucho, Cusco, Huancavelica and Junín regions. It grows at altitudes of 3,500 to 4,200 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: O. peruviana grows in meadows and on rocky slopes (Arakaki et al. 2006) in Puna and inter-Andean valleys. The generation length is 15 years.
iucn_doc_threats: Threats to O.peruviana include illegal collection, intensive cattle grazing, mining activities, and fires caused by human activity (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_rationale: Oroya peruviana has a restricted range (extent of occurrence of 8,500 km2). A few subpopulations have disappeared mainly due to urbanization, but mining activities, fires, cattle ranching, agriculture and illegal collection have also affected this attractive species in the past. These impacts will not cease in the near future. This cactus has an estimated generation length of 15 years, an it is inferred that including both the past and future, the species population has been reduced by at least 50%. Hence, it qualifies for listing as Endangered under criterion A4ac.

checklist_taxon: Pachycereus grandis Rose sec. Arias & Terrazas 2009
iucn_locations: Morelos; Oaxaca; Puebla; México State; Mexico
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, and is known to occur in the states of México State, Morelos, Puebla and Oaxaca (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations between 900 and 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in tropical deciduous forest. It flowers between February and May (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: In the States of Mexico and Morelos changes in land use for urban development are the main threats to the species. Disturbance from cattle and goats is also preventing seedling establishment in some areas.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Vulnerable because its extent of occurrence is less than 20,000 km², the population is severely fragmented and there is continuing decline in both the quality of the habitat due to livestock impacts and the number of mature individuals due to urban expansion and lack of recruitment.

checklist_taxon: Pachycereus pringlei (S.Watson) Britton & Rose sec. Arias & Terrazas 2009
iucn_locations: Baja California; Sonora; Baja California Sur; Mexico
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Medicine - human & veterinary; Construction or structural materials; Fuels
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in the Mexican states of Baja California, Baja California Sur, and Sonora (Hunt et al. 2006). It generally occurs at altitudes below 200 m asl, but can be found up to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: On the continent the species is usually coastal, but with some populations inland (less than 50-75 km inland). On the peninsula it is widespread, except at tops of mountains.
iucn_doc_threats: Most subpopulations on the peninsula are stable, but in some areas they have been extirpated particularly by coastal developments. Subpopulations on the continent have been heavily impacted by coastal developments for tourism and shrimp farming. Subpopulations on the islands are very stable.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pachycereus pringlei is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread and locally abundant. However, the subpopulations on the continent (which include the largest individuals) are in serious decline.

checklist_taxon: Pachycereus tepamo S.Gama-López & S.Arias sec. Arias & Terrazas 2009
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is known only from the state of Michoacán. It grows at elevations of 150 to 1,100 m asl (Gama and Arias 1998, Guzmán et al. 2003).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits tropical deciduous forest (Gama-López and Arias 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: In some portions of the range the subpopulations are being reduced due to expanding cattle ranching and subsistence agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pachycereus tepamo is listed as Least Concern due to large remaining populations and because it is not currently declining at a rate to qualify for listing in a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Pachycereus weberi (J.M.Coult.) Backeb. sec. Arias & Terrazas 2009
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Construction or structural materials; Food - human
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Guerrero, Morelos, Oaxaca and Puebla (Guzmán et al. 2003). It grows at elevations of 600 to 1,500 m asl (Arias et al. 1997, Luna-Morales and Rogelio-Aguirre 2001).
iucn_doc_habitats:
This species can form forests where it is the dominant species, which are known as "cardonales" (Valiente-Banuet et al. 2000).
It occurs in tropical deciduous forest (Arias et al. 1997, Jiménez-Ramírez et al. 2003).
iucn_doc_threats: The species habitat is being destroyed in some areas for cattle ranching and small-scale farming.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pachycereus weberi is listed as Least Concern due to a wide distribution, and because it is locally abundant and is not declining at nearly the rate sufficient to qualify for listing in a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Parodia alacriportana Backeb. & Voll sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Rio Grande do Sul; Santa Catarina; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: Parodia alacriportana is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Rio Grande do Sul and Santa Catarina (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of 800 to 1,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in rocky outcrops in grasslands, and is tolerant to humidity.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are cattle grazing, agriculture, plantation of exotique tree species and fire. The tree plantations, mainly pines, have a severe effect on the population because it provides shady environments in addition to the land transformation, there is soil alteration too.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because it has a relatively small extent of occurrence (EOO= 9,000km<sup>2</sup>), it is severely fragmented and it faces several threats that cause a continuing decline.

checklist_taxon: Parodia concinna (Monv.) N.P.Taylor sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Brazil; Rio Grande do Sul; Uruguay
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Grassland - Temperate
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul) and in practically all of Uruguay, at elevations from 20 to 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops in grasslands or pampas. A generation length of 10 years has been inferred based on field observations.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are cattle grazing, illegal collection and forestry of Eucalyptus.
iucn_doc_rationale: A population decline is evident (30% over 30 years considering past and future, generation length is 10 years) and major threats continue to affect Parodia concinna. Land use change for Eucalyptus plantations is ongoing in the area where the species occurs, and illegal collection is becoming more popular and will not cease in the near future. The species is therefore listed as Vulnerable. A regional assessment in Brazil is recommended as the species is more threatened there.

checklist_taxon: Parodia haselbergii (F.Haage ex Rümpler) F.H.Brandt sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Santa Catarina; Rio Grande do Sul; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Grassland - Temperate
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Dams (size unknown); Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Rio Grande do Sul and Santa Catarina at elevations of 200 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops, in grasslands or pampas, on plains and walls. Generation length is 10 years.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are grazing, agriculture, illegal collection, Eucalyptus forestry and dam development.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia haselbergii has a relatively wide range, but several subpopulations have been extirpated. The major threats are grazing, agriculture, illegal collection, Eucalyptus forestry and dam development, and they continue to affect the subpopulations. It is inferred that in the past and near future the reduction of the population will reach 40% (30 year time span; generation length is 10 years). Hence, Parodia haselbergii is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Parodia horstii (F.Ritter) N.P.Taylor sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Avalanches/landslides; Dams (size unknown); Recreational activities; Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Rio Grande do Sul (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of 200-800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on walls, in semi-deciduous forest. The species has significant allopatric morphollogical variations, it is likely that speciation is underway.
iucn_doc_threats: Some populations are threatened from mining, dam construction and mud slides that occur naturally but can become worse because of agriculture. Another threat is the practice of rappelling sport on the walls this plant grows on, and local extraction for ornamental use.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered becasue it has less than 2,500 mature individuals and at least 20% of the populatuion has been lost in the last 20 years (generation length is 10 years), mainly as a result of dam construction, illegal collection and land slides that are augmenting as a result of land use change for agriculture. The threats to the species will not cease in the near future.

checklist_fullName: Parodia lenninghausii (F.Haage) F.H.Brandt ex Eggli & Hofacker
checklist_taxon: Parodia lenninghausii (F.Haage) F.H.Brandt ex Eggli & Hofacker sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
Parodia maassii (Heese) A.Berger sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_locations: Argentina; Jujuy; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Salta
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Argentina in Jujuy and Salta, and in Bolivia in Chuquisaca, Oruro, Potosi and Tarija, at elevations of 3,000 to 4,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in high altitude grasslands on rocky outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is locally collected but this is not a major threat. It is locally threatened by sheep overgrazing (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia maassii is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is abundant, occurs in protected areas, and only very localized threats occur.

checklist_taxon: Parodia magnifica (F.Ritter) F.H.Brandt sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Rio Grande do Sul (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 300 to 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on walls in deciduous forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for Parodia magnifica are dam construction, which is underway, and erosion due to agriculture provoking landslides.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered, because it has a small range (extent of occurrence is 2,600 km<sup>2</sup>), it is severely fragmented and thre is continuing decline due to land slides. Some subpopulations are threatened by the construction of a dam that is underway,  as a result it is expected that 60% of the populations will disappear in the near future. This species could rapidly change status to Critically Endangered and therefore conservation action is needed.

checklist_taxon: Parodia mammulosa (Lem.) N.P.Taylor sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_locations: Córdoba; Rio Grande do Sul; Uruguay; San Luis; Mendoza; Catamarca; Rio Negro; La Pampa; La Rioja; Buenos Aires; Argentina; Brazil; Santiago del Estero
iucn_location_codes: AGE-CO; BZS-RS; UY; AGW-SL; AGW-ME; AGW-CA; AGS-RN; AGE-LP; AGW-LR; AGE-BA; AR; BR; AGW-SE
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Temperate; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Argentina (Buenos Aires, Catamarca, Córdoba, La Pampa, Mendoza, La Rioja, Santiago del Estero, Río Negro and San Luis provinces), Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul) and in all of Uruguay. It grows from 0 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on rocky outcrops, in grasslands or pampas, monte desert and high altitude grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: In Brazil the major threats are cattle grazing, agriculture and forestry. The subpopulations in Argentina and Uruguay, however, are not exposed to any major threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia mammulosa has a wide range and is abundant and it is therefore listed as Least Concern. This species, however, needs taxonomic review, and it might split into different species that will need to be assessed separately. Considering that some subpopulations, such as the Brazilian, are affected by several threats, some of them may fall under a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Parodia ottonis (Lehm.) N.P.Taylor sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Grassland - Temperate
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species can be found in Argentina, Brazil, Paraguay, and Uruguay (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of up to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops and sandy areas, in grasslands or pampas. Field observations suggest that the generation length is 5 years.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats to this species are grazing, agriculture, illegal collection, and forestry of Eucalyptus plantations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia ottonis has a relatively wide range (extent of occurrence of approximately 18,000km2). Significant reductions have occurred, and are ongoing, in some subpopulations while part of the range is not under pressure. Overall, a 30% decline due to grazing, agriculture, illegal collection, and forestry is estimated for a time span of 15 years (generation length is five years), taking into account both the past and the future. Hence, the species is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Parodia rudibuenekeri (W.R.Abraham) Hofacker & P.J.Braun sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Temperate; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is from Brazil occurring in Rio Grande do Sul (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 200-400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Parodia rudibuenekeri grows on exposed rocks in grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: There are many threats affecting this species, the most important are trampling from cattle, mining, fires and agro-forestry of exotic species (Eucalyptus). The trees and the leaves that drop from them and land on the plants,  provide unnecessary shade that affects the species by interfering with floral buds, seed dispersal and its general physiology.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a small range with an extent of occurrence of 2,000 km<sup>2</sup>, it is known from 5 or less locations, and the population can also be considered severely fragmented. Many threats cause a continuing decline in the population. Therefore, this species is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Parodia schumanniana (Nicolai) F.H.Brandt sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_threat_names: Avalanches/landslides; Intentional use (species is the target); Dams (size unknown)
iucn_doc_range: This species can be found in Argentina (Misiones), Brazil, and Paraguay (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations between 400 and 700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in deciduous forests. It grows on steep areas on hills in open areas. Generation length is 5 years
iucn_doc_threats: In Brazil the major threats are dam construction and landslides resulting from the erosion caused by agriculture. In Paraguay the major threat is illegal collecting. A decline of approximately 30% has occurred in sub-populations throughout its range.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia schumanniana has a restricted range (extent of occurrence is 16,000 km2), is sparsely distributed, and there is a continuing decline in the extent and quality of its habitat as well as in the number of mature individuals. Dam construction, landslides, and illegal collection have led to an estimated decline of 30% throughout its range in the last 15 years (generation length five years) and this decline and threats are unlikely to cease in the near future. It is therefore listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Parodia scopa (Spreng.) N.P.Taylor sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Uruguay; Brazil; Rio Grande do Sul
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Temperate
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Intentional use (species is the target); Agro-industry plantations; Recreational activities; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: Parodia scopa has been reported from Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul) and Uruguay (Melo, Lavalleja, Treinta y Tres, Rocha and Maldonado). It occurs at elevations from 0 to 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky outcrops and sandy soils in shrublands, grasslands or pampas. The generation length is 10 years.
