iucn_doc_threats: The major threats to the long-term survival of this species are grazing, agriculture, illegal collection, and forestry. Also, rappel sports, as the ropes and climbers damage, extract or kill the plants.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia scopa has a relatively large range, however it faces many major threats including agricultural and farming activities, forestry, and illegal collection. These threats are not reversible and will not cease in the near future. Considering past declines and the continued expansion of the threats, it is estimated that around 40% of the population will be lost in a 30 year period (considering past and future; generation length is ten years). The decline in the Brazilian subpopulation is estimated to be even higher (60%), and a regional assessment is recommended. At a global scale, the species is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Parodia stuemeri (Werderm.) Backeb. sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina. It occurs in Salta and Jujuy (Hunt et al. 2006), at altitudes between 2,300 and 3,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in prepuna, in riparian vegetation among rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is not exposed to any major threat. However, it is collected locally at small scales.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although it has a relatively restricted range, the population of Parodia stuemeri seems to be stable and not subject to any major threat. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Parodia subterranea F.Ritter sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it grows in Chuquisaca at elevations of 2,800 to 3,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in puna grassland with very stony ground and little vegetation cover.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia subterranea has a relatively restricted range (its extent of occurrence is 1,200 km2), however it is abundant, the population has new regeneration, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Parodia tenuicylindrica (F.Ritter) D.R.Hunt sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Uruguay; Rio Grande do Sul; Brazil
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul) and in Uruguay (Rivera, Artigas, Salto, Tacuarembó), at elevations of 50 to 200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops, in grasslands or pampas.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are fires, cattle grazing due to trampling, agriculture, and forestry.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia tenuicylindrica has a relatively small range with an extent of occurrence of 5,000-6,000 km2, the populations are declining and the threats causing this reduction will not cease in the near future. Considering past reductions and assuming that the expansion of agriculture and forestry continues at the same rate that has been observed until today, it is estimated that at least 50% of the population will be lost over a timespan of 3 generations (21 years; generation length 7 years), including both past and future. Hence, the species is listed as Endangered under criterion A4ac.

checklist_taxon: Pediocactus simpsonii (Engelm.) Britton & Rose sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_locations: Utah; Wyoming; Colorado; Idaho; Montana; Nevada; South Dakota; Arizona; New Mexico; United States
iucn_location_codes: UTA-OO; WYO-OO; COL-OO; IDA-OO; MNT-OO; NEV-OO; SDA-OO; ARI-OO; NWM-OO; US
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Temperate; Forest - Temperate; Grassland - Temperate
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in southern and eastern Idaho, Wyoming, Nevada, Arizona, New Mexico, Dakota, Utah and Colorado; questionably in Montana and Kansas. At elevations of 1,800 to 3,000.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in powdery soils of valleys, flats, and hillsides in dry areas, rocky soils of high valleys, mountainsides and grasslands. It grows in pinyon-juniper woodland, sage scrublands and mountain forests.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for this species. Local threats are mining and land-use change.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide range, is abundant, there are no major threats affecting it and it occurs within several protected areas. Hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pelecyphora aselliformis C.Ehrenb. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of San Luis Potosí, where it occurs in the north and northwest at elevations of 1,800 to 2,400 m asl. The species is spread over an area of no more than 500 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in semi-desert among calcareous rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: Other than some limited illegal collecting at one site in particular and destruction of plants by road construction (Glass and Fitz Maurice 1993), there appears to be relatively little human impact.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pelecyphora aselliformis is listed as Least Concern because of its wide range, large population, and low threat level.

checklist_fullName: Pelecyphora strobiliformis (Werderm.) Frič & Schelle ex Kreuz.
checklist_taxon: Pelecyphora strobiliformis (Werderm.) Frič & Schelle ex Kreuz. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is found in central San Luis Potosí at an altitude around 1,900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in semi desert, among outcrops of calcareous rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species. There is some illegal collection.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pelecyphora strobiliformis is listed as Least Concern because of its relatively wide distribution, large population, and lack of major threats. However, this species is removed from the wild by collectors, which represents a minor threat.

checklist_taxon: Peniocereus greggii (Engelm.) Britton & Rose sec. Arias & al. 2005
iucn_common_name: Desert Night-blooming Cereus; Reina de la Noche; Queen of the Night
iucn_locations: Chihuahua; Durango; Sonora; United States; Texas; Zacatecas; Coahuila; New Mexico; Mexico; Arizona
iucn_location_codes: MXE-CU; MXE-DU; MXN-SO; US; TEX-OO; MXE-ZA; MXE-CO; NWM-OO; MX; ARI-OO
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Utility & service lines; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range: This wide-ranging species is distributed in the Mexican states of Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, Sonora, and Zacatecas, and in Texas, New Mexico and Arizona in the United States.
iucn_doc_habitats: Peniocereus greggii grows in xerophyllous scrub. It grows under creosote bushes in desert flats and washes, which further reduces visibility (Benson 1982). It also grows on rocky mountain slopes.
iucn_doc_threats:
This species is threatened by development from roads, power lines, pipelines, and windmill farms. There is probably some illegal collection.
It has been reported that the populations of the pollinator hawk moth are decreasing by the use of pesticides (Nabhan pers. comm. 2000 in Paredes et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though some populations have been extirpated, Peniocereus greggii occurs in a wide area and is widely scattered and very difficult to find, which confers the species some protection. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern. However, special attention must be given to some subpopulations, especially near urban areas such as Phoenix, El Paso and Tucson.

checklist_taxon: Peniocereus johnstonii Britton & Rose sec. Arias & al. 2005
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Unintentional effects (species is not the target); Nomadic grazing
iucn_doc_range: Peniocereus johnstonii is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the state of Baja California Sur inland and in at least seven islands (J.L. León de la Luz pers. comm. 2012), at elevations of 0 to 500 m asl (Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 20011). Hunt et al. (2006) report the species erroneously in Baja California (Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 20011).
iucn_doc_habitats: Peniocereus johnstonii grows on alluvial soils in desert scrub in association with Mezquite (Prosopis sp.). The species is very difficult to spot.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are goat trampling, cattle ranching and logging of Mezquites, the species nursing plant.
iucn_doc_rationale: Peniocereus johnstonii is listed as Least Concern in spite of its restricted range (its extent of occurrence is ~ 2,000 km2) because it occurs in at least seven islands where there are no threats and where the species presents the highest abundance. The subpopulations on the mainland are affected by several threats, however the species is very difficult to detect and it might be more common than it is thought.

checklist_taxon: Peniocereus marianus (Gentry) Sánchez-Mej. sec. Arias & al. 2005
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in the states of  Sonora and Sinaloa (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations form 0 to 500m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on a wide range of habitat, from fine-textured alluvial soils to bajada slopes and basalt rock soils of hills to steep rocky slopes and canyon sides.
iucn_doc_threats: Part of range of this species was lost to agriculture, but most of its population is quite stable.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is wide spread, agriculture affected it in the past but now a days its population is stable and it does not face any major. Hence, Peniocereus marianus is here listed as Least Concern

checklist_taxon: Peniocereus marnierianus Backeb., nom. inval., sec. ???
Peniocereus striatus (K.Brandegee) Buxb. sec. Arias & al. 2005
iucn_locations: Baja California Sur; Mexico; Arizona; Sinaloa; Baja California; Sonora; United States
iucn_location_codes: MXN-BS; MX; ARI-OO; MXN-SI; MXN-BC; MXN-SO; US
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Mexico in the states of  Baja California Norte, Baja California Sur, Sonora and Sinaloa and in the Unites States in Arizona and on many islands in the Gulf of California.It grows at elevations from 0 to 400m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is specially common in coastal thorn-scrub and desert scrub (Paredes et al. 2000),  it grows beneath trees and shrubs in flats and on hills.
iucn_doc_threats: Land use change is a threat for Peniocereus striatus, presently mainly for establishment of exotic grasslands and in the past for agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: In parts of the range the species has declined markedly and is highly threatened. However, given the current state of understanding, this is not mirrored across Baja California, resulting in a Least Concern listing.

checklist_taxon: Peniocereus viperinus (F.A.C.Weber) Kreuz. sec. Arias & al. 2005
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: Peniocereus viperinus is endemic to Mexico, where it is known to occur in the states of Morelos, Oaxaca and Puebla (Guzmán et al. 2003, Méndez-Larios et al. 2004, Paredes-Flores et al. 2007). It grows at elevations from 1,000 to 1,700 m asl (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits tropical deciduous forests, xerophyllous scrub and cardonales (Guzmán et al. 2003, Méndez-Larios et al. 2004, Paredes-Flores et al. 2007).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by habitat modification due to expanding small-scale farming and cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Peniocereus viperinus is listed as Least Concern due to its wide range and its occurrence in a protected area. The species is affected by habitat modification due to expanding small-scale farming and cattle ranching, but it is not declining at a rate sufficient to warrant listing in a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Peniocereus zopilotensis (J.Meyrán) Buxb. sec. Arias & al. 2005
iucn_doc_range: Peniocereus zopilotensis is endemic to Mexico, and is known only from Zopilote Canyon in the State of Guerrero (Jiménez-Ramírez et al. 2003). The species has a very restricted range.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits tropical deciduous forest (Jiménez-Ramírez et al. 2003).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened primarily by free range grazing of goats. Goats are left to graze in the areas where the species occurs and directly eats these cactus and tramples the plants and habitat.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered to be Critically Endangered at it is known from less than 10 locations (each with less than 5 mature individuals; population size ca 50 mature individuals) which are in decline due to trampling and eating by grazing goats.

checklist_taxon: Pereskia aculeata Mill. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_common_name: Barbados Shrub; Azedinha; Cipó Santo; Espinho de Santo Antonio; Espinho Preto; Lobolobo; Ora pro nobis; Surucucú; Barbados Gooseberry
iucn_locations: Saba; Curaçao; Venezuela (mainland); Maranhão; Paraguay; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Espírito Santo; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Cuba; Bonaire; Dominica; Saint Martin (French part); Peru; Sint Maarten (Dutch part); Suriname; Haiti; Alagoas; Trinidad and Tobago; Guyana; Florida; Bahia; Argentina; Brazil; Sint Eustatius; Ceará; French Guiana; Sergipe; United States; Minas Gerais; Panama
iucn_location_codes: NLA-SA; CW; VEN-OO; BZE-MA; PY; VE; BZL-ES; BQ; CU; NLA-BO; DM; MF; PE; SX; SR; HT; BZE-AL; TT; GY; FLA-OO; BZE-BA; AR; BR; NLA-SE; BZE-CE; GF; BZE-SE; US; BZL-MG; PA
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_threat_names: Motivation Unknown/Unrecorded; Mining & quarrying; Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Food - human; Food - animal
iucn_doc_range: This species is widely distributed. It has been recorded from southeastern and southern Brazil, eastern Paraguay, eastern Argentina, Central America, the Caribbean, northern South America and in Florida in the U.S.  It was once recorded from Peru. In Brazil it occurs in Maranhão, Pernambuco, Alagoas, Sergipe, Bahia, Minas Gerais, Ceará and Espírito Santo (Taylor and Zappi 2004, N. Taylor pers. comm. 2010).  In Venezuela, it is mainly found in the north-central part of the country in the states of Miranda, Carabobo, and Distrito Federal. It is a rare occurrence in the states of Falcón, Zuliá, Táchira, and Bolívar, Venezuela (Ponce 1989). In the Caribbean it occurs in Cuba, Dominican Republic, Haiti, Lesser Antilles and Trinidad and Tobago. In northern South America it is found in    It occurs from sea level to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in humid to sub-humid evergreen forests on inselbergs of gneiss or granite and also on limestone. The plant is often seen growing over vegetation and rocks. Capuchin and brown howler monkeys feed on the fruits of P. aculeata, and in some regions of Brazil they are its main dispersers (Pedroni and Sanchez 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats to this species are tourist development in coastal areas and logging in forests. Where the species occurs on limestone, mining can pose a threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pereskia aculeata is listed as Least Concern because it is common, widespread, and an aggressive invader in parts of the world where it has been introduced.

checklist_taxon: Pereskia bahiensis Gürke sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bahia, Brazil. It grows in the caatinga surrounding the Chapada Diamantina, planalto de Maracás, northern Serra do Espinhaço and Serra Geral (Bahia), east and south of the Rio São Francisco. It occurs at altitudes between 300 and 900 m asl.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species, it tends to regenerate well after disturbance.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pereskia bahiensis is fairly widespread and common. It has been taken into cultivation as a hedge plant in the region where it is native. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pereskia grandifolia Haw. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Espírito Santo; Rio de Janeiro; Paraná; Ceará; Alagoas; Pernambuco; Bahia; Minas Gerais; Sergipe; São Paulo; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZL-ES; BZL-RJ; BZS-PR; BZE-CE; BZE-AL; BZE-PE; BZE-BA; BZL-MG; BZE-SE; BZL-SP; BR
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_threat_names: Motivation Unknown/Unrecorded; Agro-industry farming; Housing & urban areas; Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Construction or structural materials; Food - human
iucn_doc_range: This species has been recorded in Brazil from southernmost Ceará, Pernambuco (native and cultivated), central-eastern and south-eastern Bahia to southern Espírito Santo and south-western Minas Gerais. Perhaps it is also native to Rio de Janeiro and São Paulo. It is widely cultivated and ranges south to Santa Catarina and southeastern Mato Grosso. It is commonly introduced in the Neotropics.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a humid/subhumid evergreen forest element.
iucn_doc_threats: There is widespread habitat loss due to logging, expansion of urban areas and clearance of forest for agricultural expansion.
iucn_doc_rationale: The natural range of this Pereskia grandifolia remains poorly known, probably through early destruction of its habitat and for the uncertainty as to its native status caused by its widespread introduction as a cultivated ornamental. It has been taken into cultivation as a hedge plant in the region where it may be native. Given the wide range and apparent abundance of this species it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pereskia nemorosa Rojas Acosta sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Argentina; Corrientes; Misiones; Brazil; Uruguay; Entre Ríos; Paraguay; Rio Grande do Sul
iucn_location_codes: AR; AGE-CN; AGE-MI; BR; UY; AGE-ER; PY; BZS-RS
iucn_doc_range: This cactus occurs in Argentina, in Corrientes, Entre Ríos and Misiones; in Brazil, in Rio Grande do Sul; and in Paraguay and Uruguay (Hunt et al. 2006). Pin and Simon (2004) report it for northeastern Argentina, southern Brazil, northwestern Uruguay, and in Paraguay in the Alto Paraguay, Alto Paraná, Amambay, Central, Concepción, Cordillera, Itapúa, Paraguarí, and San Pedro departments. In Uruguay it may be locally extinct because it has not been recorded since 1930. It occurs at elevations between 10 and 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs inside and in the edges of the humid forest and in humid soil. It also occurs in the low forest and in the sotobosque of secondary forests (Pin and Simon 2004)
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats affecting this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pereskia nemorosa is listed as Least Concern because it is common and there are no major threats affecting it. It is present in several protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Pereskia sacharosa Griseb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Mato Grosso do Sul; Formosa; Chaco; Argentina; Salta; Paraguay; Jujuy; Mato Grosso; Brazil; Bolivia, Plurinational States of
iucn_location_codes: BZC-MS; AGE-FO; AGE-CH; AR; AGW-SA; PY; AGW-JU; BZC-MT; BR; BO
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Argentina, in Chaco, Formosa, Jujuy and Salta; in Bolivia, in Chuquisaca, Cochabamba, Santa Cruz, and Tarija; Paraguay and in Brazil in Mato Grosso and Mato Grosso do Sul (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations of up to 1,500 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in sotobosque, forest edges, and clearings.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pereskia sacharosa is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, is common and abundant, and there are no major threats affecting it. It occurs in many protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Pereskia weberiana K.Schum. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This Bolivian endemic grows in La Paz and Cochabamba (Hunt et al. 2006), at approximately 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in wet forests.
iucn_doc_threats: It is not known whether this species is facing any threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pereskia weberiana is listed as Least Concern due the lack of any known major threats and fact that the species does not appear to be declining at a rate sufficient to qualify for a threatened category. The exact range, population size, and potential threats are not known and thus additional field studies are needed to realistically assess this species.

checklist_taxon: Pereskiopsis aquosa (F.A.C.Weber) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Medicine - human & veterinary; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Food - human
iucn_doc_range: Pereskiopsis aquosa is endemic to Mexico, it is distributed in the states of Jalisco, Colima and Nayarit in tropical deciduous forest at elevations between 300 to 1,800 m (S. Arias pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: Pereskiopsis aquosa inhabits tropical deciduous forest and tropical subdeciduous forest (CONABIO 2001; Padilla-Velarde et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats, however habitat destruction occurs locally within the range of the species due to agriculture and cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide range, is abundant and even though there are indirect threats these are not sufficient to trigger a red listing. Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pereskiopsis diguetii (F.A.C.Weber) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Jalisco; Querétaro; Michoacán; Morelos; Nayarit; México State; Guanajuato; Guerrero; Oaxaca; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXS-JA; MXE-QU; MXS-MI; MXC-MO; MXS-NA; MXC-ME; MXE-GU; MXS-GR; MXS-OA; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Guanajuato, Guerrero, Jalisco, México, Michoacán, Morelos, Nayarit, Querétaro, and Oaxaca (Hernández et al. 2004). It grows at elevations from sea level to 1,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is distributed in tropical dry forests, crassulacean scrub, low thorn scrub and disturbed areas (CONABIO 2001).
iucn_doc_threats: Fast land use change, mainly for tourist developments and clearance of forests for cattle grazing, in the habitats where the species inhabits is the main threat (CONABIO 2001), but the species like others in the genus is probably tolerant of disturbance.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pereskiopsis diguetii is fairly widespread and abundant with no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_fullName: Pereskiopsis porteri (K.Brandegee ex F.A.C.Weber) Britton & Rose
checklist_taxon: Pereskiopsis porteri (K.Brandegee ex F.A.C.Weber) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Sonora; Sinaloa; Baja California Sur; Nayarit; Mexico
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Agro-industry farming; Industrial aquaculture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the sates of Baja California Sur, Nayarit, Sinaloa and Sonora (Hunt et al. 2000). It occurs from sea level to 400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: P. porteri is most common in coastal thornscrub, but it also occurs in foothill thornscrub and low dry forest (Paredes et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat to this species is the continuing development of the coastal plains for agriculture and shrimp farming.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pereskiopsis porteri is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed and is locally abundant where it occurs. However, there are several threats in the coastal plains that are affecting its population.

checklist_taxon: Pereskiopsis rotundifolia (DC.) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Medicine - human & veterinary; Construction or structural materials; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: Pereskiopsis rotundifolia is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the States Guerrero and Oaxaca (Arias et al. 1997, CONABIO 2001). In Oaxaca the species is found in coastal areas like Zimatán and Huatulco (Salas-Morales et al. 2003, 2007), and the higher catchment of Tehuantepec River (Acosta et al. 2003). It occurs at elevations ranging from 1,300 to 1,900 m asl (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits tropical deciduous and sub-deciduous forests. It flowers between June and July and produces fruits between September and January (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: Land use change for the development of tourist facilities in coastal areas is the main threat. Illegal harvesting has been reported but this is probably not a significant threat at present.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pereskiopsis rotundifolia has a fairly restricted range, but is sufficiently widespread and common with no major threats to warrant listing as threatened. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_fullName: Pfeiffera asuntapatensis (M.Kessler, Ibisch & Barthlott) Ralf Bauer
checklist_taxon: Pfeiffera asuntapatensis (M.Kessler, Ibisch & Barthlott) Ralf Bauer sec. Korotkova & al. 2010
iucn_doc_range: This species is from Bolivia, occurring in La Paz, Saavedra and Charazani-Apolo, between a 1,300 - 1,500 m asl (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: It is unknown in which type of habitat this species grows in.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: There is no sufficient information to evaluate the conservation status of this species, it is therefore listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Pfeiffera boliviana (Britton) D.R.Hunt sec. Korotkova & al. 2010
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia , it occurs in La Paz and Yungas (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: It is not known in what kind of habitat this species is found.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a poorly known taxon and information on its population and threats is needed to assess its conservation status. Hence, the species is here listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Pfeiffera ianthothele (Monv.) F.A.C.Weber sec. Korotkova & al. 2010
iucn_locations: Tucumán; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Argentina; Jujuy; Salta; Catamarca
iucn_location_codes: AGW-TU; BO; AR; AGW-JU; AGW-SA; AGW-CA
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Argentina in Catamarca, Jujuy, Salta and Tucumán, and in Bolivia in Cochabamba, Chuquisaca, Santa Cruz and Tarija. It grows at altitudes between 600 and 2,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows as an epiphyte on several species of trees in dry forests in the limit between Yunga forests and the Chaco (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat loss due to land use changes is affecting this species. Furthermore, collectors are extracting mature individuals, but this is considered to represent a low risk to the survival of the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pfeiffera ianthothele has a wide distribution and is relatively common. Although it is affected by habitat loss and extraction of mature individuals, these factors are not considered to have a sufficiently elevated impact to classify this species in a threatened category. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pfeiffera miyagawae Barthlott & Rauh sec. Korotkova & al. 2010
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in La Paz at  elevations of 750m to 900m asl (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: It is unknown in which type of habitat this species grows in.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are not known.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a poorly known taxon and the information on population and threats needed to assess its conservation status is not available. Hence, the species is here listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Pfeiffera monacantha (Griseb.) P.V.Heath sec. Korotkova & al. 2010
iucn_locations: Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Argentina; Salta
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Salta, Argentina, and in Cochabamba, Santa Cruz and Tarija, Bolivia, at elevations of 800 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in seasonally dry east Andean valleys or Argentine yungas, mainly on cliffs.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is land use change due to cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pfeiffera monacantha is listed as Least Concern because it is wide-ranging, locally abundant, has no major threats, and occurs in many protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Pfeiffera paranganiensis (Cárdenas) P.V.Heath sec. Korotkova & al. 2010
iucn_doc_range: This Bolivian endemic occurs in Cochabamba and La Paz, at altitudes between 2,000 and 2,900 m asl (Ibisch et al. 2000, M. Lowry pers. comm. 2010).
iucn_doc_habitats: This epiphytic cactus grows among bushes and trees in Yungas forests.
iucn_doc_threats: There are some land use changes within its range that are affecting the species (Ibisch et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: Pfeiffera paranganiensis is listed as Least Concern due to the lack of any major known threats. However, only three or four localities are known for this species, but it is unclear whether this is related to its range being restricted or due to lack of fieldwork. Additional field studies are needed to realistically assess this species.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus albisummus P.J.Braun & Esteves sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Savanna - Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in the state of Minas Gerais, and possibly in Bahia and Goias (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 550-650m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in bambuí limestone outcrops (Zappi 1994) in shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is the extraction of limestone for road construction.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient given that there is not sufficient information on its distribution, population size and trends, and threats.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus alensis (F.A.C.Weber) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Franco-Estrada & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Sinaloa; Jalisco; Durango; Michoacán; Chihuahua; Guerrero; México State; Sonora; Oaxaca; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXN-SI; MXS-JA; MXE-DU; MXS-MI; MXE-CU; MXS-GR; MXC-ME; MXN-SO; MXS-OA; MX
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is found in the states of Guerrero, Jalisco, Michoacán, Sinaloa, and Sonora, its occurrence in the state of Chihuahua and Durango is uncertain. This species is found at elevations from 500 to 800 m asl (Lebgue et al. 2005, Hunt et al.  2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical deciduous forests (Lebgue et al. 2005).
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is threatened by habitat conversion, especially forest clearance to create grazing areas for cattle and clearance of coastal habitats for expansion of tourist developments.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus alensis is a widespread and abundant species with stable populations despite the increasing number of threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus arrabidae (Lem.) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Rio de Janeiro; Espírito Santo; Bahia; Brazil
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Housing & urban areas; Small-holder farming; Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the States of Bahia, Espirito Santo and Rio de Janeiro in Brazil. It occurs in Restinga forests and sand dunes. It occurs from sea level to 100 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: Occurs  in dense or sparse restinga and rocky cliffs by the coast (Zappi 1994).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are coastal development and tourism, plantation of tropical fruits and plantation of trees.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Near Threatened because it nearly qualifies for Vulnerable (B criteria) and should be monitored in the future to ensurer that it does not cross the population decline threshold. It  has a very narrow distribution along the coast and it occurs in areas that are potentially affected by further development and plantations.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus aureispinus (Buining & Brederoo) F.Ritter sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the state of Bahia, Brazil. It is known only from two localities near Ibotirama. It is known from 450-550 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in sandy areas, associated with termites.
iucn_doc_threats: A potential threat for this species is intentional fire.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because it has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence is approximately 300 km<sup>2</sup>) and is known from only two locations which occur near a highway, there has been increasing fire events that can affect these populations triggering a higher listing.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus aurisetus (Werderm.) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Taylor & Zappi 2004
iucn_threat_names: Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to central and north of  Minas Gerais, Brazil. It occurs from 650 m -1,300 m (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in quartzitic rock outcrops (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: Some subpopulations may be affected by excessive burning of their habitats, but threats are otherwise quite limited. The surrounding habitats are declining from charcoal production activities (Taylor and Zappi 2004), and may affect this species´ pollinators (moths and bats).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread, it occurs in protected areas and there are no major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus azulensis N.P.Taylor & Zappi sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the region of Pedra Azul, Minas Gerais (and from perhaps the same area recorded as a vaguely localized collection from south of Vitória da Conquista, Bahia) in Brazil. It is only known from two subpopulations near the above mentioned locality, one of which may have already disappeared.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in south-eastern caatinga, it is associated with gneissic inselbergs in caatinga-agreste (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is habitat loss as it is currently known from only a small remnant dry forest vegetation, which is being cleared for agriculture and charcoal production (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Critically Endangered as it has a restricted range (extent of occurrence <100km<sup>2</sup>) and the only location from which the species is currently known, corresponds to a small remnant of dry forest vegetation which is being cleared for agriculture and charcoal production.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus brasiliensis (Britton & Rose) Backeb. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Espírito Santo; Minas Gerais; Rio de Janeiro; Bahia; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_threat_names: Named species; Commercial & industrial areas; Housing & urban areas; Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the States of Bahia, Espírito Santo, Minas Gerais and Rio de Janeiro in Brazil. It occurs at elevations from 80 to 700 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in the humid/subhumid Atlantic forest in gneissic inselbergs (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: This species has apparently declined in the southern portion of its range (around the city of Rio de Janeiro) and will be affected by ongoing touristic developments along much of the length of its coastal habitat (Taylor and Zappi 2004). In inland areas, there is the threat of exotic grasses invading the gneisses occupied by Pilosocereus brasiliensis after clearing for agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus brasiliensis is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide distribution and is locally abundant in suitable habitat. The southern portion of the distribution is the most affected by major threats, such as touristic developments and exotic grasses invading the area occupied by P. brasiliensis after clearing for agriculture.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus brooksianus (Britton & Rose) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Franck & al. 2019
Pilosocereus catingicola (Gürke) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Rio Grande do Norte; Pernambuco; Alagoas; Sergipe; Paraíba; Bahia; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZE-RN; BZE-PE; BZE-AL; BZE-SE; BZE-PB; BZE-BA; BR
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Agro-industry farming; Housing & urban areas; Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Bahia, Rio Grande do Norte, Paraíba, Pernambuco, Alagoas and Sergipe. It grows at elevations from sea level to 1,100 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is widespread in the Caatinga dry forest and disjunct in the coastal restinga. It can occur in different substrates.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is habitat loss due to clearance of forest, mainly in the Caatinga, for agricultural purposes. In the restinga, the major threat is habitat loss due to urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus catingicola is listed as Least Concern as it is widely distributed and is locally abundant in suitable habitats.

checklist_fullName: Pilosocereus chrysacanthus (F.A.C.Weber ex K.Schum.) Byles & G.D.Rowley
checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus chrysacanthus (F.A.C.Weber ex K.Schum.) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Franco-Estrada & al. 2022
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - animal; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Food - human
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Oaxaca and Puebla, at elevations between 900 and 1,800 m asl (Arias et al. 1997, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical deciduous forest and xerophyllous scrub. It flowers between March and September (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus chrysacanthus is listed as Least Concern due to relatively abundant populations and its occurrence in protected areas. There are no major threats affecting the species.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus chrysostele (Vaupel) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_common_name: Facheiro de Serra; Rabo de Raposa; Facheiro
iucn_locations: Ceará; Paraíba; Rio Grande do Norte; Pernambuco; Brazil
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to north-eastern Brazil in Ceará, Rio Grande do Norte, Paraìba and Pernambuco. It occurs at elevations from  430-1,190 m (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in grey or whitish, granitic inselbergs with surface broken into blocks or stone, associated with very dry, highland caatinga (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are local mining of the rocks where it occurs, and invasive of exotic grasses after disturbance from agricultural activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although this species has a large Extent of Occurrence (EOO), it is listed as Near Threatened because it is substrate-specific and, as a consequence, is patchily distributed in areas that are highly vulnerable to habitat conversion in the future. Further information is needed to determine if rates of decline are significant enough to qaulify for a threat category (B criteria). Moreover, it is not locally abundant where it occurs.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus collinsii (Britton & Rose) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Franco-Estrada & al. 2022
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican states of Oaxaca and Chiapas, where it is widely distributed in the Tehuantepec Isthmus region (Pérez-García et al. 2001, Gallardo-Cruz et al. 2005).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits tropical deciduous forests (Pérez-García et al. 2001, Gallardo-Cruz et al. 2005).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by habitat conversion, mainly forest clearance for cattle grazing.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although the range of Pilosocereus collinsii is fairly restricted (its extent of occurrence is less than 20,000 km²),  is abundant and not under any major threat at present. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus densiareolatus F.Ritter sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_common_name: Facheiro da Lapa; Cabeça de Velho
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Eastern Brazil in the states of Bahia and Minas Gerais. It occurs at elevations from 450 m -800 m (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is sometines locally co-dominant with other arborescent cacti and other woody plants on Bambuí limestone outcrops in caatinga (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The habitat of this species is liable to decline through limestone quarrying, particulary in the southern portion of its range.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is locally abundant, however, it is substrate-specific and the substrate where it occurs is being exploited for cement, and the southern populations are affected by sugar cane agricultural activities. Therefore, it is listed as Near Threatened as further information may in fact indicate that rates of population decline are significant enough to qualify for a threat category (B criteria).

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus diersianus (Esteves) P.J.Braun sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Savanna - Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the northern part of the state of Goiás, Brazil (Hunt et al. 2006). It is known from only one locality (P. Braun, pers. comm. 2010). It occurs from 600-700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on limestone rock outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species. However, the type of rock where this species occurs is usually quarried.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Critically Endangered because its population is very small (< 50 mature individuals) and restricted. Further research is needed to determine if it occurs more widely.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus flavipulvinatus (Buining & Brederoo) F.Ritter sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Maranhão, Piauí and Ceará. It grows at altitudes from sea level to 350 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is a northern caatinga element: it occurs in the caatinga/cerrado ecotone, in dense, high, and low shrubby caatinga, open seasonally flooded carnauba (Copernicia prunifera) forest (often as epiphyte), as well as in the caatinga-mangrove ecotone, in northern draining river valleys, and at the coast (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is habitat loss for mango and cashew plantations, particularly in areas with higher rainfall.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus flavipulvinatus is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide distribution, is locally abundant where it occurs, and is present within a protected area.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus flexibilispinus P.J.Braun & Esteves sec. Zappi 1994
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Bahia, and Tocantins (Hunt et al. 2006). It has been collected at 600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on Bambuí limestone outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: There are small scale quarrying of limestone throughout the range of this species and may be affecting it.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is very habitat specific and known from only two populations. Although there are no major threats affecting these population at this time - they are highly vulnerable to threats that are encroaching on its limited range and could in the future qualify for a threat category based on limited Area of Occupancy/number of populations and significant rate of population decline (B or D criteria). Therefore it is listed as Near Threatened.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus floccosus Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the states of Minas Gerais and Bahia, Brazil. It occurs at elevations from 250 to 850 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The subpopulations in the northern portion of the range grow on gneiss whereas in the south they grow on limestone soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is potential habitat loss due to the extraction of the substrates where it occurs: limestone in the southern part of the range and granite and gneiss in the north.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus floccosus is listed as Least Concern given that it has a wide distribution, it is present in protected areas, and there are no major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus fulvilanatus (Buining & Brederoo) F.Ritter sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_synonyms: Pseudopilocereus fulvilanatus Buining & Brederoo; Pilosocereus fulvilanatus (Buining & Brederoo) Ritter subsp. fulvilanatus Pilosocereus; Pilosocereus fulvilanatus (P.J.Braun) Zappi subsp. rosae Pilosocereus; Pilosocereus  rosae P.J.Braun; Pseudopilocereus fulvilanatus Buin. & Brederoo
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the state of Minas Gerais, Brazil.
iucn_doc_habitats: South-eastern campo rupestre (Grão Mogol region and Rio São Francisco drainage) element: locally co-dominant with other woody vegetation on quartzitic rock outcrops, campo rupestre, Serra do Espinhaço, in the drainage of the Rio Jequitinhonha and Rio das Velhas.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species. Locally, it can be affected by mining of quartzite rocks.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although this species has a restricted distribution, and although are currently no major threats affecting the species, mining is cause a decline in numerous local populations and if trends continue this could be a major threat in the future (within the next 10 generations). Therefore, it is listed as Near Threatened.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus gaumeri (Britton & Rose) Backeb. sec. Franck & al. 2019
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic from Mexico where it occurs in the state of  Yucatán.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical dry deciduous and subdeciduous forest. The species tolerates low levels of disturbance.
iucn_doc_threats: In Yucatán the major threat for this species is habitat fragmentation due to land use change for henequen (Agave) plantations, however this happened in the past. In Chiapas this species is severely affected by urbanization. The subpopulation from Chiapas is not threatened at all.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a large extent of occurrence (EOO ca 30,000 km2), there are no known threats, it is tolerant to low levels of disturbance and it occurs in several protected areas. Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus glaucochrous (Werderm.) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to central Bahia, Brazil. It occurs at elevations of 740 to 950 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky soils in high altitude caatinga.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is habitat degradation due to livestock grazing.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Pilosocereus glaucochrous has a relatively restricted range, it is locally common where it occurs, present in a protected area, and there are no major threats affecting it. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus juaruensis (Buining & Brederoo) P.J.Braun sec. Hunt 2006
Pilosocereus lanuginosus (L.) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_synonyms: Pilosocereus tweedyanus (Britton & Rose) Byles & G.D.Rowley; Cactus lanuginosus L.
iucn_locations: Ecuador; Peru; Saint Martin (French part); Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Colombia; Sint Eustatius; Bonaire; Curaçao; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Saba; Sint Maarten (Dutch part)
iucn_location_codes: EC; PE; MF; VE; CO; NLA-SE; NLA-BO; CW; BQ; NLA-SA; SX
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_doc_range: Pilosocereus lanuginosus is found in Colombia, Ecuador, Netherlands Antilles, Peru, and Venezuela (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at altitudes from 0 to 1,700 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: Pilosocereus lanuginosus is a predominantly outcrossing species, but it is partially self-compatible. It is bat-pollinated (Nassar et al. 1997), and bats and birds disperse the seeds (Soriano and Ruiz 2002). In Venezuela, it occurs in thorny forest and spiny scrubs in slopes, flatlands, and rocky offshore islands (Nassar et al. 1997). In addition to sexual reproduction, this species reproduces clonally by fragmentation of the branches.  It is autotetraploid (Nassar et al. 2003).
iucn_doc_threats: Pilosocereus lanuginosus is considered to be a "fever-giving" weed by some of the local people in the Netherlands Antilles. In most development projects, the columnar cacti, as well as the other native vegetation, are systematically removed (Petit and Pors 1996).
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus lanuginosus is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed and locally abundant, occurs in many protected areas, and there are no major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus leucocephalus (Poselg.) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Franco-Estrada & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Tamaulipas; Chiapas; Mexico; San Luis Potosí; Guatemala; Veracruz; Hidalgo; Querétaro; Honduras
iucn_location_codes: MXE-TA; MXT-CI; MX; MXE-SL; GT; MXG-VC; MXE-HI; MXE-QU; HN
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: The species is found in Guatemala, Honduras, and Mexico. In Mexico it occurs in the States of Chiapas, Hidalgo, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas and Veracruz (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits tropical deciduous forest, xerophyllous scrub, and submontane forest (Goettsch and Hernández 2006). This species can tolerate some habitat modification.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by localized small-scale deforestation for cattle and agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus leucocephalus is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread and common. There are some threats, but they are very localized.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus machrisii (E.Y.Dawson) Backeb. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Minas Gerais; Tocantins; Piauí; Bahia; Goiás; São Paulo; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZL-MG; BZN-TO; BZE-PI; BZE-BA; BZC-GO; BZL-SP; BR
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the states of Bahia, Goiás, Minas Gerais, Piauí, Tocantins and São Paulo in Brazil. It occurs at elevations from  500 to 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a western cerrado element: it grows on quartzitic, arenitic or limestone rock outcrops associated with cerrado, cerrado de altitude or campo rupestre (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: Some subpopulations may be affected by habitat loss due to large-scale agriculture, particularly in the western part of Bahia.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus machrisii is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, it occurs in a protected area and there are no major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus magnificus (Buining & Brederoo) F.Ritter sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Named species
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the northeast of Minas Gerais, Brazil. It occurs at five locations at elevations from 250 - 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in gneiss and granitic flat outcrops in caatinga.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species is habitat loss and degradation due to mining of granitic rocks, livestock and the invasion of exotic grasses after habitat disturbances.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has a restricted distribution (extent of occurrence is approximately 1,100 km<sup>2</sup>), it occurs at a small number of locations (five locations), and there is continuing decline in its habitat.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus millspaughii (Britton) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Franck & al. 2019
Pilosocereus moritzianus (Otto) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Franck & al. 2019
Pilosocereus multicostatus F.Ritter sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the northeast of Minas Gerais in Brazil. It occurs at elevations from 670 - 900 m asl. It is known from only four localities.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows exclusively over gneiss or granitic outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat to this species is mining of gneiss. In addition, there is urbanization in the northern portion of its range and small scale livestock and farming.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has a restricted distribution (extent of occurrence of approximately 1,300 km<sup>2</sup>), based on the threats the species has four locations and there is continuing decline in the extent of the habitat where it occurs.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus oligolepis (Vaupel) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Zappi 1994
iucn_locations: Venezuela (mainland); Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Roraima; Guyana; Brazil
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Roraima in Brazil and in Guyana (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations from 150-550 m asl. It is known from two ill-defined and old locations in Brazil and one old but well-defined location in Guyana.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in exposed granite rock outcrops in cerrado (Zappi  1994).
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because some of the locations where it has been collected are old and not very well defined, and there is very little information on its distribution, population size and trends, and threats.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus parvus (Diers & Esteves) P.J.Braun sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Savanna - Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Bahia and Goiás (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations from 500 - 600 m asl (Braun, pers. comm. 2010).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in the cerrado on limestone outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is the expansion of the agricultural frontier, particularly the cultivation of soybean.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because it has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence is approximately 2,200 km<sup>2</sup>).  It is known from two areas that have the same potential threat and can be treated as two locations. The expansion of agriculture could drive the species to extinction in the near future in a very short period of time.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus pentaedrophorus (Labour.) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Minas Gerais; Bahia; Sergipe; Pernambuco; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Pernambuco, Sergipe, Bahia, and Minas Gerais. It occurs at elevations from 5 to 1,000 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in the eastern caatinga element: in agreste and dense caatinga, on rocky substrates, rarely reaching into restinga sand-dunes (Taylor and Zappi 2004). It usually occurs in association with shrubs and trees.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no direct threats to this species but the extraction of wood in the caatinga may indirectly affect it.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus pentaedrophorus is listed as Least Concern because it is common, widely distributed, and it occurs in at least one protected area.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus piauhyensis (Gürke) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Rio Grande do Norte; Ceará; Piauí; Brazil
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the states of Piauí, Ceará, and Rio Grande do Norte in Brazil. It occurs at elevations of 200 to 500 m asl. It is known from only four widely separated locations.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species inhabits sandstone outcrops in Caatinga. It is very exclusive of this particular substrate and will not grow in other substrates.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown. However, it is unlikely that it is suffering from any particular threat because of the inaccessibility of where it occurs.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Pilosocereus piauhyensis has a restricted distribution and its population is fragmented, it is present in two protected areas, there is no continuing decline of its habitat or population, and there are no major threats affecting it. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus polygonus (Lam.) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Franck & al. 2019
iucn_synonyms: Pilosocereus robinii (Lem.) Byles & G.D.Rowley; Cactus polygonus Lam.
iucn_locations: Cuba; United States; Florida; Dominican Republic; Haiti; Bahamas
iucn_location_codes: CU; US; FLA-OO; DO; HT; BS
iucn_threat_names: Unspecified species; Intentional use (species is the target); Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_doc_range: Pilosocereus polygonus is found in the Bahamas, Cuba, the Dominican Republic, Haiti, and in the state of Florida in the United States (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations between 0.5 and 100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species inhabits dry shrublands in coastal rocky areas. In South Florida, Pilosocereus polygonus is found in rockland hammocks (Gann et al. 2002).
iucn_doc_threats: According to Gann et al. (2002) the main threats to Pilosocereus polygonus are habitat destruction, poaching and exotic pest plant invasion. In the Caribbean, the main threats are related to habitat destruction from urban developments for tourism. In Florida, and possibly in other areas within its distribution, it is threatened by sea-level rise (J. Maschinski pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus polygonus is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, is relatively abundant in many parts of its range, and is present in many protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus purpusii (Britton & Rose) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Franco-Estrada & al. 2022
iucn_locations: Colima; Jalisco; Nayarit; Michoacán; Guerrero; Sinaloa; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXS-CL; MXS-JA; MXS-NA; MXS-MI; MXS-GR; MXN-SI; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the States of Colima, Guerrero, Jalisco, Michoacán, Nayarit and Sinaloa (Guzmán et al. 2003; Hunt et al. 2006; Padilla-Velarde et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is distributed in tropical deciduous forest and in xerophyllous scrub (Padilla-Velarde et al. 2006; Ramírez-Delgadillo 2006). On "La Cocina" island, off the coast of Chamela, Jalisco, Mexico, it is very often found in the limits of the tropical deciduous forest on rocky soil and sites exposed to the wind (Ramírez-Delgadillo 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is threatened by habitat conversion, the forest is cleared for cattle grazing and the coastal habitats are being destroyed by the expansion of tourist developments.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus purpusii has a fairly wide range and is reasonably abundant with no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus pusillibaccatus P.J.Braun & Esteves sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Goiás, Maranhão, Piauí and Tocantins (Hunt et al. 2006). It has been found at an altitude of 500 m asl. It is known from four locations.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in sandstone outcrops in Cerrado.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known direct threats to this species. However, it is likely that it may be indirectly affected by tourism activities and agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus pusillibaccatus is listed as Least Concern because its population is stable, there are no major threats affecting it, and it occurs in one protected area.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus robinii (Lem.) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Franck & al. 2019
Pilosocereus vilaboensis (Diers & Esteves) P.J.Braun sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Goiás, Brazil. It occurs at elevations from 900 to 1,000 m asl (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in sandstone rock outcrops (Zappi 1994) in Cerrado.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats known   to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pilosocereus vilaboensis is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, is locally common and abundant, it occurs in at least one protected area, and there are no known threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Polaskia chende (Rol.-Goss.) A.C.Gibson & K.E.Horak sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican States of Oaxaca and Puebla, where it is limited to the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán region. It grows at elevations of 1,500 to 2,000 m asl (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species occurs in xerophyllous scrub and riparian vegetation. It flowers between August and January (Arias et al. 1997).
Studies comparing the genetic variability between managed and wild populations of P. chende show that there are no significant differences, showing that management can be effective for the conservation of the genetic variability of the species (Blancas et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to the species. Many subpopulations are stable because the species is locally managed  and preserved for its edible fruits, however, other subpopulations  may be affected by land use change and fragmentation.
iucn_doc_rationale: Polaskia chende is a relatively widespread and abundant species, experiencing no known direct threats or continued decline. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Polaskia chichipe (Rol.-Goss.) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_use_trade_names: Handicrafts, jewellery, etc.; Food - human; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán Valley and adjacent Mixteca Alta in the Mexican States of Oaxaca and Puebla (Arias et al. 1997, Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at elevations of 1,600 to 2,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits in xerophyllous scrub and commonly grows on alluvial limestone fans and shallow black soil. The species flowers between March and June, and produces fruits between June and August (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_rationale: Polaskia chichipe is abundant, tolerated and even promoted in agricultural lands, has no threats, and occurs within protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Hawaiian Islands, between 10 and 115 m elevation on small islets near Maui and Kahoʻolawe.
iucn_doc_habitats: The taxon is a stout perennial herb found in rocky coastal sites and sea cliffs in the Hawaiian Islands.
iucn_doc_threats: Major threats to this taxon include direct competition by invasive non-native plants and direct consumption and habitat degradation by non-native animals. In addition, the taxon is threatened by sea level rise, hurricanes, landslides, fire, and drought. Fortini et al. (2013) characterized this taxon as extremely vulnerable to climate change (vulnerability index 0.826).
iucn_doc_rationale: Portulaca molokiniensis is assessed as Critically Endangered. The taxon is endemic to Hawai'i and has an extent of occurrence of 40 km2. It has experienced a severe and ongoing decline in habitat and numbers due to the impacts of invasive plants and animals. Its area of occupancy is 12 km2 and declining. Its distribution is severely limited, occurring in only two small subpopulations, with a population size of fewer than 30 in total. This qualifies the taxon for a ranking of Critically Endangered C2a(i); D.

checklist_taxon: NA
iucn_threat_names: Recreational activities; Intentional use (species is the target); Roads & railroads; Commercial & industrial areas
iucn_doc_range: Portulaca okinawensis is endemic to the central Ryukyu areas in the Nansei-shoto Islands, Japan. More specifically, it is known from Amami-Oshima, Kakeroma, Ukejima and Tokunoshima Islands in the Amami Island Group, and from the Okinawa Island Group, Okinawa, Kume, Aguni and Tonaki Islands as well as Kerama Islands including Yakabi, Aka and Geruma Islands (Okinawa Prefectural Government 2018). The plants in the Amami Island Group are considered a separate variant  P. okinawensis var amamiensis based on a molecular and morphological study (Kokubugata et al. 2013).
iucn_doc_habitats: Portulaca okinawensis is a evergreen perennial herb with fleshy leaves. It is about 5 to 10 cm tall and grows in clumps on rocky areas along the coast including old coral reefs (Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan 2015, Kagoshima Prefecture 2016, Okinawa Prefectural Government 2018).
iucn_doc_threats: This species has never been abundant. It grows in coastal areas and is impacted by the development of seaports and is frequently unintentionally  trampled by people. It is collected for cultivation purpose, but the scale of collection is not large (Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan 2015, Kagoshima Prefecture 2016, Okinawa Prefectural Government 2018; M. Yokota pers. comm. 2015).
iucn_doc_rationale: Portulaca okinawensis is endemic to several islands in the central Ryukyu area of the Nansei-shoto Islands, Japan. The plants on Amami Island Group and Okinawa Island Group are considered to be different variants . The species is a small herb growing on rocky areas along the coast. It is distributed on several islands but its distribution is scattered and the population is small and decreasing. Major threats are coastal development and trampling, and are ongoing. Therefore, the species is listed as Vulnerable.

iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in tropical Africa to the Arabian Peninsula and the Mediterranean to Pakistan. It may have a nearly worldwide distribution, primarily in tropical and subtropical areas, but its native origin is uncertain and it is widely naturalised (USDA, National Plant Germplasm System 2020).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is an annual fleshy herb that forms mats in open places in short grassland and bushland. It is a weed of cultivation and disturbed places, especially on sandy soils (African plant database 2020).
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is widespread with no known major threats. It is therefore assessed as Least concern.

iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude
iucn_threat_names: Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Named species; Avalanches/landslides; Droughts
iucn_doc_range: Endemic to the Hawaiian islands, between 30 and 1,630 m elevation on the islands of Hawaiʻi and from a small islet off of the island of Lanaʻi.
iucn_doc_habitats: The taxon is a perennial herb found in Hawaiian coastal shrubland, subalpine woodland, bare cinders and near steam vents.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats to this taxon include direct competition by invasive non-native plant species, and predation and habitat degradation by non-native animals, particularly pigs and goats, which degrade the habitat and harm the plants through predation, trampling, and rooting for food. Alien plants also threaten the species by altering its habitat and competing with it for moisture, nutrients, light, and space. Rats pose a threat to the species through predation of its plant parts and fruits; and introduced slugs and snails threaten the species by feeding on its leaves, stems, and seedlings. In addition, the taxon is threatened by landslides, fire, and drought.
iucn_doc_rationale: Portulaca sclerocarpa is assessed as Endangered. The taxon is endemic to the island of Hawaiʻi and Lanaʻi and has experienced severe and ongoing decline in habitat and numbers due to the impacts of alien invasive plants and animals. Its area of occupancy is 12 km² and declining. Its distribution is severely limited, occurring in only three subpopulations (two threat- defined locations) with about 206 mature individulas. This qualifies the taxon for a ranking of Endangered under criteria B2ab(ii,iii,iv,v); C2a(i); D.
