iucn_doc_range: This species has been reported from Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, Paraguay, Peru, and Venezuela. In Ecuador and Peru it grows from sea level to 2,000 m. In Venezuela it grows at elevations up to 1,500 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops, in Atlantic forest and on soil in tropical, seasonally dry forest. It also grows on the borders of humid forests.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat to this cactus is deforestation for agriculture and cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Praecereus euchlorus is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed and locally abundant, occurs in many protected areas, and there are no major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Praecereus saxicola (Morong) N.P.Taylor sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Brazil; Argentina; Paraguay; Bolivia, Plurinational States of
iucn_habitat_names: Savanna - Moist; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Savanna - Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Agro-industry farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Argentina, Brazil, Bolivia, and Paraguay (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at low elevations of 100 to 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in many different habitats in the Chaco region. It frequently forms dense colonies in the sotobosque. In the northeastern portion of its range it grows in rocky areas.
iucn_doc_threats: In most of its range there are no major threats to this species. In Brazil, the main threat is habitat loss from deforestation for plantations for biofuels and livestock ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Praecereus saxicola is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide distribution, is abundant and common throughout its range, and there are no major threats affecting it. It is present in many protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Pseudorhipsalis acuminata Cufod. sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: Species endemic to Costa Rica, where it occurs in the departments of Alajuela, Heredia, and Limón (is on both sides of the central Cordillera), from elevations near to sea level to 1,100 m (and up to 1,400 m) (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits in wet lowland and montane tropical forests (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is the expansion of  commercial agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: The extent of occurrence for this species is just greater than the 20,000 km² threshold for Vulnerable, however, the area of occupancy may well be less than 2,000 km² especially as it is mostly known just from isolated individuals. The degree of isolation, however, is probably not enough to call it severely fragmented as this is a bird dispersed species. There is continuing decline in quality and extent of habitat due to expanding commercial agriculture. The species is close to being listed as Vulnerable under criterion B2ab(iii) hence it is assessed as Near Threatened.

checklist_taxon: Pseudorhipsalis alata (Sw.) Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_doc_range: Pseudorhipsalis alata is endemic to Jamaica (Hunt et al. 2006), and is known from 100 to 170 m asl. The modern record is from Cockpit Country. Even though there is no information available about exact point localities where the species was recorded, it is considered to be within a single location.  The area of occupancy was estimated to be 400 km2, based on the vegetated area within the Cockpit Country.  However, this estimate is conservative as suitable habitat for the species is likely considerably smaller.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species has been observed in humid lowland tropical rainforest.
iucn_doc_threats: Deforestation and bauxite extraction are the major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pseudorhipsalis alata is listed as Endangered because it is known from only one location with an inferred area of occupancy of less than 400 km2, and it is likely threatened by deforestation and mining (bauxite extraction).

checklist_taxon: Pseudorhipsalis amazonica (K.Schum.) Ralf Bauer sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_locations: Ecuador; Panama; Brazil; Colombia; Nicaragua; Costa Rica; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Peru
iucn_location_codes: EC; PA; BR; CO; NI; CR; VE; PE
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Agro-industry farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: Pseudorhipsalis amazonica is widely distributed. It occurs in semi-arid environments in Brazil, Colombia, Costa Rica, Ecuador, Nicaragua, Panama, Peru, and Venezuela (Albesiano and Fernández-Alonso 2006, Hunt et al. 2006). In Costa Rica it occurs at elevations from sea level to 900 m, while it may be found as high as 1,500 m in other range countries.
iucn_doc_habitats: In Costa Rica, this epiphytic cactus grows in tropical deciduous lowland forest. In Panama, this species occurs in wet forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are urban development, tourism, and agro-industrial farming.
iucn_doc_rationale: Given its wide range and its abundance in parts of its range, Pseudorhipsalis amazonica is listed as Least Concern despite the existence of some threats.

checklist_taxon: Pseudorhipsalis himantoclada (Rol.-Goss.) Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: Pseudorhipsalis himantoclada occurs in Costa Rica (departments of Puntarenas and San José) and Panamá (province of Coclé) at elevations from 50 to 660 m. It also possibly occurs in Colombia in the department of Valle from 800 to 1,000 m but this requires confirmation (Bauer 2002, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in lowland to montane wet forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat to this species is loss of habitat due to expanding coffee plantations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pseudorhipsalis himantoclada is listed as Near Threatened because it is experiencing continuing decline and the extent of occurrence is close to the threshold for Vulnerable (B criteria).

checklist_taxon: Pseudorhipsalis lankesteri (Kimnach) Barthlott sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is supposedly endemic to Costa Rica, distributed in the department of Punta Arenas, where it grows at elevations of 670-1,000 m asl  (Bauer 2002, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in lowland and montane wet forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: Due to the taxonomic uncertainties surrounding this taxon, it is listed as Data Deficient. It may well be conspecific with P. himantoclada.

checklist_taxon: Pterocactus australis (F.A.C.Weber) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Chile; Chubut; Santa Cruz; Rio Negro; Aisén; Argentina
iucn_location_codes: CL; AGS-CB; AGS-SC; AGS-RN; CLS-AI; AR
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Argentina from northern Río Negro province to southern Santa Cruz province, and in Chile in Aisén region. It grows at elevations from 0 to 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in sandy and stony hills often between grass (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide range, it is frequent and there are no threats affecting it. Therefore is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pterocactus fischeri Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in the western part of Río Negro, Neuquén and south of Mendoza (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009) at elevations of 500 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on loose sandy soils (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009) in monte vegetation.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pterocactus fischeri is listed as Least Concern because is has a wide range, it is abundant, and there are no threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Pterocactus gonjianii R.Kiesling sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in San Juan and Mendoza at elevations of 1,500 to 2,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in monte shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pterocactus gonjianii is listed as Least Concern, because even though the species range is small and it is not abundant, there are no major ongoing or potential threats as it grows in an area with no economical interest. It is found within a protected area.

checklist_taxon: Pterocactus hickenii Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Chile; Argentina; Aisén; Chubut; Santa Cruz
iucn_doc_range: This species is present in Argentina (Chubut and Santa Cruz) and Chile (Chile Chico, Aisén). It grows at 0 to 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is geophytic and grows on sandy soils of Patagonian lands.
iucn_doc_threats: No threats exist to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pterocactus hickenii is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is locally abundant, is present in protected areas and is not affected by any threats.

checklist_taxon: Pterocactus megliolii R.Kiesling sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in San Juan, at elevations of 500 to 1,000 m asl (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in monte shrubland on rocky soils in alluvial fans.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pterocactus megliolii is listed as Least Concern, because even though the species range is very small and it is not abundant, there are no major ongoing or potential threats. Population monitoring is recommended.

checklist_taxon: Pterocactus neuquensis R.Kiesling, E.Sarnes & N.Sarnes sec. Kew 2019
Pterocactus reticulatus R.Kiesling sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in San Juan and Mendoza at elevations of 1,500 to 3,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in monte shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pterocactus reticulatus is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is abundant, there are no major threats and it occurs within a protected area.

checklist_taxon: Pterocereus gaumeri (Britton & Rose) T.MacDoug. & Miranda sec. Franco-Estrada & al. 2021
Pygmaeocereus bieblii Diers sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Desert - Cold
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Peru, where it grows in the northern Ancash region (Arakaki et al. 2006) at elevations of 600 to 1,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in coastal hills (lomas costeras) and desert scrub on rocky soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat to this species is illegal collection. It is sought after by collectors because it is a relatively newly described species. It is a small species and therefore easily smuggled.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pygmaeocereus bieblii has only recently been described and is sought after by specialized collectors. It is known from a restricted range (extent of occurrence less than 500 km2) and consists of three close subpopulations, all threatened by illegal collection, which are therefore considered to represent a single location. Illegal collection is causing continuing declines in the population. Hence, it is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Pygmaeocereus bylesianus Andreae & Backeb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru (Arequipa department), and is found at altitudes between 50 and 1,000 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in sandy soils of the desert.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by urbanization in the region.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has an extent of occurrence of 1,800 km2, there are five locations and the threats are causing continuing decline in the number of mature individuals and quality of the habitat. In the last 20 years the population reduction was at least 80%, hence it is listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Quiabentia verticillata (Vaupel) Vaupel ex A.Berger sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Salta; Paraguay; Chaco; Argentina; Formosa; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Jujuy
iucn_location_codes: AGW-SA; PY; AGE-CH; AR; AGE-FO; BO; AGW-JU
iucn_doc_range: This species grows in Argentina, in Chaco, Formosa, Jujuy, and Salta; in Bolivia, in Cochabamba, Chuquisaca, Santa Cruz, and Tarija; and in northwestern Paraguay (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009). It occurs at elevations from 0 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in dry and low areas, sometimes associated with Pereskia sacharosa (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009). It grows in clayey soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Quiabentia verticillata is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide distribution, is locally abundant, and there are no major threats affecting it. It is present in several protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Quiabentia zehntneri (Britton & Rose) Britton & Rose sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_common_name: Cai Cai; Espinho de Santo Antonio; Flor de Cera; Quiabento
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species has been recorded from both sides of the Rio São Francisco valley, in west-central/southern Bahia and central Minas Gerais, Brazil. It grows at altitudes between 450 and 750 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This southern Rio São Francisco caatinga element occurs in bare or thinly wooded limestone (Bambuí) outcrops or gneiss/granite inselbergs amidst high caatinga forest (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: Mining of the limestone areas is a minor threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Quiabentia zehntneri is a common, widespread species that is is locally very abundant. Its population trend is increasing as the species is tolerant of disturbance, and it occurs in protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

Rapicactus booleanus (G.S.Hinton) D.Donati sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2019

Rapicactus subterraneus (Backeb.) Buxb. & Oehme sec. Aquino 2021

checklist_fullName: Rapicactus zaragosae (Glass & R.A.Foster) D.Donati ex D.Aquino
checklist_taxon: Rapicactus zaragosae (Glass & R.A.Foster) D.Donati ex D.Aquino sec. Aquino 2021
Rauhocereus riosaniensis Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Shifting agriculture; Small-holder farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru. It is present in the Amazonas and Cajamarca regions (Arakaki et al. 2006, Hunt 2006), where it occurs at an altitude of 500 to 1,000 m asl. The distribution is not continuous; there are two isolated subpopulations that correspond to different subspecies.
iucn_doc_habitats: It occurs in lomas costeras and dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: Agricultural expansion and urban sprawl are affecting the species in Jaén, but not so in the other locations.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a relatively large range with an extent of occurrence of  9,000km2. It has three main subpopulations, and according to field explorations, they are isolated from each other. The population as a whole is stable, however one of them is being impacted by urban sprawl and agricultural activities. Rauhocereus riosaniensis is at present classified as Least Concern and monitoring of the species is recommended as the threats might affect the other subpopulations in the future.

checklist_taxon: Rebutia fabrisii Rausch sec. ???
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Jujuy, Argentina, between Santa Ana and Valle colorado, occurring at 2,500-2,800m  (Pilbeam 1997).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky hills in selva vegetation.
iucn_doc_threats: The region where this plant grows is not suitable for human activities such as agriculture or housing developments. The only threat that slightly affects the species are intentional fires. Grasslands are burnt to promote grass growth, however this is done before the rain season when Rebutia fabrisii is slender and practically buried in the ground, because of this the fire only burns on the surface and the plants survive the fires. Grazing animals are not a threat for the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very limited range of approximately 100 km<sup>2</sup>, however there are no threats and the population is stable, hence is here listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Rebutia minuscula K.Schum. sec. Ritz & al. 2007
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in Catamarca, Jujuy, Salta and Tucumán at elevations of 1,700 to 2,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in yunga forest on rocky outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are illegal collection and land use change due to agriculture and farming.
iucn_doc_rationale: Rebutia minuscula has a wide range and is locally abundant. There are threats, mainly related to land use change and illegal collection, in part of its range. With the currently available information the species is listed as Least Concern. However, monitoring of trade and the populations is urgently needed because the experts suspect that extraction might be heavily affecting the subpopulations.
