Rhipsalidopsis rosea (Lagerh.) Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
Rhipsalis agudoensis N.P.Taylor sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_doc_range: The species' distribution is unknown, it is supposed to occur in Rio Grande du Sol, Brazil, but there is no supporting information.
iucn_doc_habitats: The habitat of this species is unknown.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are not known.
iucn_doc_rationale: There is no information whatsoever on this species. It was reported from in Rio Grande do Sul (Hunt et al. 2006), however there is no information to support this. The type specimen was described from a cultivated individual. In recent field expeditions, experts have been searching for this species without success, which makes the validity of the collection locality very dubious (J. Larocca pers. comm. 2012). Without information on its origin, it is impossible to establish, its range, population size, and threats. It is therefore listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis baccifera (Sol. ex J.S.Muell.) Stearn sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_common_name: Conambaia; Duhudumna rubban; Enxerto; Irahuka"ara; Tjimoe; Tripa de Galinha; Tyimu; Mistletoe Cactus
iucn_locations: Anguilla; South Africa; Grenada; Sri Lanka; Honduras; French Guiana; Costa Rica; Brazil; Nicaragua; Jamaica; Florida; Paraguay; Ecuador; Colombia; Hidalgo; Peru; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Quintana Roo; Bonaire; Virgin Islands, U.S.; Puerto Rico; Belize; Puebla; Mpumalanga; Haiti; Saint Martin (French part); Saint Vincent and the Grenadines; Paraíba; Sint Eustatius; Chiapas; Barbados; Saba; Pernambuco; Jalisco; Montserrat; San Luis Potosí; Saint Lucia; Veracruz; Guadeloupe; Trinidad and Tobago; Tamaulipas; São Paulo; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Yucatán; Oaxaca; Querétaro; Antigua and Barbuda; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Guatemala; Martinique; Dominican Republic; Curaçao; Guyana; Saint Kitts and Nevis; United States; KwaZulu-Natal; Tabasco; Sint Maarten (Dutch part); Campeche; Eastern Cape Province; Bahia; Dominica; Suriname; Cuba; Madagascar; Maranhão; Panama; Aruba; Ceará; Virgin Islands, British; El Salvador; Mexico; Argentina
iucn_location_codes: AI; ZA; GD; LK; HN; GF; CR; BR; NI; JM; FLA-OO; PY; EC; CO; MXE-HI; PE; BQ; MXT-QR; NLA-BO; VI; PR; BZ; MXC-PU; TVL-MP; HT; MF; VC; BZE-PB; NLA-SE; MXT-CI; BB; NLA-SA; BZE-PE; MXS-JA; MS; MXE-SL; LC; MXG-VC; GP; TT; MXE-TA; BZL-SP; VE; MXT-YU; MXS-OA; MXE-QU; AG; BO; GT; MQ; DO; CW; GY; KN; US; NAT-OO; MXT-TB; SX; MXT-CA; CPP-EC; BZE-BA; DM; SR; CU; MG; BZE-MA; PA; AW; BZE-CE; VG; SV; MX; AR
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Swamp; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Mangrove Vegetation Above High Tide Level; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_doc_range:
The species is widely distributed in tropical America and the Caribbean. It can be found in Argentina, Aruba, Bolivia, Brazil, Belize, Colombia, Costa Rica, Cuba, Dominican Republic, Ecuador, French Guiana, Guatemala, Guyana, Haití, Honduras, Jamaica, Lesser Antilles, Mexico, Netherlands Antilles, Nicaragua, Panama, Peru, Puerto Rico, Paraguay, Suriname, Trinidad and Tobago, United States and Venezuela (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations between sea level and 1,700 m.
The species is found in the Mexican states of Campeche, Chiapas, Hidalgo, Jalisco, Oaxaca, Puebla, Querétaro, Quintana Roo, San Luis Potosí, Tabasco, Tamaulipas, Veracruz, and Yucatán (Guzmán et al. 2003). In Belize, the species is distributed in the southwestern part of the country in the Chiquibul Forest Reserve (Bridgewater et al. 2006) but its range probably extends to other areas of this country. In Brazil it occurs in Maranhão, Ceará, Paraíba, Pernambuco, and Bahia from sea level to 800 m (Taylor and Zappi 2004). In Venezuela, it is broadly distributed in the central coastal states and all over the western part of the country, while in the east it is found in Sucre, Monagas, and Delta Amacuro states (Ponce 1989). In Peru, the species is found in Jaén, Cajamarca; Tingo, Luya and Chachapoyas, Amazonas; and in Quillabamba, Cusco (Ostolaza 2011).

The species also occurs in tropical Africa from the Ivory Coast to Ethiopia and as far south as South Africa, Madagascar and the Mascarene Islands. It is also reported from Sri Lanka (Hunt et al. 2006; Korotkova 2011).
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species occurs in a wide variety of habitats. In the northern Mexican State of Tamaulipas it is found in low and medium elevation forests (Hernández-Sandoval et al. 1991). In the United States, in the state of Florida, the species occurs in mangrove tidal swamp, epiphytic on buttonwood (Conocarpus erectus; ETFLORA 2002). In the West Indies, Rhipsalis baccifera can be found in moist forests (Tatje 1980 in ETFLORA 2002). In Belize, it occurs in forests (Bridegewater et al. 2006). In Brazil, it grows as an Amazonian forest element: it is epiphytic in mata de brejo and mata tabuleiro and in disjunct Bahian humid forest (Taylor and Zappi 2004). In Africa it is widespread.

In Africa: Epiphytic on rain forest trees and in riverine forest, also in humus on shady rocks (0-14,00 m)
iucn_doc_threats: Given the huge range of this species, it is likely to be affected by a number of minor threats. However, none of these threats is significant enough to warrant any concern.
iucn_doc_rationale: An extremely widespread species, Rhipsalis baccifera is usually abundant where it occurs. Even though it may be exposed to threats in some areas, none of them are significant to the overall status of the species. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis barthlottii Ralf Bauer & N.Korotkova sec. Bauer & Korotkova 2016
Rhipsalis burchellii Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Santa Catarina; Paraná; Espírito Santo; São Paulo; Brazil
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in southeastern and southern Brazil in the states of São Paulo, Espírito Santo, Paraná and Santa Catarina. It occurs at elevations up to 900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This epiphytic cactus grows in Atlantic Forest (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: There is gradual reduction in the extent of forest due to agricultural and urban expansion, but not to the same degree as in the northern Atlantic forest areas.
iucn_doc_rationale: Rhipsalis burchellii has a fairly wide range, is common and although there are some threats these are not significant enough to warrant any concern. Hence, the species is listed as Last Concern.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis cereoides (Backeb. & Voll) Backeb. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Espírito Santo; Rio de Janeiro; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_growth_form_names: Epiphyte; Lithophyte; Succulent - form unknown; Succulent - shrub
iucn_doc_range: Found in southern Espírito Santo (Domingos Martins); Rio de Janeiro (both sides of the Baía de Guanabara). Area of occupancy <2,000 km<sup>2</sup>. Known from three locations - Sugar Loaf Mt, Miteroi Mts across the bay from Rio and Domingos Martins. The elevation range is not known, although the sites around Rio are below 200 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: Southern humid forest (inselberg) element: lithophyte on gneissic inselbergs (rarely epiphytic on nearby trees).
iucn_doc_threats: There may have been loss of habitat in the past because of the expansion of the city of Rio de Janeiro, however, the subpopulations that remain appear to be in secure habitats at present: However, as the urban areas grow there is increasing human impacts on these areas - rock climbing is becoming more popular and this may affect the plants and the urban expansion may result in a change in the micro-climate.
iucn_doc_rationale: Altogether known from less than five locations, some being close to the cities of Rio de Janeiro and Niterói, plants at lower elevations were almost affected by urban expansion and coastal tourism, the remaining subpopulations are not declining at present, but may be impacted in the future through human disturbance and a changing micro-climate. Hence although this species has a small area of occupany of < 2,000 km2, is known from three subpopulations which are not currently declining, hence it is listed as Near Threatened as it almost qualifies for threatened under criterion B2ab(iii).

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis cereuscula Haw. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Bahia; Santa Catarina; Entre Ríos; Alagoas; Argentina; Minas Gerais; Corrientes; Sergipe; Brazil; Rio Grande do Sul; Misiones; São Paulo; Rio de Janeiro; Uruguay; Mato Grosso do Sul; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Paraíba; Pernambuco; Paraguay; Paraná
iucn_location_codes: BZE-BA; BZS-SC; AGE-ER; BZE-AL; AR; BZL-MG; AGE-CN; BZE-SE; BR; BZS-RS; AGE-MI; BZL-SP; BZL-RJ; UY; BZC-MS; BO; BZE-PB; BZE-PE; PY; BZS-PR
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Agro-industry farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas; Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in northeastern Pernambuco, eastern Bahia and central-southern to southern Minas Gerais, then across all of south-central Brazil to La Paz in Bolivia. It also occurs in northern Argentina, Paraguay and Uruguay. It grows at altitudes between 200 and 1,400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows as an epiphyte in mata atlântica, including mata de brejo and mata agreste, and mata do planalto (Taylor and Zappi 2004). It grows in lowland to submontane tropical moist forest.
iucn_doc_threats: There is destruction of its habitat in northeastern Brazil (Taylor and Zappi 2004) due to agricultural and urban expansion. This is likely to occur in other parts of the range, too.
iucn_doc_rationale: Rhipsalis cereuscula has a very wide range and is presumably abundant. Although there are threats in places, they are not expected to affect the species in a way that would warrant listing in a threatened category. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis clavata F.A.C.Weber sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: São Paulo; Minas Gerais; Rio de Janeiro; Brazil
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Brazil in southern Minas Gerais, São Paulo and Rio de Janeiro. It grows from sea level to 1,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a southern humid forest element: it grows as an epiphyte in mata atlântica (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this cactus is loss of suitable habitat due to urban expansion.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a fairly small area of occupancy (less than 2,000 km<sup>2</sup>), it is known from about six sites that are all mostly limited to protected areas, and hence the species is not currently in decline. However, should the status of any of the protected areas change this could trigger a threatened listing. It is therefore listed as Near Threatened as it almost qualifies for Vulnerable under criterion B2ab(iii).

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis crispata (Haw.) Pfeiff. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Rio de Janeiro; São Paulo; Pernambuco; Brazil
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry farming; Tourism & recreation areas; Recreational activities; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in Mun. Caruaru, eastern Pernambuco, in southeastern Brazil (coast E of Rio de Janeiro between Niterói and Cabo Frio, and inner São Paulo). The single record from Pernambuco is markedly disjunct from other known sites in Rio de Janeiro (Cabo Frio, Silva Jardim, Saquarema and Itacoatiara) and São Paulo (Rio Claro and Altinópolis). There are two known collections from southern Bahia, one of which was lost recently as the tree the plant occurred in was chopped down. The continued occurrence in Pernambuco is also doubtful. The species is thus only known for cetain now from eight to ten very small and highly disjunct localities. Hence the area of occupancy is unlikely to exceed 500 km2. It occurs from 0 - 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: A humid/sub-humid forest element, an epiphyte in mata de brejo. Rhipsalis crispata is a species of more markedly seasonal or drier habitats, where it has been found as an epiphyte or lithophyte, both near the coast and far inland, but it can also grow in the sand of the restinga (Cabo Frio, RJ).
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat loss due to a variety of factors, but in particular expanding urbanization, is a major threat as all the known localities are very close to expanding urban settlements.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has a restricted range (area of occupancy is less than 500 km²), its range is severely fragmented, it is known from eight to ten extant localities, all of which are subject to anthropogenic impacts. This species is rarely observed. There has been widespread destruction of its habitat.  At least two or more subpopulations may no longer be extant, although this needs to be confirmed.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis cuneata Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in La Paz and Cochabamba. It grows at altitudes ranging from 700 up to 1,500 m asl (Ibisch et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_habitats: This is an epiphytic species found growing on trees in moist montane forest (yungas) (Ibisch et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species. However, land use change potentially affects some of the known subpopulations (Ibisch et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_rationale: Rhipsalis cuneata has been collected from a few localities and it is very likely that it occurs more widely in suitable habitat. Land use change is a probable threat to some of its subpopulations but this is not considered to be a significant threat. It has a wide range (its extent of occurrence is over 20,000 km2) and it is locally abundant. Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis dissimilis (G.Lindb.) K.Schum. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: São Paulo; Santa Catarina; Paraná; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder plantations; Recreational activities; Named species
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Paraná, Sao Paulo and Santa Catarina (not sure if still there) at 800 to 1,100 m asl (Hunt at el. 2006). The species is only known with certainty from three sites in Sao Paulo, the fourth site in Paraná may be a different species.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows as a lithophyte on rocks in open seasonal habitats (Hunt et al. 2006). The surrounding vegetation would have been lowland forest but it has mostly been destroyed.
iucn_doc_threats: The rocks are used for sporting activities, for climbing and as platforms for taking off on hang gliders. The areas are also impacted by eucalyptus plantations and these activities result in the introduction of invasive species, particularly various species of African grasses.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very particular habitat requirement and as a result it has a very small area of occupancy which is certainly much less than 500 km<sup>2</sup>, and may even be close to the Critically Endangered threshold of 10 km<sup>2</sup>. The species is only known from less than five locations all quite far apart and is hence severely fragmented and there is continuing decline in the quality of the habitat due to a variety of threats.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis elliptica G.Lindb. ex K.Schum. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Minas Gerais; Santa Catarina; São Paulo; Paraná; Rio de Janeiro; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZL-MG; BZS-SC; BZL-SP; BZS-PR; BZL-RJ; BR
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use: (large scale) [harvest]; Agro-industry farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species has been recorded from central, southeastern and southern Minas Gerais; it is common elsewhere in southeastern and southern Brazil (Rio de Janeiro, São Paulo, Paraná, Santa Catalina). It occurs from sea level to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows as an epiphyte or lithophyte in Mata atlântica, including mata de neblina. It is often found on very steep slopes that are less impacted by deforestation.
iucn_doc_threats: Deforestation for urbanization and expanding agriculture are the main threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Rhipsalis elliptica has a wide range, is fairly common and although there are threats in places they do not warrant listing in a threatened category. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis ewaldiana Barthlott & N.P.Taylor sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is reported from Brazil (Hunt et al. 2006), but the type locality is unknown. The plant has been seen in cultivation at the Parque Nacional Serra des Organs, so it may well be from that area and further surveys are required.
iucn_doc_habitats: The habitat of this species is unknown, but possibly lowland or montane moist forest. Probably an epiphyte, but not certain.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: There is no information on the origin of the species and therefore its range, populations trends and threats are unknown. Hence, the species is listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis flagelliformis N.P.Taylor & Zappi sec. Taylor & al. 2014
Rhipsalis floccosa Salm-Dyck ex Pfeiff. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Mato Grosso do Sul; Bahia; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Rio de Janeiro; Alagoas; Brazil; Paraguay; São Paulo; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Paraná; Argentina; Minas Gerais; Sergipe; Pernambuco; Santa Catarina; Peru; Misiones; Rio Grande do Sul; Espírito Santo
iucn_location_codes: BZC-MS; BZE-BA; BO; BZL-RJ; BZE-AL; BR; PY; BZL-SP; VE; BZS-PR; AR; BZL-MG; BZE-SE; BZE-PE; BZS-SC; PE; AGE-MI; BZS-RS; BZL-ES
iucn_habitat_names: Artificial/Terrestrial - Urban Areas; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Artificial/Terrestrial - Plantations; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Unintentional effects: (large scale) [harvest]; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range:
Rhipsalis floccosa occurs in Brazil, Argentina, Bolivia, Peru, and Venezuela (Nassar et al. 2013) from sea level 1,750 m asl.
In Brazil, it is recorded from eastern Pernambuco to Espírito Santo; extending southwestwards into the interior of southeastern Brazil (Minas Gerais and São Paulo), where it is frequent; it is replaced by subspecies oreophila in the East Brazilian Highlands, and by subspecies pulvinigera in mountains and coastal zones of southeastern and southern Brazil. In eastern Paraguay and northeastern Argentina, it is replaced by subspecies hohenauensis. In the eastern Andes of Argentina, Bolivia and Peru (Junín), it is represented by subspecies tucumanensis. In northern Venezuela, it is replaced by subspecies pittieri.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is widespread in humid forests (both lowland and montane). It is epiphytic or epilithic in Mata atlântica, including mata de brejo and the eastern foot of the Chapada Diamantina (Bahia). The species also grows on trees in plantations and trees planted in urban areas.
iucn_doc_threats: There is habitat loss in parts of the range due to logging and clearance for agricultural and urban expansion; however, given the wide range of this species, these are not significant threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Rhipsalis floccosa is listed as Least Concern because it is a widespread and abundant species across South America with many subspecies. Although there are threats in parts of the range, these are not significant to warrant any concern.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis goebeliana Backeb. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
Rhipsalis grandiflora Haw. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Santa Catarina; São Paulo; Paraná; Rio de Janeiro; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Artificial/Terrestrial - Urban Areas; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas; Small-holder plantations
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Paraná, Rio de Janeiro, Santa Catarina and São Paulo. It grows from sea level to 1,200 m asl (Hunt et al.  2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This epilithic and epiphytic cactus grows on coastal rocks and in Atlantic and seasonal forest (Hunt et al. 2006). It also grows on trees in urban areas.
iucn_doc_threats: There is loss of habitat due to expansion of coastal touristic developments, expanding agriculture and urbanization, and from forestry (Eucalyptus).
iucn_doc_rationale: Rhipsalis grandiflora has a wide range and although there are some threats to its habitat the species is tolerant of disturbance and can colonize trees in urban areas. Hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis hoelleri Barthlott & N.P.Taylor sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_doc_range: This species is reported from Domingos Martins in the state of Espírito Santo, Brazil  (Taylor and Zappi 2004). This species is only known from the type collection made in 1987.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in southern humid forest element, but the precise habitat details are unknown.
iucn_doc_threats: The area is undergoing rapid urban development (high cost housing), and it is possible that the habitat where this species occurred has been lost completely, but without further investigation it is difficult to say so with any cetainty.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is known only from the type collection made in 1987. Still awaiting rediscovery to find out further details about  the population, habitat, threats, etc. So although it is possible that the type locality has been completely destroyed , it is better to list this species as Data Deficient until a field survey to determine what has happened to the species has been undertaken.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis juengeri Barthlott & N.P.Taylor sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Espírito Santo; São Paulo; Minas Gerais; Brazil
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in southeastern and southern Brazil in the states of Minas Gerais, São Paulo and Espírito Santo. It grows at altitudes between 700 and 1,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This epiphytic cactus grows in the Atlantic Forest (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Rhipsalis juengeri appears to have a small area of occupancy, there are no declines at present because most of the sites are within protected areas. As long as the protection of these areas is maintained, the species should be secure. Any reduction in their protection status could, however, result in this species moving to a very threatened category within a short time. The species could potentially qualify as Vulnerable under criterion D2 if there were serious concerns about the future of these protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis lindbergiana K.Schum. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Espírito Santo; Sergipe; Bahia; Pernambuco; Minas Gerais; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZL-ES; BZE-SE; BZE-BA; BZE-PE; BZL-MG; BR
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Artificial/Terrestrial - Urban Areas; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Brazil, where it can be found in Pernambuco, Sergipe, Bahia, Minas Gerais and Espírito Santo; mostly within 150 km of the coast and on the lower eastern slopes of the Chapada Diamantina, Serra da Jacobina and Serra do Espinhaço. It grows from sea level to 1,000 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows as an epiphyte or less commonly a lithophyte in high restinga forest, mata atlântica, mata de brejo and rarely at edges of caatinga-agreste and in campo rupestre (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species as it is very tolerant of habitat loss and rapidly colonizes trees growing in urban areas. It is probably more common in urban areas than in its natural habitat.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a widespread and very common species with no threats. It is very tolerant of habitat disturbance and grows readily in urban areas. Hence, Rhipsalis lindbergiana is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis mesembryanthemoides Haw. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Rio de Janeiro in the city of Rio and on the other side of the bay at Nitaroi from sea level up to 600 m asl (Hunt et al.  2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs as an epiphyte on trees in public parks. The original habitat was presumably lowland forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat is urbanization, although all that is left of the species are plants growing on trees in public parks.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very small extent of occurrence and area of occupancy, and today it is only known from public parks in the greater urban area of Rio de Janeiro. There is likely to be continuing decline due to the impacts of the urban environment, especially as the city gets warmer and drier. The species is therefore listed as Criticially Endangered and it is potentially extinct in the wild as it no longer occurs in any natural habitat even though it has colonized the urban areas.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis micrantha (Kunth) DC. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Colombia; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Ecuador; Peru; Costa Rica
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Agro-industry farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species has been reported from Colombia, Costa Rica, Ecuador, and Peru, where it occurs at altitudes between 0 and 2,500 m (Best and Kessler 1995, van den Eynden et al. 1999, Hunt et al. 2006).  Hunt et al. (2006) list the species as occurring in Venezuela; however, it is not included in the catalog of Venezuelan Vascular Plants (Hokche et al. 2008) under R. micrantha or any synonym.
iucn_doc_habitats: This epiphytic cactus inhabits tropical dry forests, shrubby vegetation, and rain forest (Best and Kessler 1995, van den Eyden et al. 1999).
iucn_doc_threats: In Costa Rica, its habitat is being degraded by expansion of commercial agriculture (coffee) and urban and tourist development.
iucn_doc_rationale: Given the wide range of Rhipsalis micrantha, its apparent abundance and the lack of threats, it is listed as Least Concern. However, in parts of its range, such as Costa Rica, it may be necessary to list it as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis oblonga Loefgr. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Espírito Santo; Rio de Janeiro; São Paulo; Bahia; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Agro-industry farming; Unintentional effects: (large scale) [harvest]
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in southeastern Bahia (and possibly in Espírito Santo but there are no definite records yet); to southeastern Brazil (São Paulo, Serra do Mar). The northern most localities have probably been lost due to habitat destruction. The range is coastal from southern Bahia to southern São Paulo but is very scattered and disjunct. It occurs from sea level up to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It is a humid forest element: epiphyte in very humid Mata atlântica, at low elevations.
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat loss due to expanding agriculture and urbanization plus logging are the main threats, especially in the northern part of its range. The northern-most localities have probably been lost.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species almost certainly has a small area of occupancy of less than 2,000 km<sup>2</sup>, it is severely fragmented, and there is continuing decline because of habitat destruction especially in the north. The species therefore qualifies for listing as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis occidentalis Barthlott & Rauh sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_doc_range: Rhipsalis occidentalis occurs in Ecuador (provinces of Morona-Santiago, Napo and Zamora-Chinchipe), Peru (state of San Martín), and in Suriname at elevations of up to 950 m asl (Hunt et al. 2006). It has a rather fragmented distribution.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species inhabits very humid forests.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is deforestation for timber.
iucn_doc_rationale: Rhipsalis occidentalis is listed as Least Concern because it has a relatively widespread distribution and there are no known major threats affecting it. However, close monitoring of the forests where it occurs is recommended as they may be subject to deforestation.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis olivifera N.P.Taylor & Zappi sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Rio de Janeiro; São Paulo; Brazil
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: Species endemic to Brazil, occurring in Rio de Janeiro and Sao Paulo at 1,500 to2,000 m (Hunt et al. 2006). The species is known from four sites ranging over 2.5 degrees of longitude.
iucn_doc_habitats: An epiphyte growing in cloud forest.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species as the known areas where it occurs are all currently protected.
iucn_doc_rationale: The area of occupancy of this species is probably less than 2,000 km2, it is known from four sites which are far enough apart to be treated as severely fragmented, however, there is no continuing decline because all the sites are currently protected. The species is therefore listed as Near Thratened as it almost qualifies for a Vulnerable listing under criterion  B2ab(iii). The survival of this species is completely dependent on the maintenance of these protected areas. it is possible that it occurred more widely and was lost in the areas in between the current known sites because of habitat loss.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis ormindoi N.P.Taylor & Zappi sec. Taylor & Zappi 1997
iucn_doc_range: Species occurs in Brazil, in Rio de Janeiro (Hunt et al. 2006). Known from a single area only - Sitio Sophronites - a private reserve (RPPN)belonging to David Miller and in the adjacent Macae de Cima biological reserve. Occurs at 1,500 to 1,600 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is an epiphyte growing in cloud forest.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to the species as the area is protected.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is a restricted range endemic confined as far as is known to a private reserve and presumably the adjacent biological reserve, althought it is quite secure where it is at present the species has to be listed as Near Threatened as it is entirely dependent on the maintence of the current protected areas and almost qualifies for listing as threatened under criterion B2ab. The species may occur in the area outside of the reserves to the southwest which at present is inaccessible as there are no roads in the area and the slopes are very steep, but as soon as the area is opened up, the area will rapidly be clared for agricutltural purposes.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis pachyptera Pfeiff. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Santa Catarina; São Paulo; Paraná; Rio de Janeiro; Brazil
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Housing & urban areas; Tourism & recreation areas; Small-holder plantations
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Paraná, Rio de Janeiro, Santa Catarina and São Paulo (Hunt et al. 2006). Records from Rio Grande do Sul are probably a different species. It occurs from sea level to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows as a lithophyte and as an epiphyte in Atlantic forest. It is common at low elevations (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The habitats, especially at lower levels, are threatened by expanding tourist developments, urbanization, Eucalyptus plantations and small-scale crop farming.
iucn_doc_rationale: Rhipsalis pachyptera is listed as Least Concern as it has a wide range, is fairly common, and although there are a number of threats, none are significant enough to warrant listing in a more threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis paradoxa (Salm-Dyck ex Pfeiff.) Salm-Dyck sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Santa Catarina; Minas Gerais; Rio de Janeiro; Espírito Santo; Pernambuco; São Paulo; Bahia; Paraná; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZS-SC; BZL-MG; BZL-RJ; BZL-ES; BZE-PE; BZL-SP; BZE-BA; BZS-PR; BR
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use: (large scale) [harvest]; Housing & urban areas; Agro-industry farming
iucn_doc_range: This species has been recorded from eastern Brazil in the states of Pernambuco, eastern Bahia, central-eastern Minas Gerais, Espírito Santo, Rio de Janeiro, São Paulo, Paraná and Santa Catarina. It occurs from sea level to 950 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a humid forest epiphyte that can be found in the Atlantic forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The species (in particular subspecies septentrionalis) is impacted by habitat loss due to the effects of expanding urbanization and agriculture, plus general deforestation.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species as a whole is widespread and abundant (particularly subspecies paradoxa), occurs in many protected areas, and is fairly secure across much of its range. Hence, Rhipsalis paradoxa is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis pentaptera Pfeiff. ex A.Dietr. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_habitat_names: Artificial/Terrestrial - Urban Areas; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil in Rio de Janeiro (Hunt et al. 2006); the species is confined to a few private properties in the city, growing on trees in their gardens at less than 100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species would have occurred as an epipyhte in lowland moist forest, but the area is now entirely an urban environment. The trees the species grows on, may be remnants of the former forest, but the species cannot be considered to be growing in a truly wild state.
iucn_doc_threats: Urbanization would have destroyed all the natural habitat and the future safety of the remaining plants depends entirely on the property owners and whether or not they keep the trees on their properties. As the demand for housing increases it is likely that many of these trees will be removed or if they die are unlikely to be replaced, so the species could well become completely extinct in the wild in the not too distant future.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is only known to occur in private gardens within a tiny area in the city of Rio de Janeiro, as far as we know it is probably still declining due to the ongoing impacts of urbanization and the area can be considered a single location (the extent of occurrence and area of occupancy are the same, just over two square kilometres). The species is therefore listed as Critically Endangered and it might be Extinct in the Wild very soon.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis pilocarpa Loefgr. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Espírito Santo; Rio de Janeiro; Paraná; Minas Gerais; São Paulo; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZL-ES; BZL-RJ; BZS-PR; BZL-MG; BZL-SP; BR
iucn_threat_names: Motivation Unknown/Unrecorded; Housing & urban areas; Agro-industry farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in southern Minas Gerais (Rio Preto) and southern Espírito Santo (Domingos Martins); southeastern and southern Brazil (Sao Paulo, Rio de Janeiro to Paraná). It is known only from eight sites across its range. It occurs at 500 to 900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It is a southern humid forest element: epiphyte (rarely lithophyte) in Mata atlantica. It occurs in eight locations, and is widely distributed but rarely observed in nature.
iucn_doc_threats: Loss of habitat due to expanding agriculture and urbanization (especially at the type locality) is a major threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is known from only eight localities throughout its entire range and is apparently rare. Its area of occupancy is estimated to be less than 2,000 km², its range is severely fragmented, and there is and ongoing decline in the extent and quality of its habitat.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis pulchra Loefgr. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Rio de Janeiro; Minas Gerais; São Paulo; Brazil
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in southeastern Brazil in the states of Minas Gerais, Rio de Janeiro and São Paulo. It grows at altitudes above 1,500 m and up to 2,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows as an epiphyte in cloud forest (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no real threats to this species as most of the known localities are within protected areas.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Rhipsalis pulchra has a fairly restricted range, it is locally common, and there are no major threats as most of the sites are within well-managed protected areas. As long as these areas remain well protected, the future safety of this beautiful plant is secure. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis puniceodiscus G.Lindb. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Paraná; São Paulo; Rio de Janeiro; Santa Catarina; Brazil
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder plantations; Tourism & recreation areas; Small-holder farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Paraná, Rio de Janeiro, Santa Catarina and São Paulo, from near sea level to 1,400 asl (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs as an epiphyte in Atlantic forest (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: There is habitat loss outside of protected areas due to expansion of tourist developments, expanding urbanization, forestry for Eucalyptus and clearance for small-scale crop farming.
iucn_doc_rationale: Rhipsalis puniceodiscus is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range and is still abundant. There are some threats that are impacting the habitat, but none of these are severe enough to warrant listing in a more threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis rhombea (Salm-Dyck) Pfeiff. sec. Bauer & Korotkova 2020
Rhipsalis russellii Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Mato Grosso do Sul; Bahia; Mato Grosso; Minas Gerais; Paraná; São Paulo; Espírito Santo; Paraguay; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZC-MS; BZE-BA; BZC-MT; BZL-MG; BZS-PR; BZL-SP; BZL-ES; PY; BR
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Unintentional effects: (large scale) [harvest]; Agro-industry farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species can be found from the Chapada Diamantina and Serra do Espinhaço eastwards in Bahia, Minas Gerais and Espírito Santo, as well as in central-western (Goiás, Mato Grosso, Mato Grosso do Sul and Paraná) and southeastern Brazil (São Paulo). It has also been recorded from eastern Paraguay. It grows at altitudes from 50 to 1,050 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a widespread humid forest/campo rupestre element: it grows epilithic or epiphytic in campo rupestre, mata de brejo and mata atlântica and their ecotones with caatinga-agreste. It occurs mostly in hot places.
iucn_doc_threats: There has been extensive habitat loss, especially in Bahia, due to expanding agriculture and urbanization and logging of the forests.
iucn_doc_rationale: The range of Rhipsalis russellii is markedly disjunct. Its habitat has severely contracted, especially in southeastern Bahia, where the most recent collection was obtained in 1971. Elsewhere, it is found within some reasonably secure protected areas, such as Parque Nacional Chapada Diamantina in Bahia, but its ecological niches are invariably limited. The area of occupancy is estimated to be less than 2,000 km², the range is severely fragmented, and there is continuing decline in area of occupancy, number of locations and subpopulations, and loss of mature individuals. This cactus therefore qualifies for listing as Vulnerable under criterion B2.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis sulcata F.A.C.Weber sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_doc_range: The range of this species is poorly understood; only one collection of documented provenance has been seen - supposedly from Dominingo Martins in Espirito Santo, Brazil. The elevation is unknown.
iucn_doc_habitats: Presumably an epiphyte or lithophyte in southern humid forest: its ecology is unknown.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species, until recently, was known only in cultivation, where it was misidentified as R. micrantha (Andes to Central America). It is possible that it was first collected in the vicinity of Rio de Janeiro and may not be an endemic of the core area of Eastern Brazil, although its range is presently documented from only one collection from southern Espírito Santo. Given that so little is known about this taxon, and even its provenance is uncertain; it has to be listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis teres (Vell.) Steud. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_habitat_names: Artificial/Terrestrial - Urban Areas; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in southeastern and southern Brazil in Minas Gerais and Espírito Santo (Taylor and Zappi 2004). It grows at elevations below 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows as an epiphyte or lithophyte in mata atlântica, where it is common in the Serra da Mantiqueira and Serra do Mar (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: There is ongoing deforestation for urban expansion and agricultural expansion. However, these do not pose a problem as the species grows on street trees in towns.
iucn_doc_rationale: Rhipsalis teres is a widespread and common species. Although it is experiencing some loss of habitat, this is not seen as a major threat because the species can colonize street trees. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Rhipsalis triangularis Werderm. sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is only known from west of the city of Rio de Janeiro near Prainha, Brazil.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is a lithophyte growing on rocks on an inselberg near to the coast.
iucn_doc_threats: Human activities are likely to pose a threat to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a tiny extent of occurrence and area of occupancy (less than 2 km<sup>2</sup>), it is known from a single location and there is ongoing human disturbance, hence it is listed as Critically Endangered although the area where it occurs is a Municipal Reserve.

checklist_taxon: Rimacactus laui (Lüthy) Mottram sec. Guerrero & al. 2020
Salmonopuntia salmiana (J.Parm. ex Pfeiff.) P.V.Heath sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
Salmonopuntia schickendantzii (F.A.C.Weber) Font & M.Köhler sec. Köhler & al. 2021
Schlumbergera kautskyi (Horobin & McMillan) N.P.Taylor sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_synonyms: Schlumbergera truncata (Haw.) Moran ssp. kautskyi Horobin & McMillan
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder plantations; Mining & quarrying; Motivation Unknown/Unrecorded; Housing & urban areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: The species is found in central-southern Espírito Santo and in eastern Minas Gerais, Brazil. It occurs at elevations from 900 to 1,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is considered an element of southern humid forest (inselberg), is a lithophytic species that grows on inselbergs in the Mata Atlântica and its rarely an epiphytic (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: Continued decline of habitat due agriculture activities and most recently for Eucalyptus tree plantation and granite rock extraction are the major threats. The type locality has possibly been destroyed by expanding urban developments and this trend continues through out the species range.
iucn_doc_rationale: The area of occupancy is estimated to be less than 500 km² (it is possibly even less than 10 km² but that would need to be confirmed), the two known localities are far apart and hence it is treated as severely fragmented and much of the area at the type locality is being developed as a residential centre. Hence is listed as Endangered. The population within the national park needs to be surveyed to determine its size, extent and any threats to it.

checklist_taxon: Schlumbergera lutea Calvente & Zappi sec. Calvente & al. 2011
Schlumbergera microsphaerica (K.Schum.) P.V.Heath sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Rio de Janeiro; Minas Gerais; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in Serra do Caparaó, Minas Gerais and from Rio de Janeiro (Serra de Itatiaia - the type locality) in Brazil. It has now been recorded further to the west of Serra do Caparaó on the Serra Brigadeiro in Minas Gerais. It occurs from 2,000 to 2,700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This plant is considered an element of southern humid forest, it is a lithophytic or epiphytic of the mata de neblina.
iucn_doc_threats: All the sites of occurrence are in protected areas, but the species could be impacted by people visiting the area.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although all the known range of this species is within protected areas, it has a small area of occupancy and is only known from three locations. The species occurs in areas that are well-frequented by people and human activities pose a potential future threat to the species, especially if tourism increases. Hence it is listed as Vulnerable under criterion D2.

checklist_taxon: Schlumbergera opuntioides (Loefgr. & Dusén) D.R.Hunt sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: São Paulo; Rio de Janeiro; Minas Gerais; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in Serra da Mantiqueira, southern Minas Gerais, to northwestern Rio de Janeiro (Itatiaia) and eastern São Paulo (Campos do Jordão). It occurs from 1,600 to 2,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is considered an element of southern humid forest, it is a lithophytic/epiphytic in mata de neblina (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: All of the areas where it occurs are protected to some degree. The species is collected by people - it tends to flower at the cold times of the year when tourism is at its highest.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is included within some protected areas and it is dependent on their maintenance. Its area of occupancy is estimated to be less than 500 km², it is known from seven locations and there is continuing decline in mature individuals and area of occupancy due to local illegal collection. The population is very small and if counted might well qualify the species for listing as Critically Endangered. With the current information available the species is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Schlumbergera orssichiana Barthlott & McMillan sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Rio de Janeiro; São Paulo; Brazil
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Unintentional effects (species is not the target); Recreational activities
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic of Rio de Janeiro and Sao Paulo (on the border of the two states), Brazil (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs between 1,000 and 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species presumably grows as an epiphyte in cloud forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The park where the species occurs is very visited, also locals collect firewood and may also collect this plant especially when in flower.
iucn_doc_rationale: The extent of occurrence is about 1,200 km<sup>2</sup>, is known from about three locations, and there is continuing decline because of disturbance to the habitat by people (firewood harvesting) and plants are likely to be collected when in flower as the flowers are very large and striking. Hence is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Schlumbergera russelliana (Hook.) Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Intentional use: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Housing & urban areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This cactus species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Rio de Janeiro (Hunt et al. 2006) from 1,300 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found growing on the stems of trees and supposedly occasionally on the ground but these may have been plants that had fallen from the trees (Hunt et al 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: Several threats affect this plant, away from the protected areas there has been lost and degradation of forest because of logging and expansion of urban areas - it is a popular area for holiday homes. The species is also collected by people.
iucn_doc_rationale: The extent of occurrence is only about 1,400 km<sup>2</sup> and it is known from about three locations and there is continuing decline due to a variety of threats especially outside the protected area. The species is therefore listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Schlumbergera truncata (Haw.) Moran sec. Korotkova & al. 2011
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Housing & urban areas; Recreational activities
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, where it grows from near sea level up to about 1,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is a lithophyte that grows on granite, gneiss inselbergs in Atlantic forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are illegal trade by locals, tourists that illegally collect the plants and impact its habitat, and urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: Schlumbergera truncata has a restricted range (extent of occurrence about 7,000 km2) and is known from about ten locations. The sites are severely fragmented, and there is continuing decline in mature individuals due to illegal collection and other human activities (all known sites are close to urban areas). Hence, it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus brevihamatus (Engelm.) D.R.Hunt sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_common_name: Shorthook Fishhook Cactus; Engelmann's Fishhook Cactus
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Coahuila, Mexico and Texas, USA, at elevations of 300 to 1,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in xerophyllous scrub and thorn scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though its range is not very large, Sclerocactus brevihamatus is locally abundant with no continuing declines and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus glaucus (K.Schum.) L.D.Benson sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_threat_names: Oil & gas drilling; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in western Colorado (United States) in the Navajoan Desert. It grows at elevations of 1,360 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on gravel covered clay hills and slopes overlooking major river systems, in desert scrub dominated by Atriplex.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are illegal collection, oil and gas exploration and urbanization. However, it is protected and managed. This species is threatened by hybridization with Sclerocactus parvifloris, particularly in the southern extent of its range.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus glaucus has a very narrow range (its extent of occurrence is approximately 2,500 km2), however, even though there are some threats, there are no current declines and it is protected and managed. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus mariposensis (Hester) N.P.Taylor sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_common_name: Golfball Cactus; Lloyd's Fishhook Cactus; Silver Column Cactus; Biznaga-bola de Mariposa; Lloyd's Mariposa Cactus
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: The species is known from Coahuila, Mexico and Texas, USA (Hernández et al. 2004). It grows at elevations of 250 to 900 asl (Benson 1982).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows among shrubs and rosette-forming perennials in gravelly soils on arid limestone slopes (Heil et al. 1985), primarily on the Boquillas Formation in sotol-lechuguilla (Dasylirion leiophyllum-Agave lecheguilla) shrublands.
iucn_doc_threats: There is some illegal collection from wild populations, but it is localized and does not pose a major threat.
iucn_doc_rationale:
Sclerocactus mariposensis has a very wide range, is abundant, and although there is illegal collection in some places, this impact is not sufficient to warrant any concern. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

When the species was originally listed as Threatened by the US Fish and Wildlife Service there was insufficient information on the species range and abundance.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus parviflorus Clover & Jotter sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_locations: Colorado; Utah; New Mexico; Arizona; United States
iucn_threat_names: Oil & gas drilling; Intentional use (species is the target); Nomadic grazing; Tourism & recreation areas; Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Colorado, southeast Utah, northern Arizona, and northern New Mexico. It has the widest range of all Sclerocactus species and grows at elevations of 1,000 to 2,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in sage brush, salt bush, black brush and pinyon-juniper forest. It grows on gravel or sand (especially red sandstone) or clay.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by oil and gas exploration, mining, grazing, and off highway vehicle (OHV) activity.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus parviflorus has a wide range, is abundant, the threats are not major, and it occurs within protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus polyancistrus (Engelm. & J.M.Bigelow) Britton & Rose sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_common_name: Mojave Fishhook Cactus; Redspined Fishhook Cactus; Mohave Fishhook Cactus
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Mojave Desert, in California and Nevada (United States). It grows at elevations of 500 to 2,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in rocky soils in Mojave Desert scrub to pinyon-juniper woodland.
iucn_doc_threats: No major threats to this species are known. However, in the southern extent of the range there has been habitat loss due to urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus polyancistrus has a wide range and occurs in inaccessible areas and in protected areas. No major threats are known, hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus scheeri (Salm-Dyck) N.P.Taylor sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_common_name: Tobusch Fishhook Cactus; Biznaga-bola Ganchuda; Scheer's Fish-hook Cactus
iucn_locations: Coahuila; Nuevo León; Tamaulipas; Texas; Mexico; United States
iucn_location_codes: MXE-CO; MXE-NL; MXE-TA; TEX-OO; MX; US
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in the Mexican states of Coahuila, Nuevo León and Tamaulipas and in Texas, USA (Hernández et al. 2004), from sea level to 250 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in Tamaulipan thorn scrub. It grows in grassy plains and open hills or brushy areas in grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus scheeri has a relatively wide range, even though its distribution is scattered there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus spinosior (Engelm.) D.Woodruff & L.D.Benson sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_common_name: Desert Valley Fish-hook Cactus; Desert Valley Fishhook Cactus
iucn_synonyms: Sclerocactus blainei S.L.Welsh & K.H.Thorne; Echinocactus whipplei (Engelm. & J.M.Bigelow) Britton & Rose var. spinosior Engelm.
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range:
Sclerocactus spinosior is endemic to Beaver, Juab, Millard, Iron and Sevier Counties in Utah, United States, where it grows at elevations of 1,500 to 2,000 m asl.
S. spinosior blainei can be found in Nye and Lincoln Counties, Nevada, at elevations of 1,450 to 1,850 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species occurs in pinyon-juniper, sagebrush, and shadscale communities. Soils are typically igneous or calcareous gravels.
S. spinosior blainei grows associated with greasewood, galleta grass and shade scale and sage brush.
iucn_doc_threats: Mining and grazing are moderate threats in portions of the species range.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus spinosior has a relatively large range, is very abundant, and there are no overall threats to the species. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus unguispinus (Engelm.) N.P.Taylor sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Coahuila; Chihuahua; San Luis Potosí; Zacatecas; Durango; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXE-CO; MXE-CU; MXE-SL; MXE-ZA; MXE-DU; MX
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, San Luis Potosí and Zacatecas (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in xerophytic shrubland and very dry grassland.
iucn_doc_threats: The habitat is overgrazed by goats. Plants are removed from the wild by collectors but this is not thought to be a threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus unguispinus is a widespread and abundant species with no major threats. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus warnockii (L.D.Benson) N.P.Taylor sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_common_name: Warnock's Butterfly Cactus; Warnock's Fishhook Cactus; Biznaga-bola Amarilla; Pineapple cactus
iucn_locations: Coahuila; Chihuahua; United States; Mexico; Texas
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Chihuahua and Coahuila, Mexico, and in Texas in the USA (Hernández et al. 2004). It grows at elevations of 900 to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on short grass grasslands and creosote scrub on low hills and gravel slopes of varied composition (Carr 2004). It occurs on limestone hills in desert.
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is not exposed to any major threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus warnockii has a very large range, is abundant, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus whipplei (Engelm. & J.M.Bigelow) Britton & Rose sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_locations: Colorado; New Mexico; Utah; Arizona; United States
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Temperate; Forest - Temperate; Grassland - Temperate
iucn_threat_names: Nomadic grazing; Oil & gas drilling; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in northeastern Arizona, northwestern New Mexico, extreme southwestern Colorado, and extreme southeastern Utah, United States. It grows at altitudes between 1,500 and 2,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in canyon rims, rocky hills, and mesas, in sagebrush, saltbrush, pinyon-juniper woodland and desert grassland.
iucn_doc_threats: Grazing, oil and gas exploration are major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus whipplei has a wide range, is abundant, and occurs in protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern. It is, however, worth noting that part of the range where two subspecies occur is highly threatened.

checklist_taxon: Selenicereus anthonyanus (Alexander) D.R.Hunt sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_locations: Oaxaca; Veracruz; Tabasco; Chiapas; Honduras; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXS-OA; MXG-VC; MXT-TB; MXT-CI; HN; MX
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is thought to be endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the States of Chiapas, Oaxaca, Tabasco and Veracruz (Guzmán et al. 2003, Hunt et al. 2006). However, there is supposedly an occurrence in Honduras that needs confirmation (the specimen named as Weberocereus immitans (Elisa Iriante 159) is at Missouri Botanic Garden). It grows at altitudes between 180 and 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in moist lowland forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat is deforestation for cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Selenicereus anthonyanus has a fairly wide range, is reasonably common where found, and the threats are not severe enough to warrant a threatened listing. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Selenicereus atropilosus Kimnach sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to the Mexican state of Jalisco (Guzmán et al. 2003).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in subtropical moist forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The population is being subdivided and directly impacted by the construction of new roads and highways.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered Endangered due to a very small and restricted range (extent of occurrence is approximately 800 km<sup>2</sup>), it is known from only four locations and the population size is approximately 1,000 mature individuals (however further research is necessary to define "individual" in the case of epiphytic cactus which can form clones). The species occurs in a touristic area which is developing rapidly and is currently threatened by building of new roads.

checklist_fullName: Selenicereus costaricensis (F.A.C.Weber) S.Arias & N.Korotkova ex Hammel

Selenicereus dorschianus Ralf Bauer sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
Selenicereus escuintlensis (Kimnach) D.R.Hunt sec. Korotkova & al. 2017

Selenicereus grandiflorus (L.) Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_common_name: Pitayita-nocturna Reina de la Noche; Queen of the Night
iucn_locations: Guatemala; Jamaica; Oaxaca; Tamaulipas; Cuba; Veracruz; Honduras; Peru; Bahamas; Florida; Campeche; Dominican Republic; Quintana Roo; Tabasco; Mexico; Haiti; Belize; United States; Chiapas; Virgin Islands, U.S.; Yucatán
iucn_location_codes: GT; JM; MXS-OA; MXE-TA; CU; MXG-VC; HN; PE; BS; FLA-OO; MXT-CA; DO; MXT-QR; MXT-TB; MX; HT; BZ; US; MXT-CI; VI; MXT-YU
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Temperate; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Coastal Sand Dunes
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Medicine - human & veterinary; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Food - human
iucn_doc_range:
Selenicereus grandiflorus is widely distributed. It is known from the Bahamas, Belize, Cuba, Dominican Republic, Guatemala, Honduras, Haiti, Jamaica, Mexico, Peru and the United States in the state of Florida (Hunt et al. 2006).
The species is widely distributed in Cuba, occurring in Pinar del Río, Isla de la Juventud, Habana, Matanzas, Santa Clara, and Camagüey. It has been collected in the municipalities of Camagüey, Esmeralda, Guáimaro, Minas, Najasa, Nuevitas and Sierra de Cubitas (Rifá et al. 2005). It occurs in many keys in the southeast of Ciénega of Zapata, Cuba (Borroto-Paéz et al. 2007). In Mexico, it is found in the states of Tamaulipas, Veracruz, Oaxaca, Campeche, Quitana Roo, Tabasco, Chiapas, and Yucatán (Bauer 2003).
Selenicereus grandiflorus subsp. lautneri is endemic to Oaxaca. Selenicereus grandiflorus subsp. hondurensis is found in Guatemala, Honduras, and Nicaragua. Selenicereus grandiflorus subsp. donkelarii is found in southern Mexico.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits subdeciduous sclerophyllous subcoastal forest (Borroto-Páez et al. 2007). In Santiago de Cuba Province, it is also found on exposed limestone rocks among scrubby vegetation in full exposure (N. Taylor pers. comm. 2011). It occurs in tropical dry forest, tropical subdeciduous forest, flooded forest, and coastal dunes in Mexico.
iucn_doc_threats: Forest destruction in the last two centuries has considerably reduced and fragmented the populations. This fragmentation could threaten the species in the near future, regardless of the species being relatively abundant today (Rifá et al. 2005). Local people tend to leave this species when altering native habitats because of its attractive flowers.
iucn_doc_rationale: Selenicereus grandiflorus is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread, common, there are no major threats, and it occurs in several protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Selenicereus guatemalensis (Eichlam ex Weingart) D.R.Hunt sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
Selenicereus hamatus (Scheidw.) Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Temperate; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Construction or structural materials; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: Selenicereus hamatus is endemic to Mexico, where it is known to occur in the states of Oaxaca, Puebla, and Veracruz (Guzmán et al. 2003). The presence and range in Oaxaca need to be confirmed. The species has a restricted range. It is found from 1,000 to 1,800 m asl (M. Cházaro pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in the temperate highlands, in humid, pine-oak, and cloud forest. It can be found in secondary forest. It tolerates some human disturbance. It is a climbing plant (M. Cházaro pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by deforestation for small-holder agriculture for corn and cattle-ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Selenicereus hamatus is listed as Least Concern because it is not experiencing significant declines. Because of its small range and patchy distribution, it could experience significant declines and become threatened with continued habitat loss.

iucn_locations: Oaxaca; Panama; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Chiapas; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Costa Rica; Mexico; Colombia

iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range:
Selenicereus inermis is widely distributed, occurring in Bolivia, Colombia, Costa Rica, Mexico, Panama, and Venezuela at elevations from 0 to 1,800 m asl.
It occurs in Bolivia in the department of La Paz; in Colombia in the departments of Caldas, Magdalena and Norte de Santander; in Costa Rica in Alajuela, Cartago, and Guanacaste; in Mexico in the states of Chiapas and Oaxaca; in Panamá in the Darién; and in Venezuela in Aragua, Bolívar, Carabobo, Distrito Federal, Falcón, Miranda, Monagas, Sucre, and Táchira (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in wet montane forest in Costa Rica.
iucn_doc_threats: In Costa Rica, it is threatened by urban expansion and there is loss of habitat because of logging.
iucn_doc_rationale: Selenicereus inermis is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, it is common where found, and the threats to its habitat are not sufficient to cause concern.

Selenicereus minutiflorus (Britton & Rose) D.R.Hunt sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
Selenicereus monacanthus (Lem.) D.R.Hunt sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
Selenicereus murrillii Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_doc_range: Selenicereus murrilli is endemic to Mexico, where it is known from the states of Jalisco and Colima.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in low dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is declining due to habitat destruction following urbanization and development for tourism.
iucn_doc_rationale: Selenicereus murrillii is considered to be Vulnerable due to its very small and restricted range (extent of occurrence is 5,000 km2) and subpopulations. The species is known from only two disjunct locations and is declining due to habitat loss and conversion as a result of tourist development.

checklist_taxon: Selenicereus nelsonii (Weing.) Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, probably occurring in the states of Chiapas and Oaxaca (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species lives in humid rainforest.
iucn_doc_threats: Very little is known about this species and there is no information on the threats affecting it.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because more research is needed on its taxonomy and ecology to be able to assess its conservation status.

In Mexico it is known from the states of Hidalgo, Oaxaca, San Luis Potosí, Tabasco, and Veracruz (Hunt et al. 2006). Records from Campeche and Quintana Roo are doubtful (Bauer 2003).
iucn_doc_habitats: It is uncertain in which type of habitat the species occurs.
iucn_doc_threats: There is no information on threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because of confusion about its status as a species and difficulty in distinguishing it from other species in the genus.

It grows at elevations of 330 to 1,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical low dry forest, tropical thorny forest, and low and medium deciduous forest.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Selenicereus spinulosus is widely distributed, and it is very abundant in some populations. There are no major threats for the species, hence it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is widely distributed along the western coast of Mexico from southern Sonora to Chiapas and Tabasco.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in subtropical scrub, tropical deciduous forest and tropical subdeciduous forest (CONANP 2007).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Selenicereus vagans is listed as Least Concern due to its wide distribution, presumed large population and because it is not declining at a rate sufficient to qualify for a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Selenicereus validus S.Arias & U.Guzmán sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_doc_range: Selenicereus validus is endemic to Mexico, where it is known from the states of Jalisco and Michoacán. It grows at altitudes around 600 m asl (Guzmán et al. 2003, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in subtropical dry forest. It is rupiculous and grows on top of rocks, in rock crevices and in rocky areas.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Selenicereus validus is listed as Least Concern because it is abundant and has a relatively large range. However, more research is needed on the species' life history, ecology and population trends.
