Stenocactus crispatus (DC.) A.Berger sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_locations: Tlaxcala; San Luis Potosí; Guanajuato; Puebla; México Distrito Federal; Veracruz; Tamaulipas; Oaxaca; México State; Querétaro; Hidalgo; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXC-TL; MXE-SL; MXE-GU; MXC-PU; MXC-DF; MXG-VC; MXE-TA; MXS-OA; MXC-ME; MXE-QU; MXE-HI; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Temperate
iucn_doc_range: Stenocereus crispatus is endemic to Mexico where it is widely distributed. The species is known from the states of Guanajuato, Hidalgo, México State, Oaxaca, Puebla, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas, Tlaxcala, Veracruz and Federal District (Hernández et al. 2004; Chávez et al. 2006). It grows at elevations ranging from 2,000-2,600 m (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_habitats: It inhabits in xerophyllous scrub, crassulacean scrub, rosetophyllous scrub, oak shrub, grasslands, and disturbed grasslands (Arias et al. 1997; Lemus et al. 2006; González-Costilla et al. 2007; López-Flores et al. 2007).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is locally threatened by disturbance from goat farming.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because it is difficult to distinguish from other species in the genus. The range and population cannot be defined because of this taxonomic confusion.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus alamosensis (J.M.Coult.) A.C.Gibson & K.E.Horak sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry farming; Small-holder farming; Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, and is distributed in the states of Sinaloa and Sonora (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations from close to sea level up to 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is common in coastal thornscrub, but also occurs in foothill thornscrub, low dry forest and desert scrub (Paredes et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_threats: Most of the species' range has been affected by land conversion for agriculture and cattle ranching. However, land is not being converted any more.
iucn_doc_rationale: Close to one third of the species' range has disappeared because of land use change, there is an inferred population decline of more than one third and causes of reduction have ceased. Hence it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus beneckei (Ehrenb.) A.Berger & Buxb. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_locations: Puebla; Guerrero; México State; Morelos; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Guerrero, Mexico, Morelos and Puebla (Fernández-Nava et al. 1998, Areola and Terrazas 2003, Jiménez-Ramírez et al. 2003, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical deciduous forest (Fernández-Nava et al. 1998, Jiménez-Ramírez et al. 2003, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by land use change for farming, especially for goat ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a small population size between 2,500 and 10,000 mature individuals, there are less than 50 individuals in each subpopulation, but there is no continuing decline in mature individuals. It has an extent of occurrence close to 10,000 km², there is continuing decline in the quality of the habitat, however it is known from more than ten locations and is not severely fragmented. Hence listed as Near Threatened because it almost qualifies for a Vulnerable listing under both criteria C2a(i) and B1ab(iii).

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus chacalapensis (Bravo & T.MacDoug.) Buxb. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_doc_range: Stenocereus chacalapensis is endemic to the Mexican state of Oaxaca, in which it is distributed in the coastal areas (Acosta-Catellanos 2002; Guzmán et al. 2003; Salas-Morales et al. 2007). Hernández and Godínez (1994) consider this species to have an extremely restricted distribution. It is found at elevations of 180-600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in low deciduous forest (Acosta-Castellanos 2002, Salas-Morales 2007, Arreola 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by change in land use, especially forest clearance to create pastures for cattle grazing.
iucn_doc_rationale: Stenocereus chacalapensis is known only from a very small population of less than 50 mature individuals, there are only three subpopulations known each of which is very small, and there is continuing decline because only old mature plants are known and there are no signs of regeneration. Hence is listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus chrysocarpus Sánchez-Mej. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Food - human; Food - animal
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Mexico, distributed in the catchment of the Balsas River in the states of Guerrero and Michoacán, between 500 and 700 m (Arreola-Nava and Terrazas 2003, Guzmán et al. 2003). This species is often confused with S. montanus (Britton & Rose) Buxb. and S. queretaroensis (F.A.C.Weber ex Mathes.) Buxb. and its distribution has as a result been over-estimated in the past.
iucn_doc_habitats: A succulent tree which grows in tropical deciduous forest (Arreola-Nava and Terrazas 2003).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is naturally scarce and patchily distributed, thus it is very vulnerable to habitat conversion. Loss of habitat may well have impacted the species in the past, but there is no information to confirm this. Although the species is not currently in decline it should be monitored to ensure remaining subpopulations do not disappear, and there is a need to study the use and trade of the fruits. The collection of fruits appears to be having an impact on recruitment and it is inferred that there is likley to be a continuing decline in mature individuals if there is not recruitment.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered to be Endangered due to a restricted range (extent of occurrence is <5,000 km²), only three remaining subpopulations (= three locations) and a demographic shift driven by use and trade of the fruits which has resulted in very low recruitment and thus subpopulations are skewed in age-structure towards older individuals. The inferred result of this is that in the future this species will have a continuing decline in mature individuals. It is recommended that research be undertaken to understand the impacts of use and trade on the demography and that efforts are made to protect the remaining subpopulations.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus eruca (K.Brandegee) A.C.Gibson & K.E.Horak sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Baja California Sur, where it occurs at elevations from sea level to 20 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in the Magdalena plains, on sandy soils. The growth form of this species is unique in North America.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by land use change by agriculture for organic strawberries and tomatoes.
iucn_doc_rationale: There has been some decline in the populations of Stenocereus eruca due to agriculture, but the species grows in areas that are currently very unlikely to be transformed. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus fricii Sánchez-Mej. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_locations: Guerrero; Colima; Sinaloa; Michoacán; Jalisco; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXS-GR; MXS-CL; MXN-SI; MXS-MI; MXS-JA; MX
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Construction or structural materials; Food - human
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Colima, Guerrero, Jalisco, Michoacán and Sinaloa (Guzmán et al. 2003). It is found in the low catchment of Balsas River, close to El Infiernillo dam (Fernández-Nava et al. 1998). It grows at altitudes between 30 and 600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in low coastal dry forests. In the low catchment of the Balsas River, the species occurs in low deciduous forest (Fernández-Nava et al. 1998). S. fricii produces fruits during the wet season (García-Suárez et al. 2007).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is declining in some areas due to habitat loss and conversion to both cattle ranching and touristic activities (T. Terraza pers. comm 2009).
iucn_doc_rationale: Stenocereus fricii may be declining due to overcollecting of the fruits, but the declines are not yet occurring at a rate that would warrant listing in a threatened category. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern. Research is needed on the use and trade of the species, especially its harvest levels.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus griseus (Haw.) Buxb. sec. Britton & Rose 1920
iucn_locations: Saba; San Luis Potosí; Mexico; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Tamaulipas; Guerrero; Sint Eustatius; Oaxaca; Querétaro; Jalisco; Trinidad and Tobago; Guanajuato; Curaçao; Venezuelan Antilles; Venezuela (mainland); Puebla; Bonaire; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Saint Martin (French part); Yucatán; Veracruz; Nuevo León; Sint Maarten (Dutch part); Colombia
iucn_location_codes: NLA-SA; MXE-SL; MX; BQ; MXE-TA; MXS-GR; NLA-SE; MXS-OA; MXE-QU; MXS-JA; TT; MXE-GU; CW; VNA-OO; VEN-OO; MXC-PU; NLA-BO; VE; MF; MXT-YU; MXG-VC; MXE-NL; SX; CO
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range:
The native distribution of this species is in Colombia, Mexico, Netherland Antilles, Venezuela, and Trinidad and Tobago (Hunt et al. 2006).
The species occurs in the Mexican states of Guanajuato, Guerrero, Jalisco, Nuevo León, Oaxaca, Puebla, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas, Veracruz and Yucatán (Hernández et al. 2004). In Venezuela, the species is distributed along the coast and inland in western Venezuela, in the Falcon-Lara complex. Also, it is found in the intra-Andean arid patches of Táchira, and Mérida states. In Colombia, it is found in the coast of Santa Marta and the xeric patches of La Chicamocha and La Tatacoa.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in tropical deciduous forest and xerophyllous scrub with Prosopis.
iucn_doc_threats: No major threats exist for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Stenocereus griseus is widespread, abundant, and there are no major threats. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus gummosus (Engelm.) A.C.Gibson & K.E.Horak sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Sonora; Baja California Sur; Baja California; Mexico
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry farming; Commercial & industrial areas; Housing & urban areas; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Baja California, Baja California Sur, and Sonora (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at elevations from sea level to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: In Sonora S. gummosus occurs in desert scrub (Paredes et al. 2000), coastal scrub, and subtropical thorn scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: Land use change for tourist development, housing, agriculture and farming affects part of the species range.
iucn_doc_rationale: Stenocereus gummosus has a wide range, occurs in nearly every desert island of the Gulf of California, and is locally abundant. Land use change affects part of the species range but this is not a major threat. Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus kerberi (K.Schum.) A.C.Gibson & K.E.Horak sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Industrial aquaculture; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Colima, Jalisco, Nayarit and Sinaloa (Guzmán et al. 2003). It grows at elevations from sea level to 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: S. kerberi inhabits tropical deciduous forest (Padilla-Velarde et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat where this species occurs is being converted to cattle ranches, shrimp farms and urban areas.
iucn_doc_rationale: Stenocereus kerberi is listed as Least Concern although it is naturally rare and in decline due to habitat conversion for cattle ranching, as it is not currently declining at a rate sufficient to qualify for listing in a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus martinezii (J.G.Ortega) Buxb. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, and is reported only for the state of Sinaloa. Its distribution is very restricted and it is only known from two to five localities (Hernández and Godínez 1994), at elevations of 0-100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: S. martinezii inhabits tropical dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: This very restricted range species is experiencing a reduction in habitat and extent of occurrence due to the conversion of dry forests for agriculture (mangoes, etc.) and for cattle ranching activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered to be Endangered due to a very restricted range (extent of occurrence is less than 5,000 km<sup>2</sup>), it is known from only five locations, and there is an ongoing decline in habitat and remaining populations due to expanding cattle ranching and agricultural activities.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus montanus (Britton & Rose) Buxb. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Construction or structural materials; Food - human
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in the states of Colima, Chihuahua, Sinaloa and Sonora. At elevations from 300 to 1,075 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in mountainous vegetation and tropical deciduous forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by deforestation and burning practices for agriculture in part of its range.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide range, it is locally abundant, occurs within a protected area and there are no major threats. Hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus pruinosus (Otto ex Pfeiff.) Buxb. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_synonyms: Stenocereus laevigatus (Salm-Dyck) Buxb.; Echinocactus pruinosus Otto ex Pfeiff.
iucn_habitat_names: Artificial/Terrestrial - Subtropical/Tropical Heavily Degraded Former Forest; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Construction or structural materials; Food - human; Establishing ex-situ production *; Fuels; Food - animal
iucn_doc_range: This species is widely distributed in Mexico in the states of Oaxaca, Puebla and Veracruz. It is found in the low Balsas Basin, close to El Infiernillo dam (Fernández-Nava et al. 1998). It grows at elevations of 800 to 1,900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: In the higher catchement of the Tehuantepec river S. pruinosos is found in tropical deciduous forest and in vegetation associations of Bursera morelensis-Escontria chiotilla (Fernández-Nava et al. 1998). It is also found in secondary vegetation and cultivated in orchards. S. pruinosus flowers between April and September and produces fruits between May and September (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Stenocereus pruinosus is widespread, very abundant, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus queretaroensis (F.A.C.Weber) Buxb. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_locations: Michoacán; Querétaro; Nayarit; Guanajuato; Jalisco; Zacatecas; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXS-MI; MXE-QU; MXS-NA; MXE-GU; MXS-JA; MXE-ZA; MX
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Food - human; Handicrafts, jewellery, etc.
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to western Mexico, where it is widespread in the states of Guanajuato, Jalisco, Michoacán, Nayarit, Querétaro, and Zacatecas (Ibarra-Cerdeña et al. 2005, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is distributed in tropical deciduous forests, and it is pollinated by bats (Cabrera-Luna and Gómez-Sánchez 2005, Ibarra-Cerdeña et al. 2005).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to the species as it is common and widespread. It is often cultivated for export of its fruits. Some subpopulations are threatened by land use change, tropical deciduous forests are one of the fastest disappearing habitats in Mexico.
iucn_doc_rationale: Stenocereus queretaroensis is listed as Least Concern in light of its broad distribution and because it is relatively common.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus standleyi (J.G.Ortega) Buxb. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_locations: Jalisco; Sinaloa; Oaxaca; Nayarit; Michoacán; Guerrero; Colima; Mexico
iucn_location_codes: MXS-JA; MXN-SI; MXS-OA; MXS-NA; MXS-MI; MXS-GR; MXS-CL; MX
iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Coastal Sand Dunes; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the coastal areas of the Pacific in the states of Colima, Guerrero, Jalisco, Michoacán and Oaxaca (Guzmán et al. 2003, Hunt et al. 2006). It also occurs on the Islas Marías (Nayarit state; CONANP 2007).
iucn_doc_habitats: S. standleyi inhabits subtropical deciduous woodland, where it is a subcanopy species, and tends to favor flat sites (Cody 1986). In Chamela, Jalisco, S.standleyi is particularly common on rocky shores (Martínez and Atkinson 1986) and rocky cliffs in "La Cocina Island" (Ramírez-Delgadillo 2006). It is a predominant species in the subtropical scrub (Crassulacean scrub with low thorn forest), and it also occurs in tropical deciduous forest in Islas Marías, Mexico (CONANP 2007). In the Parque Nacioanl de Huatulco the species is reported for low deciduous dune forest (CONANP 2003).
iucn_doc_threats:
The main threat is land use change - clearance of habitat for cattle grazing and expansion of tourist developments, especially in Jalisco and Michoacán.
Cactopinus setosus Wood, 1983, known only from Chamela area, attacks wounds on living cacti, frequently at the base of spines. The area mined by the insects seems to be relatively limited, usually blackened and dry in appearance. It is unclear whether the insect invades tissues killed by other agents or is capable of invading healthy tissue. It has not been found in dry stems. Three cacti are utilized as hosts: Acanthocereus occidentalis (Britt. & Rose); Stenocereus standleyi (Gonz. Ort.) Buxbaum; S. fricii (Sanchez- Mejorada) (Martínez and Atkinson 1986).
In most areas the populations of this species are healthy, however, in some areas the presence of these insects has been noted but they are not considered to be major pests.
iucn_doc_rationale: Stenocereus standleyi is a widespread and abundant species and although there are some threats these are not major. Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus stellatus (Pfeiff.) Riccob. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - animal; Pets/display animals, horticulture; Construction or structural materials; Food - human; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Morelos, Oaxaca and Puebla (Arias et al. 1997). Its distribution is clearly restricted to hot and dry regions of the Tehuacán Valley and the portion of the Balsas catchment that includes the northeast of Oaxaca and southeast of Puebla (Casas-Fernández and Caballero-Nieto 1997). It grows at elevations of 500 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits tropical deciduous forest and xerophyllous scrub. Flowers are produced between June and September, and fruits in October (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats, in addition this species is tolerant to some degree of habitat change.
iucn_doc_rationale: Stenocereus stellatus is listed as Least Concern due to a relatively wide range, abundant populations, and its occurrence in protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus thurberi (Engelm.) Buxb. sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_locations: United States; Chihuahua; Arizona; Sinaloa; Mexico; Baja California Sur; Baja California
iucn_location_codes: US; MXE-CU; ARI-OO; MXN-SI; MX; MXN-BS; MXN-BC
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry

iucn_use_trade_names: Medicine - human & veterinary; Food - human; Construction or structural materials
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in the north of Sinaloa, west Chihuahua, southern Baja California and Baja California Sur (Mexico), and in southern Arizona, United States (Paredes et al. 2000). It grows at elevations between sea level and 1,470 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is widespread in Sonoran desert scrub, coastal and foothills thornscrub, and tropical deciduous forest (Paredes et al. 2000). It shows latitudinal variation in growth form, from a multi-stemmed shrub branching from the base in dry northern habitats to a ten metre columnar arborescent cactus in tropical deciduous forest.
iucn_doc_threats: Part of the species range is affected by land-use change for agriculture and shrimp farms, and clearing and planting of buffel grass (Pennisetum ciliare) for cattle forage and subsequent fires in central Sonora.
iucn_doc_rationale: Stenocereus thurberi has a wide range and is abundant. Some of the densest populations have been lost, but most of the range in not under threat. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Food - animal; Handicrafts, jewellery, etc.
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Chaco region. It occurs in Argentina in Catamarca, Córdoba, Chaco, Formosa, Jujuy, La Rioja, Salta, Santiago del Estero, and Santa Fe, in Bolivia in Santa Cruz and Tarija, and in Paraguay (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows between 200 and 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in low mountains and plains, often at the edges of salt flats (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Stetsonia coryne is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, is locally abundant, and there are no major threats affecting it. It occurs in many protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Strombocactus disciformis (DC.) Britton & Rose sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2013
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Mexico in the states of Guanajuato, Hidalgo and Querétaro (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is reported in matorral on steep, calcareous, mudrock (lutita) alluvial cliffs along stream beds or canyon walls (Lüthy 2001).
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection for the ornamental plant trade is an increasing threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Strombocactus disciformis is considered to be Vulnerable because of its limited range (extent of occurrence approximately 4,500 km2), it occurs in less than 10 locations, and is experiencing a decline in mature individuals due to illegal overcollection.

checklist_taxon: Strophocactus brasiliensis (Britton & Rose) S.Arias & N.Korotkova sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
Strophocactus sicariguensis (Croizat & Tamayo) S.Arias & N.Korotkova sec. Korotkova 2021

iucn_locations: Venezuela (mainland); Colombia (mainland); Colombia; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Amazonas; Peru; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: VEN-OO; CLM-OO; CO; VE; BZN-AZ; PE; BR
iucn_doc_range: This species is reported from Brazil in Amazonas, in Colombia in Guainía and Vaupés, in Peru in Loreto and in Venezuela in Amazonas at 100 - 250 m (Hunt et al. 2006).  In Venezuela, it is marginally distributed in the southern portion of Amazonas state in a small patch near the border with Colombia (Ponce 1989).
iucn_doc_habitats: S. wittii is an epiphytic cactus which clings by aerial roots from the midrib. Its principle habitat is seasonally flooded forests (igapós) of Amazonian blackwater rivers. The seeds are adapted for water dispersal and can float (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species. The major potential threat is climate change.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide range, occurs in protected areas and in areas where no threats are recorded. Hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Tacinga funalis Britton & Rose sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Motivation Unknown/Unrecorded; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Pernambuco, Piauí and Bahia at elevations of 380–950 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in caatinga forest growing on granite/gneiss inselbergs.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats is deforestation for agriculture including crops and cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is widespread and fairly common.  At present it is categorized as Least Concern, but habitat destruction is happening throughout its range and can become a threat in the future.

checklist_taxon: Tacinga inamoena (K.Schum.) N.P.Taylor & Stuppy sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Maranhão; Tocantins; Bahia; Minas Gerais; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Savanna - Dry
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This Brazilian endemic is widespread throughout eastern Brazil: it occurs from the middle drainage of the Rio Jequitinhonha (Minas Gerais) northwards to northernmost Maranhão and Tocantins, and westwards on sandstone outcrops in the cerrado of western Bahia. It is one of the most common cacti in the region. It grows at elevations between 0 and 1550 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Tacinga inamoena is usually found on rocks (including inselbergs) or very stony ground in open caatinga and campo rupestre.
iucn_doc_threats: Mining and deforestation of the caatinga for agriculture, including crops and livestock holding, are minor threats for Tacinga inamoena. This plant tolerates some disturbance.
iucn_doc_rationale: Tacinga inamoena is widespread and common. Its population is currently stable despite the existence of local threats. Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_locations: Pernambuco; Bahia; Sergipe; Paraíba; Ceará; Alagoas; Rio Grande do Norte; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZE-PE; BZE-BA; BZE-SE; BZE-PB; BZE-CE; BZE-AL; BZE-RN; BR
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to north-eastern Brazil, it occurs in Rio Grande do Norte, Paraíba, Pernambuco, Alagoas, Sergipe, Ceara and Bahia at 200-1,020 m asl(Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: Tacinga palmadora is an eastern caatinga element, it grows in caatinga, agreste and carrasco, it is frequent on deep sandy substrates but not restricted to these.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats for this cactus are deforestation for agricultural purposes including crops and cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is widespread and frequent, it is found in protected areas throughout its range and although there are existing threats they are unlikely to cause the populations to severely decline in the near future.Hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Tacinga saxatilis (F.Ritter) N.P.Taylor & Stuppy sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Minas Gerais; Bahia; Tocantins; Goiás; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Temperate; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: Tacinga saxatilis has been recorded from southern Rio São Francisco, western and southern Bahia (east and west of the Rio São Francisco) to northwestern, northern and central Minas Gerais. Also recorded from, eastern Goias and southeastern Tocantins. It occurs at elevations of 450-700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Tacinga saxatilis is a caatinga element, where it grows on forest-covered limestone (Bambuí) outcrops surrounded by caatinga (mata seca semidecídua and cerradão).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is found only on limestone and therefore is potentially threatened by quarrying activities in the long term at some of its sites (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern at present because it has a relatively wide range, is locally abundant and occurs in protected areas. However, it is found only on limestone and is therefore potentially threatened by quarrying activities in the long term at some of its sites, hence it requires monitoring.

checklist_taxon: Tacinga werneri (Eggli) N.P.Taylor & Stuppy sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This Brazilian endemic is found from northern Bahia south to Minas Gerais but only east of the river Sao Francisco.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in eastern caatinga element: in the south it is mainly found on margins of gneiss/granite outcrops, on inselbergs and in caatinga-agreste. It has also been recorded on calcareous soils (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is habitat loss due to deforestation for crops and small holder grazing. The species can become rare as the caatinga disappears. Its habitat at Rui Barbosa, Bahia is been destroyed by mining activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern because it is relatively widespread, common and occurs in protected areas. There are some threats but they are localised and are unlikely to cause severe declines.

iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: There are two disjunct subpopulations of Tephrocactus nigrispinus. Both seem to be stable and not exposed to any major threat. The species is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Turbinicarpus alonsoi Glass & S.Arias sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2019
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Guanajuato, Mexico. It grows in a single location of less than 10 km² near Xichú, at about 1,900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in semi-desert scrub on steep rocky calcareous slopes.
iucn_doc_threats: These plants are threatened by illegal collecting. The habitat has become widely known to collectors. Since its discovery, the population has been reduced by more than 50%.
iucn_doc_rationale: Turbinicarpus alonsoi is considered to be Critically Endangered due to its occurrence in a single location with an area of less than 10 km², and because it is continuing to rapidly decline as a result of illegal collecting.

iucn_synonyms: Gymnocatus gielsdorfianus (Werderm.) Backeb.; Pediocactus gielsdorfianus (Werderm.) Halda; Echinocactus gielsdorfianus Werderm.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of San Luis Potosí. It grows on a slope, in an area of about 10 km², at about 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in xerophytic shrubland on rocky calcareous slope. It is strongly dependent on edaphic conditions. The habitat is locally classified as matorral rosetófilo (Rzedowski 1965).
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is threatened by illegal collecting in a portion of its range. The only known locality has become widely known to collectors. Habitat loss due to overgrazing by goats is also affecting this cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Critically Endangered under Criterion B, because it has an extent of occurrence and area of occupancy of approximately 10 km2, it is known from one location and is in continuing decline due to illegal collection and habitat loss.When it was first assessed, the population of Turbinicarpus gielsdorfianus was thought to be very small (only about 250 plants), surveys after the assessments showed that there were 4,000 mature individuals, and an estimated 80% reduction was calculated, however, it is necessary to estimate more accurately the population size and the rate of decline in order to use other criteria to assess the status of the species.

Turbinicarpus hoferi Lüthy & A.B.Lau sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2019

iucn_doc_habitats: These plants grow on a gypsum cliff in xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collecting is a major threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Turbinicarpus hoferi has a very limited range of less than 1 km² and a population of less than 1,000 individuals. There is continuing decline in the number of individuals due to illegal collecting. Large numbers of plants have been removed by collectors at the only known site. checklist_taxon: Turbinicarpus jauernigii Gerhart Frank sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2019
Turbinicarpus laui Glass & R.A.Foster sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2019
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Roads & railroads; Housing & urban areas; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in the Cerritos and Villa Juárez Municipalities, San Luis Potosí, Mexico at about 1,000 m asl. Six disjunct subpopulations are known in a restricted area of about 250 km² and an area of occupancy of less than 5 km². There are about 6 known localities, which comprise 5 locations.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in semi-desert (Chihuahuan Desert, although not typical) among bare areas with Helieta parvifolia and Ptelea trifoliata as neighboring vegetation. It occurs on gypsum-calcareous or gypsum soils (a stenoendemic). One of the localities is characterized by massive, dark-colored limestone rocks (Anderson et al. 1994). The habitat is locally classified as matorral submontano (Rzedowski 1965).
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collecting and fires are the major threats. The type locality has been quarried for limestone in the past, but it seems unlikely that this would be reactivated. There is intense road construction in the area. One of the areas where there is a hybrid between this species and T. pseudopectinatus was destroyed by urban expansion (housing).
iucn_doc_rationale: There are six known disjunct subpopulations of Turbinicarpus laui, which are distributed over an area of 250 km² (extent of occurrence) and add up to an area of occupancy of less than 5 km². The population of about 4,000 mature individuals is severely fragmented and is suffering from a continuing decline due to illegal collecting and fires. Its distribution is highly dependent on edaphic conditions. The type locality appears to have been quarried for limestone at some point in the past, but it seems unlikely that quarrying would be reactivated. Hence, the species is listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(iii,v).

checklist_taxon: Turbinicarpus lophophoroides (Werderm.) Buxb. & Backeb. sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2019

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of San Luis Potosí. It can be found in the Río Verde, Villa Juárez, Ciudad del Maíz and Ciudad Fernández Municipalities. There are no less than 15 disjunct subpopulations and at least four distinct geographic locations.
iucn_doc_habitats:
This cactus grows in semi-desert or short grassland vegetation with Prosopis juliflora. It occurs in agricultural and livestock areas and grows on calcareous-gypsum and gypsum soils. The habitat is locally classified as zacatal (Grassland) and matorral desértico micrófilo with Larrea tridentata (Rzedowski 1978).
This species always grows in association with Coryphantha maiz-tablasensis, but there are some areas with C. maiz-tablasensis and without T. lophophoroides.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are illegal collecting, agricultural activities, mining and road construction.  Insect parasitism is also a problem in some places. This is currently a major problem and the plants in one of the subpopulations have declined by more than 30%.
iucn_doc_rationale: The population size, extent of occurrence and area of occupancy of Turbinicarpus lophophoroides are declining due to human developmental activities, insect parasitism, and minor illegal collecting. The species is strictly dependent on edaphic conditions. There are no less than 15 disjunct subpopulations in at least four geographically distinct locations containing about 20,000 mature individuals. The species would qualify as Endangered under criterion B1ab(iii,v)+2ab(iii,v) based on its limited extent of occurrence (about 1,700 km2) and area of occupancy (less than 5 km2), as well as the continuing decline. However, the number of locations is greater than ten and the species is very abundant. Hence, it is listed as Near Threatened.

checklist_taxon: Turbinicarpus nieblae García-Mor., Mart.-Aval. & Bergm.Beck. sec. Aquino 2021
Turbinicarpus pseudopectinatus (Backeb.) Glass & R.A.Foster sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2019
iucn_locations: Nuevo León; Coahuila; San Luis Potosí; Tamaulipas; Mexico
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: The range of this cactus is about 30,000 km², extending over the states of Coahuila, Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, and Tamaulipas, Mexico. There are five subpopulations in San Luis Potosí.
iucn_doc_habitats: Most of the known sites are at a higher altitude than true Chihuahuan Desert. Some of the sites occur in pinyon pine and juniper woodland or even grassland with virtually no woody vegetation (Anderson et al. 1994). All the sites have gravelly calcareous soils. It also occurs in montane matorral.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collecting is the main threat. Anderson et al. (1994) reported that alarming numbers were being exported, but collecting has reduced considerably since then (M. Sotomayor pers. comm 2009). Agricultural development does not appear to be a problem as the habitats are not suitable; although trampling by livestock and increased erosion as a result of over-grazing may be a problem in some places, this is not a threat at present. There is no known local use of the plant.
iucn_doc_rationale: Turbinicarpus pseudopectinatus has a wide range (its extent of occurrence is 30,000 km²), the population size is less than 10,000 individuals, and even though there is illegal collecting this is not a major threat. This species occurs at about ten locations and is not severely fragmented. The species is listed here as Least Concern, however, continued monitoring is required.

checklist_taxon: Turbinicarpus rioverdensis Gerhart Frank sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2019
Turbinicarpus saueri (Boed.) John & Říha sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2019
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is found in the states of San Luis Potosí and Tamaulipas (Hunt et al. 2006). There is one subpopulation of the nominate subspecies, one of nelissae, and it is uncertain how many of knuthianus.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on limestone (Hunt et al. 2006). The habitat is very variable - dense to sparser montane scrub (matorral xerófilo) on limestone hills. It grows in association with Boteloua curtipendula, Setaria macrostachya and Tridens muticus. Soils are deep calcareous and loamy.
iucn_doc_threats: There is a lot of illegal collection of the form formerly known as ysabelae, but not of the nominate subspecies. In general, the individuals are healthy in all localities (apart from ysabelae), with no signs of disease or of parasites. Some localities show signs of overgrazing but that does not appear to affect the taxon as a whole. A road built in the 1990s northwest of Cerritos affected dozens of hectares of habitat and the species was impacted. There are no signs of recent fires, but all areas where this species has been found are at risk from fire due to the dense nature of the habitat where knuthianus grows.
iucn_doc_rationale:
Turbinicarpus saueri is listed as Vulnerable under criterion B1ab(v) as it has a relatively restricted range (extent of occurrence just larger than 5,000 km²), it is known from fewer than ten locations, is considered to be severely fragmented, and there is continuing decline in the number of mature individuals at least in the subpopulation of the form formerly known as T. ysabelae near Tula.
The individual subspecies would probably have very different assessments - T. saueri Critically Endangered, T. knuthianus Near Threatened, and T. nelissae Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Turbinicarpus schmiedickeanus (Boed.) Buxb. & Backeb. sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2019
iucn_locations: Nuevo León; San Luis Potosí; Tamaulipas; Mexico
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is found over a wide area in the States of Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí and western Tamaulipas, Mexico. It is known from many localities.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in xerophytic shrubland (Chihuahuan Desert) among calcareous rocks, often in rock cracks or in the shade of Hechtia spp.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collecting is the major threat, and overgrazing and subsequent erosion poses a lesser threat.  Other agricultural activities and building of infrastructure seem to have minimal impact because of the habitats where it grows.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although all the subspecies, including the typical one, are severely threatened, Turbinicarpus schmiedickeanus as a whole is not. As a whole the species has a wide distribution, and it has more than ten subpopulations and consists of more than 10,000 individuals. As most subpopulations are declining and some of the subspecies are on the brink of extinction, a Near Threatened listing is warranted. The species nearly meets the requirements for listing in a threatened category under criterion B.

checklist_taxon: Turbinicarpus swobodae Diers sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2019
iucn_habitat_names: Desert - Hot; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: It occurs near Rayones, Nuevo León, Mexico, over a range of 1 km². It grows at an altitude of about 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in semi-desert among steep calcareous rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collecting is the major threat for this species, although access is difficult (up a steep mountain).
iucn_doc_rationale: Turbinicarpus swobodae is listed as Critically Endangered because it has an extremely narrow range of 1 km², it is known from a single location, the population size is of less than 2,000 individuals, and there is a contining decline due to the impacts of illegal collecting.

checklist_taxon: Turbinicarpus valdezianus (H.Moeller) Glass & R.A.Foster sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2019
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Nuevo León; Coahuila; Mexico
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Commercial & industrial areas; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This cactus occurs in Mexico from Saltillo, in Coahuila state southwards nearly to Matehuala in San Luis Potosí state, in an area of 40,000 km².  It grows at altitudes ranging from 1,400 to 1,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in typical Chihuahuan Desert vegetation among calcareous rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are illegal collecting and human activities.  The northernmost subpopulations near Saltillo are affected by the construction of an airport and the expanding chicken farms.
iucn_doc_rationale: Turbinicarpus valdezianus qualifies as Vulnerable due to a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals. A population decline of at least 30% in the last 12 years (generation time is estimated to be four years) has taken place. Large numbers of plants were destroyed by the construction of the Saltillo airport and the creation of large poultry farms south of Saltillo. Large numbers of plants have been and continue to be removed by collectors at known sites.

checklist_taxon: Turbinicarpus viereckii (Werderm.) John & Říha sec. Vázquez-Sánchez & al. 2019
iucn_locations: Tamaulipas; Nuevo León; San Luis Potosí; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: This cactus occurs in Mexico in the states of Tamaulipas, Nuevo León and San Luis Potosí, over a range of 2,000 km².
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in semi-desert (Tamaulipan Shrubland) among calcareous rocks.
iucn_doc_rationale: Turbinicarpus viereckii has a limited range (extent of occurrence about 2,000 km²) and the population consists of less than 10,000 individuals. It is, however, not declining rapidly enough to qualify for listing at this time. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Uebelmannia pectinifera Buining sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_synonyms: Uebelmannia flavispina Buining & Brederoo; Uebelmannia pectinifera (Buining & Brederoo) P.J.Braun & Esteves subsp. flavispina Uebelmannia; Uebelmannia pectinifera (P.J.Braun) P.J.Braun & Esteves subsp. horrida Uebelmannia; Uebelmannia pectinifera Buining & Brederoo var. multicostata Uebelmannia; Uebelmannia pectinifera Buining subsp. pectinifera Uebelmannia; Uebelmannia pectinifera Buining var. pseudopectinifera Uebelmannia
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Dry
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Small-holder farming; Other ecosystem modifications
iucn_doc_range: Uebelmannia pectinifera is found at ca 650 to 1,350 m asl on the Serra do Espinhaço in Minas Gerais, Brazil.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in campo rupestre, on crystalline rocks (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are illegal plant collection, cattle ranching, agriculture and fires. Also, the plants are now eaten by Moco (Dasyprocta sp.), a species that has increased in numbers as a consequence of the predation of its predator, which is not tolerated by farmers.
iucn_doc_rationale: Uebelmannia pectinifera has small range (extent of occurrence 3,000 km2) and the population is at risk due to several threats, the main one being overcollection, which cause a continuing decline of individuals. The population is severely fragmented. Hence, it is listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(iii,v).

checklist_taxon: Weberbauerocereus cephalomacrostibas (Werderm. & Backeb.) F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Arequipa department of Peru (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows from 200 to 600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in sandy soils of lomas costeras.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is indirectly threatened by cattle grazing and urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a small range (extent of occurrence 200 km2). The two known subpopulations are not abundant and are considered two locations; one has been practically extirpated, livestock and urbanization are the major threats. It is estimated that at least 50% of the population has been lost in the last 60 years (probably less; generation length 20 years). Hence, Weberbauerocereus cephalomacrostibas is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Weberbauerocereus cuzcoensis Kníže sec. Arakaki 2003
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Peru, where it is found widely in the Apurímac and Ayacucho regions, but also present in the Cusco and Huancavelica regions. W. cuzcoensis grows at an altitude of 500 to 2,000 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in shrublands, on rocky and sandy soils.
iucn_doc_rationale: Weberbauerocereus cuzcoensis is listed as Least Concern because it is locally common and there are no major threats affecting it. In addition, the area of occurrence is difficult to access.

checklist_taxon: Weberbauerocereus madidiensis Quispe & A.Fuentes sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Habitat shifting & alteration; Shifting agriculture; Small-holder farming; Housing & urban areas; Roads & railroads; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, found in dry montane forests (“Yungas”) of La Paz Department, Bolivia (Kiesling et al. 2014).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is a succulent tree 5–8 m tall, 33 cm diameter (tropicos.org 2023), which inhabits xeric-seasonal lower sub-andean forest 'Yungas'.
iucn_doc_threats: In general, it is assumed that the threats are road construction, agricultural expansion, fire, climate change and destruction of natural areas for unplanned human settlements.
iucn_doc_rationale: Weberbauerocereus madidiensis is a columnar cactus endemic to Bolivia. It has an extent of occurrence of 71.61 km2 and an area of occupancy of 20 km2. This species was recorded from five locations and is threatened by urbanisation, agricultural expansion, climate change, road construction and fires. It is assessed as Endangered (EN).

checklist_taxon: Weberbauerocereus rauhii Backeb. sec. Arakaki 2003
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Peru. It can be found in the Arequipa, Ayacucho, Huancavelica and Ica regions, at an altitude of 900 to 2,500 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in shrublands on rocky soils.
iucn_doc_threats: Subpopulations of W. rauhii near Galeras Pampas show signs of reduction in their subpopulation and little presence of young individuals, perhaps due to the extreme and increasing dryness of the zone (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_rationale: Weberbauerocereus rauhii is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, is locally common, and there are no major threats affecting it. It occurs in a protected area that preserves 20% of the population.

checklist_taxon: Weberbauerocereus weberbaueri (K.Schum. ex Vaupel) Backeb. sec. Arakaki 2003
iucn_doc_range: Weberbauerocereus weberbaueri is endemic to Peru's Arequipa region, where it is the most abundant columnar cactus. It grows at an altitude of 2,000 to 3,000 m asl (Sahley 1996, Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats:
The species occurs in desert thorn scrub.
W. weberbaueri is a columnar cactus that flowers overnight between 15:00 and 11:00 h. It is pollinated by the very rare and endemic Long-nosed bat (Platalina genovensium), as well as by the Giant hummingbird (Patagona gigas), the Oasis hummingbird (Rhodopis vesper), nocturnal moths, diurnal flies and bees (Sahley 1996). It is also self-compatible.
iucn_doc_threats: A population decline close to the city of Arequipa has been observed in recent years in response to urban expansion and mining operations (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_rationale: Weberbauerocereus weberbaueri has a relatively wide range (its extent of occurrence is approximately 35,000 km2), it is abundant and the threats are only localized. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Weberbauerocereus winterianus F.Ritter sec. Arakaki 2003
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru, where it can be found in La Libertad and Cajamarca regions, where it grows at an altitude of 1,000 to 2,000 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006, Hunt 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: W. winterianus is pollinated by bats (Arakaki et al. 2006). It occurs on rocky soils in thorny dry matorral.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is not exposed to any imminent threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Weberbauerocereus winterianus has a restricted range with an extent of occurrence of approximately 1,000 km2 and it is known from two subpopulations.  The species is listed as Least Concern because it is currently not exposed to any threat, however monitoring is recommended as any negative impact on the species would severely jeopardize its.

checklist_taxon: Weberocereus bradei (Britton & Rose) G.D.Rowley sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Costa Rica, where it grows in the Pacific lowlands of the Puntarenas and San José provinces at elevations of 20 to 850 m asl (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits dense, seasonally wet forests at low altitudes (Britton and Rose 1919). Because of its large, thick, ovoid "pads" it looks like an epiphytic Opuntia species.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by urban expansion and by wood harvesting of the trees on which the cactus grows as an epiphyte.
iucn_doc_rationale: Weberocereus bradei has a restricted range (extent of occurrence is 6,481 km²), the population is severely fragmented, and there is continuing decline in the quality and extent of the habitat due to logging of the trees on which it grows as an epiphyte. Hence, this cactus qualifies for listing as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Weberocereus frohningiorum Ralf Bauer sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Costa Rica, distributed in the botanically rich Pacific coastal range just south of San José, along the border between San José and Puntarenas provinces. It grows at 1,300 m asl (Hammel 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on limestone rocks in wet montane forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is small-scale ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Weberocereus frohningiorum has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence 236 km2, area of occupancy 4 km2) and its population consists of less than 50 mature individuals in two locations. There is continuing decline in the quality of the habitat due to small-scale ranching.

checklist_taxon: Weberocereus imitans (Kimnach & Hutchison) Buxb. sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry farming; Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Costa Rica, and is distributed in Puntarenas province (Osa Peninsula and nearby) and the Valley (Valle de El General) subregion of the Northern hillsides region (Hunt et al. 2006, World Bank 2006). The supposed record from Honduras is likely to be a misidentification. It is found at elevations of 20 - 900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows in seasonally wet lowland forest.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by habitat destruction through the expansion of commercial agriculture especially for growing pineapples, and also clearance for urban and tourism developments.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a relatively small extent of occurrence of less than 5,000 km², it is known from about five locations and there is a continuing decline in the quality of its habitat due to human activities.

checklist_taxon: Weberocereus rosei (Kimnach) Buxb. sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Ecuador (Cañar province, Hunt et al. 2006), where grows at altitudes between 500 and 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This epiphyte grows on rocks in seasonally dry areas.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats for this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: Weberocereus rosei is classified as Data Deficient becasue there is no information on its population trends or threats where it occurs.  More research is needed for this species.

checklist_taxon: Weberocereus trichophorus H.Johnson & Kimnach sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Costa Rica, and is known only from the Atlantic coast (Caribbean) lowlands south of Cahuita, at elevations below 330 m asl. The type specimen is reported from Limón, (Hunt et al. 2006; Gargiullo et al. 2008).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is a high climber and pendant in trees (Gargiullo et al. 2008). It grows in wet lowland forest.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by the expansion of tourism developments in the coastal areas and also by expanding commercial agriculture especially bananas.
iucn_doc_rationale: It has a very small extent of occurrence (less than 1,000 km²), and is known from 3-4 locations, and the habitat is declining in quality and extent due to the expansion of tourism developments and agriculture. Hence it is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Weberocereus tunilla (F.A.C.Weber) Britton & Rose sec. Korotkova & al. 2017
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry farming; Housing & urban areas; Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species has a wide distribution, occurring from Honduras in the north southwards through Nicaragua, over much of Costa Rica and into Panama. It grows at elevations ranging from sea level to 2,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits lowland and montane wet forest (Tschapka 2004). It is pollinated by glossophagine bats (Tschapka et al. 1999).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats to this species are the expansion of tourist development in coastal areas and the expansion of agro-industry, especially pineapples and coffee, at higher elevations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Weberocereus tunilla is listed as Least Concern because it has a very wide distribution, is locally common, and existing threats are not causing significant declines.

checklist_taxon: Xiquexique frewenii (Zappi & N.P.Taylor) Lavor & Calvente sec. Lavor & al. 2020
Xiquexique gounellei (F.A.C.Weber) Lavor & Calvente sec. Lavor & al. 2020
Xiquexique tuberculatus (Werderm.) Lavor & Calvente sec. Lavor & al. 2020
Yavia cryptocarpa R.Kiesling & Piltz sec. ???
iucn_locations: Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Jujuy; Argentina
iucn_doc_range: The locality reported for this species is Quebrada de Toqueros, Jujuy province (Argentina), at 3,700 m asl (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009). It is known only from the type locality, although it possibly also occurs in Potosí, Bolivia.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in schistose rocks on slopes, and is exposed to the sun in rock crevices (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: Extraction by collectors is the major threat to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Yavia cryptocarpa has a small range (extent of occurrence approximately 200 km2), and its presence has only been confirmed in one location, although it remains to be verified whether it may also be present in a second location. It is not known to occur in any protected areas and extraction by collectors may pose a threat to its long-term survival. It is therefore listed as Endangered.

iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in the Mexican states of Tamaulipas and San Luis Potosí. Early observations indicated that this species occurred in few areas around the city of Tula, Mexico, and were being heavily impacted by illegal collecting and urban development (Anderson et al. 1994). However, relatively recent fieldwork has clearly shown the species to occur over 400 km² (Fitz Maurice 1999; Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in xerophile shrubland among outcrops of calcareous rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collecting is the main threat. It is not known if the local harvesting for the roots is a threat or not.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ariocarpus agavoides qualifies as Endangered because of its reduced extent of occurrence (EOO is 2,072 km²) and because the population is severely fragmented (although known from six locations). Even though the population is quite large and appears to be reproducing, there is a continuing decline in the overall population due to illegal collection.

checklist_taxon: Ariocarpus mcdowellii J.N.Haage & Schmidt sec. WFO 2019
Ariocarpus pulvilliger K.Schum. sec. WFO 2019

checklist_fullName: Armatocereus cartwrightianus (Britton & Rose) Backeb. ex A.W.Hill
checklist_taxon: Armatocereus cartwrightianus (Britton & Rose) Backeb. ex A.W.Hill sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Desert - Cold
iucn_doc_range: Armatocereus cartwrightianus is distributed across northern Peru and in Ecuador (Nano 2006). In Peru it is found in the regions of Tumbes, Lambayeque and Piura. In Ecuador it occurs in the provinces of El Oro, Loja, Guayas, Santa Elena and Manabí (Hunt et al. 2006). This species grows from 0 to 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Armatocereus cartwrightianus grows on slabs, rocky slopes and scrubland (Nano 2006). Is a characteristic plant of the dry coastal plains.
iucn_doc_threats: This species does not have any threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Armatocereus cartwrightianus is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, is locally abundant and there are no major threats affecting it. It occurs in many protected areas.

checklist_fullName: Armatocereus ghiesbreghtii (K.Schum.) F.Ritter ex M.H.J.van der Meer
checklist_taxon: Armatocereus ghiesbreghtii (K.Schum.) F.Ritter ex M.H.J.van der Meer sec. Brako & Zarucchi 1993 (sub Armatocereus ghiesbreghtii)

checklist_fullName: Armatocereus godingianus (Britton & Rose) Backeb. ex E.Salisb.
checklist_taxon: Armatocereus godingianus (Britton & Rose) Backeb. ex E.Salisb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: Armatocereus godingianus is endemic to Ecuador, where it is found in the Azuay and Chimborazo provinces at altitudes between 1,000 and 2,500 m asl (Madsen and Aguirre 2004, Hunt et al. 2006). It does not occur in Loja (C. Loaiza pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_habitats: Armatocereus godingianus grows in northern inter-Andean dry forests and low montane dry forests.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is very abundant and there is no intensive land use in the area of the main population. A large human settlement exists in Huigra but it does not affect A. godingianus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Armatocereus godingianus is categorized as Least Concern because it is abundant, common, and is not affected by any threats. However, it should be taken into account that it is endemic to Ecuador, has a small extent of occurrence (1051 km2), and does not occur in any protected area.

checklist_taxon: Armatocereus riomajensis Rauh & Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Desert - Cold
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru and distributed along the Majes river valley. In the Arequipa region, it can be found in Aplao (Castilla province), Chuquibamba and Pampa Acolca (Condesuyos province), Yura (Arequipa province), Atiquipa (Caravelí province), Cotahuasi (La Unión province), Cabanaconde (Caylloma province), Pologaya, Yura, Yarabamba (Arequipa province). It is also present in the Moquegua region, specifically in Omate, Puquina (Sanchez Cerro province), Torata and Saamegua (Mariscal Nieto province). In the Tacna region, it is present in Tarata and Ticaco (Cáceres 2003, 2006, 2008; Ostolaza Nano 2006). It can be found at altitudes from 900 to 3,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs on rocky slopes, "lomas costeras", and inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Armatocereus riomajensis is widespread, common, not subjected to any threats, and present in at least one protected area. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Arrojadoa bahiensis (P.J.Braun & Esteves) N.P.Taylor & Eggli sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
Arrojadoa dinae Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_synonyms: Arrojadoa dinae Buining & Brederoo ssp. dinae
iucn_threat_names: Unintentional effects: (large scale) [harvest]; Intentional use (species is the target); Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Agro-industry plantations
iucn_doc_range: Arrojadoa dinae is found in southern Bahia and northern Minas Gerais (Brazil), from north of Rio Pardo de Minas to south of Urandi in Bahia. It grows at altitudes between 800 and 1,400 m asl. The extent of occurrence is below 5,000 km2 and the area of occupancy is certainly smaller than 500 km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus is found in sandy patches among campo rupestre and cerrado vegetation.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats are deforestation for Eucalyptus plantations used for charcoal production. Collection of the plant and fires can impact on its reproductive capacity.
iucn_doc_rationale: Arrojadoa dinae is listed as Vulnerable because it has a small extent of occurrence (less than 5,000 km2) and a limited area of occupancy (less than 500 km2), it is found at less than 10 locations, there are less than 10,000 mature individuals in total, and it is estimated that over 10 percent of the population has disappeared over the course of three generations. These declines are assumed to be continuing, as deforestation for Eucalyptus plantations is still the major threat to the species. It is also affected by collection and fires.

checklist_taxon: Arrojadoa eriocaulis Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_synonyms: Arrojadoa dinae Buining & Brederoo ssp. eriocaulis (Buining & Brederoo) N.P.Taylor & Zappi
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Agro-industry plantations; Unintentional effects: (large scale) [harvest]; Mining & quarrying; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it is found in the eastern drainage of Serra do Espinhaço in northern Minas Gerais up to the border area of Bahia. It occurs in two disjunct areas, at elevations of 700 to 1,100 m asl: the northern subpopulation grows in the mountains east of the towns of Monte Azul and Mato Verde, while the southern subpopulation is found in Grao Mogol. The extent of occurrence is quite large but the area of occupancy is estimated to be below 500 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: A species of the campo rupestre element, it is found in sandy cerrado. The generation length is around 10 years.
iucn_doc_threats: Arrojadoa eriocaulis is threatened by deforestation for Eucalyptus plantations, quarrying of sands, fires, and illegal collection.
iucn_doc_rationale: Arrojadoa eriocaulis is listed as Endangered because it has a very fragmented distribution and its extent of occurrence is approximately 4,700 km2. This cactus is threatened by habitat destruction through deforestation for Eucalyptus plantations, quarrying of sand, fires, and collection. The causes of the population declines have not ceased and are not reversible.

checklist_taxon: Arrojadoa leucostele (Gürke) Anceschi & Magli sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
Arrojadoa luetzelburgii (Vaupel) N.P.Taylor sec. Romeiro-Brito & al. 2023
Arrojadoa luetzelburgioides N.P.Taylor & Zappi sec. ???
Arrojadoa marylaniae Soares Filho & M.Machado sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_synonyms: Arrojadoa marylanae Soares Filho & M.Machado [orth. error]
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This cactus occurs in a single location in the municipality of Tanhacu, in the southeastern part of Bahia (Brazil). The species has an extent of occurrence and area of occupancy of less than 10 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in caatinga, growing on one big rock outcrop composed of white quartz. Generation length is around 20 years. It grows as a tall unbranched column up to 4 m high.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats are fires, as the species is not able to survive them and has already lost 30 percent of its population due to this threat. The site has been prospected for quarrying of the quartz rock and if the permission is granted the species will disappear completely.
iucn_doc_rationale: The only site where Arrojadoa marylanae grows has an extent of occurrence and area of occupancy of less than 10 km2. This site counts as a single location, as quarrying of the quartz sand would affect the whole population and cause its disappearance. The only known population consists of several hundred, but less than 5,000 mature individuals. The population trend is decreasing, and one man-made fire destroyed about 30 percent of the population in 2008. The causes of the population declines are understood, but they have not ceased. The species is therefore listed as Critically Endangered under criterion B.

checklist_taxon: Arrojadoa multiflora F.Ritter ex N.P.Taylor sec. ???
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Dry
iucn_doc_range: This plant is found in southern Bahia (Brazil) from the south of Caetite to northwest of Riacho de Santana and east of Tanque Novo. It is only known from four fragmented sites, at elevations of 600 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus is found in campo rupestre and cerrado. It also grows among crystalline rocks and in sand. The generation length of this succulent shrub is ten years or less.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats are deforestation for agro-industry farming and urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: Arrojadoa multiflora has a small extent of occurrence (EOO <5,000 km2), its severely fragmented, has suffered from a large population reduction (30%) in the past, and the threats have not ceased. Habitat quality is continuously declining due to deforestation for agricultural purposes, mining, and urbanization. Furthermore, this cactus does not occur in any protected areas, is not common, and the subpopulations are small. Hence, the species is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Arrojadoa olsthoorniana Hofacker, M.Machado & R.Pontes sec. Taylor & al. 2022
Arrojadoa penicillata (Gürke) Britton & Rose sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Savanna - Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Housing & urban areas; Mining & quarrying
iucn_doc_range: Arrojadoa penicillata occurs in southwestern Pernambuco, northern and eastern Bahia, and in the valley of Rio Jequitinhonha, in the northeastern part of Minas Gerais (Brazil). It grows at altitudes from 200 to 850 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species can be found in granite/gneiss inselbergs or lajedos, sand dunes, and stony ground (rarely in limestone), where it grows under or through shrubs in the caatinga-agreste and caatinga-campo rupestre (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The habitat of this plant is under degradation due to deforestation for logging, agriculture, cattle ranching, urbanization, quarrying, and due to fires.
iucn_doc_rationale: Arrojadoa penicillata is declining due to several threats, but it is still widespread and common throughout its range. It is unlikely to qualify for a threatened category in the near future. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Arrojadoa pusilliflora (F.Ritter) N.P.Taylor sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
Arrojadoa violaciflora (Buining) N.P.Taylor sec. Romeiro-Brito & al. 2023
Arthrocereus glaziovii (K.Schum.) N.P.Taylor & Zappi sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+: 16 May 2022
iucn_doc_range: This species grows east and south of Belo Horizonte, central-southern Minas Gerais (Brazil), at elevations between 1,300 and 1,750 m asl.  The species has an approximate extent of occurrence of 1,000 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a south-eastern campo rupestre element that grows on iron-rich rock (canga).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat, which is historical, is mining for iron. Many habitats (Ouro Preto, Brumadhino) have been eliminated due to intensive mining.
iucn_doc_rationale: Arthrocereus glaziovii has a small range (extent of occurrence ca 1,000 km2), it grows in small subpopulations and only on canga, which is an iron rich rock historically mined in the area. The whole area where the species occurs is affected by mining. There is a continuing decline in the subpopulations unlikely to cease. Hence, it is listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(iii).

checklist_taxon: Arthrocereus melanurus (K.Schum.) Diers, P.J.Braun & Esteves sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Dry
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in central, southern and south-western Minas Gerais, at elevations of 700 to 1,700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is a south-eastern campo rupestre element. It grows in sandy, gravelly and rocky places in campo rupestre. The species also occurs in cerrado.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are mining for tin, urbanization and agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: Arthrocereus melanurus has a large extent of occurrence (EOO ca 75,000 km2). However, based on expert opinion the species area of occupancy is certainly less than 500 km2. The population has a continuing decline and is under threat by mining activities, urbanization and agriculture, severely fragmented, not abundant, and only one subpopulation is protected. Hence, the species is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Astrophytum caput-medusae D.R.Hunt sec. Vázquez-Lobo & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Nomadic grazing
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in the state of Nuevo León at elevations of 100 to 200 m asl (Hernández and Gómez-Hinostrosa 2011).
iucn_doc_threats: It occurs in a very small area which is frequently visited by collectors, and thus the species is in decline. In addition plants are trampled by grazing of livestock goats.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is considered Critically Endangered due to its very limited distribution (<100 km²), occurrence in a single location and rapid and ongoing decline due to illegal over collecting and trampling by livestock.
