Austrocactus philippii (Regel & Schmidt) Buxb. sec. Böhnert & al. 2025
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Recreational activities; Renewable energy
iucn_doc_range: The species is distributed in central southern Chile in Maule and O'Higgins regions and in the Argentine department of Mendoza, at elevations of 2,000 to 2,450 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats:
This species grows at high altitude close to the timber line. It seems to prefer flat, but rocky areas, exposed to sun. The soil where the roots are growing has a considerable organic matter concentration, despite the fact that the superficial soil around is rather barren. It forms a community with Mulinum spinosum, Ephedra chilensis and Berberis empetrifolia (www.chileflora.com).

The species is buried under snow for as long as six months during the winter.
iucn_doc_threats: In Chile, both of the known localities are threatened by the construction of hydro-electric stations, and road construction related to this. When this goes ahead the subpopulations are very likely to go extinct. In Argentina the main threats are localised and these correspond to tourism, parking lot construction for skying resorts, all-terrain motorcycles that damage and many times kill the plants by pulling them out of the soil with the wheels, and to a lesser extend snow skying.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species was first thought to have a very restricted range in  Argentina, however, it occurs in a relatively wide area which is in most  of its extension of no interest for development or agriculture. The species extent of occurrence is approximately 7,000 km<sup>2</sup> and there are localised threats in the Argentine part of its distribution. However, on the Chilean part of its range, both known subpopulations are threatened with extinction in the near future. The species is here assessed as Near Threatened because it is unknown what percentage of the population will be affected in Chile by the construction of  hydro-electric stations, but it is likely that the species can qualify as Vulnerable under criterion A3c or A4ac.

checklist_taxon: Austrocactus praecox E.Sarnes & N.Sarnes sec. Böhnert & al. 2025
Austrocephalocereus cactacearum Backeb. sec. TROPICOS 1986+
Austrocylindropuntia cactacearum Backeb. sec. TROPICOS 1986+
Austrocylindropuntia cylindrica (Lam.) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: Austrocylindropuntia cylindrica is present in Ecuador (provinces of Pichincha, Chimborazo, Cotopaxi, Tungurahua and Cañar; Hunt et al. 2006, Loaiza et al. 2009) and in northern Peru (Piura department; Anderson 2001). It occurs at altitudes from 1,500 to 3,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in high altitude shrublands. The seeds of A. cylindrica are sealed in a thick aril, and germination is probably poor under normal conditions.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats to this cactus are habitat loss and modification due to human sprawl and livestock farming.
iucn_doc_rationale: Austrocylindropuntia cylindrica has a restricted range and is being affected by cattle ranching and urban sprawl. It will probably qualify for classification in a Threat category under criterion B1ab(i,ii,iii) in the near future. However, at present the lack of precise field data impedes determination of the appropriate category. It is therefore listed as Near Threatened.

checklist_taxon: Austrocylindropuntia exaltata (A.Berger) Backeb. sec. Iliff 2002
Austrocylindropuntia floccosa (Salm-Dyck) F.Ritter sec. Ritz & al. 2012
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Peru (Cajamarca, La Libertad, Ancash, Lima, Junín, Huancavelica, Ayacucho, Cuzco, Arequipa, Puno, Moquehua) across the Andean uplands at high altitudes and in Bolivia in La Paz (Hunt et al. 2006). It can be found at altitudes between 3,500 and 4,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in the Puna.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Austrocylindropuntia floccosa is abundant and widespread. It occurs in at least one protected area, and is currently not exposed to any threat. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Austrocylindropuntia shaferi (Britton & Rose) Backeb. sec. Ritz & al. 2012
iucn_locations: Jujuy; Argentina; Bolivia, Plurinational States of
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude
iucn_doc_range: The species is distributed in Argentina in Jujuy, and in Bolivia in Chuquisaca, Oruro, Potosí and Tarija, at elevations of 2,800 to 3,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in very rocky soils with very little vegetation, in prepuna and puna. It grows on dry hills (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009). Santecchia and Rajal (2009) report that it frequently grows near river shores.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Austrocylindropuntia shaferi is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is abundant, occurs in protected areas, and there are no major threats.

Brachycereus nesioticus (K.Schum.) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_common_name: Cactus de Lava; Lave cactus; Lava Cactus
iucn_doc_range: Brachycereus nesioticus is endemic to the Galápagos Islands (Ecuador), where it is found on the islands of Bartolomé, Fernandina, Genovesa, Isabela, Pinta and San Salvador (Hunt et al. 2006). The area of occupancy is probably less than 100 km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: This succulent shrub forms dense clumps which grow on lava flows in the arid parts of the islands and is generally found amongst open vegetation. It is one of the first species to colonize fresh lava flows.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Brachycereus nesioticus is found on several islands of the Galápagos archipelago, it is known from at least 11 subpopulations and it is thought to be widespread on recent lava flows, but individuals are dispersed and the area of occupancy is small. There are no major threats to the species and it is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Brasilicactus catarinensis Doweld sec. WFO 2019
Brasiliopuntia schulzii (A.Cast. & H.V.Lelong) Backeb. sec. Pin 1998
Browningia altissima (F.Ritter) Buxb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Motivation Unknown/Unrecorded
iucn_doc_range: Browningia altissima is endemic to northern Peru, where it occurs in the Cajamarca and Amazonas region, at altitudes of 400 to 700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in dry forests.
iucn_doc_threats: In parts of Amazonas (Bagua), the species is affected by human  activities, although it is not known why the locals are logging the cacti. In Cajamarca, the species is indirectly affected by logging. Furthermore, there is land conversion going on. However, part of the species range is safe.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a restricted range (extent of occurrence < 5,000 km<sup>2</sup>) and it is known from a maximum of six locations. Different threats impact the species range, however part of it is safe. Hence the species is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_fullName: Browningia amstutziae (Rauh & Backeb.) Hutchison ex Krainz
checklist_taxon: Browningia amstutziae (Rauh & Backeb.) Hutchison ex Krainz sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to central Peru, occurring solely in Paucartambo in the Pasco region. It grows at altitudes of 3,500 to 4,000 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are not known.
iucn_doc_rationale: The information on Browningia amstutziae is insufficient to assess it; nothing is known about its population size or trend, and the threats are unknown. It is therefore categorized as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Browningia chlorocarpa (Kunth) W.T.Marshall sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_doc_range: It is endemic to Peru, where it occurs in the Cajamarca, Lambayeque and Piura regions, at altitudes of 400 to 2,200 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in humid thorny matorral and inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats for now, but an irrigation project is planned in the area that may affect this species. This potential impact should be closely monitored.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very restricted range and its populations are naturally fragmented. Although no current threat is affecting this cactus, a planned irrigation project in the area may be changing the status of this species soon by causing a continuing decline or a one off decline in the population. If this plan continues then the species will face significant declines over the next three generations and would certainly qualify for threat category using future projections. Further research on threats and monitoring of populations is needed to reduce future rates of decline. The species is therefore classified as Near Threatened.

checklist_taxon: Browningia hernandezii Fern.Alonso sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Scale Unknown/Unrecorded; Mining & quarrying; Roads & railroads
iucn_doc_range: Esta especie es endémica de Colombia, donde se distribuye en la región Andina con registros en el departamento de Boyacá.
iucn_doc_habitats: Browningia hernandezii es un arbusto que crece en hábitats de matorrales xerofíticos altoandinos (elevación mayor a 2.000 msnm), específicamente en sitios rocosos. Esta especie es posiblemente polinizada por vertebrados y dispersada por vertebrados, tal como se ha reportado en otras especies para el género (Ossa et al. 2016).
iucn_doc_threats: Las tres subpoblaciones conocidas de esta especie se encuentran en sitios con destrucción y degradación del hábitat (zona noreste de Boyacá, en la cuenca alta del Río Chicamocha). La destrucción y degradación de los parches de hábitat remanentes se debe principalmente a actividades relacionadas con la ampliación de la frontera agrícola (cultivos de papa, arveja y pasto), minería de metales y ampliación de la frontera urbana y de obras de infraestructura como vías y viviendas.
iucn_doc_rationale: Browningia hernandezii es una especie endémica de Colombia. Esta especie tiene una distribución geográfica restringida (extensión de presencia menor a 200 km2), se conoce solamente de tres localidades y su hábitat está siendo degradado por actividades humanas (deforestación para la ampliación de la frontera agropecuaria, minería y construcción de infraestructura). Esta especie es categorizada como En Peligro.

checklist_taxon: Cephalocereus columna-trajani (Karw. ex Pfeiff.) K.Schum. sec. Tapia & al. 2017
iucn_threat_names: Unintentional effects (species is not the target); Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: Cephalocereus columna-trajani is endemic to the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán-Zapotitlán Valley system and adjacent areas. It is the most emblematic species of the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán Biosphere Reserve. It occurs at elevations of 600-1,800 m.
iucn_doc_habitats:
Cephalocereus columna-trajani occurs in xerophyllous scrub and thorn scrub, it can grow on steep, rocky slopes (Pavón and Briones 2001). The species has been reported to flower and fruit before the rainy season, which occurs July-August (Pavón and Briones 2001), and also to flower from March to September (Zavala-Hurtado and Díaz-Solís 1995).

Cephalocereus columna-trajani is the major component of the 'cardonal-tetechera' vegetation association, and it is the dominant species of the 'cardonal' vegetation association (García-Suárez 2003; Jiménez-Sierra et al. 2007; Hernández et al. 2003). Cardones (C. columna-trajani) are widely used for support by epiphytes such as bromeliads (García-Suárez 2003).

It can be inferred that 88-123 years ago recruitment rates were high for this species, and that there might be a delay of ca 60 years after physical damage before the individual reproduces again (Zavala-Hurtado and Díaz-Solís 1995).
iucn_doc_threats: Cephalocereus columna-trajani seems not to be threatened directly and occurs within a protected area. However, the pollinators (bats) are considered as a malign species in the area and are threatened. This could affect the species reproductive success in the long term. Habitat fragmentation can be a threat to the population in the near future (C. Hernández pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_rationale: Within its restricted range Cephalocereus columna-trajani is abundant and there are no major threats. It is found in a protected area, hence is listed as Least Concern. However, attention is needed on the conservation status of the pollinating bats and the trend on the fragmentation of the habitat where the species occurs. Also, long term monitoring of population trends is needed.

iucn_doc_range: Cereus fricii is endemic to Venezuela, where it is restricted to the north-central coast and southern portion of Falcon state (Ponce 1989).  The species is rare and its occurrence is scarce.  Its elevation ranges from 0-970 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It occurs in spiny scrublands and thorny forest.  The mating system has not been studied.  However, it is likely pollinated by bats and dispersed by birds and bats.
iucn_doc_threats: Due to land conversion to urban areas, roads, and tourism development in much of the species' range, the current subpopulations are mostly restricted to steep slopes.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because it has a restricted range (extent of occurrence is 5,200 km<sup>2</sup>) with scarce occurrence.  It occurs in less than 5 locations and its area of occupancy and quality of habitat are continuing to decline due to urban expansion.

Cereus horrispinus Backeb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in northeastern Colombia (Hunt et al. 2006) and Venezuela (Nassar et al. 1997).  Its elevation ranges from 0-700 m.  Its population is presumably disjunct between Venezuela and Colombia although it has not been studied.  The species occurs in in low densities and in fewer than 10 locations throughout its range. Its area of occupancy is estimated to be less than 2,000 km2 using the 100 km2 grid cells.
iucn_doc_habitats: It occurs in clearings in dry forest and spiny scrublands.  Although it occurs in clearings, it is associated with other vegetation such as shrubs and trees that provide support for its branches. Cereus horrispinus is an obligate outcrosser and bats are crucial for maintaining cross pollination (Nassar et al. 1997). The bats and cacti are strongly interdependent, suggesting that a disturbance affecting one species would impact strongly on the other (Nassar et al. 2003). These bats are relatively rare and threatened by human disturbance in Curacao and the Netherlands Antilles (Petit 1995).  Bat species such as Leptonycteris curosoae in northern Venezuela feed on the fruit of C. horripinus, among other cactus species (Martino et al. 2002). Leptonycteris curasoae is important in maintaining xeric communities in tropical and subtropical dry areas (Martino et al. 2002). Cereus horrispinus flowers from August to November, and fruits from June to July (Martino et al. 2002).
iucn_doc_threats: This species has no major threats.  However, habitat reduction, mainly for agricultural purposes, could affect the status of this species in the near future considering current development plans.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Near Threatened because it has a restricted and disjunct range, and future projects suggest a decline in some populations if trends continue. Fewer than 10 locations have been determined for this species and it is highly susceptible to habitat conversion. However, more information is needed on the number of locations,  population trends, and threats for this species in order to determine if  it is threatened.

checklist_taxon: Cereus pierre-braunianus Esteves sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Goiás and adjacent Tocantins, at elevations of 650 to 700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on raised outcrops of karstic bambuí limestone in seasonal tropical dry forest. Its fruits are eaten by parrots and it is pollinated by bats.
iucn_doc_threats: The only major potential threat for the species is quarrying.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a very restricted range (estimated extent of occurrence =100km<sup>2</sup>), and it is known from only one locality  At the moment there is no significant continuing decline, however the rocks where the species grows could soon become the target of mining for cement companies as it has happened in other neighbouring areas. Hence it is listed as Vulnerable.

iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Serra do Caraça in south-central Minas Gerais, Brazil. It is only known from this location. It occurs at elevations from 1,000 - 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in the south-eastern campo rupestre element: deeply fissured quartzitic outcrops on steep hill slopes and cliffs in constant association with Philodendron cipoense. The plant is most abundant at lower elevations, below 1,400 m asl. Above this elevation the populations are very small and appear to be marginal, probably being limited by winter frosts. Plants at these elevations occur only under the protection of rocks or bushes and exposed stems are invariably damaged and re-sprout from below.
iucn_doc_threats: This species may be affected by tourism.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because the number of mature individuals in the entire population is very likely to be less than 250. The species is known from only one locality and even though it occurs within a protected area, the site is in need of management to provide adequate protection.

iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in dry pre-Puna on the eastern slopes of the Andes.
iucn_doc_threats: Clearance for subsistence farming is impacting the species. Reduced seedling establishment due to trampling by goats further affects this cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cleistocactus buchtienii is listed as Least Concern due to its wide distribution and the lack of major threats.

iucn_doc_habitats: It occurs on rocky slopes in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: The native habitat has been replaced by agricultural fields. This impact is not ongoing because the range of this cactus is now limited to rocky slopes, where agriculture is not feasible.

checklist_taxon: Cleistocactus reae Cárdenas sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in La Paz, at elevations of 2,750 to 3,000 m (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in forests within seasonally dry inter-Andean valleys, just above the Yungas.
iucn_doc_threats: There are agriculture activities in the area the species is known to occur, clearance for subsistence farming might be impacting the species but it is known if this is causing a continuing decline.
iucn_doc_rationale: With the current information, the species could qualify as Critically Endangered under B1ab(iii), because it has a small range, and there are agricultural activities in the area where it occurs. However, there is uncertainty on the extent of the species range and whether or not the threats are causing declines. Hence the species is here listed as Near Threatened.

Coleocephalocereus aureus F.Ritter sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Mining & quarrying; Named species
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in northeastern Minas Gerais at elevations between 280 and 910 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus is locally dominant on crystalline rocky outcrops surrounded by caatinga vegetation.
iucn_doc_threats: The species habitat is often subject to quarrying for granite rock as well as small-scale cattle ranching and invasive pasture grasses.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coleocephalocereus aureus has a limited area of occupancy of less than 2,000 km2, but it is found at many locations. The species is declining due to habitat loss by mining and habitat degradation by agricultural activities. It is, nevertheless, listed as Least Concern at the moment because the declines are not severe enough to threaten the survival of this cactus in the near future.

checklist_taxon: Coleocephalocereus braunii Diers & Esteves sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in western Espírito Santo, Brazil, where it is known from only one site near the source of the Rio Vitoria.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows as a pioneer plant on gneiss inselbergs on steep slopes. The habitat was originally surrounded by tropical rainforest.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats are human settlements and deforestation for logging as well as clearance for cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coleocephalocereus braunii is only known from a single site with an extent of occurrence and area of occupancy of around 4 km2. The population size is probably less than 1,000 mature individuals, and it is declining due to a nearby settlement and deforestation. It can be inferred that more than 30% of the population has been lost in the last 30 years. This trend is predicted to worsen in the future, as the causes of past reduction have not ceased. Hence, it is currently listed as Critically Endangered. Further surveys of the site and nearby habitats are urgently needed as the species was last surveyed in 1983.

checklist_taxon: Coleocephalocereus buxbaumianus Buining sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Small-holder farming; Unspecified species; Named species
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Minas Gerias in Brazil, occurring at 100 -1,000 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in southern humid/sub-humid forest (inselberg) element, in gneissic/inselbergs/lajedos (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The vegetation around the inselbergs where the taxon occurs has largely been destroyed, but the actual habitat of the plant is less affected. It is probably threatened in the west, where mining operations have destroyed much of its habitat. (Taylor and Zappi 2004). When the natural forest is removed from around the outcrops where the species grows, weedy invasive species (such as elephant grass and other alien invasives) start occupying the species niche, affecting its ability to establish. The pollinators are also impacted by the destruction of surrounding habitat.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide extent of occurrence, however the western most part of its range has probably disappeared due to habitat destruction. This species only grows on outcrops, and has an area of occupancy of less than 500 km<sup>2</sup> which is estimated based on expert opinion. All subpopulations are experiencing a continuing decline and are affected by several threats such as habitat destruction, invasive species and decline in pollinators. Hence it is listed as Endangered.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs at a single locality north of Pico da Bandeira in Minas Gerais, in the border area to Espírito Santo.
iucn_doc_habitats: The main habitat consists of gneiss inselbergs, where the species grows on steep slopes as a pioneer plant. The habitat was originally surrounded by tropical rainforest.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats are human settlements, deforestation for logging, and clearance for cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coleocephalocereus diersianus is known from a single site with an extent of occurrence and area of occupancy of around 4 km2. The population probably consists of less than 1,000 mature individuals and has a declining trend due to a nearby human settlement and deforestation. It is estimated that at least 30% of the population has disappeared over the last 30 years, and this trend is predicted to worsen in the future as the causes of the past reduction have not ceased. Hence, it is listed as Critically Endangered. Further surveys of the site and nearby habitats are urgently needed as the species was last surveyed in 1983.

iucn_locations: Rio de Janeiro; Espírito Santo; Bahia; Minas Gerais; São Paulo; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZL-RJ; BZL-ES; BZE-BA; BZL-MG; BZL-SP; BR
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Savanna - Dry
iucn_threat_names: Named species; Small-holder farming; Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Minas Gerais, Espírito Santo, São Paulo, Bahia and Rio de Janeiro from sea level to 900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits the southern humid/subhumid forest (inselberg) element. It is locally dominant on gneissic inselbergs or lajedos within the Mata Atlântica and restinga zones. The inselbergs are usually inaccessible (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The vegetation around the inslebergs where the taxon occurs has largely  been destroyed, the actual habitat of the plant is less affected.  When the natural forest is  removed from around the outcrops where the species grows, weedy invasive  species (such as elephant grass and other alien invasives) start  occupying the species niche affecting its ability to establish. The  pollinators are also impacted by the destruction of surrounding habitat. The species is also affected by urbanization (subpopulations close to Rio do Janeiro) and development for tourism (subpopulations on the coast).
iucn_doc_rationale: The subpopulations of Coleocephalocereus fluminensis have declined and continue to do so mainly due to habitat loss, however the threats are localized. The species occurs over a vast area and within protected areas, hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Coleocephalocereus goebelianus (Vaupel) Buining sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the core area of eastern Brazil in Bahia and Minas Gerais, occurring at 300 - 1,000 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in southern caatinga on quartz, gneiss/granite inselbergs (rarely in limestone outcrops) and stony soil (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is habitat loss due to deforestation for agriculture and timber.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though the species has a relatively wide range, over a 50% reduction of the population is estimated to have occurred in the last 60 years (generation length 20 years) from habitat loss (deforestation), which affects its whole range and will not cease in the foreseeable future, hence it is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Coleocephalocereus neoestevesii N.P.Taylor sec. Romeiro-Brito & al. 2023
Coleocephalocereus pluricostatus Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to southeastern Brazil, where it is found in Minas Gerais, east of Governador Valadares towards Espírito Santo. It is known from ca five locations and occurs at elevations of 100 to 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The main habitat consists of gneiss inselberg, where the species grows on steep slopes as a pioneer plant. The sites were originally surrounded by humid tropical forest. It is suspected that deforestation causes the habitat to become drier, and it has been observed  in several places that the plants start to die from the top. This is a slow growing cactus that reaches heights of up to 6 m.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats are settlements and deforestation for logging, as well as clearance for cattle ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coleocephalocereus pluricostatus is known from around five locations and has extent of occurrence of less than 5,000 km2. The population probably consists of less than 5,000 mature individuals and is declining due to a nearby settlement and deforestation. The population has been reduced by more than 30 percent in the last 30 years. This trend is predicted to worsen in the future, as the causes of past reduction have not ceased. Hence, the cactus is listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(iii). Further surveys of the site and nearby habitats are urgently needed, as the last survey was done more than 20 years ago.

checklist_taxon: Coleocephalocereus purpureus (Buining & Brederoo) F.Ritter sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying; Unspecified species; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species is found at elevations of 250-300 m asl near the Rio Jequitinhonha, north-eastern Minas Gerais, Brazil. The range of this species deserves further investigation, but it is clearly much more restricted than that of its sister species, C. aureus. It is currently known only from a single locality and from three recently discovered subpopulations within a few kilometres of it. The area of occupancy is estimated by experts to be less than 5km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in south-eastern caatinga (inselberg) element, on gneissic inselbergs and rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is quarrying of the rock where it grows. It is also adversely affected by collection, deforestation, invasive species, fire and cattle trampling.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species only grows on outcrops and there is a high demand from the quarrying industry for these rock and mining is already happening in the area. Based on this, a decline of over 90% of the population in the next 30 years is projected if mining activities continue at the present rate (the species has a generation length of 10 years), hence it is listed as Critically Endangered. Attempts at legally protecting the species' rocky habitat from exploitation have so far failed.

Consolea macracantha (Griseb.) A.Berger sec. Majure & al. 2021
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Medicine - human & veterinary; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range:
The species is endemic to Cuba, occurring in the southern coast of the provinces of Granma, Santiago and Guantanamo and on the northeast coast, west of Holguín, and in Sancti Spiritus, east of Trinidad (N. Taylor pers. comm. 2011). It occurs at elevations from sea level to about 20 m.

[New distribution after split of Consolea species. Need to confirm with Vivian the ID of C. moniliformis in Sancti Spiritus]
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in coastal thickets, dry deciduous forests and semi-deciduous forests. It is pollinated by hummingbirds and it establishes from fallen stem segments.
iucn_doc_threats: This species may be affected by forest fires.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern because it has a relatively wide distribution, is very abundant, and there are no major threats affecting it. Furthermore, it may be favoured by clearance of the forest.

checklist_taxon: Consolea millspaughii (Britton) A.Berger sec. Majure & al. 2021
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs on the Cayman Islands, Cuba and the Bahamas (Areces-Mallea 2001, Strittmatter 2006). It occurs at elevations from 0-25 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs on limestone and potentially on sandy soils in the coastal area in dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: In the Bahamas, the major threat to Consolea millspaughii is Cactoblastis cactorum.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern as it occurs in multiple sites and it is relatively common. However, the spread and impact of the invasive Cactoblastis moth needs to be monitored.

checklist_taxon: Consolea picardae (Urb.) Areces sec. Piñeyro & al. 2023
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the island of Hispaniola - Dominican Republic and Haiti (Anderson 2001, Strittmatter 2006). It occurs at elevations from 0-25 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in sandy soils in the coastal dry forest area.
iucn_doc_threats: Threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because there is not  enough information on range, population size, trends and potential threats  affecting the species and its habitat.

checklist_fullName: Copiapoa angustiflora Helmut Walter, G.J.Charles & Mächler
checklist_taxon: Copiapoa angustiflora Helmut Walter, G.J.Charles & Mächler sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Named species; Roads & railroads; Droughts
iucn_doc_range: Esta especie es endémica de Chile, en donde se distribuye en la parte sur de la región de Antofagasta, en elevaciones de 200 a 400 msnm. Se conoce de una localidad, una quebrada, con un área de aproximadamente 8 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: Esta especie crece en colinas costeras de granito.
iucn_doc_threats: Copiapoa angustiflora se encuentra amenazada por la recolección ilegal, la depredación por guanacos y la construcción y reconstrucción de caminos después de la temporada de lluvias. Este cactus también está amenazado por insectos (Coleoptera) que depredan a las plantas, las larvas de los escarabajos se las comen de adentro hacia afuera, matando a la planta. Esta especie se ve afectada por sequías extendidas por la disminución de la temporalidad y duración de la camanchaca (niebla costera) por efecto del cambio climático.
iucn_doc_rationale: Esta especie está catalogada como En Peligro Crítico ya que presenta una extensión de la presencia (EOO) de 16 km<sup>2 </sup>y un área de ocupación (AOO) de 8 km<sup>2</sup>. Con base en las amenazas, esta área se considera una localidad en donde la especie sufre una disminución continua en el número de individuos maduros por la recolecta ilegal para colecciones internacionales, la depredación por guanacos y el cambio climático. Además, el hábitat donde crece la especie se ve afectado por la construcción de carreteras, lo que causa una disminución continua de la calidad del hábitat.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa aphanes Mächler & Helmut Walter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_doc_range: Copiapoa aphanes is a species with a restricted distribution, endemic to the Antofagasta province of Chile (Muñoz and Serra 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found in the coastal desert of Taltal (Gajardo 1994). It is present in rocky slopes from the north to the east of Taltal (Muñoz and Serra 2006). It grows between rocks, from 10 to 450 m asl, along with Copiapoa cinerea and Eriosyce taltalensis (H. Walter pers. comm. 2022).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is affected by prolonged droughts due to the reduction in the length of the camanchaca season (coastal fog) due to climate change. The plants depend on this fog as their main source of water. It is probably also affected by grazing (Muñoz and Serra 2006).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a restricted range with an extent of occurrence (EOO) and area of occupancy (AOO) of 4 km2. The species is known from a single location where the population is experiencing a continuing decline due to grazing and extended droughts resulting from a reduction of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. The species is therefore assessed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa armata (F.Ritter) Helmut Walter & Larridon sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Copiapoa coquimbana (Karw. ex Rümpler) Britton & Rose var. armata F.Ritter
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Mining & quarrying; Nomadic grazing; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile. It occurs near the Choros River, east of the Coquimbo region, at an altitude of 1,000 to 1,432 m asl (Schultz 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on the edge of a dry ravine in shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats to this species are off-road vehicles and goat grazing that affect not only the plants but the soil and habitat as well. Illegal collection is also a threat to this species. Mining is plausible future threat. This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the only source of water.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa armata has a small range with an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 1,471 km2 and an area of occupancy (AOO) of 40 km2. Threats include goat grazing, illegal collection, and extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. The species is known from 6 to 10 locations. The population is experiencing a continuing decline due to these threats and it is therefore assessed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa cinerea (Phil.) Britton & Rose sec. Larridon & al. 2018
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Roads & railroads; Mining & quarrying; Recreational activities; Droughts
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the regions of Antofagasta and Atacama, at elevations of 0 to 1,300 m asl. It is found from Caleta Colorada to the National Park Pan de Azúcar.  The subspecies are spatially separated, however, their distributions overlap in some areas (Schulz and Kapitany 1996).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in coastal hills (lomas costeras) on rocky, sandy soils and it seems to be an edaphic specialist.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is heavily impacted by illegal collection. Other threats include off-road vehicles, road building and mining. These activities affect both the species directly, and its habitat. The species and its habitat, especially the coastal subpopulations, are severely affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is their main source of water.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a restricted range, with an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 1,577 km<sup>2</sup>, however, it is very abundant and even though there are threats in portions of its range these are not sufficient to trigger a threatened listing. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa conglomerata (Phil.) Lembcke sec. Walter & Guerrero 2022
iucn_synonyms: Copiapoa ahremephiana N.P.Taylor & G.J.Charles; Echinocactus conglomeratus F.Phil.
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Intentional use (species is the target); Named species; Roads & railroads
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the low part of Botija Valley and the area between this valley and the sea (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations between 0 and 800 m asl. Its extent of occurrence (EOO) is estimated to be 12 km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on coastal hills, in extremely dry areas with very little rainfall. The dry season lasts eight to twelve months and in some years it may not rain at all. From coastal fog (camanchaca), the plants obtain water mainly by condensation (Belov 2005). The species grows near populations of Copiapoa solaris, Copiapoa decorticans and Copiapoa atacamensis.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are illegal collection and invertebrate predators (Coleoptera) that eat the plants from the inside. However, the extent of the damage to the population is unknown. This species and its habitat are affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is their main source of water. It is also affected by road work and widening.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa conglomerata has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of approximately 12 km2. It is known from one location that is subject to several threats that are causing continuing declines in the number of mature individuals and habitat quality. These threats include extended droughts, illegal collection, and attacks by beetles which eat the plants. The species is listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa corralensis I.Schaub & Keim sec. Kew 2019
Copiapoa dealbata F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Copiapoa carrizalensis F.Ritter; Echinocactus malletianus Cels ex Salm-Dyck; Copiapoa cinerea (Phil.) Britton & Rose var. dealbata (F.Ritter) Backeb.; Copiapoa malletiana (Cels ex Salm-Dyck) Backeb.
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Desert - Cold
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Tourism & recreation areas; Droughts; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in Carrizal Bajo, towards Totoral in the north (Hunt et al. 2006), and Huasco in the south, in the Atacama region. It grows at elevations of 0 to 700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in coastal hills (lomas costeras) and desert shrubland on sandy soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are off-road vehicles and road construction. Urban development for tourism impacts part of the range of this species. Climate change affects this species, but not as severely as other species of the same genus, through extended droughts and reduction of coastal fog (camanchaca).
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa dealbata has a relatively wide range, with an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 384 km2, is abundant, and even though there is some population decline, it is stable. The species is assessed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa decorticans N.P.Taylor & G.J.Charles sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in Antofagasta at elevations of 250 to 800 m asl. It is restricted to a few valleys and hills near Blanco Encalada (Hoffmann and Walter 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in coastal hills (lomas costeras), on slopes.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection is the main threat, which affects recruitment as reproductive individuals are collected.  There is prospecting for mining in the area, which could have a very severe impact on the only known location. This species and its habitat are affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is their main source of water.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa decorticans is listed as Critically Endangered because it has a very restricted extent of occurrence (EOO) of 8 km2, is known from only one location which is affected by illegal collection and droughts, and there is prospecting for mining in the area. There is a decline in the number of mature individuals, and area of occupancy, as the population is dying on the slopes, probably as a result of droughts. Also, illegal collection of mature plants is affecting recruitment.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa desertorum F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Copiapoa taltalensis (Werderm.) Looser subsp. desertorum (F.Ritter) G.J.Charles; Copiapoa rupestris F.Ritter subsp. desertorum (F.Ritter) D.R.Hunt
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it is found in Antofagasta at elevation between 10 to 400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on coastal hills (lomas costera).
iucn_doc_threats: This species and its habitat are affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is their main source of water. Road construction is also a threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has an extremely restricted range with an area of occupancy (AOO) and extent of occurrence (EOO) of 8 km<sup>2</sup>. It is known from two localities affected by extended droughts that decrease habitat quality and kill individuals. It is therefore assessed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa echinata F.Ritter sec. ???

checklist_fullName: Copiapoa echinoides (Lem. ex Salm-Dyck) Britton & Rose
checklist_taxon: Copiapoa echinoides (Lem. ex Salm-Dyck) Britton & Rose sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Copiapoa cuprea F.Ritter; Copiapoa dura F.Ritter; Echinocactus echinoides Lem. ex Salm-Dyck; Copiapoa griseoviolacea I.Schaub & Keim; Copiapoa marginata (Salm-Dyck) Britton & Rose var. bridgesii (Pfeiff.) A.E.Hoffm.; Echinocactus bridgesii Pfeiff.
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Droughts; Intentional use (species is the target); Commercial & industrial areas
iucn_doc_range: This species can be found in Chile, along the coast from north of Huasco up to 50 km inland in the Atacama region. It grows at elevations of 0 to 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in coastal hills (lomas costeras), on relatively compact and rocky soils.
iucn_doc_threats: This species and its habitat are affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is their main source of water. Road widening and illegal harvesting are also threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa echinoides has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 2,073 km2. There are less than 10 but more than five locations. Threats include climate change and illegal collection, which cause a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals and habitat quality, area of occupancy and extent of occurrence. In recent years, the decline in number of mature individuals has increased markedly because of droughts, with the vast majority of individuals dead in at least two inland subpopulations. The species was previously assessed as Near Threatened, but is now assessed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa fiedleriana (K.Schum.) Backeb. sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Echinocactus fiedlerianus K.Schum.; Copiapoa megarhiza Britton & Rose subsp. echinata (F.Ritter) Doweld
iucn_threat_names: Recreational activities; Housing & urban areas; Droughts; Type Unknown/Unrecorded; Nomadic grazing; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in Atacama, around Huasco. It grows at elevations of 0 to 600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in coastal hills (lomas costeras).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are urban development of illegal settlements and off-road vehicles in the region of Tres Playitas-Baratillo. An additional threat to the subpopulations in Huasco are the ashes from a thermoelectric plant for steel grinding that cover the plants. Mining is also a threat. Two of the four subpopulations are not threatened. Goat grazing degrades soil in the area making the establishment of seedlings and regeneration challenging (Schulz 2006). Some of the subpopulations occur in an area that has been developed for agriculture of olive trees and grapes for pisco. This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water. This species is also illegally collected from the wild for international trade.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Vulnerable because it has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of approximately 3,607 km<sup>2 </sup>and an area of occupancy (AOO) of 100 km<sup>2</sup>. Based on the many threats affecting this species and its habitat there are between 10 to 15 locations. The subpopulations occur in isolated patches that are distant from each other with a minimal probability of genetic exchange due to small insect pollination and ant seed dispersal.  There is a continuing decline in habitat quality, extent of occurrence, subpopulations and number of mature individuals due to illegal collection, agriculture, droughts, off-road vehicles, urbanization and industry.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa gigantea Backeb. sec. Walter & Guerrero 2022
iucn_synonyms: Copiapoa cinerea (Phil.) Britton & Rose subsp. haseltoniana (Backeb.) N.P.Taylor
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Intentional use (species is the target); Oil & gas drilling; Garbage & solid waste; Renewable energy; Mining & quarrying; Roads & railroads
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it is found in Antofagasta at elevations of 0 to 1,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on coastal hills (lomas costeras).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats to this species are road construction, illegal collection, copper mining and transmission lines. This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa gigantea has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 784 km2. It is known from ten locations where it is threatened by several threats including climate change, mining and illegal collection, which cause a continuing decline in quality of the habitat and number of mature individuals. The species is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa grandiflora F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Roads & railroads; Droughts
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the Atacama and Antofagasta regions at elevations of 450 to 600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in coastal hills (lomas costeras) on rocky slopes where sandy pockets are formed (Belov 2005).
iucn_doc_threats: Road reconstruction is a major threat, as roads are washed away by the rains and need reconstruction every year, which has impacts on the population of this cactus. There is illegal collection, with mainly the younger individuals being extracted because they are easier to transport. This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa grandiflora has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of approximately 138 km2 and an area of occupancy of 44 km2. It occurs in three to four locations, and there is a continuing decline in habitat quality and the number of mature individuals due to road construction, illegal collection and other threats. Therefore the species is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa intricata Meregalli sec. WFO 2019
Copiapoa laui Diers sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_synonyms: Copiapoa hypogaea F.Ritter subsp. laui (Diers) G.J.Charles; Copiapoa hypogaea F.Ritter var. laui (Diels) A.E.Hoffm.
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it is found from Cifuncho to Pan de Azúcar National Park, where it occurs mostly on relatively flat ground on hillsides.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on coastal hills (lomas costeras), in extremely dry areas with very little rainfall. The plants obtain water mainly from coastal fog (camanchaca). The species grows near populations of Copiapoa cinerea subsp. columna-alba, Copiapoa grandiflora, Copiapoa angustiflora and Copiapoa longistaminea.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by illegal collection for specialized collections. It is also affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as their main source of water.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very restricted range with an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 47 km<sup>2 </sup>and area of occupancy (AOO) of 16 km<sup>2</sup>. Based on threats this species is known from two locations, where it is experiencing a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals and habitat quality due to prolonged droughts and illegal collection. The species is therefore listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa longistaminea F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Intentional use (species is the target); Droughts; Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs from south of Cifuncho to north of Pan de Azúcar, 0 to 400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on coastal hills (lomas costeras). It is able to colonize rocky areas very close to the sea.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are road construction and reconstruction of roads after the rainy season. It is illegally collected, but this is not considered a threat because typically individual branches are removed. This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa longistaminea has a restricted range, with an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 263 km2, however, it is very abundant and there are no major threats. There is illegal collection but the extraction is non-lethal and does not have major impacts on the healthy populations. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa magnimamma F.Ritter & Y.Itô sec. WFO 2019
Copiapoa marginata (Salm-Dyck) Britton & Rose sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Intentional use (species is the target); Unspecified species
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in Atacama, in southwest Caldera and Morro Copiapó (Hunt et al. 2006) and in the northern part of Pan de Azúcar National Park, north of Chañaral. It grows at elevations of 0 to 700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on coastal hills (lomas costeras). It occurs in Morro Copiapó, a hill southwest of Caldera that could be called a "desert island", with the west side adjoining sea cliffs and the top of the hill being a flat plain (Charles 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is illegal collection, and it is suspected that collection at the national level is increasing as more people become interested in the plants. This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water. After the rainy season, an unknown predator makes a hole in the plant to reach the roots to eat them, killing the plant.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa marginata has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 2,389 km2 and an area of occupancy (AOO) of 56 km2. Based on threats, the species is known from eight locations where it is experiencing a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals and habitat quality due to illegal collection and extended droughts. The species is therefore listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa megarhiza Britton & Rose sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Intentional use (species is the target); Housing & urban areas; Commercial & industrial areas; Roads & railroads; Renewable energy; Recreational activities; Agro-industry farming; Unspecified species
iucn_doc_range: Copiapoa megarhiza is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in Atacama in the Copiapó valley (Hunt et al. 2006). Historically, it used to be found from east of Paipote to west of Toledo. It grows at elevations of 0 to 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on coastal hills (lomas costeras) and in Atacama desert habitat. It occurs on very dry granitic hills (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are illegal collection, industry, urbanization and illegal landfills, mainly in the northern subpopulations close to Copiapó. The effect of the extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change is more severe in this species because it occurs further inland and away from the coast. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water. The extended droughts cause a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals throughout the range. Copiapoa megarhiza is also affected by industrialization and its consequences, including road construction. The healthiest population is threatened by the development of photovoltaic farms and their transmission lines. Land use change for pomegranate production is also a threat. Plants and fruit are also affected by insect predation.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa megarhiza is listed as Endangered because it has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 273 km2. Based on threats, the species is known from five locations, where it is experiencing a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals and habitat quality due to many threats including the development of solar farms, illegal collection and extended droughts. The species is listed here as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa mollicula F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Droughts
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile where it is found north of Chañaral, in Atacama.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on coastal hills (lomas costeras).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water. It is illegally collected for international collections. This is a species where the effects of the prolonged droughts are very noticeable (Schulz 2006).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is known from a very restricted area, with an extent of occurrence (EOO) and area of occupancy (AOO) of 4 km<sup>2</sup>. Based on threats, it occurs in one location, where it is experiencing a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals and habitat quality due to illegal collection and climate change. The species is listed here as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa montana F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Droughts
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the southern part of the Antofagasta region. It grows at elevations from 100 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on coastal hills (lomas costeras), on sandy and rocky soil.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is illegal collection, however, the species grows in inaccessible places. This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water.
iucn_doc_rationale: Copiapoa montana has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of approximately 183 km2, and an area of occupancy (AOO) of 24 km2. Based on threats, the species is known from four locations where it is experiencing a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals and habitat quality due to illegal collection and climate change. The species is listed here as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Copiapoa rarissima F.Ritter sec. Zuloaga & al. 2019
Copiapoa taltalensis (Werderm.) Looser sec. Larridon & al. 2015
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Named species; Recreational activities; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Chile, where it occurs in the Antofagasta region, from Quebrada La Cachina, south of Taltal to Tigrillo near Esmeralda, at elevations of 0 to 700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on coastal hills (lomas costeras) in quebradas (cliffy areas).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are predation from guanacos in the southern subpopulations and, to a lesser extent, localized vandalism of plants. This species is affected by extended droughts resulting from the reduction of the length of the camanchaca (coastal fog) season due to climate change. Plants depend on this fog as it is the main source of water. Illegal collection also affects this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has an extent of occurrence (EOO) of 44 km<sup>2</sup>. There are three locations based on threats, which include illegal collection, droughts and predation by guanacos. The population is experiencing a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals and habitat quality. It is therefore listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Corryocactus apiciflorus (Vaupel) Hutchison sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: It is endemic to the Áncash region of Peru, where it occurs at an altitude of 2,500 of 3,000 m (Arakaki et al. 2006). Hunt (2006) suggests that the species is also present in the region of La Libertad and possibly Ayacucho, according to Ostolaza (pers. comm. 2011), this is incorrect because it is a different species.
iucn_doc_habitats: In Ancash, the species occurs in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_rationale: This cactus is classified as Data Deficient because the taxonomy is very confusing and it is therefore not clear which population need to be assessed. The taxonomy must be revised before this species is re-assessed.

checklist_taxon: Corryocactus ayacuchoensis Rauh & Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru, where it is found in the Ayacucho region (Mayocc and Churcampa) at an altitude of 2,500 to 3,000 m (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: Corryocactus ayacuchoensis occurs in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: The location near Ayacucho is affected by human encroachment. The city is expanding and urbanization is threatening the survival of this subpopulation.
iucn_doc_rationale: This cactus has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence is 80 km<sup>2</sup>) and is only known from two locations. As it grows in a valley near the city of Ayacucho, it is affected by urbanization and anthropic disturbance because the city is expanding. The population is in decline, and habitat quality and quantity is being reduced at a fast pace. The species thus qualifies for Endangered.

checklist_fullName: Corryocactus brevistylus (K.Schum. ex Vaupel) Britton & Rose
checklist_taxon: Corryocactus brevistylus (K.Schum. ex Vaupel) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Other household goods; Handicrafts, jewellery, etc.
iucn_doc_range: This cactus occurs in Arequipa and Ayacucho regions of Peru, and in the Chilean region of Tarapacá (Hunt et al. 2006). The southernmost limit is in Huatacondo, Chile. It can be found at altitudes of 2,500 to 3,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in inter-Andean valleys. An epiphytic plant (Tillandsia usneoides) grows on this cactus. In presence of a large amount of T. usneoides, C. brevistylus produces fewer fruit.
iucn_doc_threats: There do not seem to be any threats to the species in Chile. In Ayacucho, Peru, the fruits are used to produce an alcoholic beverage and the stem to produce shampoo. In Arequipa, they are affected by a parasitic plant (Ligaria cuneifolia) (Mauseth et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_rationale: Corryocactus brevistylus has a wide range and a stable population. However, it is not abundant, and the subpopulations are isolated from each other and consist of only a few specimens. C. brevistylus is classified as Least Concern, but the population should be monitored due to its low abundance and its use as a food source, for shampoo and handicrafts production.

checklist_fullName: Corryocactus chachapoyensis (Ochoa & Backeb.) Ochoa & Backeb. ex D.R.Hunt
checklist_taxon: Corryocactus chachapoyensis (Ochoa & Backeb.) Ochoa & Backeb. ex D.R.Hunt sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This cactus species is endemic to Peru, where it grows in Chachapoyas, Conila and Luya (Amazonas region) as well as in the Cajamarca region, very close to the river dividing Amazonas and Cajamarca, at an altitude of 1,650 to 2,500 m (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: Corryocactus chachapoyensis is a climbing species that occurs in thorny dry matorral (Arakaki et al. 2006). It can also be found in dry forests.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a species that has a restricted range (extent of occurrence = 1,300 km<sup>2</sup>) and is not abundant. However, there are no threats and the population is stable. Hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Corryocactus dillonii A.Pauca & Quip. sec. Pauca & Quipuscoa Silvestre 2015
Corryocactus erectus (Backeb.) F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru, where it grows in the Cuzco region, at an altitude of 3,000 to 3,500 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006), on the bluffs of the Río Urubamba between Chillca and Urcos (Hunt 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: It is found in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: The area is frequently visited by tourists due to the presence of Inca ruins, which may affect the species negatively.
iucn_doc_rationale: Corryocactus erectus has a restricted range (Extent of Occurrence around 430km2) and occurs in no more than 10 locations. The habitat quality is being affected by tourist activities related to the presence of Incaic ruins. The species is therefore classified as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Corryocactus pulquinensis Cárdenas sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs near San José de Pulquina, in Caballero province of Santa Cruz department. It has been recorded at altitudes around 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in seasonally dry inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are habitat destruction through subsistence farming and goat grazing, the goats nibble on the cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Endagered, because it has an extent of occurrence of  ca 500 km2, and it has less than five locations, the whole area where the species occurs is affected by over-grazing by goats. In addition of the land being transformed and degraded by the goats, it is very likely that these eat the plants because their spination is not very strong.

checklist_fullName: Corryocactus quadrangularis (Rauh & Backeb.) F.Ritter ex M.H.J.van der Meer
checklist_author: (Rauh & Backeb.) F.Ritter ex M.H.J.van der Meer
checklist_taxon: Corryocactus quadrangularis (Rauh & Backeb.) F.Ritter ex M.H.J.van der Meer sec. Brako & Zarucchi 1993 (sub Corryocactus quadrangularis)
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru, where it is present in the Ayacucho region at an altitude of 3,000 - 3,500 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in matorral and inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: This species may be affected by the expansion of agricultural activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species occurs at low population densities. However, the range is uncertain, and the extent of the impact of agricultural activities is uncertain. It is therefore classified as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Corryocactus serpens F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Peru. It grows in the Huancavelica region, specifically in Colcabamba and Villa Azul, at an altitude of 2,200 to 2,600 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: It is not known whether the species is affected by any threat, further research is necessary.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is not sufficiently understood to be assessed against the threat categories at this time - further field studies may reveal that threats are greater than currently assumed in the absence of information. It has a very restricted range, but nothing is known about the population status and trends, as well as the threats to which it is exposed. The species is therefore classified as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Corryocactus squarrosus (Vaupel) Hutchison sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: The type specimen of C. squarrosus is in Tarma, Junín (Peru). There are other records from the Ayacucho, Huancavelica and Huánuco regions (Hunt 2006), but these most probably represent other species. Field studies recently performed by M. Lowry (pers. comm. 2002) confirmed the presence of C. squarrosus only in the type locality, but not in the other areas listed by Hunt (2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: It is not possible to describe the suitable habitat, as the different sightings that are attributed to C. squarrosus probably correspond to different species (see taxonomic note) which occur in different habitat types.
iucn_doc_threats: It is not known whether it is affected by any threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is insufficiently known to be realistically assessed at this time, especially because there are taxonomic confusions. It is therefore classified as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Corryocactus tarijensis Cárdenas sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in Mendez Province of Tarija Department. It can be found from 2,800 to 3,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in the pre-puna, in seasonally dry inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: Goat grazing and trampling is affecting the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is classified as Endangered because it has a very restricted extent of occurrence (EOO is 40 km<sup>2</sup>), is known from only two locations and it faces threats that are impacting its population.

checklist_taxon: Corynopuntia nigrispina D.Donati sec. Korotkova & al. 2021
Coryphantha bumamma (Ehrenb.) Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
Coryphantha conspicua Lem. sec. Britton & Rose 1923
Coryphantha gracilis Bremer & A.B.Lau sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is known only from a small area (two locations) in the state of Chihuahua, Mexico.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in dry grassland on flat ridges on or in between hills in calcareous soils. The plants are usually solitary, but may occasionally branch and form small clumps.
iucn_doc_rationale:
Although Coryphantha gracilis has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence is only about 3,000 km²) and is known from only two places, the population size is in the thousands and there are no threats to the species. Hence it is listed as Least Concern.
This assessment is based on one published in 2002; it is assumed that there has been no change in status because it is an abundant species with no threats.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha hookeri Lem. sec. Britton & Rose 1923

checklist_fullName: Coryphantha maiz-tablasensis Fritz Schwarz ex Backeb.
checklist_taxon: Coryphantha maiz-tablasensis Fritz Schwarz ex Backeb. sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Roads & railroads; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Scale Unknown/Unrecorded
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the state of San Luis Potosí, Mexico. It is found in the Río Verde, Villa Juárez and Cd. del Maíz Municipalities.  The plants at all locations are scattered and form large groups. There are two locations south of Huizache on highway 57. According to Fitz-Maurice this species should not be listed as Endangered given that there is evidence that it is widely scattered.
iucn_doc_habitats: The main site is in the dry lagoon of Las Tablas in gypsum soil with grass.  The habitat is locally classified as follows: eastern subpopulation - Zacatal (Grassland); central subpopulation - Matorral Desértico Micrófilo with Yuccas (Rzedowski 1978).
iucn_doc_threats: There is constant loss of plants due to agricultural cultivation. Road construction decimated one of the locations. Illegal collection is an ongoing problem.
iucn_doc_rationale: Coryphantha maiz-tablasensis is known only from five locations, some of which are potentially threatened by cultivation. Large portions of the range have already been lost. The extent of occurrence is 4,000 km², there is continuous decline due to loss of habitat. The location near Matehuala was nearly destroyed by road construction, and only a few plants are left, which are likely to be removed by collectors. The species is therefore listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(ii,iii,v).

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha piercei Fosberg sec. WFO 2019
Coryphantha pulleineana (Backeb.) Glass sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico occurring in a restricted area in northern San Luis Potosí and Tamaulipas, where six localities are known.
iucn_doc_habitats: It is usually found on limestone hill slopes in xerophylous scrub. At the San Luis Potosí locality, the plants are quite scattered and well hidden.
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat destruction is a major threat mostly due to overgrazing goats but also due to some mining activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered Endangered because it has a limited range (extent of occurrence approximately 2,500km2), it is known from only two locations, and is experiencing a continuing population decline due to the impact of livestock trampling (goats) across its entire range. The habitat is expected to decline in the future mostly due to overgrazing goats and mining.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha recurvata (Engelm.) Britton & Rose sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_common_name: Golden chested beehive; Santa Cruz Beehive Cactus; Biznaga-partida de Espinas Curvas; Recurved Corycactus
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: Coryphantha recurvata is distributed in the Mexican state of Sonora and in the United States in Arizona (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations from 850 to 1,800 m asl (Paredes et al. 2000). In Arizona, the species occurs in western Santa Cruz County, from Nogales and Tumacacori Mountains west (Paredes et al. 2000, Dicht and Lüthy 2005).
iucn_doc_habitats: Coryphantha recurvata occurs in the desert grassland and in oak woodland (Paredes et al. 2000). The southernmost subpopulations are in Sonoran desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for this species. It is a slow growing species and may not be conducive for cultivation or trade. The entire U.S. range of the species is currently subject to poorly-managed livestock grazing, which may be adversely affecting recruitment rates. Livestock grazing also occurs in the Mexican range, but the quality of grazing management is unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: Corypantha recurvata is listed as Least Concern because its geographic range is larger than 20,000 km2, it is abundant, and there are no known threats to this species.

checklist_taxon: Coryphantha sublanata Lem. sec. Britton & Rose 1923
Coryphantha tripugionacantha A.B.Lau sec. Sánchez & al. 2022
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Zacatecas (Dicht and Lüthy 2003).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species probably grows in desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has an extent of occurrence well over 20,000 km<sup>2</sup>,<sup> </sup> even though the population size and trend is unknown and there might be threats in parts of its range, it is unlikely the species qualifies for a threatened category given the large area the species is found in. Hence, the species is here listed as Least Concern, however, more research is necessary to more accurately assess its conservation status.

checklist_taxon: Cumulopuntia boliviana (Salm-Dyck) F.Ritter sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: San Juan; Argentina; La Rioja; Salta; Jujuy; Catamarca; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Chile; Peru
iucn_location_codes: AGW-SJ; AR; AGW-LR; AGW-SA; AGW-JU; AGW-CA; BO; CL; PE
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs from southern Peru (Puno, Tacna, Moquegua, Arequipa), the Bolivian altiplano, and Arica y Parinacota, Tarapacá, and Antofagasta regions of Chile to Argentina (Jujuy, Salta, Catamarca, La Rioja, and San Juan provinces) at elevations of 2,000 to 4,400 m asl (Hoffmann and Walter 1989, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in high altitudes close to the line of high Andes. It is present in areas fully exposed to the sun, generally in areas or slopes facing north. It never occurs on rocks, but rather on sandy grounds (Hoffmann and Walter 1989, Belov 2005). Exceptionally, wild and domesticated New World camelids forage on this species.
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus does not seem to be affected by any threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is locally abundant where it occurs, and is present in several protected areas.

checklist_taxon: Cumulopuntia chichensis (Cárdenas) E.F.Anderson sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Jujuy; Argentina; Bolivia, Plurinational States of
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Potosí, Tarija and Chuquisaca, Bolivia, and in Jujuy, Argentina. It can be found at elevations of 3,250 to 3,850 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on sand amongst rocks in puna grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cumulopuntia chichensis has a wide distribution and is not exposed to any known threat. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.
