iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Mexico in the states of  Baja California and Sonora and in the United States in Arizona, Utah, California and Nevada (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of 300 - 1,300 m. (Paredes et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on the high plain of the Sonoran Desert of Arizona (Paredes et al. 2000). The species grows in a variety of habitats including dry thorny scrub and oak woodlands. The species also occurs on a variety of substrates including sandy alluvium, steep rocky hillsides, and on a variety of rock types.
iucn_doc_threats: Given its wide range, this species is not facing any major threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is an extremely widespread and abundant species and there are no major threats.  Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia alcahes (F.A.C.Weber) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Baja California; Baja California Sur; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in the states of Baja California and Baja California Sur (Hunt et al. 2006) from 5 - 1,100 m. The species also occurs on most of the off-shore islands in the Gulf of California.
iucn_doc_habitats: Cylindropuntia alcahes occurs in the Sonoran Desert scrub in association with Olneya tesota, Opuntia bigelovii, Pachycereus pringlei, and Stenocereus gummosus (Paredes et al. 2000).  In the state of Sonora, this species only grows on San Estaban Island.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this common species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a widespread and abundant species on the Baja California Peninsula and has no major threats.  Hence, this species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia bigelovii (Engelm.) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Baja California; Mexico; California; Sonora; Arizona; United States; Baja California Sur; Nevada
iucn_location_codes: MXN-BC; MX; CAL-OO; MXN-SO; ARI-OO; US; MXN-BS; NEV-OO
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is reported in Mexico from the states of  Baja California, Baja California Sur and Sonora.  The species occurs in the Unites States in Arizona, California, and Nevada.  The species occurs at elevations from 300 to 900 m (Benson 1982, Paredes et al. 2000). Records from New Mexico (Hunt et al. 2006) are not confirmed.
iucn_doc_habitats: Cylindropuntia bigelovii  grows on rocky slopes in the Sonoran desert scrub (Paredes et al. 2000). The species also grows on sand dunes along the beach in coastal Atriplex dominated scrub, and further inland on flats and on rocky slopes (mostly volcanic rock) in desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this common species.  It is more likely to be treated as a pest plant in some places.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a very widespread and extremely common species with no major threats.  Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia californica (Torr. & A.Gray) F.M.Knuth sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_locations: United States; Baja California; California; Mexico
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Commercial & industrial areas; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is reported in Mexico from the states of Baja California and in the United States from the state of California (Hunt et al. 2006). The species does not occur in the state of Sonora as recorded by Hunt et al. (2006). It grows at altitudes from 0 to 1,900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in coastal sage scrub and chaparral, oak scrub, juniper woodlands, and grassy rolling hills. The species is usually found on sandy soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The population in southern California is being impacted by expanding urban development. Elsewhere, the species appears to be secure with no major threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: The extent of occurrence of Cylindropuntia californica is probably close to the 20,000 km² threshold. The population is declining in southern California; however, the Mexican portion of the population is not under threat.  Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_location_codes: US; TEX-OO; MXE-CU; MX; OKL-OO; NWM-OO
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in the United States, in the states of New Mexico, Oklahoma and Texas (Hunt et al. 2006), and in northeastern Chihuahua in Mexico (R. Corral-Díaz pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows mainly in grasslands and occasionally in oak scrublands.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species; however, land use change may affect some subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia davisii has a very large range, is abundant, and there are no known major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia densiaculeata Backeb. sec. WFO 2019
Cylindropuntia ganderi (C.B.Wolf) Rebman & Pinkava sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_synonyms: Opuntia acanthocarpa (Engelm. & J.M.Bigelow) F.M.Knuth ssp. ganderi C.B.Wolf
iucn_locations: California; United States; Baja California; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: This species is reported from the Mexican state of Baja California and in the United States from the very southern part of the state of California (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at altitudes from 100 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in sandy, gravelly to rocky substrates and on alluvial flats in desert scrubland and in oak woodland.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to the species at present.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylidropuntia ganderi is a fairly widespread species that is locally common. The species has no major threats and is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia hystrix (Griseb.) Areces sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: Cylindropuntia hystrix is endemic to Cuba and reported from San Antonio del Sur, Saltadero and Baracoa (Hunt et al. 2006). Gonzalez-Torres (2007) only reported it from Guantanamo Bay and Imias in south Cuba. All of them are considered one location. Its area of occupancy is less than 10 km2 (Gonzalez-Torres 2007), and its extent of occurrence is approximately 80 km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs on coastal thickets on soil derived from shallow limestone. Its reproduction is vegetative.
iucn_doc_threats: According to local people of the region, Cylindropuntia hystrix used to grow in large areas from the surroundings of Imias to Batiquiri (González-Torres 2007), but most of these areas are now covered by crop plantations. Local farmers burn the plants because their spines are harmful to livestock (González-Torres 2007).
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia hystrix is listed as Critically Endangered because it is known from only one location in Cuba, its area of occupancy is less than 10 km2 (Gonzalez-Torres 2007), and its extent of occurrence is approximately 80 km2. In addition, human activities are degrading both the quality and overall area of habitat for this species.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia libertadensis Rebman sec. Kew 2019
Cylindropuntia multigeniculata (Clokey) Backeb. sec. Baker 2016
iucn_synonyms: Opuntia multigeniculata Clokey; Cylindropuntia × multigeniculata  (Clokey) Backeb.
iucn_doc_range: This species is recorded from southeastern Nevada and northwestern Arizona in the United States.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky limestone and basalt hill sides in desert scrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no significant threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Cylindropuntia multigeniculata has a relatively restricted range (extent of occurrence is about 10,000 km²), the species is known from ten subpopulations, most of which occur in protected areas. There are no significant threats; hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in the Mexican states of Sinaloa and Sonora (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations from 0 to 1,070 m asl. There is a verbal report of an occurrence in Arizona but this is not confirmed (D. Pinkava pers. comm. 2010).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in the Sonoran Desert scrub and on the plains of Sonora in the coastal thornscrub, the foothills and the low dry forest (Paredes et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Cylindropuntia thurberi is widespread, locally abundant, and there are no threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Cylindropuntia waltoniorum Rebman sec. Kew 2019
Deamia funis Hammel & S.Arias sec. Hammel & Arias 2022
Discocactus bahiensis Britton & Rose sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Mining & quarrying; Housing & urban areas; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Intentional use (species is the target); Dams (size unknown)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil. It grows in the Rio São Francisco drainage of northern Bahia and Pernambuco; in Ceará it is found along the drainage of the river Jaguaribe, and in northwestern Piauí near the Rio Canindé. It occurs at altitudes between 380 and 650 m asl. The species has a very patchy distribution; and its area of occupancy is less than 500 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: This northern caatinga element grows on exposed, gravelly river terraces amongst limestone or iron-stained quartzite as well as in seasonally inundated river floodplains within the caatinga. Its generation length is 15 to 20 years.
iucn_doc_threats: Part of the range of this caatinga endemic was flooded as a consequence of the construction of the Sobradinho dam in the 1970s. Other parts suffer the impact of urbanization, agriculture, construction activities, and quarrying of limestone. The plant is also subject to collection.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable based on an estimated past population decline of greater than 30% as a result of the ongoing decline in area and quality of the species habitat.

checklist_taxon: Discocactus biformis Walp. sec. WFO 2019
Discocactus boliviensis Buining & Brederoo sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Brazil; Mato Grosso do Sul
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Persecution/control; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This plant is found in the border area between Brazil and Bolivia. It is only known from two very small sites.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus is found on calcareous rocks surrounded by dense humid forest and a dense humid type of cerrado.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to the species as its sites are difficult to access. The area around the two sites is being explored for cattle ranching, and the forest is already being cut down. As soon as this development reaches the area of the two known subpopulations it could destroy them in a very short time, perhaps less than three years. Moreover, it is expected that the livestock farmers will collect and destroy the cacti in order to avoid injuries to their cattle, thus further accelerating the population declines.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because it has a very restricted area of occupancy (AOO) of 4 to 10 km2, and is known from only two locations. Although there are no major threats affecting the species at present, it is predicted that livestock farming may impact the species in the near future which would likely result in the species becoming Critically Endangered or even Extinct in a very short time period.

checklist_taxon: Discocactus buenekeri W.R.Abraham sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+: accessed 23 Sep. 2022
Discocactus catingicola Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_synonyms: Discocactus heptacanthus (Barb.Rodr.) Britton & Rose ssp. catingicola (Buining & Brederoo) N.P.Taylor & Zappi
iucn_locations: Maranhão; Minas Gerais; Tocantins; Piauí; Bahia; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZE-MA; BZL-MG; BZN-TO; BZE-PI; BZE-BA; BR
iucn_doc_range: Discocactus catingicola is endemic to Brazil, where it is found in southwestern Piauí, western and central-southern Bahia, northwestern Minas Gerais, Maranhão, and Tocantins. It grows at altitudes between 450 and 700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This western cerrado element grows on exposed gravel or sand amongst cerrado vegetation and in the cerrado-caatinga ecotone.
iucn_doc_threats: The species habitat is declining due to large-scale agriculture including soy bean, cotton crops, and Eucalyptus plantations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Discocactus catingicola is one of the most common cacti of its genus. It is widespread and occurs in several protected areas. There are population declines in part of its range as the area is transformed into agricultural land at a very fast rate. Population monitoring is therefore recommended. It is assessed as Least Concern for the moment.

checklist_taxon: Discocactus diersianus Esteves sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Dry
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Persecution/control
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Goiás, Brazil, where it can be found from Caldas Novas in the south up to Goiânia in the north. It occurs at less than 10 locations.
iucn_doc_habitats: The main habitat consists of crystalline rocks surrounded by cerrado vegetation. Generation length is estimated to be 20 years.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats to the species are quarrying of the rocks, cattle ranching, and the removal of plants by farmers so that the cacti do not harm the cattle. Collection for ornamental trade is another threat that has wiped out complete subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Discocactus diersianus occurs in fragmented patches of crystalline rock in Goiás, Brazil. It is found at five to ten locations, which add up to an area of occupancy of less than 500 km2. There has been a population decline of more than 50 percent in the last  60 years (generation length is 20 years), and it is estimated that no more than 5,000 mature individuals remain in total, with the largest subpopulation consisting of less than 1,000 individuals. The main threats to the species are quarrying of the rocks, cattle ranching, and the removal of plants by farmers to avoid cattle getting injured by the cactus spines. Collection for ornamental trade is another threat. These threats are understood, but have not ceased and are, at least in part, not reversible. This cactus is therefore listed as Endangered under criterion A2ac.

checklist_taxon: Discocactus fariae-peresii P.J.Braun sec. ???
Discocactus ferricola Buining & Brederoo sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Brazil; Mato Grosso do Sul
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Savanna - Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species grows in Mutum and San Cyrilio (Bolivia), as well as in Corumbá, Brazil.
iucn_doc_habitats: The plants grow on large, flat outcrops of iron and manganese ore surrounded by cerrado vegetation and forest. Generation length is 10 years.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to the species is mining, especially in Bolivia, and settlements on the sites. The flat rocky areas are cleaned from any remaining vegetation for urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is only found at three small sites in Bolivia and Brazil, and has an area of occupancy of no more than 20 km<sup>2</sup>. The species habitat is degrading due to mining activities and urbanization, which has led to a population decline of 30 percent over the last 30 years. The threats have not ceased, and thus continue causing declines in mature individuals and suitable habitat. It is listed as Endangered under criterion B2ab(iii,v).

checklist_taxon: Discocactus hartmannii (K.Schum.) Britton & Rose sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Paraguay; Goiás; Brazil; Mato Grosso do Sul
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry farming; Agro-industry plantations; Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Brazil and Paraguay. It stretches from the Amambay, Concepción, San Pedro, and Canindeyu departments in Paraguay, into southern Mato Grosso do Sul to southwestern Goiás in Brazil.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs on sandy soils in grasslands that are a very open form of cerrado (campo sujo). Generation length is 10 years.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is agriculture. In the past, small-scale agriculture was destroying the habitat, which has now been replaced with extensive agro-industry farming in the last ten to 20 years, mainly consisting of Eucalyptus plantations, crops for renewable energy and cattle ranching. In particular the former habitat in Mato Grosso do Sul and the border area with Paraguay is completely deforested. In Paraguay, the main threat to the species is livestock ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: In the past Discocactus hartmannii was widespread and common, but now it only occurs in fragmented patches due to severe habitat loss. As a result of deforestation for large-scale agriculture, the populations have declined by an estimated 80% in the last 30 years (generation length is 10 years) and this decline is continuing. It is therefore classified as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Discocactus heptacanthus (Barb.Rodr.) Britton & Rose sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Rondônia; Brazil; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Mato Grosso do Sul; Tocantins; Mato Grosso; Goiás; Pará
iucn_location_codes: BZN-RO; BR; BO; BZC-MS; BZN-TO; BZC-MT; BZC-GO; BZN-PA
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Dry
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Mining & quarrying; Seepage from mining; Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming; Agro-industry farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Brazil and extends into Bolivia. It grows in Mato Grosso, Rondônia, adjacent Bolivia, the northern part of Mato Grosso do Sul, western Goiás and Tocantins, and southern Pará.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in the cerrado, mostly in exposed gravel of pedra canga (iron ore). It is occasionally found in sand or quartz gravel as well as on granite and sandstone outcrops. Generation length is 10 years.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat is deforestation of the species habitat for cattle ranching and renewable energy crops. Mining is another important threat in areas where the species grows on pedra canga. Urbanization is threatening the population in the surroundings of Cuiabá. In the Pocone region, pollution from mining activities is also affecting the plants.
iucn_doc_rationale: Discocactus heptacanthus is widespread in part of Brazil and adjacent Bolivia, but only occurs in fragmented patches. It has suffered from a population decline in the range of 20 to 30 percent over the last 20 years due to large-scale deforestation for agriculture as well as mining and urbanization. The decline is presumed to continue at an even higher rate, as the region is under high pressure from agro-industrial and urban development. It is therefore listed as Near Threatened approaching criteria A2ac+A4ac.

checklist_taxon: Discocactus horstii Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is found in the Serra do Barão in northern Minas Gerais, Brazil. It is known from three sites that can be considered a single location, as one major threat could affect all of them simultaneously. It grows at altitudes between 800 and 1,200 m asl. The extent of occurrence (EOO) is no more than 100 km<sup>2</sup> and the area of occupancy is estimated at 6 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: This southeastern campo rupestre (Grão Mogol) element grows in quartz gravel and sand beneath shrubs in campo rupestre.
iucn_doc_threats: This remarkable Brazilian cactus was heavily collected in the 1970's for the European horticultural market (Buining 1974: 70). In the past mining (or digging) for quartz crystals destroyed large parts of the habitat. There is always the potential for mining to restart in the future.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very restricted range; its extent of occurrence is less  than 100km<sup>2</sup> and its area of occupancy is around 6km<sup>2</sup>, and it is known from three locations. It declined substantially in the past due to collection for the international horticulture trade as well as quartz extraction, but populations are stable at the moment, as the entire population is under strict protection and included in the Parque Nacional de Grão Mogol. However, the threat of mining could potentially return, which would bring the species to the brink of extinction in a very short time. It is assessed as Vulnerable under criterion D2, as its survival depends on the continuing protection of its sites.

checklist_taxon: Discocactus petr-halfarii Zachar sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
Discocactus piscibarbarus N.P.Taylor & Olsthoorn sec. Taylor & al. 2023
Discocactus pseudoinsignis N.P.Taylor & Zappi sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying; Housing & urban areas; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: Discocactus pseudoinsignis is endemic to Brazil. It is found in the municipalities of Cristália, Botumirim and Grão Mogol, in northern Minas Gerais, at altitudes between 700 and 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in southeastern campo rupestre (Grão Mogol) element, in pure quartz sand or sand between arenitic rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is affected by habitat modification, mainly the quarrying of sand and urbanization. In the Botumirim area, it has been severely damaged by fire.
iucn_doc_rationale: Discocactus pseudoinsignis is endemic to northern Minas Gerais, Brazil. It has an extent of occurrence of approximately 1,000 km2 and it is found in two to three locations. More than half of the subpopulations occur in the Parque Estadual de Grão Mogol, but those subpopulations outside of the protected area are declining due to habitat loss from quarrying, urbanization, and fires. The causes of declines are understood, but have not ceased and are, at least in part, not reversible. They are therefore expected to continue reducing the number of mature individuals as well as the extent of occurrence and area of occupancy of this cactus. It is therefore listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Discocactus subnudus Britton & Rose sec. Britton & Rose 1922
Discocactus zehntneri Britton & Rose sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Savanna - Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it is found from central to northern Bahia into southeastern Piauí. It grows at elevations ranging from 400 to 1,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The main habitat for the lowland subpopulations consists of crystalline rock outcrops, while the high altitude subpopulations also grow between sandstone rocks on sand and gravel. This species rarely grows on iron and manganese ore (pedra canga). It is surrounded by caatinga or campo rupestre.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats are quarrying of the rock, sand and iron ore where Discocactus zehntneri grows. Part of the species range was flooded as a consequence of the construction of the Sobradinho dam in the 1970s.
iucn_doc_rationale: Discocactus zehntneri is endemic to Brazil and has a restricted range. Its extent of occurrence is less than 20,000 km2. The subpopulations in the lowland and in some high-altitude localities are declining, while other high-altitude subpopulations occur in a protected area and are stable. The main threat is quarrying of the rock, sand and iron ore on which this cactus thrives, and which is expected to lead to further population declines. The species is therefore listed as Near Threatened approaching B1ab(iii).

iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Montane; Forest - Temperate
iucn_doc_range:
The species is distributed in Guatemala, Honduras, Mexico, Nicaragua, and El Salvador (Hunt et al. 2006), at elvations of 1,600 to 2,700 m asl.
In Mexico the species occurs in the States of Chiapas, Colima, México D.F., Durango, México State, Guerrero, Jalisco, Michoacán, Morelos, Nayarit, Oaxaca, and Sinaloa (Hernández and Godínez 1994).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in moist montane forest and Quercus and Pinus forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat is deforestation, especially logging of the trees on which the species grows.
iucn_doc_rationale: Disocactus speciosus has a very wide range, is abundant, and although there are threats in places, these are not sufficient to warrant any concern. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_range: The distribution of this species in not well known. It possible occurs south of the El Novillo dam. It has been found in Sierra de Mazatán in the state of Sonora, Mexico (Van Devender pers. comm.) and it probably also occurs in adjacent Chihuahua.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in wooded slopes in granitic, humus-filled cavities at 300-500 m (Blum et al. 1998).  In Sierra de Mazatán it occurs in oak woodland and gneiss.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because of the uncertainty on its range, the lack of data on the population trends and threats.

Echinocereus ledingii Peebles sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: E. ledingii is distributed within the United States in southeastern Arizona (Anderson 2001) at elevations of 1,219 to 2,012 m asl. It has been suggested that within the state, the species can be found primarily in the Pinaleño Mountains in Graham County (Fischer 1989) and the Sky Islands mountain ranges (USDA 1995). There is an observation on the Sierra Tigre in northeastern Sinaloa, Mexico that may correspond to this species (Van Devender pers. comm. 2010).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in Chaparral on rocky, gravelly, sandy slopes (Benson 1982).
iucn_doc_threats: Fire poses a major threat, the species grows in chaparral which is very prone to fires.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus ledingii occurs on the top on several mountains. Given the isolation of its populations, the probability of the species being affected by the same threat is low. Furthermore, it is locally abundant and not exposed to any major threat. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_threats: There is no information on the threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is assessed as Data Deficient because the taxonomy is unsolved, it is not certain if it is a subspecies of Echinocereus pacificus or E. polyacanthus.

iucn_doc_threats: The threats for this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is poorly known taxonomically and ecologically, there are no recent collections and therefore there is no information on the population trends to assess it. The area where it occurs has been inaccessible for a long time. Hence is listed as Data Deficient.

iucn_synonyms: Cereus phoeniceus Engelm. & J.M.Bigelow var. pacificus Engelm.
iucn_doc_range: This species is located in Mexico within northern Baja California [Goldman 1951; Anderson 2001 (when listed as a E. polycanthus subspecies)]. It grows at 100 to 2,600 m asl (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in rocky outcrops and in bushy and wooded slopes on sandy granites (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus pacificus is listed as Least Concern because it is frequent and locally common across its range and there are no major threats affecting it.

Echinocereus papillosus Linke ex Rümpler sec. Sánchez & al. 2018
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in the Mexican state of Tamaulipas, and in Texas in the United States, at elevations of 250 to 750 m asl (Hernández et al. 2004, Martínez-Ávalos and Jurado 2005). In south Texas, it grows south of San Antonio and east of Laredo to the coast north of the Rio Grande. In the Rio Grande plain it occurs at low elevations. It has been reported from adjacent northeastern Mexico (Tamaulipas) (Taylor 1985).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in grasslands and thorn shrublands. It is known from low-altitude brushlands in south Texas, where it grows in fine sandy loam overlying limestone (Taylor 1985). The substrate is well-drained and climate is subtropical (Taylor 1985). Blum et al. (1998) reported it from grassy brush desert in sandy loamy, red gravel and limestone soil.
iucn_doc_threats: Some subpopulations are threatened by urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: The range of Echinocereus papillosus is not very large, but it is abundant and although there are threats in places these are not sufficient to warrant any concern. Also, the species occurs within private properties that very few people have access to and is therefore well protected. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_common_name: Brownspine Hedgehog Cactus; Órgano-pequeño Chiso; Rusty Hedgehog Cactus
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Chihuahua, Mexico and Texas, United States, at elevations of 700 to 2,700 m (Blum et al. 1998, Hunt et al. 2006, Powell et al. 2008).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in grasslands and desert scrub. It prefers igneous soils, but also occurs on limestone and novaculite (Powell and Weedin 2004). It occurs in  stony slopes and in mountainous woodland on mineral igneous soil and granite rubble (Blum et al. 1998).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for the species, however, land use change is affecting some subpopulations.
iucn_doc_rationale: The range of Echinocereus russanthus is not very large, but it is abundant and although there are threats in places a significant part of the species range is protected. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is poorly known taxonomically and ecologically, there are no  recent collections and therefore there is no information on the  population trends to assess it. The area where it occurs has been  inaccessible for a long time. Hence it is listed as Data Deficient.

iucn_common_name: Green Pitaya; Nylon Hedgehog Cactus; Green-flower Hedgehog Cactus
iucn_locations: Oklahoma; Coahuila; Chihuahua; South Dakota; United States; Colorado; Texas; New Mexico; Mexico; Kansas; Wyoming
iucn_location_codes: OKL-OO; MXE-CO; MXE-CU; SDA-OO; US; COL-OO; TEX-OO; NWM-OO; MX; KAN-OO; WYO-OO

iucn_doc_range:
This species occurs in Kansas, New Mexico, Oklahoma, South Dakota, Texas and Wyoming (United States). Scientific literature also reports it from the Mexican states of Chihuahua and Coahuila, and Colorado in the United States (Hernández et al. 2004).
It occurs farther north than any other member of the genus, into the cooler regions of the Great Plains grassland, as far as South Dakota and to the Mountainous areas of New Mexico and central Colorado. It grows at elevations of 900 to 2,700 m asl (Taylor 1985).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in grasslands, mountain parklands and pinyon and juniper woodland in gravelly soils of foothills, plains, and washes in grassland or along edges of woodlands. Blum et al. (1998) report it from meadows to open bush-ground and shrub desert, in loamy- sandy soil.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Echinocereus viridiflorus is wide-ranging, abundant, and there are no major threats. Furthermore, it occurs in many protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in desert scrub, very close to the sea between rocks.

iucn_doc_rationale: The species has an extent of occurrence of 4,000 km<sup>2</sup>, and occurs in only one location, and the whole area is threatened by tourism development which is causing declines in the quality of the habitat and number of mature individuals.

iucn_doc_range: This species occurs from Nicaragua southwards to Panama. In Costa Rica it is recorded in Alajuela and San José, and from Panama in Bocas del Toro (Hunt et al. 2006). It has been recorded at elevations from sea level to 1,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits the lowland Atlantic wet forests (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat is habitat destruction due to human activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Epiphyllum grandilobum has a very wide range (the extent of occurrence is much larger than 20,000 km²), the actual area occupied is much smaller, and although there are no figures available it probably comes close to the 2,000 km² threshold for Vulnerable. The population is severely fragmented, and there is continuing decline due to habitat loss. Hence, it is listed as Near Threatened as it almost qualifies for a threatened listing under criterion B2ab(iii).

Eriosyce megliolii (Rausch) Ferryman sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in San Juan at elevations of 500 to 700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in monte shrubland, in rocky soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats for the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Eriosyce megliolii is listed as Least Concern because, even though the species has a small range and is not particularly abundant, there are no present or potential threats and the population is healthy.

Eriosyce spinosior (F.Ritter) P.C.Guerrero & Helmut Walter sec. Guerrero & al. 2020

Escobaria boregui Schmoll ex Shurly sec. WFO 2019

Espostoa humboldtii Backeb. sec. WFO 2019
Eulychnia saint-pieana F.Ritter sec. Larridon & al. 2018
Eulychnocactus bolivianus Backeb. sec. Korotkova 2021
Facheiroa bahiensis (Britton & Rose) N.P.Taylor sec. Romeiro-Brito & al. 2023
Facheiroa bragaia N.P.Taylor sec. Romeiro-Brito & al. 2023
Facheiroa cephaliomelana Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_common_name: Xique Xique Amarelo; Facheiro da Serra
iucn_synonyms: Facheiroa cephaliomelana (P.J.Braun) N.P.Taylor & Zappi subsp. cephaliomelana; Facheiroa tenebrosa P.J.Braun & Esteves
iucn_habitat_names: Savanna - Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is found at 550 -750 m asl in south-central and south-western Bahia and central-northern Minas Gerais, Brazil (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus is a southern Rio São Francisco caatinga element, it also occurs in cerrado where it grows on outcrops of raised Bambuí limestone (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for the species is mining for cement, the outcrops where it grows get destroyed.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though the species has a relatively large extent of occurrence (EOO just under 20,000 km2 ), it probably has an area of occupancy of less than 2,000 km2, and it only grows on some of the limestone outcrops which occur as islands within the caatinga and cerrado, and because of this the population is naturally severely fragmented. These outcrops are mined causing a continuing decline in the population, and this is most likely to continue. Hence, the species is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Facheiroa markgrafii (Backeb. & Voll) N.P.Taylor sec. Romeiro-Brito & al. 2023
Facheiroa phaeacantha (Gürke) N.P.Taylor sec. Romeiro-Brito & al. 2023
Facheiroa squamosa (Gürke) P.J.Braun & Esteves sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the core area within north-eastern Brazil in Piauí, Pernambuco and Bahia at elevations of 390 -1,250 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Locally co-dominant with other arborescent cacti on non-calcareous (mostly granite/gneiss) inselbergs or lajedos and in very stony ground of the caatinga (rarely on arenitic rocks in cerrado or at the margins of campo ruprestre (Taylor and Zappi 2004). The species usually grows on sloping grounds, rarely on flat lands.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is indirectly threatened by fires produced for agriculture, this mainly affects the establishment of seedlings and nurse plants.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide extent of occurrence, it grows in areas adjacent to agriculture which  are not sought after for been steep, it occurs within a protected area and the population is stable, hence is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus acanthodes (Lem.) Britton & Rose sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003 (sub Ferocactus cylindraceus)
Ferocactus californicus (Monv. ex Labour.) Borg sec. Hunt 2016
Ferocactus chrysacanthus (Orcutt) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Baja California; Baja California Sur; Mexico
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in the states of Baja California (Isla Cedros) and Baja California Sur (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of 1 - 500 m asl (Pilbeam and Bowdery 1995).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in coastal scrub near sea level, up to 500 m asl amongst pines and in a dry riverbed (Pilbeam and Bowdery 1995).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is urban development in the coastal area. Fish camps of abalone and lobster which consist of informal settlements of fishermen leave a lot of rubbish behind and they also open access paths.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has an extent of occurrence of approximately 4,000 km<sup>2</sup>, and its population is severely fragmented as the result of the settlement of fishermen and their activities, which is also causing a continuing decline that will not cease in the near future. Even though it occurs within protected areas, there are activities taking place that affects the species habitat.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus diguetii (F.A.C.Weber) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in Baja California Sur, Mexico, on the islands of Catalana, Cerralvo, Coronados, Carmen, Danzante and Montserrat in the Gulf of California, at elevations of 10 to 300 m asl (Pilbeam and Bowdery 1995).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in desert scrub on granite soil.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats for Ferocactus diguetii.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ferocactus diguetii has a restricted range (extent of occurrence is less than 2,000 km2), but the whole range falls within protected areas and the species is difficult to extract given that it occurs in islands. There are no major threats, hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus emoryi (Engelm.) Orcutt sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_common_name: Biznaga-barril de Emory; Emory's Barrel Cactus
iucn_locations: United States; Arizona; Baja California Sur; Sonora; Mexico
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Medicine - human & veterinary; Food - human; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Arizona (United States), Sonora, and Baja California Sur (Mexico), at elevations of 0-1,200m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in the Sonoran Desert (Paredes et al. 2000), in rocky hills and sandy or rocky flats (including wash margins, alluvial fans, and mesas), in the Arizona Desert and the upper edge of the Colorado Desert. Rocks are often limestone with a black patina or black lava or basalt.
iucn_doc_threats: Land conversion is a concern in the lowlands, but the species also occurs at high elevations where this is not the case.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ferocactus emoryi is widespread, with no evidence of systematic decline, and occurs in several protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus gracilis H.E.Gates sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Baja California Sur; Baja California; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: Ferocactus gracilis is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Baja California and Baja California Sur (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 20 to 300 m asl (Pilbeam and Bowdery 2005).
iucn_doc_habitats: Ferocactus gracilis grows in Sonoran desert scrub on a variety of soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The only threats are around Bahía de los Angeles due to urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ferocactus gracilis has a wide range (extent of occurrence over 20,000 km2). It is abundant, occurs in several protected areas, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus johnstonianus Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to the Mexican state of Baja California (Hunt et al. 2006), it occurs in Isla Angel de la Guarda at 1 to 300 m of altitude (Pilbeam and Bowdery 1995).
iucn_doc_habitats: According to Pilbeam and Bowdery (2005), the species occurs in a wash behind a lagoon on the east side of Isla Angel de la Guarda and high in the mountains, near the highest peak on south facing slopes rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to Ferocactus johnstonianus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ferocactus johnstonianus has a restricted range, however all of it is protected and it has no threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus lindsayi Bravo sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: Ferocactus lindsayi is endemic to the Mexican state of Michoacán, it occurs at elevations of 250 to 300 m asl. The known range of this species is small, but further studies are needed to establish the extent.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on steep rocky slopes (Taylor 1987, Pilbeam and Bowdery 2005) in low dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The construction of the Infiernillo dam in 1965 might have affected the species, however, the known locality was visited in 1973 by McCarten (Pilbeam and Bowdery 2005). No other threats were detected when N. Taylor visited the population in 1987 (N. Taylor pers. comm. 2012). It is mentioned by Pilbeam and Bowdery (2005) that a road was built in the area, but they observed healthy populations; however, it is unknown if the species was affected by it. The population has not been visited recently, hence no current information on threats exists.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ferocactus lindsayi is listed as Least Concern because even though the range is probably small, the population has been stable and has survived several major threats since the first report of it being visited in 1973 until it was last visited in 2003 by Pilbeam and Bowdery. It grows on steep slopes that are very difficult to access and it is a species not sought after by illegal collectors. However, more studies on its range, population size, trend and threats are needed in order to better assess its conservation status.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus macrodiscus (Mart.) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: The species is distributed sporadically within the known range. The species is endemic to Mexico, and is distributed  in the states of Guanajuato and Oaxaca (Hernández et al. 2004), and has a disjunct distribution. Anderson (2001) and Arias et al. (1997) report it also from from Querétaro and San Luis Potosí. It has a relatively restricted distribution, known from no more than five localities (Hernández and Godínez 1994; Hernández et al. 2010). The locality in Querétaro needs to be confirmed (H. Hernández, C. Gomez-Hinostrosa and M. Cházaro pers. comm. 2009). The species occurs at elevations of  2,300 - 2,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: It occurs in dry grasslands and oak (Quercus) forest. It flowers between April and July (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: The primary threat to this species is the destruction of habitat from the conversion of grasslands and oak forests to subsistence mixed agriculture and cattle ranching. Some subpopulations are in a steep decline.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered to be Vulnerable due to a limited geographic range (EOO <20,000 km<sup>2</sup>), it is known from less than 10 locations, and is declining in area of occupancy due to habitat reduction. This species is very habitat specific and only occurs sporadically within the extent of occurrence.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus mathssonii (Berge ex K.Schum.) N.P.Taylor sec. Hunt 2016
Ferocactus peninsulae (F.A.C.Weber) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_synonyms: Ferocactus townsendianus Britton & Rose; Ferocactus peninsulae (FA.C.Weber) Britton & Rose var. santa-maria (Britton & Rose) N.P.Taylor; Ferocactus peninsulae (FA.C.Weber) Britton & Rose var. townsendianus (Britton & Rose) N.P.Taylor; Ferocactus santa-maria Britton & Rose; Echinocactus peninsulae FA.C.Weber
iucn_locations: Baja California; Baja California Sur; Mexico
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Housing & urban areas; Other ecosystem modifications
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Baja California and Baja California Sur (Hunt et al. 2006) from sea level to 450 m asl (Pilbeam and Bowdery 1995).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in rocky places, in sandy areas and hillsides (Pilbeam and Bowdery 1995).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat is illegal collection. It is also used as fodder for cattle, and there is habitat destruction due to urbanization and creation of artificial grasslands.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ferocactus peninsulae is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range (extent of occurrence over 50,000 km2), and even though there are localized threats throughout its range, these are not sufficient to trigger a threatened listing. Illegal collection is the main concern, if it continues at the same rate it could affect the population. Hence ongoing population monitoring is urgently needed.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus piliferus (Lem.) G.Unger sec. Hunt 2006
Ferocactus pottsii (Salm-Dyck) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: Species occurs in México in the states of Chihuahua, Sinaloa and Sonora (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations from 240 to 1,000 m (Paredes et al. 2000)
iucn_doc_habitats: Ferocactus pottsii  occurs  in tropical deciduous forest from 240 to 1,000  (Paredes et al. 2000). Pilbeam and Bowdery (1995) reported in tropical deciduous forest up to the pine forest, in Sierra Canela, Chihuahua.
iucn_doc_threats: Clearing tropical deciduous forest to plant buffelgrass  (Penisetum ciliaris).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Near Threatened because it is known from only three locations and there are ongoing threats that may affect it. If existing and projected threat trends continue this species could soon qualify for Vulnerable using either criteria B or D. Further research and monitoring are needed to better quantify threats and project future population trends.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus schwarzii G.E.Linds. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Sinaloa (Hunt et al. 2006)  and occurs at 30 to 200 m (Pilbeam and Bowdery 1995).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species probably grows in xerophyllous scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats for this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is poorly known taxonomically and ecologically. It  has not been collected recently and the information on its distribution  is uncertain and on population trends unknown. Hence is listed as Data  Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus tiburonensis (G.E.Linds.) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_synonyms: Ferocactus wislizeni (Engelm.) Britton & Rose var. tiburonensis G.E.Linds.
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Industrial aquaculture; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Named species
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs on Tiburón Island in the Gulf of California, and in the state of Sonora (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 0 to 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The island subpopulation is mostly on steep rocky slopes, extending onto upper alluvial fans. The continental subpopulation is extremely limited occurring only on sandy semi-stabilized dunes and sandy salt flat margins.
iucn_doc_threats: The insular subpopulation is subject to heavy herbivory by introduced desert Bighorn Sheep. The continental subpopulation is extremely limited and influenced by road expansion, cattle grazing and shrimp farms.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because of its small range (extent of occurrence between 6,000 - 8,000 km<sup>2</sup>), fragmented population, and because the insular subpopulation has experienced past declines that are projected to continue and the continental subpopulation is declining rapidly.

checklist_taxon: Ferocactus townsendianus Britton & Rose sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
Ferocactus viridescens (Torr. & A.Gray) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_common_name: Coast Barrel Cactus; Biznaga-barril Verdosa; San Diego Barrel Cactus
iucn_synonyms: Echinocactus viridescens Nutt. ex Torr. & A.Gray
iucn_locations: United States; Mexico; California; Baja California
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in Mexico, in the state of Baja California, and in the United States in the state of California (Hunt et al. 2006) at 10 to 1,000 m asl (Pilbeam and Bowdery 2005).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in shrubland/chaparral, grassland/herbaceous in sandy or gravelly soils of hillsides in the chaparral. Pilbeam and Bowdery (2005) reported it from rocky hills or sandy flat areas with grass, near the coast and in chaparral inland, in seacoast bluffs and on rocky bluffs.
iucn_doc_threats: Urban expansion on part of its range in the United States and Baja California, Mexico (close to Tijuana) have fragmented and extirpated the species from a number of locations. This dimishes southwards, however agriculture affects this portion of the range at a smaller scale.
iucn_doc_rationale: Ferocactus viridescens has a wide range and, even though there is some decrease in the population, this is not sufficient to warrant listing in a threatened category. Hence, this cactus is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Frailea alexandri Metzing sec. Hunt 2016
Frailea altigibbera P.J.Braun & Esteves sec. Korotkova 2021
Frailea amerhauseri Prestlé sec. Metzing 2021
Frailea buenekeri W.R.Abraham sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Named species; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil, where it occurs at an altitude of 200 m asl (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky outcrops and stony fields in pampa grasslands. Its habitat has a very high potential to be used for agriculture.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats to this cactus are grazing (cattle trampling), agriculture, forestry of Eucalyptus for paper industry, invasive African grasses and fire. Human-induced fires used to be more frequent in the past, and even though they are decreasing in frequency they still are a threat to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Frailea buenekeri has a small range, with an extent of occurrence of less than 5,000 km2. Its population is naturally severely fragmented, the species is not abundant and does not occur in any protected area. Furthermore, several threats are affecting this cactus and the area where it occurs, such as agriculture, forestry, cattle ranching, fire, and invasive grasses. As a consequence, there is a reduction in the number of mature individuals and habitat quality and quantity. Hence, it is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Frailea castanea Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Brazil; Rio Grande do Sul; Uruguay
iucn_threat_names: Trend Unknown/Unrecorded; Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder farming; Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul) and Uruguay, at elevations from 150 to 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The cactus grows on rocky outcrops and stony fields in pampa grasslands, a habitat that has very high potential to be used for agriculture.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats to this species are grazing and cattle trampling, agriculture, forestry, fire and collection, but not in all of its range.
iucn_doc_rationale: There are no major conservation problems for Frailea castanea in Uruguay (more than 50% of the species range occurs in Uruguay), where the species is abundant. However, the subpopulation in Brazil has suffered declines due to a range of threats, such as agriculture, farming, fires, and collection. Furthermore, the species does not seem to occur in any protected area. The species as a whole is here listed as Least Concern, however, a regional assessment in Brazil is necessary, as the species would probably qualify under a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Frailea cataphracta (Dams) Britton & Rose sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Mato Grosso do Sul; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Paraguay; Brazil; Rio Grande do Sul
iucn_threat_names: Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Small-holder farming; Named species; Agro-industry farming; Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in in Mato Grosso do Sul and Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil, and in Paraguay. It may occur in Bolivia. In Brazil the species occurs at elevations up to 100 m asl.  In Paraguay, it is known from only a single record from one site.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on rocky outcrops and stoney fields in pampa grasslands, a habitat that has very high potential to be used for agriculture.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats to this cactus are trampling by grazing cattle, land use change due to agriculture, Eucalyptus plantations, and also invasive grasses and fire.
iucn_doc_rationale: Very little is known about the population status and threats of Frailea cataphracta in Paraguay. In the Brazilian portion of its range, the subpopulations are decreasing due to anthropogenic activities. Furthermore, it does not occur in any protected areas. It has an estimated extent of occurrence of 22,000 km2, and given that at least in one of the known subpopulation decline is certain Frailea cataphracta is listed as Near Threatened.

checklist_taxon: Frailea chiquitana Cárdenas sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic from Bolivia occurring in Santa Cruz in Chiquitos, near Cerro San Miserato, Santiago at 900m (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in subtropical dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: There is no available information on the threats affecting this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The information needed to evaluate the conservation status of this species, such as population size, trends and threats, is not available. Hence, the species is listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Frailea curvispina Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Named species; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Rio Grande do Sul (Hunt et al. 2006), at an altitude of approximately 200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops and stoney fields in pampa grasslands. This habitat has very high potential to be used for agriculture. It also occurs in subtropical shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are grazing by cattle trampling, agriculture, forestry of Eucalyptus, invasive grasses, fire, and sand deposits resulting from desertification process.
iucn_doc_rationale: Frailea curvispina has a small range (extent of occurrence is 520 km2) and its population is severely fragmented. The area where it occurs is under several pressures that have caused population reductions. The threats will not cease in the near future. Hence, this cactus is listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(iii,v).

checklist_taxon: Frailea diersiana Schädlich sec. Schädlich 2020
Frailea erythracantha R.Pontes, A.S.Oliveira & Deble sec. Pontes & al. 2018
Frailea fulviseta Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Named species; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: Frailea fulviseta is endemic from Brazil, where it is reported in Rio Grande do Sul, at elevations of up to 100 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky outcrops and stony fields in pampas grasslands, this habitat has very high potential to be used for agriculture.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are trampling by grazing cattle, land conversion for agriculture and forestry of Eucalyptus. Also, invasive grasses and fires.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a small range (extent of occurrence of 700 km<sup>2</sup>)<sup> </sup>and a severely fragmented population. The area where it occurs is under several pressures that have caused population reductions and the threats will not cease in the near future. Hence is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Frailea gracillima (Lem.) Britton & Rose sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Rio Grande do Sul; Uruguay; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Grassland - Temperate
iucn_threat_names: Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Housing & urban areas; Named species; Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This cactus occurs in Rio Grande do Sul (Brazil) and Uruguay (Hunt et al. 2006). It can be found at elevations of up to 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops and stoney fields in pampa grasslands, a habitat that has a very high potential to be used for agriculture.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are trampling by grazing cattle, agriculture, forestry of Eucalyptus, invasive grasses, fire and urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though Frailea gracillima has a wide range (extent of occurrence of 100,000 km2), it is sparsely distributed and a variety of threats have led to significant population declines in the range of 40% in the last 20 years (generation length is seven years). The reasons for these declines are understood, but they have not ceased and are not reversible. Hence, the species is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Frailea larae R.Vásquez sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
Frailea mammifera Buining & Brederoo sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Named species; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil, at elevations up to 100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops and stoney fields in pampa grasslands. This habitat has very high potential to be used for agriculture.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are grazing by cattle trampling, agriculture, forestry of Eucalyptus, invasive grasses and fire.
iucn_doc_rationale: Frailea mammifera has a small range (extent of occurrence is 1,800 km2) and its population is severely fragmented. The area where it occurs is under several pressures that have caused population reductions and these will not cease in the near future. Hence, it is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Frailea phaeodisca (Speg.) Backeb. & F.M.Knuth sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_synonyms: Echinocactus pygmaeus Speg. var. phaeodiscus Speg.
iucn_locations: Brazil; Uruguay; Rio Grande do Sul
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Grassland - Temperate
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Named species
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul) and Uruguay (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations up to 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops and stony fields in pampa grasslands, a habitat that has a very high potential to be used for cattle ranching and agriculture.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats to this cactus are grazing and cattle trampling, agriculture, Eucalyptus forestry, invasive grasses and fire.
iucn_doc_rationale: Frailea phaeodisca has a relatively large range of approximately 80,000 km2, but it is not abundant and many places where the species was once found have suffered a complete conversion of the land. It is estimated that 40% of the population has disappeared in the last 20 years due to threats that are known, but are not reversible and have not ceased. The generation length is seven years. Hence, it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Frailea pygmaea (Speg.) Britton & Rose sec. Kiesling & al. 2014
iucn_locations: Argentina; Uruguay; Rio Grande do Sul; Entre Ríos; Brazil
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Named species; Trend Unknown/Unrecorded
iucn_doc_range: This species is marginally distributed in Argentina (Entre Ríos) and Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul), and in all of Uruguay. It occurs at elevations between 0 and 400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops and stony fields in pampa grasslands, a habitat that has a very high potential to be used for agriculture.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats to this species are grazing and cattle trampling, agriculture, forestry of Eucalyptus, invasive grasses and fire.
iucn_doc_rationale: Frailea pygmaea is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is locally abundant, is present in several protected areas and is not used. However, the subpopualtions in Brazil are under great pressure and are likely to qualify for a threatened category.

Grusonia robertsii Rebman sec. Hernández & al. 2014
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Vizcaíno Desert of Baja California Sur, Mexico, at elevations of 120 to 240 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in Vizcaíno Desert mixed with succulent desert scrub on sandy soils on flat plains and the lower parts of mountains of volcanic origin (Rebman 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The threats for this species are unknown, but probably non existing.
iucn_doc_rationale: Grusonia robertsii was only fairly recently described (Rebman 2006). At the time of description it was only known from two separate localities within a protected area. Only a few individuals have been recorded so far, so the population size as currently known is less than 50 mature individuals. The species therefore potentially qualifies for Critically Endangered under criterion D. However, it may well be more common as the area is largely unexplored due to its inaccessibility (only a single access road with no trails). Given that this is a relatively new species and little is known about the species itself or the threats it may be facing, it is listed at this stage as Data Deficient.

iucn_locations: Paraguay; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Brazil
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This cactus occurs in Bolivia, Brazil and in Paraguay. In the latter country, it can be found in the Concepción department, in a region known as "infierno gris" (Pin and Simon 2004). It occurs at elevations of up to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in xerophyllous forests and rarely occurs on rocks in hills and in sandy mountain regions (Pin and Simon 2004)
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species. However, subpopulations occur near populated areas and roads, and thus may be affected by human sprawl in the near future.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium anisitsii has a fragmented population spread over a large area (extent of occurrence = 25,000 km2), where it is very likely that more popualtion occur. There are no major threats affecting it at present and the populations seem to be stable. There fore, the species is here listed as Least Concern. Monitoring is recommended as  part of the species range might be affected by urbanization and road building in the future. .

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium baldianum (Speg.) Speg. sec. Charles 2009
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Argentina in Catamarca, in Sierra Ancasti, Sierra Graciana, Sierra de Narvaes, Sierra de Guayamba, Sierra de Manchao and on the mountains east of Andalgalá. It grows at elevations of 500 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This high-altitude plant is often found among grass (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: Local collection is a major threat for the species (see Use and Trade). Fire affects the species in some areas.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium baldianum has a relatively restricted range and is collected in parts of it. However, it has a continuous distribution and is not fragmented. Collection can pose a major threat and may need attention, but at the moment the species is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in eastern Santa Cruz, at 800-1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows among grass, under bushes or ferns in the Bolivian chaco vegetation (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: There is no information about threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a small range and nothing is known about the population size, trend or threats. It is very likely that this species should be listed as Least Concern, however, given the lack of information on the existing threats and other factors it is listed as Data Deficient for now pending the collection of additional information to resolve its status.

iucn_doc_habitats:
The species grows in monte shrubland and patagonian steppe (estepa patagónica).
G. gibbosum likes the sandy or gravelly alluvial soil along the Río Negro and Río Colorado, where it grows under bushes and other plants (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats affecting this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium gibbosum has a wide range, is abundant, there are no major threats, and it occurs in protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species are natural or accidental fires.

iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Roads & railroads
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in Catamarca at elevations of 1,700 - 1,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_threats: A potential threat is the improvement of a nearby road. The species has been recently described and will soon be sought after by collectors, hence illegal collection is a potential and highly feasible threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though the population is stable at the moment, Gymnocalycium marianae is known from only a single location and the estimated population size is of ca 500 individuals. It is very likely that the population will suffer from illegal collection in the near future, and the improvement of a nearby road is already planned. Hence, the species is listed as Vulnerable.

Gymnocalycium neuhuberi H.Till & W.Till sec. Charles 2009
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in San Luis at 1,300 m asl (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in high altitude grasslands. It grows in a place described as a patch of barren granite and scree above the tree line.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is illegal collection, and this activity will not cease in the near future (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_rationale: Illegal collection is depleting the only known population of Gymnocalycium neuhuberi, which has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence less than 100 km2, considered as one location). This threat is causing a continuing decline in the number of mature individuals and hence the species is listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium ochoterenae Backeb. sec. Charles 2009
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in Córdoba and San Luis (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 500 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in chaco forest associated to mountainous soils. The species is tolerant to disturbance.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats affecting this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium ochoterenae has a relatively wide range, is abundant, there are no major threats, and it occurs in protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_locations: Tucumán; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Salta; Jujuy; Paraguay; Argentina
iucn_location_codes: AGW-TU; BO; AGW-SA; AGW-JU; PY; AR
iucn_doc_range: This wide-ranging species occurs in Argentina (Salta, Jujuy and Tucumán), Bolivia (Cochabamba, Chuquisaca, Potosí, Santa Cruz and Tarija) and Paraguay (Alto Paraguay and Boquerón). It can be found at elevations of 500 to 2,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus thrives in the sotobosque, at the border and clearings of the dry Chaco forest. It grows semi-buried in sandy soil, in groups of several individuals (Pin and Simon 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: Subsistence farming, agriculture and goat grazing are minor threats to the species in parts of its wide range.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium pflanzii has a wide range, is abundant and the threats that affect part of its range are minor. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium ragonesei A.Cast. sec. Zuloaga & al. 2019
iucn_habitat_names: Wetlands (inland) - Seasonal/Intermittent Saline, Brackish or Alkaline Marshes/Pools
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in Catamarca at elevations of 100 to 200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows semi-buried at the edge of saline grasslands. The concentration of salts is high in the surface of the ground, but it decreases deeper down where the roots grow (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is illegal collection. The species grows next to a road, hence access to individuals is easy.
iucn_doc_rationale: Illegal collection is depleting the only known population of this species that grows next to a road, and it has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence is less than 100 km2). This threat not only leads to a continuing decline in mature individuals, but also in the extent of occurrence and area of occupancy in the portions of the population more severely affected by collection. Hence, Gymnocalycium ragonesi is listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium reductum (Link) Pfeiff. ex Mittler sec. Charles 2009
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in Buenos Aires and La Pampa at 500 m of altitude (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species can be found growing in grasslands on rocky places in Sierra de la Ventana and nearby hills in the south of Buenos Aires. It also grows in organic material among rocks in Sierra de Tandil. Plants often grow in the shelter of bushes (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: The plant grows on hills in an area where there is increasing human activity and animal grazing (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium reductum is listed as Least Concern because it has a relatively wide range and occurs in protected areas. There are threats in parts of its range but these are not sufficient to trigger a threatened listing.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in the provinces of Catamarca, Córdoba, La Rioja, Mendoza, Salta, Santiago del Estero, San Juan, San Luis and Tucumán from 100 to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in flat, sandy or silty areas away from rocks and steep slopes, but often in the shade of shrubs (Charles 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: In the northeastern part of its range it may be affected by agricultural activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalucium schickendantzii is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is locally common, is present  protected areas and threats, if any, are localised.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium schroederianum Osten sec. Charles 2009
iucn_locations: Buenos Aires; Santa Fé; Corrientes; Entre Ríos; Argentina; Uruguay
iucn_location_codes: AGE-BA; AGE-SF; AGE-CN; AGE-ER; AR; UY
iucn_doc_range: According to Hunt et al. (2006) this species occurs in Argentina in  Buenos Aires, Corrientes, Santa Fe and Entre Ríos, and in Uruguay in Río Negro. Charles (2009) reports it for the same provinces in Argentina, but not for Uruguay. It occurs at elevations between 100 and 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in low open forest on white or pinkish coloured clayey soil that frequently becomes flooded. It occurs near the tops of the range of hills by the town of Olavarría, the Sierra Bayas and other nearby Sierras in the south of Buenos Aires province. It also grows in open, flat land, at the edge of woodland, or under bushes, sometimes in grass and with moss (Charles 2009). Kiesling and Ferrari (2009) report that it grows in frequently flooded, clayey soil.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat to this species may be related to habitat loss. However, because it grows in soils that are not suitable for agriculture or grazing, it is not highly impacted. The southern portion of the range of this species may be more vulnerable given that there are quarrying activities in the area and this location is small and very restricted.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium schroederianum is listed as Least Concern because it is relatively widespread, is locally common and abundant, and there are no major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium spegazzinii Britton & Rose sec. Demaio & al. 2011
iucn_locations: Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Argentina; Salta; Tucumán; Catamarca
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in two isolated subpopulations, one in Argentina (provinces of Salta, Tucumán and Catamarca) and the other in Bolivia (Tarija). It grows from 1,500 to 3,000 m asl. The population in Bolivia deserves special attention.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in shrublands, on sandy and rocky soils (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is the expansion of agricultural land and cattle ranching, the animals eat and trample on the plants. Illegal collection poses a threat to a lesser extent.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium spegazzinii is listed as Least Concern, because its extent of occurrence is over 20, 000 km2, the species is often locally abundant and the threats are not sufficient to trigger a threatened listing.

checklist_taxon: Gymnocalycium taningaense Piltz sec. Charles 2009
iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Argentina, where it has been reported from Córdoba (Charles 2009). It grows at elevations of 500 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in chaco forest.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats affecting this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium taningaense is listed as Least Concern because, even though the species range is not wide (extent of occurrence is 2,800 km2), it is abundant and there are no major threats.

iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Named species; Agro-industry plantations; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species has been reported from Uruguay and Rio Grande do Sul (Brazil), at elevations of up to 200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on rocky outcrops in pampa grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are trampling from grazing cattle, agriculture, forestry of Eucalyptus, invasive grasses and fire.
iucn_doc_rationale: Gymnocalycium uruguayense has a relatively large range, but many subpopulations throughout its range have been affected by anthropogenic pressures. A decline of 40% in the last ten years has been estimated based on direct observation and a decline in habitat quality and quantity. The threats are understood, but they are not reversible and will not cease in the near future. Hence, it is listed as Vulnerable.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the core area within north-eastern Brazil in Piauí, Ceará Paraíba, Pernambuco, Alagoas, Sergipe, Bahia and Minas Gerais. It grows at elevations of 50 -700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus species is common along road sides, often in farm hedges, amongst semi-open vegetation or  scrambling over rocks (inselberg) in caatinga agreste, especially on soils containing clay (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this plant are habitat loss due to agriculture and cattle ranching. However, the species can establish in disturbed areas.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a wide range and is frequently abundant, it is tolerant to disturbance and occurs within protected areas, hence is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_locations: Argentina; Paraguay; Mato Grosso do Sul; Bolivia, Plurinational States of; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Savanna - Dry; Savanna - Moist; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Artificial/Terrestrial - Subtropical/Tropical Heavily Degraded Former Forest
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in  Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil (Mato Grosso do Sul) and Paraguay (Hunt et al.  2006), at elevations from 0-1,000m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species inhabits a diversity of habitats such as xerophyllous forests dominated by species from the Chaco, semi-deciduous forest dominated by species typical of seasonally dry Neotropical forests, savanna dominated by species of the genus Prosopis and in disturbed areas.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat for the species is land use change due to both agro-industrial plantations and small holder agriculture. This causes population declines and loss of genetic diversity, however this is somewhat counteract by the successful colonization of this species in disturbed areas.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a very wide range, is abundant and even though in parts of its range is affected by threats overall the population is stable, it also occurs in may protected areas though out its range and can grow in disturbed areas. Hence is listed as Least Concern.
