iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Housing & urban areas; Agro-industry farming
iucn_doc_range: Harrisia eriophora is endemic to Cuba (Hunt et al. 2006), where it is widespread. It occurs from sea level to 100 m in elevation (N. Taylor and A. Franck pers. comm. 2011). Its extent of occurrence is 110,000 km2.  The number of locations is likely to be in the hundreds. Each of the subpopulations likely has 5 to 15 individuals. It is also cultivated for its edible fruit.
iucn_doc_habitats: Harrisia eriophora is probably visited by hawkmoths, although Fleming et al. (2009) indicate it is bat pollinated. This species lives in dry shrublands. The species habitat is very fragmented and degraded.
iucn_doc_threats: Land use change for agriculture and urbanization is affecting populations (Rifá et al. 2005, N. Taylor pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_rationale: Harrisia eriophora is assessed as Least Concern because it is widespread in Cuba and current threats are not known to be detrimental to the species.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Galápagos Islands (Ecuador), where it is widely distributed on six islands and some islets (Bartolomé, Fernandina, Floreana, Isabela, San Cristóbal, Santa Cruz, Santa María and Santiago). It grows from sea level up to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: A large succulent tree found in dry forest. It flowers at night and is pollinated by bats.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is affected in part of its range by predation by invasive goats but staff at the Charles Darwin Foundation are working to eradicate the invasives.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is wide ranging and protected. The only threat affecting the population (invasive goats) is localized and will hopefully be removed in the near future. Therefore Jasminocereus thouarsii is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to L. rupicola.
iucn_doc_rationale: Lasiocereus rupicola only occurs in a restricted area (its extent of occurrence is 500 km2) and it is rarely found. The species is listed as Least Concern because it currently does not face any threats. Monitoring is advisable because any negative impact might severely jeopardize its survival.

iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Storms & flooding; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Tourism & recreation areas; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: Leptocereus grantianus is a nearly spineless cactus, endemic to Culebra, an island off the northeast coast of Puerto Rico (USFWS 1994). There is only one known population of this species. It occurs at elevations between sea level and 10 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The one known population of Leptocereus grantianus occurs in dry thickets on rocky, steep slopes adjacent to a narrow beach near Punta Melones, on the southwestern part of the island of Culebra, off the northeastern coast of Puerto Rico. Associated species on this rocky slope are almácigo (Bursera simaruba), úcar (Bucida buceras), sea grape (Coccoloba uvifera), sebucán (Pilosocereus royenii), and mesquite (Prosopis pallida). Adjacent uplands have been mostly deforested for agricultural use, grazing and for residential and tourist development (USFWS 1994).
iucn_doc_threats: The population is located only eight to ten metres from high tide and is threatened by agricultural, residential, and tourist development on adjacent uplands, as well as by damage from heavy storm surges. It may have been cut in the past for use as livestock feed. Because it is an attractive and almost spineless cactus, it may be subject to collection for use as an ornamental (USFWS 1994).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Critically Endangered because the most current accessible information indicates that there are less than 50 individuals in the only known population. However, this assessment should be reviewed if new data become available.

iucn_doc_range: Leptocereus weingartianus is found in the southern coastal area of Haiti and the Dominican Republic. The type collection is from Haiti but there appear to be no further collections from this country. All the other collections are from the Dominican Republic and it appears to have a scattered occurrence with localities in the southwestern, south-central and southeastern parts of the country. This species occurs at altitudes between sea level and 200 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: Leptocereus weingartianus is a decumbent or climbing shrub found growing on limestone rocks in dry coastal forests.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is land use change due to expanding agriculture and tourist developments.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern because although little is known about its population size and trends and the degree of impact from current threats, it appears to have a fairly wide range and the species is probably more abundant than current records indicate. Fieldwork is required to confirm the presence of this species in Haiti and to gather more information on its range, population size and trends and the impact of any threats in the Dominican Republic part of its range.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it grows in Mendoza and San Juan (Hunt et al. 2006) at altitudes between 2,000 and 3,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in high-altitude steppes and monte shrublands.
iucn_doc_threats: There are several roads crossing its range. Current and projected mining activities, as well as tourism are also threatening the species. Extraction by collectors is considered low risk, as it is difficult to find and occurs in areas of difficult access.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has an extent of occurrence of approximately 3,000 km<sup>2</sup> and there are several threats affecting the population. However, the number of locations needs to be established. With the current information the species it listed as Near Threatened, but depending on the number of locations it could qualify for Endangered under Criterion B1ab(iii,v) or Vulnerable under B1ab(iii,v).

iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in grasslands at high altitudes, on sandy and rocky soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats for the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Maihueniopsis conoidea has a reduced range (its extent of occurrence is 2,000 km2). There are, however, no threats, it is abundant, it occurs in a protected area, and easily reproduces vegetatively. The species is therefore listed as Least Concern.

iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in Catamarca and Salta, at elevations of 1,800 to 3,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in monte shrubland and puna grassland on rocky soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The only major threat is illegal collection.
iucn_doc_rationale: Maihueniopsis minuta has a small range (extent of occurrence is approximately 4,200 km2). It is only known from two disjunct subpopulations, i.e. two locations, one of which is severely affected by illegal collection. This has caused the depletion of the subpopulation and an inferred continuing decline in mature individuals. It is likely that the pressure will not cease in the near future. Furthermore, the species is not found in any protected area. Hence, it is listed as Endangered under criterion B. It is necessary to explore the area since it is very likely that there are more subpopulations between the two known localities.

iucn_doc_range: This cactus grows in northern San Luis Potosí, Nuevo León and Tamaulipas, Mexico. The extent of occurrence for this species is 2,144 km², and its range is divided into two separate subpopulations with an estimated area of occupancy of less than 500 km². It is known from seven locations.
iucn_doc_habitats: Mammillaria albicoma is usually found on limestone slopes with Agave lecheguilla-Hechtia glomerata scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats for Mammillaria albicoma are habitat destruction mostly by goat over-pasturing, illegal collection, and collection of plants to use them in decorating nativity scenes.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria albicoma is listed as Endangered under criterion B because of its restricted range (extent of occurrence ca 2,144 km², and area of occupancy less than 500 km²). It is known from seven locations, and the population is severely fragmented. The species is not abundant and it is under pressure due to illegal collection and habitat destruction, which is causing a continuing decline in the population and the number of mature individuals.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Guanajuato, Mexico, and occurs in a single location to the south of Pozos. The area of occupancy is less than 5 km<sup>2</sup>. It is found around 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on sloping calcareous rock in semi-desert.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by illegal collecting. The habitat is now well known to both commercial and amateur collectors. A residential development is in progress in the area which represents a potential threat. However, the developers are interested in the idea of establishing a protected area.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria albiflora is listed as Critically Endangered due to its very restricted range (extent of occurrence and area of occupancy less than 5 km²), its occurrence in a single location, and a continuing population decline as a result of illegal collecting that is also reducing its range. Its population has been diminished by an estimated 50% over the past 20 years, and the decline in mature individuals is continuing. Although a portion of the site has recently been enclosed with a chain link fence to protect it, this did not prevent access to the site.

iucn_doc_range: This cactus occurs northeast of San Luis Potosí, Mexico, where there are many subpopulations over a range of 250 km². It grows at altitudes around 1,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus can be found on flat to sloping calcareous rocky terrain in xerophytic shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is threatened by illegal collecting, both commercial and amateur, and despite the legal measures in place this is still very evident at present. A highway was also built through one of the subpopulations. Mining of limestone for road construction is also a threat. The area is close to the state capital which is growing rapidly, hence human pressures on the area are increasing.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammilaria aureilanata is listed as Endangered because of its reduced range (extent of occurrence approximately 250 km², area of occupancy about 20 km²), the population is severely fragmented (known from 12 locations), and there is continuing decline due to the impacts of illegal collection of plants from the wild and habitat destruction because of mining and road building activities. The total population is estimated to be less than 10,000 individuals.

iucn_doc_threats: The threats are unknown, but the area close to Zacatecas City is very disturbed by people, but it is unknown if this has had any impact on the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a poorly known species, with no information available on population size and trends, or threats, plus the taxonomy of this taxon is uncertain. Hence it is listed as Data Deficient.

iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows under rocks and on cliffs, on shaley barren slopes (Hunt et al. 2006). It has also been recorded growing under shrubs.
iucn_doc_threats: Nothing is known about the threats, but the known localities are close to the expanding city of Monterrey, hence there are a number of potential future threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: From what is known, this species appears to have a very small range (extent of occurrence <100km<sup>2</sup>) and is only known with certainty from two locations. The species is extremely scarce and the area is very close to the rapidly growing city of Monterey and there is in fact much development between Monterey and Saltillo which is where one of the known localities is. Although specific threats are not known, there are a number of potential threats including expanding urban areas, collectors, overgrazing of the habitat by goats, etc. Hence the species is listed as Vulnerable D2.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Coahuila (Hernández et al. 2004) and San Luis Potosí (W.A. Fitz Maurice pers. comm. 2009). It may also occur in Durango, but this needs to be confirmed. It grows at elevations of 1,000 to 1,230 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in a very specialized habitat, in the sediments of dry lakes (these are periodically flooded in wet years). The habitat is very open with a sparse shrub cover.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection could be a problem for such a scarce species. The habitats where it grows are good for agricultural expansion provided there is water for irrigation.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria coahuilensis has a restricted range, the extent of occurrence is approximately 1,000 km². The species is known from less than five locations, it might also be considered severely fragmented, and there is continuing decline in habitat quality and quantity, as well as in mature individuals, due to the impacts of illegal collection and the potential for agro-industry to start agricultural schemes in the areas where it occurs. Hence, the species qualifies for listing as Endangered under criteria B1.

iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: The species is distributed over a large area with small isolated populations in low densities. There are records from Colombia, Costa Rica, Guatemala, Honduras, Jamaica, Mexico, Panama and Venezuela, at elevations between 0 and 2,800 m asl (Ponce and Trujillo 1991, Hunt et al. 2006, Hunt 2008).  In Mexico, it is found in the states of Chiapas, Oaxaca, Yucatán in single isolated subpopulations.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits open clearings in dry thorn forests (Ponce and Trujillo 1991). In the Yucatán, it is found on coastal dunes. In Costa Rica, it is found on a limestone outcrop in wet forest (B. Hammel pers. comm. 2009).  In Venezuela, it is found in clearings and spiny scrublands restricted to limestone soils (Ponce 1989; J. Nassar pers. comm. 2011). The mating system has not been studied. The seeds are dispersed by lizards.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by disturbance of its very limited habitat. Its small subpopulations are threatened by extraction of wild specimens for commercial and ornamental purposes, as well as by specialist collectors (Albesiano and Rangel 2006). The single location in Costa Rica is threatened by the continuing spread of local cattle-ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammilaria columbiana is listed as Least Concern because of its widespread distribution across several countries. Although the species is not locally abundant, there are many locations so it is unlikely to be significantly affected by current threats throughout its range. Some of the subpopulations, particularly those in Mexico and Costa Rica, may be more at risk.

iucn_doc_habitats: In Chiapas, Mexico, it is found in cloud and high elevation forests (Ishiki, M. pers. comm. 2009). In Nicaragua it is found from 300 - 800m asl (Stevens et al. 2000, B. Hammel pers.comm. 2009). In Guatemala it is found in dry forest to pine-oak forests (M. Véliz pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats in Chiapas and Guatemala, however some populations are declining due to habitat loss from expanding cattle ranching (lower elevations) and small scale farming and cultivation (higher elevations) (M. Ishiki and M. Véliz pers. comm. 2009). Subpopulations are affected by cattle trampling (M. Véliz pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_rationale: The extent of occurrence considering the subpopulations in Mexico and Guatemala is less than 20,000 km<sup>2</sup>, however this will change when the subpopulations from Nicaragua are included. In Guatemala the subpopulations are also considered to be Endangered, very few are known and they are affected by cattle trampling<span lang="EN-GB"> (M. Véliz pers. comm. 2009). With current knowledge the species is listed as Endangered. Further information is needed from Nicaragua in order to assess this species.

iucn_threat_names: Avalanches/landslides; Earthquakes/tsunamis; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: Mammillaria evermanniana is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs on Isla Cerralvo in Baja California Sur (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: Mammillaria evermanniana grows in desert scrub on granite and volcanic soils. The island of Cerralvo is the one that receives the most rain in the Gulf of California..
iucn_doc_threats: The only minor threat for Mammillaria evermanniana are hurricanes that cause landslides, which can wash away the plants. Earthquakes also affect the island, which has a very steep slope, because the orography rocks where the plants grow collapse easily. The plants occurring on mesas are not affected by these threats. Goat trampling is another threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria evermanniana has a very restricted range, it only occurs on an island of ca 50 km2. There are, however, no major threats for the species because the island is difficult to access and development is not permitted. The population is stable and seems healthy. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: The species is known from a single location in the Barranca de Tolimán, in the state of Hidalgo, Mexico, where it grows at about 700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on steep volcanic rock in a deep canyon in semi-desert.It seems like this species has a short life and the plants appear from one season to the other thanks to the seed bank held in the rock cracks.
iucn_doc_threats: The known threat for this species is illegal collecting, which was thought to drive the species to extinction in the past. The species grows in a mine.
iucn_doc_rationale: Following general directions from the late Charles Glass, the assessors found Mammillaria glochidiata in the Barranca Tolimán, north of Zimapan in Hidalgo state, Mexico, in 1991. The population was very small, with an estimated 50 individuals confined to a single location. A subsequent visit in 1993 showed a considerable reduction in population to about 15 individuals. During two later visits, one including an extensive search of the canyon, no plants were found. The assessors returned to the site in February 2007 and they found six small plants, this fluctuation seems to be explained by the species short life and the seed bank found in crevices in the location.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Tehuacán-Cuicatlán Valley, occurring only in the state of Oaxaca, Mexico (Arias et al. 1997, Pilbeam 1999). There are several separated sub-populations within this area (Flores-Martínez et al. 2002), and it is known from 13 locations. It occurs at elevations from 1,700 to 2,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in xerophyllous scrub and grasslands (Arias et al. 1997). It can be found on exposed, low, limestone slopes, often beneath low shrubs (Pilbeam 1999). Hunt et al. (2006) report it from stony hillsides mostly amongst grass or in the shade of low shrubs, often also on top of rocks in indirect sun light. It is often associated to nurse shrubs (Peters et al. 2008). It flowers between September and November (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is affected by indirect threats due to changes in land use, primarily driven by the expansion of small farms and cattle ranches. National and international trade of seed and adult plants and overgrazing of goats all year round are serious threats to the survival of the species (Flores-Martínez et al. 2002).
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria kraehenbuehlii is listed as Least Concern due to relatively abundant population and its occurrence in protected areas. There are several threats affecting some subpopulations but the species as a whole is not declining at a rate sufficient to warrant listing in a threatened category.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Tamaulipas (Hernández et al. 2004), where it occurs at elevations of 800 to 1,700 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in open pine oak woodland on steep mountain sides.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection for the horticultural trade is a possible threat. Plants have been seized in Mexico and the Netherlands (Bárcenas 2003).
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria laui has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence less than 100 km²), and the area can be considered as a single location. This is a highly sought after species, and there is continuing decline in the number of mature individuals because of the impacts of illegal collection. The species is therefore listed as Critically Endangered.

iucn_doc_rationale: This is a poorly known taxon and may not even be a good species. Although the extent of occurrence is small and there are known threats, this taxon is assessed as Data Deficient due to the lack of information and the taxonomic uncertainties.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Chihuahua and Durango (Hernández et al. 2004). It is found at altitudes from 2,200 to 2,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found among volcanic rocks in semi desert. It occurs in pine-oak (Pinus-Quercus) forests mixed with grasslands, and it grows on rocks (González-Elizondo and González-Elizondo 1993).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by illegal collecting and exploitation of its habitat for cattle and forestry in places like the Biosphere Reserve of La Michilia (González-Elizondo and González-Elizondo 1993).<span lang="EN-US"> Temperature extremes like the freeze of 1997 remain a threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria longiflora has a wide range and is locally abundant, even though there are threats it parts of its range these are not sufficient to trigger a threatened listing. Furthermore, it occurs in a protected area. Hence, the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria manana W.A.Fitz Maur. & B.Fitz Maur. sec. ???
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Temperate; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in one location southwest of Laguna de Chapala in Jalisco, Mexico.  It is found in two adjacent sites totalling 10,000 m<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found on steep rock walls and steep rocky slopes within humid, low scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: Until recently, the only known location of Mammillaria manana was unknown, but it has now become rather widely known (W.A. Fitz Maurice pers. comm, 2012). This is very likely to have affected the population drastically, however, it has not been visited again recently by the assessors to evaluate the current status (W.A. Fitz Maurice pers. comm, 2012). The area is quite inaccessible, which offers the species some protection.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Critically Endangered because it has an area of occupy of less than 1 km<sup>2</sup>, it is known from only one location, and is experiencing a decline in population due to collection. It is very likely that this species has experienced severe declines recently, because the only known locality is now publicly known.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria marksiana Krainz sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, from the states of  Durango and Sinaloa (Hunt et al. 2006), it grows at elevations of 400 to 2,000 m  (Pilbeam 1999). The species has been reported from Sonora, but this is information is doubtful.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on limestone cliffs (Pilbeam 1999) and in the south of Arizona in low dry forest. Flowering in January and February (Paredes et al.  2000).
iucn_doc_threats: There are threats in parts of the species' range, illegal collection might take place but does not affect the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is poorly known taxonomically and ecologically. Even though there are threats in parts of the species' range, it has numerous large populations and does not currently seem to be declining at a rate sufficient to qualify for a threat listing. However further research is necessary to determine how these threats are impacting the population. The species   has not been collected recently and the information on its distribution   is uncertain and on population trends unknown.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria matudae Bravo sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the state of Mexico close to the border with the state of Michoacán at elevations between 700 and 1,250 m (Pilbeam 1999, Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: There is no information on the species habitat, but it is probably desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are not known.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a poorly known taxon and the information on population and threats needed to assess its conservation status is not available. Hence, the species is here listed as Data Deficient.

iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Tamaulipas, where it occurs at elevations of 1,300 to 2,000 m asl (Hernández et al. 2004, Pilbeam 1999). It has a very restricted range; the extent of occurrence is 200 km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in low deciduous forests and thick scrub, in shady areas on organic soils.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection is a possible threat. Part of the population was probably impacted by the widening of a major highway through the area.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria melaleuca has a fairly restricted range (extent of occurrence ca 200 km²). It is known from five or fewer locations and there is a continuing decline in habitat quality due to the impacts of a major highway built through the population. Illegal collection may also pose a threat. Hence, it is listed as Endangered under criterion B1.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurrs in the states of Coahuila, Durango, Nuevo León, and Tamaulipas (Hernández et al. 2004). It grows at elevations of 350 to1,800 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species has been seen growing on steep cliffs by Hunt (Pilbeam 1999). It probably occurs in shrublands.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection is a possible threat to this species. Overgrazing of the habitat by goats may also be a threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria melanocentra is currently considered to be Least Concern due to the lack of any known major threats. This species appears to have a wide range, although nothing is known about the population size and trends. Some threats have been identified but it is not known how significant they are. It is very likely that this species could be Data Deficient pending the collection of additional information to resolve its status.

iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits tropical dry forests and xerophyllous scrub (Fernández-Nava et al. 1998).

iucn_doc_range: The type collection of this species was from an unspecified locality in the Mexican state of Guanajuato; a plant of this species in Woody Minnich's collection in California supposedly came from near the Rio Blanco in the border area between Querétaro and Guanajuato; and Reppenhagen and Lacoste reported it from Bucareli in Querétaro (Pilbeam 1999). Reppenhagen and Lacoste reported it to grow between 1,380 and 1,700 m (Pilbeam 1999). There are no recent records to confirm the range and identity of this species, it is possible that other collections of this taxon have been named as M. parkinsonii.
iucn_doc_habitats: A globose dwarf succulent which presumably occurs in dry shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: As there are no confirmed populations of this species in the wild it is impossible to identify any threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: The taxonomy of this species is unclear, it is thought to be a form of Mammillaria parkinsonii. Given that there is no information available on its range, population size and trends or if there are any threats, the species is listed as Data Deficient. Taxonomic work is required to determine if it is a good species.

iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus is found in oak forests and matorral, on volcanic soils. It is sometimes found is pasture.
iucn_doc_threats: Some subpopulations are threatened by livestock trampling, logging of oak forests and over-collecting.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although Mammillaria muehlenpfordtii is likely in decline, it is considered Least Concern, as its rates of decline are not currently sufficient to qualify for a threatened category. This species should be re-evaluated once additional information is available on the effects of various threats.

Mammillaria multihamata Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
Mammillaria multiradiata Mart., nom. nud., sec. Britton & Rose 1923
Mammillaria napina J.A.Purpus sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: Endemic species of the Tehuacán region, occurring only in the state of Puebla at elevations of 1,700 to 2,400 m (Arias et al. 1997; Hunt et al. 2006). It is found in a few isolated localities (Rodríguez-Ortega et al. 2006), and it has an estomated extent of occurrence of 4,000 km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows in xerophyllous scrub on limestone slopes (Arias et al. 1997; Pilbeam 1999). This species grows in open spaces (Rodríguez-Ortega et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is under threat from unscrupulous collectors, and the species has very high demand in the market (Pilbeam 1999). The species is tolerant to disturbance, however in the long term and if it increases drastically this could cause populations declines. In the case of M. napina 40% of the populations are already declining as a consequence of disturbance (Martorell and Peters 2009).
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a limited distribution and small number of subpopulations, some of which have become extinct. It currently fails to meet a threat category, but could rapidly come to do so if current threat trends continue (B criteria), and is thus listed as Near Threatened until more information is available on population trends and projected rates of decline.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria neumannii Regel ex Rümpler sec. Korotkova 2021

Mammillaria nivosa Link ex Pfeiff. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Trinidad and Tobago; Virgin Islands, U.S.; Barbados; Virgin Islands, British; Jamaica; Bahamas; Antigua and Barbuda; Cayman Islands; Turks and Caicos Islands; Puerto Rico
iucn_location_codes: TT; VI; BB; VG; JM; BS; AG; KY; TC; PR
iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: Mammillaria nivosa is distributed in the Bahamas, Puerto Rico (Mona, Desecho and Culebra Islands), US Virgin Islands (Buck Island, St. Thomas, Little St. James), British Virgin Islands (Tortola), Antigua, and the British and the West Indies (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: It has been reported from naked limestone rocks (Britton and Rose 1923).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is affected by development of urban areas and tourism. Almost all of the islands where this species occurs are tourist resorts. However, this species also occurs on Mona Island, which is a protected nature reserve, and presumably not affected by development.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria nivosa has a wide distribution and is known from various locations on different islands in the Caribbean. Its habitat quality is expected to be declining due to urban and tourism development. However, a subpopulation in the middle of its geographical range is within a protected area (Mona Island Natural Reserve) and was reported by Britton and Rose (1923) as extremely abundant. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_threats: In some areas the species is threatened by subsistence and seasonal agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria nunezii is listed as Least Concern due to its wide distribution, presumed large populations, and because it is not declining at a rate sufficient to qualify for a threatened category. This species is more common in the southern portion of the range.

iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found on sloping calcareous rocky terrain in semi-desert and oak forest.

iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria orcuttii qualifies as Least Concern due to its many subpopulations over a fairly large area and a substantial number of individuals. There is little illegal collecting; however, this is not a major threat.

iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on limestone slopes (Pilbeam 1999) in xerophyllous scrub.

iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered Vulnerable due to a limited geographic range (extent of occurrence approximately 3,000km<sup>2</sup>, known from 10 locations) and an ongoing population decline due to rural development, road construction and grazing (trampling).

iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Housing & urban areas; Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_doc_range: Mammillaria petrophila is endemic to Mexico from Baja California Sur in the Capes region, it occurs from sea level to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in desert scrub on rocky outcrops and granite.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are urban and tourism development, and mining, these impacts have an influence on the subspecies M. petrophila baxteriana and M. petrophila arida.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Vulnerable because it has an extent of occurrence of just over 5,000 km<sup>2</sup>, the population is naturally fragmented and the impact on its habitat has fragmented it even more. iucn_doc_threats: There is little interest in collecting this species and small consequent threat. Increased agricultural use and housing expansion also pose a small threat. iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, and is distributed in the State of Mexico (Hunt et al. 2006), where it is known from three locations. Sosa and Gómez-Pompa (1994) report the species in a disjunct population in the state of Veracruz, but these records are for M. rhodanthe (M. Cházaro pers. comm. 2009). Records from outside of its range in the state of Mexico are likely from other species within the species complex (M. Cházaro pers. comm. 2009). It is found from 2,000 to 2,300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found on rocks and cliffs in pine-oak forests (M. Cházaro pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by collection and it is more of a threat for this species than others because of its proximity to large population centres and its small range and population size.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because it has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence =7,000km<sup>2</sup>) and is present in only three locations. It is experiencing declines from the unsustainable collection of its flowers and whole individuals for use as a Christmas decoration. In order to prevent further declines, regulation of the collection of flowers and plants is needed.

As the location is well-known to both commercial and amateur collectors, the species is experiencing continuing declines as a result of illegal collecting.

iucn_doc_range: The species is endemic to Mexico in the states of Guanajuato, Hidalgo, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, and Tamaulipas (Pilbeam 1999). It occurs at elevations of 1,300 to 1,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species is found growing on limestone and rocky slopes (Pilbeam 1999) in submontane matorral.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by illegal overcollection for trade for use as an ornamental (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria schiedeana is listed as Vulnerable under criterion B1ab(v) because it has a restricted range (extent of eccurrence less than 20,000 km2), is known from fewer than 10 locations, and is experiencing continuing decline from over-harvesting for the trade in ornamentals.

iucn_common_name: Long Mamma Nipple Cactus; Longspine Nipple Cactus; Biznaga Esférica; Pale mammillaria
iucn_locations: Mexico; San Luis Potosí; Texas; United States; Tamaulipas
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: The species is found in the USA in southern Texas, and in Mexico in San Luis Potosí and the north of Tamaulipas, at altitudes of 100 to 1,000 m asl (Pilbeam 1999, Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species sits in thick rootstock (Pilbeam 1999) and usually grows in shade. In Tamaulipas the species occurs in several sites in Tamaulipan thorn scrub, in sparse vegetation and on flat limestones.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria sphaerica has a relatively wide range, and even though it is not abundant, there are no known threats affecting it. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria spinaurea Salm-Dyck sec. Britton & Rose 1923

iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This cactus has been reported from the Mexican states of Sonora, Chihuahua and Sinaloa, at elevations from 500 to 1,750 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in rocky slopes, in foothill thorn scrubs, in low dry forest and in pine-oak woodlands (Paredes et al. 2000)
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria standleyi is widespread and common. There are no known threats and no substantial declines. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria suaveolens hort. ex Rümpler sec. Britton & Rose 1923
Mammillaria subulata Muehlenpf. sec. Britton & Rose 1923
Mammillaria subulifera Ehrenb. sec. Britton & Rose 1923
Mammillaria surculosa Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
Mammillaria tayloriorum Glass & R.A.Foster sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the island of San Pedro Nolasco, off the Mexican state of Sonora (Felger et al. in press), and occurs at elevations from 20 to 350m asl on the north west side of the island.
iucn_doc_habitats: Mammillaria tayloriorum grows on steep rocky slopes among Agave chrysoglossa, Mammillaria multidigitata, Opuntia bravoana, and Echinocereus websterianus (Paredes et al. 2000; Felger et al. in press).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to the species at present, but there is a potential threat from an alien invasive species - Buffel Grass (Pennisetum ciliare). Since the 1970's the introduction of Buffel grass has been promoted in Sonora and it is now present in large extensions of land (Búrquez 2007). The island is only 12 km away from the coast, where there are patches of this invasive species, which makes the invasion a latent threat. Buffel Grass is already established in other islands of the Gulf, such as Tiburón Island (A. Búrquez pers. comm. 2012).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence ca 2 km2), which is a portion of the small island of San Pedro Nolasco. At present the population is stable, however, the invasion of Buffel Grass is a potential threat that can drastically affect the establishment of new seedlings and therefore the survival of the species. If the invasion of Buffel Grass happens, the species would then have to be classified as Critically Endangered. Hence it is currently listed as Vulnerable D2.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria tecta Miq. sec. Britton & Rose 1923
Mammillaria tuberculata Engelm. sec. Hunt 2006
Mammillaria uniseta K.Schum. sec. Britton & Rose 1923
Mammillaria variamamma Ehrenb. sec. Korotkova 2021
Mammillaria varieaculeata F.G.Buchenau sec. Hunt 2016
Mammillaria vetula Mart. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in the Mexican states of Hidalgo, Guanajuato and Querétaro, where it grows at altitudes from 1,600 to 3,350 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found is pine forests at high elevations.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats, however some subpopulations are affected by logging of pine forest and land-use change (mines).
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria vetula is listed as Least Concern in light of its relatively wide distribution and abundance in appropriate habitats. Even though there are threats in places, these are not sufficient to warrant concern.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria villosa Fennel ex C.F.Först. sec. Britton & Rose 1923
Mammillaria wagneriana Boed. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in Zacatecas, Jalisco and Aguascalientes at elevations of 1,700 to 2,300 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: A hemispherical species found growing on rocky slopes in open oak woodland on volcanic soils.
iucn_doc_threats: No major threats are known to affect this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: There are taxonomic uncertainties concerning the validity of this taxon as a good species, hence little is known about its true range, population size and trends. Hence it is listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria wegeneri Ehrenb. sec. Britton & Rose 1923
Mammillaria werdermanii H.Weiss sec. WFO 2019
Mammillaria xaltianguensis Sánchez-Mej. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Guerrero and Michoacán at elevations of 500 to 850 m (Pilbeam 1999; Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: According to the type specimen collected by Sánchez-Mejorada in 1970 (Sanchéz-Mejorada 70-0802, MEXU, holotype) reported in Hunt et al. (2006), the species inhabits in deciduous woodland on sandy granite hills.
iucn_doc_threats: The threats to this species are unknown.
iucn_doc_rationale: This is a poorly known taxon and the information on population and threats needed to assess its conservation status is not available. Hence, the species is here listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Mammillaria xanthispina Sencke sec. Korotkova 2021
Mammillaria zegschwitzii Pfeiff. sec. ???
Mammillaria zepnickii Ehrenb. sec. Britton & Rose 1923
Mammillaria zublerae Repp. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs near Ocampo in the state of Tamaulipas, at an altitude of 1,500 m asl (Pilbeam 1999).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on vertical limestone rocks in dense tropical deciduous forest.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection of the species has probably resulted in population declines.
iucn_doc_rationale: Mammillaria zublerae has a very small range (extent of occurrence 270 km², area of occupancy 25 km2). As the main threat is illegal collection, the whole area can be considered a single location and there is continuing decline in number of mature individuals due to the illegal collection. The population size is less than 2,500 mature individuals, with each subpopulation having less than 250 individuals. Furthermore, it does not occur in any protected area. The species is therefore listed as Endangered.

checklist_fullName: Matucana haynei (Otto ex Salm-Dyck) Britton & Rose
checklist_taxon: Matucana haynei (Otto ex Salm-Dyck) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This Peruvian endemic is found in three fragments, which correspond to different subspecies. It is found in the regions of Áncash, Lima, Ica, Arequipa, and Ayacucho, at altitudes of 1,500 to 4,000 m asl (Arakaki et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species, other than collection to use it as an ornamental plant.
iucn_doc_rationale: Matucana haynei has a wide distribution, is common where it occurs, and is not exposed to any major threat. It is therefore classified as Least Concern.
