Mediolobivia carminata Backeb. ex Krainz sec. Korotkova 2021

checklist_fullName: Mediolobivia turbiniformis Backeb. ex Frič & Kreuz.
checklist_taxon: Mediolobivia turbiniformis Backeb. ex Frič & Kreuz. sec. WFO 2019
Melocactus acispinosus Buining & Brederoo sec. ???
Melocactus acunae León sec. Majure & al. 2022
Melocactus amethystinus Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
Melocactus andinus R.Gruber ex N.P.Taylor sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Garbage & solid waste; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Venezuela (Merida and Trujillo) and Colombia (Boyaca). It occurs in intra-Andean arid valleys at elevations between 1,500 and 2,500 m asl. Its distribution is patchy and disjunct in both countries, occurring in only 3-4 locations.
iucn_doc_habitats: Melocactus andinus is capable of selfing, but hummingbirds are the only pollinators responsible for outcrossing and gene flow events. This species occurs in intra-Andean arid enclaves in very steep areas in rain shadows. It co-occurs with a rare endemic succulent, Echeveria compresicaules. It is self-compatible, but it is mainly pollinated by hummingbirds. Melocactus andinus is autogamous, relying heavily on self-pollination that leads to high rates of inbreeding (Nassar et al. 2007).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no obvious threats for this species but the proximity to urban areas may affect it through the collection of specimens and degradation of its habitat by the disposal of garbage. Given the very restricted distribution of the species, this might be a concern in the future. The conversion of land to agriculture or for cattle raising might also be a threat for this species (Fernandez-Alonso and Xhonneux 2002).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has a very restricted and patchy distribution (extent of occurrence between 300 - 400 km<sup>2</sup>), it occurs at only 3-4 locations, it has very low population recruitment, and there is a continuing decline in the extent and quality of its habitat due to its proximity to urban areas.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus atrovirens C.F.Först., nom. nud., sec. Britton & Rose 1922
Melocactus azureus Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_synonyms: Melocactus azureus Buining & Brederoo ssp. azureus
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This cactus can be found in the region of Irecê and lower drainage of the Rio Jacaré, drainage of Rio Verde in central-northern Bahia, Brazil. It grows at altitudes between 450 and 750 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This northern Rio São Francisco caatinga element grows on flat, exposed Bambuí limestone in caatinga.
iucn_doc_threats: The outcrops where this species grows are potential targets for mining. The species is not tolerated, farmers clear the plants to avoid cattle getting hurt. Habitat loss for agriculture is a major threat affecting the survival of pollinators, Melocactus azureus is self-incompatible and thus relaying on pollinators. Also, the change in the surrounding vegetation has an impact on the outcrops were the species grows, making them prone to invasive species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a small range (extent of occurrence is 3,100 km<sup>2</sup>), and it was known from a few localities where it was not very abundant. Recently more abundant subpopulations have been found, the population is severely fragmented and it faces several threats that cause a continuing decline. Hence, the species is listed as Endangered. It is important to have the Rio Verde area reassessed to evaluate what is occurring with the subpopulations there, as they are the only abundant known populations of the species.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus bahiensis (Britton & Rose) Luetzelb. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Brazil, in Pernambuco, Bahia and Minas Gerais, at elevations between 300 and 1,300 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in exposed crystalline rock formations (quartzitic-arenitic, granite/gneiss), quartz gravel, rarely on limestone, under and between shrubs (Taylor and Zappi 2004), in caatinga and campo rupestre.
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat destruction occurs due to fire wood collection and rock quarrying; however, these are very localized and do not affect the species as a whole.
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus bahiensis has a wide range and numerous subpopulations. There are some localized threats but none affect the species as a whole, hence it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus bellavistensis Rauh & Backeb. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_doc_range: Melocactus bellavistensis is found in Ecuador in the valleys of Catamayo, Loja province (Loaiza 2008). It also occurs in the Amazonas, Cajamarca and La Libertad regions of Peru (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows from 500 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: In Ecuador, M. bellavistensis is found only in the driest areas of the Catamayo Valley, on steep, rocky slopes near watercourses, where it grows alongside Acacia macracantha, Anadenanthera colubrina, Ceiba insignis and Echinopsis pachanoi, amongst others (Loaiza 2008).
iucn_doc_threats: M. bellavistensis is at serious risk from overcollection for sale as a decorative plant (especially between the months of September and December, while it is in bloom), as well as - to a lesser extent - for use in shamanic rituals (Loaiza 2008).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Defficient because there is no information on the population size, trend or threats for the Peruvian part of its range, which seems to be around 2/3 of the species' range. However, detailed information for the species exists in Ecuador, where only 70 individuals exist in two locations, it has been extirpated from other places where it used to be abundant and it seems the species is now confined to very remote areas. The subpopulation in Ecuador is severely affected by collection as an ornamental plant and for shamanic rituals. Approximately 30% of the population has been lost over the past 10 years. The species would qualify as Vulnerable under criterion A2ad. For this reasons, it is recommended to conduct a regional assessment. However, in order to assess the global conservation status of the species more research is needed in Peru.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus bohiensis Britton sec. TPL 2013
Melocactus braunii Esteves sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil occurring in Bahia at 1,000 m asl (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in campo rupestre element in Northern Bahia.
iucn_doc_threats: Local collection, mining and cattle ranching are threats to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a very small range (extent of occurrence <100km<sup>2</sup>, one location), there is an inferred continuing decline of the population as there is mining in the area and the species is locally collected. Hence it is listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus brederooianus Buining sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Savanna - Dry
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the northern portion of the state of Bahia, Brazil (Hunt et al. 2006). It is known from two locations (near Andorinha and near Carnaiba). It occurs from 430-800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in caatinga in loamy soils and in campos rupestres on rocky areas.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are farming and urbanization. It is highly probable that the subpopulations have already been extirpated or drastically diminished as a result of land use change. The subpopulation found in caatinga was already severely affected by this expanding threats 20 years ago (P. Braun peers.comm. 2013). Field work is needed in order to assess the status and threats of the subpopulation in campo rupestre.
iucn_doc_rationale: The sites where this species grows were last visited 20 years ago, in both areas land use change was rapidly increasing but more so in the subpopulation found in caatinga. For this reason it is inferred that the subpopulation in caatinga habitat has disappeared since it was last visited, while further filed work is needed in order to establish the status of the subpopulation and threats of the minute (1x1km<sup>2</sup>) location found in campo rupestre habitat . Hence, this species is listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus broadwayi (Britton & Rose) A.Berger sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Grenada; Saint Vincent and the Grenadines; Trinidad and Tobago
iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in coastal areas on the islands of Grenada, Tobago, and St. Vincent.
iucn_doc_habitats: It occurs in coastal volcanic rock areas.
iucn_doc_threats: This species may be affected by tourist development.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Near Threatened because it has a restricted distribution and it may be affected by tourism, however there is currently insufficient information on rates of decline to apply threat categories and criteria. Further research is needed to better determine the species distribution, population size and trends, and threats, in addition to population monitoring to determine rates of decline.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus calderanus Eerkens sec. Korotkova 2021
Melocactus caroli-linnaei N.P.Taylor sec. Majure & al. 2022
iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/supratidal - Coastal Caves/Karst; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: Melocactus caroli-linnaei is endemic to southern Jamaica (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations between sea level and 50 m. The distribution for this species is likely fragmented.
iucn_doc_habitats: It is found in dogtooth limestone on the southern coast of Jamaica. It does not occur with other vegetation.
iucn_doc_threats: Urban development is likely the major threat to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although this species has a restricted distribution and it meets the threshold of geographic range for Endangered, it is listed as Near Threatened since the the number of locations is unknown and could exceed the threshold for threat categories. Further research should be carried out to determine this species' distribution, population status, and trends.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus columna-trajani Pfeiff. sec. Britton & Rose 1920
Melocactus concinnus Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Dams (size unknown)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the core area of Eastern Brazil, occurring in Bahia and Minas Gerais at 550m -1,300m (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in the open and beneath low to tall shrubs, and trees in caatinga, carrasco and cerrado de altitude, in stony soil, quartz sand and gravel, or between crystalline rocks (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat loss (caatinga) mainly for fuel wood (charcoal) is the main threat for this Melocactus. Some subpopulations are affected by human caused fires to burn the vegetation (campos rupestres). The southern most subpopulation is likely to be submerged under an hydro-electric dam (Taylor and Zappi 2004). This plant is collected for local use (see use and trade).
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though the species faces some major threats, it has a large extent of occurrence (EOO > 100, 000 km2) and it is abundant. The species occurs in the ecotone of caatinga and campo ruspestre, one habitat is threatened (caatinga), but the second one is in good conservation state. Hence the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus deinacanthus Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas; Small-holder farming; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is known from central-southern Bahia, Brazil, where it appears to have a very limited distribution east of the Rio São Francisco and west of the Chapada Diamantina. It grows at altitudes between 450 and 600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in caatinga element in canga (where it hybridizes with other melocacti) and on gneissic inselbergs.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats for this species are loss of habitat for agriculture (small scale), ranching (small scale), urbanization and local collection.
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus deinacanthus was known from only one location, however many more subpopulations were detected later on. Still, the range is restricted (extent of occurrence 800 km2) and fragmented. There is continuing decline and the species has no protection. Hence, it is listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(iii,v).

checklist_taxon: Melocactus estevesii P.J.Braun sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in northwest Roraima (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rock outcrops in Amazon forest.
iucn_doc_threats: There is no information on the threats affecting this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Data Deficient, it was collected in the early 1980's and a part from the miner that collected the species nobody has seen it as it occurs in a very remote area.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus evae Z.Mészáros sec. Majure & al. 2022
Melocactus excavatus G.Don sec. Britton & Rose 1922
Melocactus ferreophilus Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_synonyms: Melocactus azureus Buining & Brederoo ssp. ferreophilus (Buining & Brederoo) N.P.Taylor
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Roads & railroads; Mining & quarrying; Intentional use: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]
iucn_doc_range: This cactus can be found in the upper drainage of the Rio Jacaré and tributaries, between América Dourada, Barro Alto and Souto Soares, in central Bahia, Brazil, at elevations of 700 to 850 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on raised, exposed Bambuí limestone in caatinga.
iucn_doc_threats: Major threats are mining, road construction and the impact of collectors. The habitat is being affected by extraction of calcareous rocks and modification of the environment surrounding its habitat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus ferreophilus occurs in a very small area (extent of occurrence less than 100 km2). It occurs at a single location, subpopulations are scarce, and there is continuing decline in the number of mature individuals and subpopulations, mainly from quarrying. Hence, this cactus qualifies for listing as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus glaucescens Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Desert - Hot; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Agro-industry farming; Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bahia, Brazil, where it occurs in Chapada Diamantina, Municipio Morro do Chapéu, at altitudes between 400 and 850 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in caatinga and the ecotone with campo rupestre (Chapada Diamantina) element: in the open and between low shrubs of the caatinga on flat or sloping, more or less exposed, crystalline rock outcrops with accumulation of gravel.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by small-holder and agro-industrial agriculture, cattle ranching and local collection. Man-made fires also pose a threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus glaucescens has a restricted range (extent of occurrence ca 1,500 km2), the subpopulations are severely fragmented and declining due to several threats. The species was listed as Critically Endangered, but M. Machado (pers. comm. 2011) has found more subpopulations since the species was last assessed. There are potentially more subpopulations but further exploring is needed. Hence, the species now qualifies for Endangered under criterion B1ab(iii).

checklist_taxon: Melocactus harlowii (Britton & Rose) Vaupel sec. Majure & al. 2022
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Marine Coastal/supratidal - Coastal Caves/Karst
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Housing & urban areas; Trend Unknown/Unrecorded
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Cuba and common at the eastern end of the island (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations between sea level and 350 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in coastal thickets and dry forests (Gonzalez-Torres 2007).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats for this species are agriculture, urban expansion, and fire (Gonzalez-Torres 2007).
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus harlowii is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed in eastern Cuba and some of the subpopulations are quite large.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus holguinensis Areces sec. Majure & al. 2022
Melocactus hookeri Lawr. sec. Korotkova 2021
Melocactus hookerianus Forbes ex C.F.Först., nom. nud., sec. Britton & Rose 1922
Melocactus hystrix Parm. ex Miq. sec. Britton & Rose 1922
Melocactus inconcinnus Buining & Brederoo sec. Taylor & Zappi 2004
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas; Mining & quarrying
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the state of Bahia, Brazil. It has a wide range in southern Bahia and is known from at least nine locations. It grows at elevation of 450 to 600m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in caatinga on limestone, quarzitic rocky outcrops, pedracanga, and granite.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species would be quarry of limestones and granitic rocks, urbanization and small scale farming.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern given its wide range, abundance and lack of major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus lagunaensis (Z.Mészáros) D.Barrios & Majure sec. Majure & al. 2022
Melocactus lanssensianus P.J.Braun sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Nomadic grazing; Agro-industry farming; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the state of Pernambuco, Brazil. It is known only from two locations separated by at least 100 km. There is suitable habitat in between these locations, but it has not been thoroughly surveyed. Thus, it is very likely that the species occurs in the intervening habitat.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in caatinga vegetation, on and between granite outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are small scale farming and pineapple plantations. Local livestock (goats) feed on this cactus species. The species is also collected to make candy.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence ca 500 km2), it is known from two locations (although it may occur in intervening habitat) and there is a continuing decline in its habitat. This species should be reassessed when the taxonomy is resolved.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus lasyacanthus Sprenger sec. Korotkova 2021

checklist_fullName: Melocactus lemairei (Monv. ex Lem.) Miq. ex Lem.
checklist_taxon: Melocactus lemairei (Monv. ex Lem.) Miq. ex Lem. sec. Majure & al. 2022
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands
iucn_doc_range: Melocactus lemaire occurs in the Dominican Republic and Haiti (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations from 10 to 200 m (cactuspro.com).
iucn_doc_habitats: It occurs in dry rocky soils, although it may occur in humid depressions (cactuspro.com).
iucn_doc_threats: Based on current knowledge of habitat degradation in Haiti, this species is probably under threat there.  This species is threatened by collection from the wild in the Dominican Republic (Gann pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Near Threatened because there are fewer than 10 locations and it is threatened by collection from the wild and by land clearing for agriculture, industry, and urban development - but there is insufficient information to apply the threat categories and criteria.  While the extent of occurrence is large, we are lacking information on the area of occupancy. Further research is needed to better determine its distribution as well as population size and trends.

checklist_fullName: Melocactus leopoldii De Smet ex T.Moore & Mast.
checklist_taxon: Melocactus leopoldii De Smet ex T.Moore & Mast. sec. Britton & Rose 1922
Melocactus levitestatus Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Bahia; Tocantins; Minas Gerais; Goiás; Brazil
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Bahia, Tocantins, Goiás and Minas Gerais. It grows at elevations of 450 to 780 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in mata calcárea (seasonal dry forest on calcareous rocks), growing specifically on silurian Bambuí limestone.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is quarrying for limestone, minor threats are collection for human consumption, agriculture and ranching.
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus levitestatus has a very wide range and it is often abundant where it occurs. It is therefore listed as Least Concern. However, it needs monitoring due to the potential threat from limestone quarrying.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus longicarpus Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
Melocactus macracanthos (Salm-Dyck) Link & Otto sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Curaçao; Bonaire; Aruba
iucn_habitat_names: Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands; Marine Coastal/supratidal - Coastal Caves/Karst; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in the Netherlands Antilles, on the islands of Aruba, Bonaire and Curaçao (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations from sea level to 100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs on rocky coastal limestone pavement, karst soils, and in spiny scrublands. It is pollinated by hummingbirds and dispersed by lizards.
iucn_doc_threats: The likely threats to this species are tourist and urban development and collection from the wild for ornamental purposes. In Aruba and Curaçao, part of the habitat used by this species  is subject to land conversion due to urban development including  buildings and recreation facilities.
iucn_doc_rationale: Despite having a restricted distribution and areas with highly fragmented suitable habitat, Melocactus macracanthos still has large subpopulations, is fairly abundant in some of them, and is present in protected areas. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus matanzanus León sec. Majure & al. 2022
iucn_threat_names: Scale Unknown/Unrecorded; Housing & urban areas; Avalanches/landslides; Intentional use (species is the target); Unspecified species
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Cuba with a disjunct distribution in Matanzas Province (Robledo et al. 2005) and in Santa Clara Province. It is known only from these two localities. It occurs at elevations between 200 and 300 m asl. The western population in Matanzas Province occupies an area of 0.32 km2 (Robledo et al. 2005).
iucn_doc_habitats: The western subpopulation occurs on serpentine soils, whereas the eastern one grows on ultramafic soils (Robledo et al. 2005, Gonzalez-Torres et al. 2005) in shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to the western subpopulation is bacterial infection and deforestation of Pinus caribaea (Robledo et al. 2005). The eastern subpopulation is affected by collection, habitat destruction, erosion and landslides (Gonzalez-Torres et al. 2005).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because the latest survey indicates there are less than 250 mature individuals. The national Red List assessment in Cuba correctly assessed this species as Critically Endangered in 2005, but conservation action measures were taken since then and it is recovering (Robledo and Enríquez 2010).

checklist_taxon: Melocactus mazelianus Říha sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Moist
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Work & other activities
iucn_doc_range: Melocactus mazelianus is found in the Colombian department of Vichada and in the Venezuelan states of Amazonas, Apure and Bolívar (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations between 50 and 200 m asl. Its distribution is very patchy, occurring in inselbergs (gneissic and granite rocky outcrops). Localities are separated by as much as 400 km.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species inhabits bare rocky outcrops. It occurs in highly isolated populations with likely very low genetic exchange (Nassar pers. comm. 2011). It is pollinated by hummingbirds.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats to this species are likely vandalism and removal for ornamental purposes.
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus mazelianus is listed as Least Concern because, although it has a restricted and patchy distribution, there are no known major threats affecting it. However, more research is needed to better establish its distribution, population size, and the threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus monvillianus Miq. sec. Britton & Rose 1922
Melocactus nagyi Z.Mészáros sec. Majure & al. 2022
Melocactus neryi K.Schum. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Brazil; Amazonas; Suriname
iucn_habitat_names: Savanna - Moist; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: This species distributed in Brazil in Amazonas, in Suriname in Nickerie and Saramacca, and in Venezuela in the states of Amazonas, Bolívar, and Guárico (Hunt  et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations between 100 and 500 m asl (Taylor 1991). It is known from many disjunct localities throughout its range. In the northern portion of the range it occurs in the Llanos and in the south in the Amazon forest (on rocky outcrops).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs on gneissic granitic rocky outcrops. It is pollinated by hummingbirds and dispersed by lizards (Nassar pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_threats: One potential threat may be burning of the grasslands in the savanna (Llanos of Venezuela). Another potential threat is vandalism and removal of the plant from the wild.
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus neryi is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, some subpopulations are large, and there are no known major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus octogonus J.Forbes sec. Britton & Rose 1922
Melocactus oreas Miq. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Other (free text); Pets/display animals, horticulture; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range: This species endemic to Bahia, Brazil, where it grows at 300-1,000 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in caatinga/northern campo rupestre (Chapada Diamantina) element: on more or less exposed, usually horizontal, crystalline/sandstone and granitic rocks (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: In the caatinga region the major threats are quarrying, cattle ranching and agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a relatively wide range and has many subpopulations. Only the part of the range occurring in caating needs conservation attention, but the species as a whole is listed under Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus pachyacanthus Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Desert - Hot
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is found near the Rio Salitre and in the region of Irecê, northern Bahia, Brazil. The distribution of the species is very restricted, and is limited to two locations and is severely fragmented. In Caatinga it occurs between 520 - 750 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs on Northern Rio São Francisco caatinga element on flat, exposed Bambuí limestone (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are loss of habitat and to a lesser extent collection for local use. Loss of habitat is mainly due to mining of limestone and agriculture.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is assessed as Vulnerable because it has a restricted and fragmented distribution (extent of occurrence is 6,500 km<sup>2</sup>) and there is continuing decline in its extent of occurrence, area of occupancy and extent and quality of habitat. The loss of habitat is mainly due to mining and agriculture.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus paucispinus Heimen & R.Raul sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Intentional use (species is the target); Persecution/control
iucn_doc_range: This species is found in the Chapada Diamantina and northern Serra do Espinhaço, northern to southern Bahia, Brazil. It grows at altitudes between 1,000 and 1,550 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in sand or quartz gravel, cerrado de altitude and campo rupestre.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is sand quarrying. The species is not tolerated by farmers, they dig up plants to avoid cattle getting hurt. The species is collected, mainly at the type locality.
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus paucispinus was previously listed as Endangered because at the time of the last assessment it was only known from a small area. Since then, many additional subpopulations have been found and the species, even though it is not abundant where it occurs, has a wide range. There are threats, such as collection and sand quarrying, but they are localized, and it occurs within a protected area. The species is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus pedernalensis M.M.Mejía & R.G.García sec. Majure & al. 2022
Melocactus perezassoi Areces sec. Majure & al. 2022
Melocactus peruvianus Vaupel sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Desert - Cold; Shrubland - Mediterranean-type Shrubby Vegetation
iucn_doc_range: Melocactus peruvianus is found on the border between Peru and Ecuador's El Oro province (Hunt et al. 2006, Castro-Cepero and Ceroni-Stuva 2010), and in the Peruvian Regions of Tumbes, Piura, Lambayeque, La Libertad, Ancash, Lima, and Arequipa. It grows at altitudes of 30 to 2,000 m asl (Castro-Cepero and Ceroni-Stuva 2010).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus peruvianus is listed as Least Concern because it is widely distributed, locally abundant, occurs in protected areas, and there are no major threats affecting it. Only one record is known from Ecuador, where it is probably extinct.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus praerupticola Areces sec. Majure & al. 2022
iucn_common_name: Melón de breña; Mélon espinoso; Melón espinoso
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: Melocactus praerupticola is found in the Dominican Republic, in the province of La Vega (Global Biodiversity Information Facility 2010). It is known only from one site, at an altitude of around 1,000 m (Cactuspro.com).
iucn_doc_habitats: Melocactus praerupicola grows on an unstable rocky cliff with poor water retention, exposed to the sun and the wind (Cactuspro.com).
iucn_doc_threats: Nothing is known about the threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Data Deficient because it is known from only one locality, and there is no information on population size and trends and if there are any threats affecting it. Further research is needed on the validity of this species as it is suggested that it may be a dwarf form of another, more widely distributed species.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus radoczii Z.Mészáros sec. Majure & al. 2022
Melocactus salvadorensis Werderm. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Housing & urban areas; Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Other (free text); Pets/display animals, horticulture; Food - animal; Food - human; Medicine - human & veterinary
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Bahia and Minas Gerais. It grows at elevations from 300 to 1,040 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is usually found on or adjacent to exposed gneiss/granitic rocks/inselbergs and in stony soil of the caatinga (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are habitat loss due to agriculture, cattle ranching, gathering of fuel wood, urbanization and collection of plants for ornamental use.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a relatively wide range, however the populations are decreasing steadily, and this is inferred based on observations made over the last 30 years. A decrease in 30% of the population is inferred, there are several threats throughout the species range and it is not within any protected areas, hence it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus santiagoensis D.Barrios & Majure sec. Majure & al. 2022
Melocactus schatzlii W.Till & R.Gruber sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: Melocactus schatzlii is found in Colombia (Boyaca, Santander) and a single valley in Venezuela (Lagunillas, Merida) (Hunt et al. 2006), between altitudes of 500 m and 2,200 m asl (Nassar et al. 2007). The estimated Extent of Occurrence is 20,000 km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: M. schatzlii grows on steep rocky valley slopes and spiny scrublands within xeric patches (Nassar et al. 2007). A predominantly outcrossing species, hummingbirds are the only pollinators responsible for outcrossing and gene flow events in this species. In their absence, M. schatzlii sets seeds by selfing (Nassar et al. 2007).
iucn_doc_threats: In Venezuela, the main threat to this species is urban development (Nassar pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because it has a restricted distribution (extent of occurrence less than 20,000 km<sup>2</sup>), it occurs at a few locations, and there is continuing decline in the extent and quality of its habitat due to urban expansion.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus sergipensis N.P.Taylor & Meiado sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
Melocactus smithii (Alexander) Buining ex G.D.Rowley sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Savanna - Moist
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Intentional use (species is the target); Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Roraima, Brazil and in Guyana (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in the Amazon forest and savanna on rock outcrops and riverine rocks.
iucn_doc_threats: One subpopulation in Brazil is threatened by collection, tourism, and road construction.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern because it is widespread, occurs in inaccessible places without roads, and there are no major threats. However, more research is needed on this species.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus stramineus Suringar sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: Melocactus stramineus is endemic to Aruba (Hunt et al. 2006). It occurs at elevations from sea level up to 100 m asl. This species is present in 2-5 locations. The extent of occurrence is estimated to be 52 km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: Melocactus stramineus occurs in rocky outcrops in co-existence with M. macracanthos and M. curvispinus.  The species is pollinated by hummingbirds and dispersed by lizards (Nassar pers. comm. 2011).
iucn_doc_threats: Development for tourism is likely the major threat to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although this species is present in a protected area, it is listed as Endangered because it has a very restricted distribution (extent of occurrence is 52 km<sup>2</sup>), it occurs at only a few locations (two to five locations), and there is a continuing decline from urban development that is affecting the extent and quality of its habitat.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus violaceus Pfeiff. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Bahia; Pernambuco; Rio Grande do Norte; Paraíba; Alagoas; Minas Gerais; Espírito Santo; Sergipe; Brazil
iucn_location_codes: BZE-BA; BZE-PE; BZE-RN; BZE-PB; BZE-AL; BZL-MG; BZL-ES; BZE-SE; BR
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Savanna - Dry
iucn_threat_names: Scale Unknown/Unrecorded; Recreational activities; Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas; Roads & railroads
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs from Rio Grande do Norte to Rio de Janeiro, at elevations from 0 to 1,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This widespread humid forest (Mata atlântica/restinga) and Northern campo rupestre (cerrado) element grows between shrubs in sand of the coastal restinga, riverine sand-dunes, and similar habitats further inland.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is habitat loss due to urbanization, tourism (resorts), road construction, artificial landscaping, and agro-industrial plantations of coconut, pineapple, sugar cane, eucalyptus and forestry.
iucn_doc_rationale: Melocactus violaceus has a wide range, occurring along the coast of Brazil from Rio de Janeiro northwards. In total, a 70% population loss is inferred based on historical land use change for tourist development. A 30% loss or higher is estimated over the past 15 years (or three generations). The habitat quantity and quality is being reduced due to many reasons, such as tourism, agroindustry, and urbanization. Hence, this cactus is listed as Vulnerable under criterion A2c.

checklist_taxon: Melocactus viridis Lawr. sec. Kew 2019
Mesechinopsis nakajimae Y.Itô sec. WFO 2019
Micranthocereus albicephalus (Buining & Brederoo) F.Ritter sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Savanna - Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry farming; Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Agro-industry plantations; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]
iucn_doc_range: Micranthocereus albicephalus is native from Serra do Espinhaço (Serra Geral) northern Minas Gerais and adjacent southernmost Bahia, at elevations of 800 to 1,200 m asl. The Extent of Occurrence is below 20,000 km2. The Area of Occupancy is estimated to be less than 2,000 km².
iucn_doc_habitats: Micranthocereus albicephalus is a northern campo rupestre element, where it grows between crystalline rocks and on cliffs (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for Micranthocereus albicephalus is loss of habitat due to logging for charcoal and the conversion of its habitat for agriculture or plantations of Eucalyptus and Pines. Man-made fires also pose a threat as they kill this cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Micranthocereus albicephalus has an extent of occurrence and area of occupancy below the thresholds for Vulnerable, the species is found at less than 10 locations, and the habitat is threatened and continuously declining due to fires, forest plantations, logging and agriculture.

checklist_taxon: Micranthocereus alvinii (M.Machado & Hofacker) N.P.Taylor & M.Lowry sec. Taylor & al. 2023
Micranthocereus aureispinus F.Ritter sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
Micranthocereus auri-azureus Buining & Brederoo sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Dry
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry farming; Agro-industry plantations; Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: Micranthocereus auriazureus is native from Grao Mogol Cristalia and the northernmost part of Botumirim, northern Minas Gerais. It is endemic to the core area within Minas Gerais. It grows at elevations of 750-1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus is an element of south-eastern campo rupestre (Grão Mogol, Cristalia and Botumirim), where it grows between crystalline rocks and in quartz gravel (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: Micranthocereus auriazureus is mainly affected by deforestation for charcoal production, conversion of the habitat into agricultural land or Eucalyptus plantations and man-made fires are the main threats to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Micranthocereus auriazureus is listed as Endangered due to its very restricted distribution, the extent of occurrence below the threshold for this category and the species has less than five locations. Although more than half of the population is within a protected area, the quality of habitat for the species outside is continuously declining due to fires, deforestation, forest plantations and agriculture.

checklist_taxon: Micranthocereus flaviflorus Buining & Brederoo sec. Taylor & Zappi 2004
iucn_habitat_names: Savanna - Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Mining & quarrying; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in northern Bahia at altitudes between 700 and 1,200 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004). The extent of occurrence is just above 20,000 km<sup>2</sup> and the area of occupancy is smaller than 2,000 km<sup>2</sup>.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs in the northern campo rupestre (Chapada Diamantina) element, in the campo rupestre/caatinga ecotone on quartz sand or sandstone between low shrubs (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: Quarrying of sand, fires, and deforestation for small-scale farming are the main threats. The plant can regrow from stumps, but regeneration is affected by fire.
iucn_doc_rationale: The area of occupancy of Micranthocereus flaviflorus is smaller than 2,000 km2, and the extent and quality of its habitat are declining due to sand quarrying and agriculture. Fire is another threat and individuals have already been lost. The number of locations is higher than 10, and the populations are not severely fragmented. The species is therefore listed as Near Threatened approaching B2ab(iii,v).

checklist_taxon: Micranthocereus hofackerianus (P.J.Braun & Esteves) M.Machado sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_synonyms: Arrojadoa multiflora F.Ritter ssp. hofackeriana P.J.Braun & Esteves
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: This cactus is endemic to the campo rupestre of central Bahia (Brazil), near the towns of Piata and Boninal. It grows at altitudes between 1,500 and 2,000 m asl. The extent of occurrence is certainly below 5,000 km2 and the area of occupancy is smaller than 500 km2.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus is found in campo rupestre on sandy soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats are quarrying of the sand and collection of the plants for ornamental use. Fire is a minor threat and impacts the reproductive capacity of the plants.
iucn_doc_rationale: Micranthocereus hofackerianus has a restricted extent of occurrence (less than 5000 km2). It occurs at no more than five locations, and quarrying of sand is affecting the quality of its habitat. Fires are reducing its reproductive capacity, and the collection of seeds and whole plants for ornamental use is further reducing the populations. These threats are understood, but have not ceased and at least some of them are not reversible. The species is therefore listed as Endangered under criterion B.

checklist_taxon: Micranthocereus polyanthus (Werderm.) Backeb. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Dry
iucn_doc_range: Micranthocereus polyanthus is only known from the municipality of Caetité in southern Bahia, Brazil. This species grows at elevations of 900 to 1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus is an element of the northern campo rupestre, where it is found growing in quartz sand amongst crystalline rocks (Taylor and Zappi 2004). Generation length is 10 years.
iucn_doc_threats: Habitat loss due to quarrying of sand, mining of minerals, and urban expansion are the major threats affecting Micranthocereus polyanthus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Micranthocereus polyanthus occurs in three locations within a restricted range (extent of occurrence less than 3,000 km2). Its population has been reduced by more than 50 percent in the last 30 years due to quarrying, mining, and urbanization. These impacts are not reversible and have not ceased. There are no more than 2,500 mature individuals in total, with less than 250 per subpopulation. Hence, the species is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Micranthocereus purpureus (Gürke) F.Ritter sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Dry
iucn_doc_range: Micranthocereus purpureus is endemic to Brazil, where it occurs in Bahia at elevations of 350 to 1,900 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004). It also occurs in high altitude areas in the mountains of Chapada Diamantina and Serra do Sincora.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus occurs on crystalline rocks in campo rupestre and its ecotones with caatinga and cerrado, in eastern flanks and highest peaks of the Chapada Diamantina (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is common throughout its range with a stable population, there are no major threats affecting it, and part of its population is protected within a national park. Micranthocereus purpureus is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_fullName: Micranthocereus streckeri Van Heek & Van Criek.
checklist_taxon: Micranthocereus streckeri Van Heek & Van Criek. sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Savanna - Dry
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Increase in fire frequency/intensity; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Recreational activities
iucn_doc_range: Micranthocereus steckeri is restricted to the west of Seabra in central Bahia, Brazil. It is only known from one site, where it grows at elevations of 800 to 1,250 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is a northern campo rupestre element, where it grows on rocks and quartz gravel and sand (Taylor and Zappi 2004). It has a generation length of 10 to 15 years.
iucn_doc_threats: The habitat of this cactus is being altered, as it is above a main highway (Taylor and Zappi 2004). There is a religious site within the species' range, and the species is  negatively affected by the people frequenting the site as well as by  clearing of  vegetation for improved access. Logging and fires are  additional  threats in the area, while collection of plants and seeds is  considered to be a minor threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Micranthocereus streckeri has a very restricted range. It is known from a single location in Bahia, Brazil, where its total extent of occurrence is no more than 100 km2. The population is declining due to factors that are known, but have not ceased. The species is therefore listed as Critically Endangered.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the driest area of eastern Guatemala, and is found at elevations of 100-500 m <span lang="EN-GB">asl (M. Véliz pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in semi-arid environments (Ordónez 2003).
iucn_doc_threats: The expansion of the agricultural frontier for plantations of neem, Aloe vera, limes and melon is the major threat (M. Véliz pers. comm. 2009).
iucn_doc_rationale: There are around 65 mature individuals of Myrtillocactus eichlamii in each of the known subpopulations, and the overall estimate infers no more than 200-300 mature individuals. Due to ongoing habitat loss for agriculture, the population is extremely threatened and rapidly declining with an estimated loss of 25% in the last 3 years, hence the species is listed as Critically Endangered.

iucn_locations: Curaçao; Sint Maarten (Dutch part); Sint Eustatius; Colombia; Bonaire; Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of; Saba; Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba; Aruba; Saint Martin (French part)
iucn_location_codes: CW; SX; NLA-SE; CO; NLA-BO; VE; NLA-SA; BQ; AW; MF
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: Opuntia curassavica occurs in the Netherlands Antilles, Colombia, Lesser Antilles, Venezuela, Curacao, Aruba, and Island Tortuga (Hunt et al. 2006, Ponce 1989). It may be present in Redonda (Howard 1974) but this seems to be a dubious record and is not considered for the assessment (L. Majure pers. comm. 2011). According to the literature, this species is present in the Lesser Antilles but specific information on the island localities is missing. The distribution in the eastern Lesser Antilles may be presumed. No information is available for Colombia (Albesiano, Adriana,S. pers. comm.).
iucn_doc_habitats: It occurs in rocky outcrops in coastal areas in tropical dry shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: In some parts of its range, this species occurs in highly populated areas. The only suitable habitat is in patches of vegetation and continuous decline in the quality of the habitat is observed in these places. The Curacao and Aruba subpopulations are highly affected by urban development. Habitat for this species on some islands is largely undisturbed, including the supbopulation in Bonaire, which is believed to be the best place for its survival  (J. Nassar pers. comm. 2011). There is no information on threats to this species in Colombia.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Near Threatened due impacts of urban development across much of its range.  Even though some subpopulations are located in well protected areas, such as Bonaire Island, others are in highly populated and urban areas such as La Guaira, Venezuela and Santa Marta, Colombia.  Although its area of occupancy (3000 km<sup>2</sup>) is too large to be considered threatened, this species is close to meeting criteria that would qualify it as Vulnerable (B criteria). Research is needed to provide a better estimate of the current distribution and population size of this species, and rates of decline.

iucn_doc_range: This species is restricted to some fragments of the summits of La Trinidad and Álvarez mountain ranges, in San Luis Potosí, Mexico. The extent of occurrence is ca 1,895 km², and it is fragmented into three subpopulations with an area of occupancy of ca 20-50 km².
iucn_doc_habitats:
It is found in areas of disturbed grassland of Bouteloua gracilis or Heteropogon contortus, and chaparral of Quercus striatula or Q. edwardii on the tops of mountains. The soils are relatively deep-dark clay, with limestone outcrops.
The cladodes of this species die back in the winter months and regrow in the spring as an adaptation to withstand freezing and the impacts of fire.
iucn_doc_threats: The natural vegetation is almost totally modified, due to livestock raising.  The species is under some pressure due to its medicinal uses.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species qualifies as Endangered because it has a small extent of occurrence (less than 1,895 km²), its population comprises three disjunct subpopulations comprising three locations, and the quality of its habitat is expected to decline in the long term due to the intensification of human activities, and there is significant harvest occuring from the wild.

iucn_doc_range: Opuntia triacantha is found in Cuba, Lesser Antilles, Puerto Rico and the and the Unites States Virgin Islands (Hunt et al. 2006). It is estimated occur at elevations from 0 to 10 m, although there is no precise elevation information available. Although the extent of occurrence is approximately 85,000 km2, the area of occupancy is conservatively estimated to be less than 500 km2 (G. Gann pers. comm 2011, L. Majure pers. comm. 2011). There are approximately 15 locations.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs on rocky substrate not too far inland (from record Mori et al. 4 march 2007; voucher number: 26693 NY; and Britton and Rose 1919) and grows to 1.5 meters tall (Hunt et. al 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: Threats to Opuntia triacantha include habitat destruction due to urban development, mining, and the invasive moth species Cactoblastis cactorum (Johnson and Stiling 1998).  Fire may also be a threat in dry shrubland habitat.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Near Threatened due to its restricted distribution (small Area of Occupancy), threats to habitat, and direct population decline due to the effects of the invasive moth species Cactoblastis cactorum.  However, more research is needed to estimate the current population size and trends; this additional information may provide sufficient information to qualify the species for a higher threat category.

iucn_doc_rationale: Oreocereus hempelianus is listed as Least Concern because it is wide-ranging and locally abundant, and there are no major threats.

iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in Andean shrubland on rocky soils.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is locally threatened by illegal collection, but this is localized.
iucn_doc_rationale: Oreocereus leucotrichus is listed as Least Concern given that it has a wide range, is abundant, and there are no major threats.

The species has been collected in the state of Zacatecas in tropical deciduous forests (Carrillo-Reyes et al. 2001).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits thorn forest, xerophyllous scrub, and tropical deciduous forest (Cue-Bar et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to the species. In some areas subpopulations are threatened by coastal development for tourism and urban expansion.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is abundant, occurs within many protected areas and has no major threats.

checklist_taxon: Parodia allosiphon (Marchesi) N.P.Taylor sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_locations: Uruguay; Rio Grande do Sul; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Temperate; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This cactus occurs in Rio Grande do Sul (Brazil) and in Uruguay, at elevations of up to 350 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops, in grasslands or pampas.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are trampling due cattle and sheep foraging and forestry.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia allosiphon has a small range (extent of occurrence is approximately 1,000 km2) which is severely fragmented and the population is declining. It is mainly affected by foraging and trampling by cattle and sheep, and these threats will not cease in the near future. Hence, it is listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(iii,v).

checklist_taxon: Parodia aureicentra Backeb. sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Salta province, Argentina, where it grows at 2,000 to 3,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in rocky areas in prepuna vegetation.
iucn_doc_threats: Parodia aureicentra is currently affected by mining (flagstone extraction), but this is still very localised. Road construction will be an additional threat in the near future.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia aureicentra has a small extent of occurrence (EOO = 2,000 km2). At present the species is abundant and the subpopulations are not severely fragmented. However, this species is substrate specific and the substrate where it grows is being exploited for flagstone extraction, but the threat is still localised. In addition, in the near future is very likely that it will be affected by road construction. It is therefore listed as Near Threatened under criterion B2ab (i,ii,iii,v) .

checklist_taxon: Parodia ayopayana Cárdenas sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it is distributed in Cochabamba and La Paz, at elevations of 2,100 to 2,700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in seasonally dry inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: No major threats are known for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia ayopayana has a relatively small range (extent of occurrence 2,000 km2), however it is abundant and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Parodia buiningii (Buxb.) N.P.Taylor sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018

iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Temperate; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)

iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul) and Uruguay (Salto, Artigas and Rivera), at elevations from 150 to 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky outcrops in grasslands or pampas. Generation length is 10 years.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this species are grazing, illegal collection and in the near future land use change for Eucalyptus plantations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia buningii is listed as Critically Endangered because a population decline of 80% in thirty years is estimated considering both, past and future (generation legth is ten years). Some subpopulations have already been extirpated as a result of grazing and illegal collection, and these threats continue to rapidly reduce the population. In Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil, Eucalyptus plantations are expected to become a major threat as they most likely expand in the near future to the area where the species occurs.

iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops in Araucaria forest.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats, there is some cattle activity within the protected area where the species occurs but the impact is minor.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a very small range (extent of occurrence 1,200 km2), however part of it is within a protected area, the population is stable and there are no major threats. Hence, Parodia carambeiensis is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Parodia chrysacanthion (K.Schum.) Backeb. sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Jujuy, Argentina, where it occurs at elevation of 500-2,500m asl (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: It grows on rocks that show between the forest  (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: The species is locally removed to be sold as an ornamental, however in very small amounts that does not affect the population.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though the species has a small range, the only threat is localised and it causes no declines on the popualtion. There fore the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Parodia claviceps (F.Ritter) F.H.Brandt sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
Parodia columnaris Cárdenas sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species grows in Bolivia in Cochabamba (Campero, between Pérez and Puente Taperas and at Angosto de Pérez) and in Santa Cruz (Vallegrande, Caballero). The species is currently known from five subpopulations, however it is probably distributed more widely. It occurs at elevations of 900-1,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky outcrops in dry inter-Andean valleys. Parodia columnaris is sympatric with Corryocactus pulquinesis.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats is habitat destruction through subsistence farming and goat grazing, the goats nibble on the cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a limited extent of occurrence (EOO = 1,600 km<sup>2</sup>), based on threat the five known subpopulations can be treated as five locations, however it is very likely that the species is more widely distributed as the area has not been intensively explored. There is decline in the population, but this is believe not to be sufficient to trigger a threatened listing. However, until more expeditions are carried out in the area and with the current state of knowledge of the species, it should be listed as Nearly Threat as it could be approaching a threatened listing under criterion B1ab(iii,v).

checklist_taxon: Parodia comarapana Cárdenas sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: Parodia comarapana occurs in Bolivia in Santa Cruz and in Florida in the north of Comarapa (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of 1,500-2,300m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in humid rocky outcrops in grassland and inter-Andean valleys (P. mairanana).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats is habitat destruction through subsistence farming and agriculture, it grows on rocks which confers it some protection from land clearance for cultivation.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a relatively small extent of occurrence (EOO = 1,600 km2), however the it is very abundant and its habitat preference protects it from land clearing for agricultural purposes. Even though there is a decline in the population this is not sufficient to trigger a threatened listing and there are more than 10 locations. This species, as is currently taxonomically treated is listed as Least Concern. When Parodia comarapana is treated as a separate entity from P. mairanana it will very likely by listed under a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Parodia commutans F.Ritter sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018

iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: Parodia commutans is endemic to Bolivia, it is found in Chuquisaca and Tarija (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of  2,300-3,500m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in rocky areas cover by very little scrub, in very dry  inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: There are very little threats for this species, few goats that can not really harm them because of their challenging spines. In addition, the land where it is found is not suitable for agriculture and there is low human population in the area.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia commutans is listed as Least Concern because even though it has a relatively small extent of occurrence (EOO = 2,800 km2), the species is abundant and there are no threats affecting its population.

checklist_taxon: Parodia crassigibba (F.Ritter) N.P.Taylor sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+

iucn_doc_range: Parodia crassigibba is a Brazilian endemic, occurring in Rio Grande do Sul (Hunt et al.  2006) at elevations of up to 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops, in grasslands or pampas. It prefers to grow on hill tops.
iucn_doc_threats: For the remaining fragments of habitat the major threats are goat and sheep grazing. Goat trampling is a major threat for Parodia crassigibba.
iucn_doc_rationale: It is estimated that 80% of the population of Parodia crassigibba has already been extirpated as a result of land use change for grazing, and the remaining fragments of habitat where the species occur are at risk from the same threat. Hence it is listed as Critically Endangered.

iucn_doc_range: This species has been observed in Jujuy and Tucumán (Argentina), and in Tarija and Santa Cruz (Bolivia), from 900 to 1,600 m asl. It may also occur in Salta.
iucn_doc_habitats: It occurs in rocky outcrops within the forests of seasonally dry inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern due to lack of major threats and presumed large populations. However, not much is known about this species. The known localities are far away from each other, and it is not known whether they represent isolated populations or if the species is common over all of its extent of occurrence. More studies are needed to realistically assess this species in the future.

iucn_doc_range: Parodia fusca is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Rio Grande do Sul (Hunt et al.  2006) at elevations of up to 300m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops, in grasslands pampas with shrubs.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are grazing and forestry of Eucalyptus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Several subpopulations of this species have been extirpated and the major threats continue to affect the subpopulations. Land use change for Eucalyptus plantations is a major threat and it is very likely to expand to the area where the species occurs, the populations are sparse and a reduction of 40% in a time span of 30 years (generation length is 10 years) considering both, past and future is inferred. The species is listed as Vulnerable, but further field work is needed.

checklist_taxon: Parodia gaucha M.Machado & Larocca sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: Parodia gaucha is endemic from Brazil, occurring in Rio Grande do Sul, at elevations of 80-850 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky outcrops and stony fields, in grasslands or  pampas.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this cactus are cattle and sheep ranching, plants are trampled and eaten. Fire are poses a threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: This recently described species is known from a small area (extent of occurrence 400 km2), is not abundant and is naturally severely fragmented. There are major threats in the area where it occurs and the population is affected by them. The species is been monitored. Parodia gaucha is here assessed as Endangered.

iucn_doc_range: Parodia hausteiniana is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in Cochabamba near Mizque (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of 2,100-2,500m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky outcrops in inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat is land use change due to both small holder and medium scale agriculture to provide produce to Cochabamba, the scale does not reach agro-industry.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence is 300km<sup>2</sup>), there are three known subpopulations but there are probably many more. This cactus grows on rocky outcrops and this confers it some protection as these are left intact in arable lands, however the subpopulations are probably affect by goats, even though this poses a minor threat. In the known locations there are no more than 100 mature individuals, at most 20 have been observed in each subpopulation, based on this observations the species is listed as Endangered. However, fieldwork is necessary in order to establish the extent of the species range and the impact goats have on the population.

It is not abundant and has several ongoing major threats that have drastically reduced the species' population and its habitat. Based on direct observation and a decline in habitat quality and quantity, an 80% decline is estimated to occur over a time frame of 30 years (generation length is 10 years), taking into account both the past and the future.

iucn_doc_range: This species can be found in Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul) and in Uruguay. It occurs at elevations between 150 and 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on rocky outcrops in grasslands or pampas.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats to this species are extraction, grazing, agriculture, forestry (Eucalyptus) and desertification processes that are intensified by agricultural activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia mueller-melchersii has a small range and is not abundant. Like many other species in the area, it is under intense pressure due to land use change for Eucalyptus plantations and cattle ranching. A population decline of at least 50% is estimated to occur over a timespan of 30 years (generation length is ten years), including past and future. Several subpopulations have already disappeared, and others continue to decline due to threats that are understood, but have not ceased and are not reversible. Hence, the species is listed as Endangered under criterion A4ac.

checklist_fullName: Parodia muricata (Link & Otto ex Pfeiff.) Hofacker
checklist_taxon: Parodia muricata (Link & Otto ex Pfeiff.) Hofacker sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
