iucn_doc_range: Parodia muricata is endemic from Brazil, occurring in  Rio Grande do Sul (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of 300 to 700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky outcrops, in grasslands (pampas).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are grazing, agriculture and introduction of exotic species, like pines which are planted at the side of the highway probably to reduced wind speed. The pine branches fall on the plants providing unnecessary shade and affecting flowering and seed dispersal.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a small range (extent of occurrence is 2,000 km2) and is not abundant. The populations have been reduced and continue to decline. A 50% population lost is estimated to occur in a time span of 30 years (generation length 10 years), considering both past and future due to several threats. Hence, Parodia muricata is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Parodia neobuenekeri (F.Ritter) Anceschi & Magli sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
Parodia neohorstii (S.Theun.) N.P.Taylor sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Grassland - Temperate; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Nomadic grazing; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming; Recreational activities; Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is reported from Brazil in Rio Grande do Sul (Hunt et al. 2006), growing at elevations of up to 300 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops, on hill tops, in grasslands or pampas.
iucn_doc_threats: Major threats are grazing, agriculture, and goat trampling. The area where the species occurs has potential for tourism development, and is a very popular place to visit, so there is demand for specimens for ornaments.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a small range (1,500 km<sup>2</sup>), it is not abundant, the populations have been and continue to be under high pressure from goat trampling, and the area where the species occurs has tourist potential and may be extensively transformed in the near future. Considering the past and present, it is estimated that most of the population (80%) will be lost if the current rate of decline continues and the tourist development begins. Hence the species is listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Parodia nigrispina (K.Schum.) F.H.Brandt sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_doc_range: Pin and Simon (2004) report this species as endemic to the Cordillera department of Paraguay. Hunt et al. (2006) report it in Paraguay near Paraguarí, between Carapegua and Acahay. It occurs at elevations between 100 and 400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky outcrops in open areas (Pin and Simon 2006) of selva paranaense.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this cactus is collection for commercialization.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia nigrispina is listed as Endangered under criterion B1ab(v) because it has a restricted and severely fragmented range (extent of occurrence is 3,000 km2) and is under pressure due to illegal collection for local commercialization. Local extinctions have already occurred.

checklist_taxon: Parodia nivosa Backeb. sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Roads & railroads
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Quebrada del Toro and Santa Rosa de Tastil in Salta province, Argentina, where it occurs at 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on rocky and silty soils in the pre-puna habitat.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by extraction for ornamental purposes and road construction.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia nivosa has a very restricted range of less than 100 km2 which is considered as one location. The area where it occurs is under pressure from road construction. Also, the species is extracted for its trade as an ornamental. These threats have caused population declines and will not cease in the near future. Hence, it is listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Parodia nothorauschii D.R.Hunt sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is only known from Uruguay, where it occurs in Rivera and in Cuchilla Negra (Hunt et al. 2006). It may also occur in Brazil, north of Libramento.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in basaltic soils in rocky, flat areas.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats to this species are illegal collecting and urbanization.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia nothorauschii has a restricted range (extent of occurrence is 200 km2), and there is continuing decline in the extent and quality of its habitat and the number of mature individuals. It is inferred that in Uruguay, illegal collection and urbanization have led to an 80% decline over the past 30 years (generation length 10 years). These threats have not ceased and are not reversible. Hence it is listed as Critically Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Parodia ocampoi Cárdenas sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it grows in Cochabamba and in Campero near Puente Arce (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of 1,700 to 1,900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in inter-Andean valleys on rocky outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat is a major highway that runs in this valley. When the road is damaged by rain, a diversion is temporary enabled and the plants in the area are affected. This can presumably cause extreme fluctuations in the subpopulations because the plants are ran over and dug out, however, the subpopulations are likely to recover after this disturbance. It is an intermittent threat that appears every rainy season.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia ocampoi has a restricted range (extent of occurrence approximately 300 km2), there is no severe fragmentation, and the only major threat is intermittent, probably causing a temporary decline in the affected subpopulations. The species is known from more than 10 locations. It is listed as Least Concern. However, it would benefit from monitoring to assess to what extent it is affected by the temporary threat.

checklist_taxon: Parodia otaviana Cárdenas sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species grows in Bolivia in Potosi, Linares and Pampa de Otavi (Hunt et al. 2006) at 3,100-3,400m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Parodia otaviana grows in prepuna on rocky areas.
iucn_doc_threats: Only part of its range is affected by land use change for subsistence agriculture, it prefers rocky areas which confers it some protection.
iucn_doc_rationale: Even though the species has a restricted range (extent of occurrence = 500 km<sup>2</sup>) it is very abundant, easily regenerates and has localized threats in a small portion of its range. The species is here listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Parodia oxycostata (Buining & Brederoo) Hofacker sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_locations: Uruguay; Rio Grande do Sul; Argentina; Paraguay; Brazil
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Temperate; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: Parodia oxycostata is reported from Brazil in Rio Grande do Sul, as well as from Argentina, Paraguay and Uruguay, at elevations of up to 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on rocky outcrops and stony fields, in grasslands or pampas. The generation length is 10 years.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are grazing, agriculture, illegal collection, and  forestry.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia oxycostata has a very wide range (extent of occurrence 120,000 km2). Significant reductions in some subpopulations are ongoing but part of the range is not under pressure. An overall population decline of at least 30% in a time span of 30 years, considering past and future, is estimated. The generation length of the species is 10 years. Hence, it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Parodia pontesiana Hofacker sec. ???
Parodia prestoensis F.H.Brandt sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic from Bolivia where it is found growing in Chuquisaca (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of 2,800-3,100m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Paradio prestoensis occurs in inter-Andean valleys on rocky outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: There is a new road and bridge being built in the area where Paradio prestoensis grows that probably had an impact on the population, but this only affected the species locally.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a restricted range (extent of occurrence = 900 km2), the species is fairly common in the few locations it is known from and even though there have been localized threats in the past the popualtion does not have a continuing decline. Paradio prestoensis occurs within a reserve. Hence the species is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Parodia procera F.Ritter sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in Chuquisaca, Potosi and in Tarija (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: There is no information on the threats for this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: The exact range extent of Parodia procera is unknown. One of the subpopulations grows in pristine vegetation, the rest of the subpopulations are in a very remote area that is only accessible on-foot and the trip takes one day. There are human settlements in this area and the current threats for the species and its population trends are unknown. Hence, the species is listed as Data Deficient.

checklist_taxon: Parodia rechensis (Buining) F.H.Brandt sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Work & other activities; Housing & urban areas; Named species
iucn_doc_range: Parodia rechensis is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Rio Grande do Sul (Hunt et al. 2006), at elevations of close to 1,100 m asl
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on a hill top (single location), in forest with Araucaria.
iucn_doc_threats: Major threats for this species are urbanization, habitat modification as a result of people collecting large amounts of pine-nuts from Araucaria and using the paths close to the population, and mining in the area. There has been an introduction of pines that give unwanted shade to the plants. The species is very difficult to cultivate and none or very little population regeneration is observed.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Critically Endangered because it is known from a single very small locality (extent of occurrence and area of occupancy =0.015 km<sup>2</sup>), the population is decreasing, and there are many major threats affecting it.

checklist_taxon: Parodia ritteri Buining sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it is found in Chuquisaca, Potosí and Tarija, at elevations of 2,300 to 3,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in prepuna on rocky slopes.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia ritteri has a relatively small range (extent of occurrence 3,900 km2). However, it is abundant and there are no major threats. It is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Parodia schwebsiana (Werderm.) Backeb. sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_habitat_names: Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: Parodia schwebsiana is endemic to Bolivia, where it is found in Cochabamba (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of 3,000-3,200m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: Parodia schwebsiana inhabits inter-Andean valleys, it grows on rocky outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for Parodia schwebsiana are urbanization and intensive farming, the number of subpopulations is probably decreasing, however regeneration is good. In the areas close to the city the outcrops where this species grows are blasted to clear land for construction. In agricultural fields, however, the outcrops are generally left untouched.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia schwebsiana has a restricted range (extent of occurrence = 300 km2) in the surrounding areas of Cochabamba. Even though there are threats that cause declines in the population, the species is very abundant and the number of locations is far more than 10. There are numerous rocky outcrops in the area where this cactus grows, particularly in the areas outside Cochabamba. The species is therefore listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Parodia stockingeri (Prestlé) Hofacker & P.J.Braun sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Temperate; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in  Rio Grande do Sul (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of between 300 to 400 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on rocky outcrops, in grasslands or pampas.
iucn_doc_threats: The major to this species threats are grazing because cattle trample on the plants killing them and forestry of Eucalyptus plantations.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a small range with an extent of occurrence of 800 km<sup>2</sup> and the estimated number of mature individuals is around 500. There are four locations and two major threats, forestry and farming, cause a continuing decline in the habitat quality and number of mature individuals. Hence this species is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Parodia taratensis Cárdenas sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_habitat_names: Grassland - Subtropical/Tropical High Altitude; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in Cochabamba, at 2,500-3,200m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in puna vegetation and in seasonally dry inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is common and is not affected by any major threats.

checklist_taxon: Parodia tuberculata Cárdenas sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_threat_names: Housing & urban areas; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: Parodia tuberculata is endemic from Bolivia where it occurs in Chuquisaca and in Potosi (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of 2,800-3,400m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This plant grows in pre-puna on rocky outcrops.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this cactus are urbanization and small holder farming.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia tuberculata has a relatively restricted range (extent of occurrence = 6,000 km2) in the surrounding areas of Sucre. Even though there are threats that cause declines in the population, the number of locations is far more than 10. There are numerous rocky outcrops (species' habitat) in the area where this cactus grows, particularly in the areas outside Sucre. The species is listed as Least Concern but it would benefit from monitoring the population trends because of the effects of urbanization.

checklist_taxon: Parodia unguiculata Diers sec. POWO 2017+
Parodia warasii (F.Ritter) F.H.Brandt sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Temperate; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Moist Lowland; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_doc_range: Parodia warsii is endemic from Brazil, occurring in Rio Grande do Sul (Hunt et al. 2006) at elevations of 800-1,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on walls in mixed Araucaria forest and deciduous forest.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats for this cactus are landslides resulting from agriculture erosion. Some potential threats are rappel sport and dam construction.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very narrow range with an extent of occurrence of 800 km<sup>2</sup> and four locations. The subpopulations are declining due to landslides. The fact that the species grows on walls provide some protection, but development of extreme sports such a rappelling in the area and the construction of dams are very feasible potential threats. Hence it is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Parodia werdermanniana (Herter) N.P.Taylor sec. Anceschi & Magli 2018
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Agro-industry plantations
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Uruguay, where it only occurs in a very restricted range (extent of occurrence =35km2) in Tacuarembó (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows at elevations between 50 and 200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs on mountain tables in sandstone cliffs (up to 60 m), as well as in vertical surfaces at lower elevations. Almost all subpopulations are facing south or southeast.
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats to this species are afforestation (mainly Eucalyptus) and illegal collecting. The collection of specimens is usually focused on individuals of intermediate age.
iucn_doc_rationale: Parodia werdermanniana is listed as Critically Endangered under criterion B1ab(iii,v) because it has a very restricted and severely fragmented range (extent of occurrence is 35 km2), and there has been a continuing decline in habitat quality and the number of mature individuals. The population has experienced a reduction of approximately 50% over the past 30 years due to afforestation and illegal collecting, and this reduction is likely to persist in the future as the threats have not ceased.

checklist_taxon: Pediocactus bradyi L.D.Benson sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_common_name: Brady's Hedgehog Cactus; Brady's Pincushion Cactus
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Recreational activities; Tourism & recreation areas; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Coconino County, in northern Arizona. It grows at elevations of 100 to 1,200 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in desert shrubland.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats for Pediocactus bradyi are overcollecting for the horticultural trade, highway construction and maintenance, off-road vehicles and grazing.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is considered to be Near Threatened as it is close to qualifying for Vulnerable (B criteria). It occurs in a naturally small population (>300 individuals) where it is potentially threatened by future land use changes - thus requiring some level of management to avoid future extirpation. It has a narrow range, and even though there is some pressures on the population, it is locally protected and there is no evidence of rapid decline and its been managed by government agencies (Bureau of Land Management and US Fish and Wildlife Service).

checklist_taxon: Pediocactus despainii S.L.Welsh & Goodrich sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_common_name: San Rafael Cactus; Despain's Pincushion Cactus
iucn_synonyms: Pediocactus bradyi L.D.Benson ssp. despainii (S.L.Welsh & Goodrich) Hochstätter
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the state of Utah in the US, where it occurs at elevations of 1,500 to 1,800 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on gravel covered hillsides of clay in pinyon-juniper woodland.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collection and off road vehicles are the main threats for the species. Given the size of the population illegal collection pose a real threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has very narrow range (extent of occurrence is 400 km<sup>2</sup>), is not abundant, it is threatened by illegal collection, however, the species is protected and has a recovery and management plan. Hence based on the species range it is listed as Near Threatened (almost qualifies for listing as threatened under criterion B1ab(v)).

checklist_taxon: Pediocactus knowltonii L.D.Benson sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_threat_names: Droughts; Intentional use (species is the target); Scale Unknown/Unrecorded
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species occurs in northwestern New Mexico in San Juan County, in the United States, at elevations of 1,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in forest/woodland, pinyon-juniper woodland, in tertiary alluvial deposits that have formed gravelly, dark, sandy loams on slopes or hills. It is found under the shade of trees and shrubs.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats to this species are illegal collection, urban development and droughts.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very narrow range (extent of occurrence and area of occupancy approximately 0.1 km<sup>2</sup>) and there is only one location. It has suffered from drastic declines in the past and even though it has recovered there is an ongoing decrease and extreme fluctuations in the population. The species is protected, but still it is affected by illegal collection. The current trend of the population suggests further declines in the next years. Hence is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Pediocactus nigrispinus (Hochstätter) Hochstätter sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_synonyms: Pediocactus simpsonii (Engelm.) Britton & Rose var. nigrispinus Hochstätter
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Eastern Washington, Eastern Oregon and West-Central Idaho in the United States. It grows at elevations of 1,500 to 3,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in gravelly soils and lava, in sage brush, pinyon-juniper and conifer forests.
iucn_doc_threats: Part of the range might be affected by cattle ranching, however no major threats are known.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pediocactus nigrispinus has a wide range, is locally abundant, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pediocactus paradinei B.W.Benson sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_common_name: Kaibab Pediocactus; Kaibab Plains Cactus; Park Hedgehog Cactus; Park Pincushion-cactus
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Roads & railroads; Supression in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Northern Arizona, in Houserock Valley and the Peraia plateau, Coconino County and doubtfully in Mohave County, in the United States, at elevations of 1,500 to 1,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in forest/woodland, woodland-conifer, shrubland/chaparral in open pinyon-juniper woodlands and sagebrush valleys on Kaibab limestone gravels.
iucn_doc_threats: This species is threatened by illegal collecting, fire suppression, and road construction. Fire suppression results in the accumulation of organic matter, because of this accumulation when wild fires occur they are extremely hot and they burn the plants, causing many times 100% mortality of individuals.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a narrow range (extent of occurrence is approximately 1,600 km2) and less than five or less locations.  There is evidence of decline in most of the subpopulations as a result of fire and illegal trade. Hence Pediocactus paradinei is listed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Pediocactus peeblesianus (Croizat) L.D.Benson sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_common_name: Navajo Pincushion Cactus; Peebles' Pediocactus; Peebles' Navajo Cactus
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in northern Arizona (United States) in the Navajoan Desert in the vicinity of Colorado and Little Colorado rivers on region of Grand Canyon. It grows at elevations between 1,200 and 1,560 m (Benson 1982).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in alluvial hills on the upper slopes in desert and in sandy clay. It grows in salt bush scrub, sage and desert grassland.
iucn_doc_threats: Some of the major threats are grazing and illegal collection.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pediocactus peeblesianus has relatively wide range. It has many subpopulations, and even though there are some threats, especially grazing, these are not relevant enough to trigger its classification in a threatened category. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pediocactus sileri (Engelm. ex J.M.Coult.) L.D.Benson sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_threat_names: Nomadic grazing; Mining & quarrying; Tourism & recreation areas; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in scattered areas along the Utah-Arizona border, from the vicinity of Fredonia, Coconino County, to an area south and southeast of St. George, Utah in Mohave County, Arizona and at a few places in Washington and Kane County, Utah. It occurs at elevations of 900 to 1,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on salt desert scrub dominated by Atriplex.
iucn_doc_threats: The main threats are gypsum strip-mining, ORVs, grazing and trampling animals stepping on plants, overcollection, and mining. However, these threats are mitigated in part of the range by management plans. The Utah population could be impacted by the Warner Valley Power Project.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pediocactus sileri has a relatively small range, however only parts of it are threatened, it is protected and managed. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pediocactus winkleri K.D.Heil sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_synonyms: Pediocactus bradyi L.D.Benson ssp. winkleri (K.D.Heil) Hochstätter
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the state of Utah, US, where it grows at elevations of 1,400 to 1,900 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs on clay substartes often high in gypsum, frequently covered with gravel or oyster shell. It occurs in desert shrub and pinyon-juniper forests.
iucn_doc_threats: Some of the major threats are gypsum mining and off road vehicle activity.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a narrow range, it occurs in a very isolated areas. The species has several protected and monitored populations. More subpopulations have been discovered in the last years which mitigates the threats. Therefore the species is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, occurring in the states of Michoacán and possibly in Guerrero.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in dry forest.
iucn_doc_threats: Expanding cattle and agricultural activities continue to threatened the species across its range and result in loss of subpopulations and mature individuals. Cattle also eat the plants and result in direct mortality.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is considered to be Endangered due to a small populations size (ca 2,000 mature individuals). The subpopulations are small with 10 mature individuals in average. The population is severely fragmented and many threats affect it causing declines in subpopulations and number of mature individuals. The species range is small (extent of occurrence approximately 1,000 km2).

checklist_taxon: Pereskia diaz-romeroana Cárdenas sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Bolivia, where it occurs in Cochabamba, Chuquisaca and Santa Cruz at altitudes between 1,100 to 1,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus is an integral component of the forests in the seasonally dry inter-Andean valleys.
iucn_doc_threats: There is no known threat to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pereskia diaz-romeroana is listed as Least Concern because it is common within its range. There are not many localities because it is usually not recognized as a cactus due to its large size and woody trunk, and because it grows inside the forest and not on forest edges. Furthermore, it is not exposed to any major threats.

Pereskia horrida (Kunth) DC. sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Agro-industry farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: Pereskia horrida is endemic to Peru. It occurs in the Amazonas, Cajamarca and La Libertad regions, at altitudes of 450 to 580 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This is one of the most primitive cacti. It can be found in dry forests.
iucn_doc_threats: Agricultural activities and human sprawl are threatening the natural habitat of this cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pereskia horrida has been observed in two separate locations, but the area between them has not been explored. It is common where it occurs. The quality and quantity of suitable habitat are declining due to human sprawl and agricultural activities, but only a minority of the population is affected by these human activities and the population as a whole is considered to be stable. Pereskia horrida is therefore classified as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_range: This species has been recorded from the valley of the Rio São Francisco, in western and central-southern Bahia and central-northern Minas Gerais. It grows at altitudes between 450 and 750 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus prefers calcareous habitat and grows on limestone soil in caatinga, but also extends to surrounding areas.
iucn_doc_threats: A minor threat to the species is mining where it occurs on limestone soil.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pereskia stenantha is fairly widespread and common. It has been taken into cultivation as a hedge plant in the region where it is native. Furthermore, it is not exposed to any major threat. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

Pereskiopsis blakeana J.G.Ortega sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it is distributed in the states of Durango and Sinaloa. It grows at elevations of 50 to 300 m asl (CONABIO 2001).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits tropical deciduous forest (CONABIO 2001).
iucn_doc_threats: This species is tolerant to some degree of habitat modification as it can survive in margins of fields, tree fall, and the like. However, there is habitat loss within the range that is suspected to be causing a decline - but this is not considered to be a major threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pereskiopsis blakeana is listed as Least Concern as it is tolerant to some degree of habitat modification (it can survive in margins of fields and tree fall) and thus does not appear to be declining at a rate sufficient to qualify for listing in a threatened category.

checklist_taxon: Pereskiopsis kellermanii Rose sec. Hunt 2006
iucn_locations: Guatemala; Veracruz; Mexico; Campeche; Oaxaca; Chiapas; Morelos; Yucatán
iucn_location_codes: GT; MXG-VC; MX; MXT-CA; MXS-OA; MXT-CI; MXC-MO; MXT-YU
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry farming; Housing & urban areas; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Agro-industry grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: The species is found  in the Mexican states of Campeche, Chiapas, Morelos, Oaxaca, Veracruz and Yucatán, and in Guatemala in the deparments of Jalapa and Santa Rosa, at elevations ranging from 0 to 1,250 m (CONABIO 2001; Arias and Véliz 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: Pereskipsis kellermanii is known from tropical deciduous forests (CONABIO 2001). In the region of Nizanda the species grows exclusively on karstic hills with tropical deciduous forest that grows on the exposed rock and xerophyllous scrub (Pérez-García et al. 2001). The species flowers between July and August. Pollination studies suggest that the species is auto-incompatable and that most individuals are derived from vegetative reproduction (Salazar et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_threats: Deforestation and habitat destruction are major threats for this species particularly in Yucatán. The deforestation is mainly cause by the clearing of land for the cultivation of Henequén for fiber. In the past, farming was conducted on an industrial scale, but is now small-holder. One of the known populations of Pereskiopsis kellermanii in the state of Yucatán disappeared as a consequence of the expansion of the urban area (CONABIO 2001).
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is listed as Least Concern because of its relatively wide range and overall abundance, however there are some populations, like those found in Yucatán, which are in need of urgent conservation action because of their rapid decline.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Bahia, Minas Gerais, Piauí, Ceará, Rio Grande do Norte, Paraíba, Pernambuco and Alagoas. It occurs at elevations from 50 m -1,550 m (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in central-southern caatinga/ northern campo rupestre element: on quartzitic outcrops and in scrub associated with the  campos rupestres, and locally co-dominant with other  woody vegetation in caatinga, on stony ground within and on either side of the Chapada Diamantina and in sandy or rocky substrate (including sandstones and gneissic inselbergs) (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat to this species is habitat loss, mainly due to the expansion of agricultural areas (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is wide ranging, is abundant, it occurs within protected areas and the threats are only localised. Therefore, is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus tillianus R.Gruber & Schatzl sec. Franck & al. 2019
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Lagunillas, Merida state, Venezuela (Figueredo et al. 2010, Hokche et al. 2008).  It is known from one locality in Lagunillas in an intra-Andean xeric patch.  It occurs from 600-1200 m (Hokche et al. 2008).  The extent of occurrence, which is equal to the area of the distribution of the Lagunillas' xeric patch, is 262 km2 (Soriano and Ruiz 2002).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs in flatlands and slopes within the intra-Andean xeric patch. It is predominantly an outcrossing species but partially self-compatible.  It is bat-pollinated (Nassar et al. 1997) and bats and birds disperse the seeds (Soriano and Ruiz 2002).  It is autotetraploid (Figueredo et al. 2010).
iucn_doc_threats: The main threat to this species is urban expansion into the only location.  A secondary threat is land use change for the agricultural production of sugar cane and corn.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has a very restricted distribution (extent of occurrence is approximately 262 km<sup>2</sup>) and it is known from only one location which is under high pressure from urban and agricultural development.

checklist_taxon: Pilosocereus ulei (K.Schum.) Byles & G.D.Rowley sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, occurring in Rio de Janeiro (Hunt et al. 2006). It is known from only two localities on the coast of Rio de Janeiro. It occurs from sea level up to 100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species occurs on rocky coastal cliffs, near sea level up to 100 m asl in Cabo Frio, Rio de Janeiro (Zappi 1994).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats to this species are coastal development and tourism.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has a very restricted distribution (extent of occurrence is approximately 200 km<sup>2</sup>) and there is continuing decline in the extent and quality of its habitat. Although it occurs in a protected area, this is a marine protected area that allows extraction of resources and is not limiting the housing within it.

iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on sandy plains of the western "Patagonia" mountains (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: No threats exist to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Pterocactus araucanus is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, is locally abundant, is present in several protected areas and is not facing any threats.

iucn_doc_rationale: Pterocactus tuberosus is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide range, there are no major threats, it is not abundant but the population is stable, and it occurs in protected areas.

Punotia lagopus (K.Schum.) D.R.Hunt sec. Hunt 2011
iucn_synonyms: Austrocylindropuntia malyana (Rausch) F.Ritter; Opuntia lagopus K.Schum; Austrocylindropuntia lagopus (K.Schum.) I.Crook, J.Arnold & M.Lowry
iucn_doc_range: The species is mainly distributed in Perú in Puno, specifically northeast of Macusani (Hunt et al. 2006), but it also occurs in Imata, Arequipa (Perú). A separate subpopulation has been reported from La Paz, Bolivia, but this information requires verification as it is very distant from Macusani and no coordinates are available. The species occurs from an altitude of 4,100 m to approximately 4,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in humid puna grasslands.
iucn_doc_threats: Collection does not occur due to its size. In Imata (Perú), the species has been affected by the construction of Pillones dam related to the mining industry; it was not flooded, but rather destroyed by the vehicular movement related to the construction. The same happened in Melgar (Puno).
iucn_doc_rationale: As the existence of the Bolivian subpopulation is uncertain, this assessment only considers the Peruvian distribution. This species has a restricted range (Extent of Occurrence is estimated to be 2,200km<sup>2</sup>), and is threatened by dam construction related to mining activities. Because of this, the populations as well as the quality of its habitat are declining. This cactus is therefore classified as Vulnerable.

iucn_doc_rationale: Rebutia steinbachii has a relatively wide range (its extent of occurrence is 14,000 km2), is abundant, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to southeastern Brazil and is found in southern Minas Gerais (a disjunct occurrence), and southern Espírito Santo (Domingos Martins) along the coast to Rio de Janeiro (Mun. Nova Friburgo). It occurs from sea level up to 900 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: An epiphyte found in the southern humid forest. It has also been recorded growing on the roofs of houses and on a cliff face.
iucn_doc_threats: Two of the sites have been destroyed by expanding urbanization and coastal tourism developments and this is likely to happen in the other areas apart from one locality which is protected.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a very small area of occupancy (less than 500 km<sup>2</sup>), its range is severely fragmented, and there is continuing decline due to the close proximity of all the known sites to urban and tourism developments. The species is therefore listed as Endangered. Population surveys are required as the population could be quite small.

iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Intentional use (species is the target)
iucn_doc_range: This species is distributed in the Mexican state of Sonora and in the United States in southern Arizona. It occurs at elevations of 450 to 1,500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in granite soil hills, alluvial soil areas with chipping, and small rocks in Sonoran desert scrub. Sometimes it grows below small scrubs. In southeastern Arizona it occurs in rocky areas, in grasslands and desert scrub (Paredes et al. 2000).
iucn_doc_threats: Grazing is a threat in portions of the species range. Illegal collection takes place in some populations, however other populations are inaccessible.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus erectocentrus has a wide range, and even though there have been reductions in some of the subpopulations, other subpopulations are very healthy and there are no major threats. In addition, there is evidence that one of the most affected subpopulations is part of a more widely spread species, S. johnstonii, but it needs taxonomic revision (see taxonomic notes). Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_common_name: White Fish-hook Cactus; Biznaga-bola Entretejida; White Fishhook Cactus

iucn_location_codes: MXN-SO; MX; US; ARI-OO; TEX-OO; MXE-CU; NWM-OO
iucn_doc_range: The species is found in the Mexican states of Chihuahua and Sonora, and in Arizona, New Mexico and Texas in the USA. It grows at elevations of 900 to 1,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in grasslands and xerophyllous scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus intertextus has a very large range, is abundant, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus johnsonii (Parry ex Engelm.) N.P.Taylor sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_common_name: Johnson's Barrel Cactus; Johnson's Fishhook Cactus; Pygmy Barrel Cactus; Eight-spine Fishhook Cactus
iucn_locations: Arizona; Nevada; Utah; California; United States
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Tourism & recreation areas; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in California (Inyo County), to southwestern Utah, northwest Arizona and southern Nevada (United States). It grows at elevations of 650 to 2,000 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs in Mojave Desert scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by mining, grazing, and off-highway vehicle activity.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus johnsonii has a relatively wide range, is abundant, occurs in protected areas, and threats are not major. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus mesae-verdae (Boissev. & C.Davidson) L.D.Benson sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_common_name: Mesa Verde Fishhook Cactus; Mesa Verde Cactus
iucn_threat_names: Tourism & recreation areas; Droughts; Named species; Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying; Oil & gas drilling
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs only in Colorado and New Mexico, United States, at elevations between 1,480 to 1,660 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This globose species grows in the desert on dry low exposed hills and mesas in full sun (NatureServe 2006).
iucn_doc_threats: The main past threats were predation by the cactus borer beetle Moneilema semipunctatum and droughts, however, the population recovered after those events. Illegal collection, energy development, potential oil and gas drilling, pipeline construction, mining, and off-road vehicles also pose threats to the species (NatureServe 2006).
iucn_doc_rationale: There have been declines in the past but the population of Sclerocactus mesae-verdae has recovered and is under management over its range, with the exception of Colorado. The species extent of occurrence is approximately 3,200 km2, but there are more than ten locations and its not severely fragmented. In addition, there are more subpopulations on private land which are presumably stable. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus nyensis Hochstätter sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_doc_range: This species is known only from Nye and Esmeralda Counties, Nevada in the United States (Hochstaeter 1992), at elevations of 1,500 to 1,700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs on volcanic tuff and other volcanic deposits, on hills and slopes in saltbush-dominated desert.
iucn_doc_threats: Gold mining is a major threat, and accompanying construction such as tracks and Off-Highway Vehicles.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a narrow range (extent of occurrence is 1,300 km<sup>2</sup>), it has been declining due to mining activities and has no protection. Hence is listed as Endagered.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus papyracanthus (Engelm.) N.P.Taylor sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_common_name: Paperspine Fishhook Cactus; Biznaga-bola de Espinas de Papel; Grama Grass Cactus
iucn_locations: New Mexico; Texas; Arizona; United States
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the United States, where it occurs in Arizona, New Mexico and Texas. It grows at elevations of 1,000 to 2,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in red sandy soils of open flats in desert grasslands, often among perennial bunchgrasses such as blue grama (Bouteloua gracilis), galleta (Hilaria jamesii; Heil et al. 1981), Sporobolus airoides, Pleuraphis muticus and juniper-pinyon woodlands.
iucn_doc_threats: Land use change for urbanization has affected several populations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus papyracanthus has a relatively wide range, is abundant, has no major threats, is artificially propagated, and occurs within protected areas. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus pubispinus (Engelm.) L.D.Benson sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Mining & quarrying
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the United States. It occurs in Nevada along the eastern edges of Elko and White Pine Counties, and in western Utah from Box Elder to Beaver County. It grows at elevations of 1,400 to 2,100 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on limestone and dolomite gravels in sage brush, juniper-pynion and shade scale.
iucn_doc_threats: Mining and grazing are moderate threats in portions of the species range.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus pubispinus has a relatively wide range, it is very abundant, and there are no overall threats to the species. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus sileri (L.D.Benson) K.D.Heil & J.M.Porter sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_synonyms: Sclerocactus pubispinus Engelm.) L.D.Benson var. sileri L.D.Benson
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Temperate; Grassland - Temperate; Forest - Temperate
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Intentional use (species is the target); Tourism & recreation areas; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in north central Arizona, in the United States, in Coconino County, at elevations of 1,600 to 1,700 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on sandy and clay soils, in Grama grasslands, sage brush and pinyon-juniper woodlands.
iucn_doc_threats: Livestock grazing, fires, off-highway-vehicle (OHV) activity and over collecting are major threats to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species has a narrow range (extent of occurrence is approximately 1,000 km<sup>2</sup>). There is an estimated 25% population reduction in the last 8 years and this is very likely to reach 30% in the next four years if the threats continue to affect the population in the same way. The species has a generation length of four years (to first flower). Hence it is listed as Vulnerable.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus wetlandicus Hochstätter sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Oil & gas drilling; Tourism & recreation areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Utah, United States, where it occurs in Uintah and  Duchesne Counties. It grows at elevations of 1,300 to 1,800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species occurs on gravel terraces in desert shrub and desert grassland.
iucn_doc_threats: The species is threatened by oil and gas exploration, grazing, and off-highway vehicle activity.
iucn_doc_rationale: Sclerocactus wettlandicus has a very narrow range (extent of occurrence is 1,800 km2), however there are no current declines, it is protected and managed. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Sclerocactus wrightiae L.D.Benson sec. Parfitt & Gibson 2003
iucn_threat_names: Mining & quarrying; Tourism & recreation areas; Intentional use (species is the target); Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in Utah in Wayne and Emery Counties, at elevations of 1,000 to 1,800 m.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows on alkaline gravel or clay hills in desert shrub.
iucn_doc_threats: Mineral exploration, potential industrial use of habitat, off road vehicles, overcollecting and grazing are major threats. Hybridazation with S. parviflorus appears to be a serious threat to portions of the population.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species has a narrow range (extent of occurrence is approximately 1,700 km<sup>2</sup>) and there are continuing declines however there are more than 30 subpopulations known and likely to increase (more than ten locations). Hence is listed as Near Threatened as in nearly qualified for Vulnerable (B1a; B1b(iii), (iv)).

Stenocereus humilis (Britton & Rose) D.R.Hunt sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Roads & railroads; Scale Unknown/Unrecorded; Mining & quarrying; Motivation Unknown/Unrecorded
iucn_doc_range: Stenocereus humilis is endemic to the Dagua Valley, Colombia (Hunt et al. 2006). It grows in a 14 km stretch in the Dagua Valley between Orten Dagua and Lobo Guerrero at elevations between 700 and 1,000 m asl. The extent of occurrence and area of occupancy of this species is estimated to be approximately 28 km2 (calculated by multiplying the length of the valley where it occurs (14 km) by the width (2 km), measured using Google maps).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species inhabits dry tropical/subtropical habitats. It forms dense thickets.
iucn_doc_threats: The valley where the species occurs is traversed by a main road/highway and there are several exploitation activities in the municipality of Dagua (population 40,000), mainly agriculture, livestock, forestry, and mining. Therefore, it is very likely that this species is impacted by some these activities.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Endangered because it has a very restricted distribution with an estimated range of 30 km<sup>2</sup>, it is known from only one location, and there are potential threats that may be causing continuous decline in the extent and quality of the habitat. Further research is needed to better determine its population size and trends and the effect of the potential threats.

Stenocereus quevedonis (J.G.Ortega) Buxb. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Food - human; Establishing ex-situ production *; Construction or structural materials; Food - animal
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Guerrero and Michoacán (Guzmán et al. 2003). However, the species seems to have disappeared from the type locality situated in the state of Sinaloa (Hunt et al. 2006), as it has not been found recently given that most of the land has been transformed for agriculture. In Michoacán, the species is widely distributed and abundant in the region of El Infiernillo dam (Arreola-Nava and Terrazas 2003). It grows at elevations of 100 to 500 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits xerophyllous scrub and tropical deciduous forest (Arreola-Nava and Terrazas 2003).
iucn_doc_threats: Free-ranging cattle are responsible for removing the fruit crop where they are left to pasture.
iucn_doc_rationale: Stenocereus quevedonis is listed as Least Concern as it is relatively common and occurs across a large range. There is a need to better understand the effects of use and trade of the fruits, but this is not currently a major threat (but could be in the near future).

checklist_taxon: Stenocereus treleasei (Rose) Backeb. sec. Guzmán Cruz & al. 2003
iucn_habitat_names: Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_use_trade_names: Fuels; Construction or structural materials; Food - human; Food - animal
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican states of Oaxaca and Puebla, where it occurs at elevations of 1,300 to 1,800 m asl (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species inhabits tropical deciduous forest and xerophyllous scrub, it is also cultivated in orchards and gardens (Arias et al. 1997).
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Stenocereus treleasei is abundant and tolerated in agricultural lands because of its fruits. It is also used as living fence, which as a result keeps the population stable. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks); Forest - Subtropical/Tropical Dry
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder farming; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Mining & quarrying; Named species
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Rio Jequitinhonha valley, north-eastern Minas Gerais in Brazil (Taylor and Zappi 2004). It occurs from 170 - 350 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus species is considered a south-eastern caatinga (inselberg) element, it grows on gneiss/granite outcrops/inselbergs (Taylor and Zappi 2004). This plant is a semi-creeping shrub. Its generation length is around 10 years.
iucn_doc_threats: Mining of the granite outcrops is the main threat for Tacinga braunii. Destruction of the surrounding caatinga for agricultural activities (crops and cattle ranching) pose an additional threat to the species. African pasture grasses (Brachiaria ssp., Pennisetum ssp., Bromus ssp.) are planted in the area for cattle fodder, and this is also affecting the populations.
iucn_doc_rationale: This species is listed as Vulnerable because it has a very restricted extent of occurrence (EOO = 1,515 km²), there are probably only six locations (certainly less than 10), and it is experiencing declines due to habitat destruction.

iucn_location_codes: BZE-BA; BZE-CE; BZE-AL; BZE-PB; BZE-RN; BZE-SE; BZE-PE; BR
iucn_habitat_names: Shrubland - Subtropical/Tropical Dry; Rocky areas (eg. inland cliffs, mountain peaks)
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Unintentional effects: (subsistence/small scale) [harvest]; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to north-eastern Brazil, where it occurs in Rio Grande do Norte, Paraíba, Pernambuco, Alagoas, Sergipe, Ceará and Bahia at altitudes from 200 to 1,020 m asl (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This eastern caatinga element grows in caatinga, agreste and carrasco. It is frequent on deep sandy substrates but not restricted to these. Generation length is 7 years, taking into account plants from seeds as well as clonal reproduction.
iucn_doc_threats: The collection of the species by local people for sale, mining, and deforestation of the caatinga for agriculture (crops and livestock) are the main threats to the species.
iucn_doc_rationale: There are no more than 1,000 mature individuals of Tacinga subcylindrica, with less than 100 individuals per subpopulation. The extent of occurrence is approximately 3,700 km2, the population is severely fragmented, and habitat quality as well as the number of mature individuals are declining. The main threats to the species are collection as well as habitat destruction by mining and deforestation for agricultural purposes. A population decline of 30 percent has occurred over the last two generations and is assumed to continue at an even higher rate. The threats are known, but are not reversible. The species is therefore assessed as Endangered.

checklist_taxon: Tephrocactus abditus D.J.Ferguson & Janeba sec. Janeba & Ferguson 2019
Tephrocactus aoracanthus Lem. sec. Las Peñas & al. 2019
iucn_locations: Catamarca; San Juan; La Rioja; Mendoza; Argentina
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina. It is present in the provinces of La Rioja, Mendoza, San Juan and Catamarca and grows at altitudes between 400 and 800 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in Monte habitat.
iucn_doc_threats: No threats exist to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Tephrocactus aoracanthus is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide distribution, is locally abundant, is present within protected areas and is not affected by any threat.

checklist_taxon: Tephrocactus bonnieae (D.J.Ferguson & R.Kiesling) Stuppy sec. Las Peñas & al. 2019
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it occurs in Catamarca at elevations of 2,500 to 3,300 m asl. The extent of occurrence is about 20 km². The range may extend to the tops of the hills or even higher up the mountains south of the present location, but this needs verification.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in puna scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: This cactus is threatened by illegal collecting. However, the type locality is no longer on the main route (a new road was made and the old one abandoned), and the other places where  T. bonnieae was found again are unknown to others (R. Kiesling pers. comm. 2010). It is also difficult to find the plants because of their geophytic habit.
iucn_doc_rationale: Tephrocactus bonniae is listed as Endangered because it has a very restricted range (extent of occurrence approximately 20km2) and it certainly has less than five locations. The population is sacked by collectors, hence a continuing decline of mature individuals is inferred. In 2002 the species was categorized as Endangered by criterion D, however considering the plants are buried underground for most of the year it is difficult to estimate the population size. With new information on the geographic range of the species (i.e. the discovery of new subpopulations), the assessors consider the species to be better assessed under criteria B1 and B2.

iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on sandy and rocky soils, at the highest elevations of the Monte habitat.
iucn_doc_threats: No threats exist to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Tephrocactus molinensis is listed as Least Concern because it is abundant and is not exposed to any threats.

Tephrocactus weberi (Speg.) Backeb. sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Tucumán; Catamarca; Salta; San Juan; La Rioja; Argentina
iucn_location_codes: AGW-TU; AGW-CA; AGW-SA; AGW-SJ; AGW-LR; AR
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Argentina, where it can be found in the provinces of Catamarca, Salta, La Rioja, San Juan and Tucumán. It occurs from 500 to 2,200 m asl (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows on dry, stony hills in monte habitat (Kiesling and Ferrari 2009).
iucn_doc_threats: No threats exist to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Tephrocactus weberi is listed as Least Concern because it has a wide distribution, is locally abundant, is present in many protected areas and is not affected by any threat.

checklist_taxon: Thelocactus buekii (Klein bis) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_use_trade_names: Pets/display animals, horticulture; Establishing ex-situ production *
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican state of Nuevo León (Hunt et al. 2006).
iucn_doc_habitats: Grows in xerophytic shrubland often on gypsum soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species, although there may be overgrazing of the habitat by goats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Thelocactus buekii has a restricted range (its extent of occurrence is less than 5,000 km²). However, the species may be common, there are no major threats, and no population declines are known. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Thelocactus hastifer (Werderm. & Boed.) F.M.Knuth sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Dams (size unknown); Commercial & industrial areas
iucn_doc_range: This cactus occurs in Querétaro, Mexico near Mesa de León in an area of about 400 km<sup>2</sup>. It is distributed in a narrow elevation band from 1,800 to 2,200 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in semi-desert (matorral in a disjunct extension of the Chihuahuan Desert) on calcareous soil.
iucn_doc_threats: Illegal collecting is a threat. The construction of a dam (in 2007) and the surrounding infrastructure has caused a serious ongoing decline.
iucn_doc_rationale: Thelocactus hastifer has a limited range of 400 km² in no more than four locations. There is continuing decline in the number of individuals due to illegal collecting and in the past 3 to 4 years there has been an ongoing decline in several subpopulations due to the construction of a dam and aqueduct system, where the road system installed to maintain the infrastructure has caused a major degradation of habitat and reduction in mature individuals. Hence, this cactus is listed as Endangered. The species might also qualify as Endangered under criterion D, as it seems the population size is fairly small (< 250 mature individuals), however, research is needed in order to accurately estimate the population size.

checklist_taxon: Thelocactus lausseri Říha & Busek sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_doc_range: The species occurs in the Mexican state of Coahuila (Hernández et al. 2004), and is only known from the type collection made at Sierra de las Ovejas (the species was described in 1986).
iucn_doc_habitats: Grows presumably in xerophytic shrubland on calcareous soils.
iucn_doc_threats: Given the small range of this species, illegal collection may pose a threat.
iucn_doc_rationale: The species is poorly known being only known from the type specimen with no information on its precise range, its population size and trends, or any threat. Illegal collection may pose a threat, and the exetent of occurrence may be very small, but without further information this species is assessed as Data Deficient.

checklist_fullName: Thelocactus leucacanthus (Zucc. ex Pfeiff.) Britton & Rose
checklist_taxon: Thelocactus leucacanthus (Zucc. ex Pfeiff.) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_threat_names: Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Nomadic grazing; Small-holder farming
iucn_doc_range: This species has been reported from Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Guanajuato, Hidalgo, and Querétaro (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: The species grows in matorral and xerophyllous scrub.
iucn_doc_threats: Grazing (trampling) and land-use changes (agriculture) threaten some subpopulations of this cactus.
iucn_doc_rationale: Thelocactus leucacanthus is listed as Least Concern because it is relatively widespread and abundant, and is not exposed to major threats.

checklist_fullName: Thelocactus multicephalus Halda & Panar. ex Halda
checklist_taxon: Thelocactus multicephalus Halda & Panar. ex Halda sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: San Luis Potosí; Nuevo León; Mexico
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to the Mexican states of Nuevo León (Hunt et al. 2006) and San Luis Potosí (M. Sotamayor pers. comm. 2009). It was only known from the type locality in Nuevo León but a disjunct subpopulation has now been found in San Luis Potosí.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows on hill slopes in xerophytic shrubland, where Agave species are dominant.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species, although the habitat is impacted by goats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Thelocactus multicephalus is known from two disjunct localities and it has a small extent of occurrence, but the species is abundant where it occurs and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Thelocactus rinconensis (Poselg.) Britton & Rose sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_use_trade_names: Establishing ex-situ production *; Pets/display animals, horticulture
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs in the states of Coahuila and Nuevo León (Hernández et al. 2004).
iucn_doc_habitats: This cactus grows in xerophytic shrubland on calcareous soils.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no major threats to this species, although the habitat is overgrazed by goats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Thelocactus rinconensis is widespread and quite common, and there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

checklist_taxon: Thelocactus setispinus (Engelm.) E.F.Anderson sec. Hunt 2016
iucn_locations: Coahuila; Nuevo León; Texas; San Luis Potosí; Mexico; Tamaulipas; United States
iucn_location_codes: MXE-CO; MXE-NL; TEX-OO; MXE-SL; MX; MXE-TA; US
iucn_doc_range: This species can be found in Mexico in the states of Nuevo León, San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas, and in Texas in the USA. It has also been reported from Coahuila from the literature (Hernández et al. 2004). It occurs at elevations of 300 m asl and below.
iucn_doc_habitats: The species is found in Tamaulipan thorn scrub. It grows on black or clay soils and leaf mold on hills and flats in grassland, usually in mesquite thickets.
iucn_doc_threats: There are no known major threats to this species.
iucn_doc_rationale: Thelocactus setispinus is widely distributed and, even though it is scattered and not abundant, there are no major threats. Hence, it is listed as Least Concern.

Uebelmannia buiningii Donald sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Intentional use (species is the target); Nomadic grazing; Increase in fire frequency/intensity
iucn_doc_range: Uebelmannia buiningii is found at ca 1,200 m asl on the Serra Negra in Minas Gerais, Brazil. It occurs in a single location according to Schulz and Machado (2000). The overall range is very limited (extent of occurrence is 40 km²).
iucn_doc_habitats: This species south-eastern campo rupestre (Serra Negra) element grows on slabs of quartzitic rock amongst gravel (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threat for this species is illegal collection. Uebelmannia buiningii is practically extinct in the wild and rare in cultivation, it has been indiscriminately collected (plants and seeds) and is threatened by fires and nomadic grazing.
iucn_doc_rationale: Uebelmannia buiningii is on the verge of extinction. It is affected by illegal collection of plants and seeds, fire, and cattle trampling. The population has been fragmented into a number of tiny subpopulations. Some subpopulations, including the type locality, have been collected to the point of extirpation. The overall range of the species is very limited (extent of occurrence is 40 km²).

checklist_taxon: Uebelmannia gummifera (Backeb. & Voll) Buining sec. Zappi & Taylor 2020+
iucn_threat_names: Agro-industry plantations; Mining & quarrying; Small-holder grazing, ranching or farming; Housing & urban areas
iucn_doc_range: This species is endemic to Brazil, where it is found in the state of Minas Gerais, in Serra Negra at elevations of 900–1,600 m asl.
iucn_doc_habitats: This species grows in south-eastern campo rupestre (Serra Negra / Serra do Ambrósio), in quartz sand (Taylor and Zappi 2004).
iucn_doc_threats: The major threats are sand quarrying,  cattle ranching, human developments, and Eucalyptus plantations.
iucn_doc_rationale: Although there are subpopulations with often large or very large numbers of individuals, the overall range of the species is very limited (extent of occurrence is 230 km2 and there are three to four locations) and there is a continuing decline of individuals as a result of the species habitat being affected by Eucalyptus plantations and collection of plants and seeds, among other threats. Hence is listed as Endangered.

iucn_doc_threats: The species has no known threats.
iucn_doc_rationale: Weberbauerocereus churinensis is known from a single locality, where it is abundant, and has no threats. Hence it is listed as Least Concern. Monitoring of the species is recommended for early detection of declines.
